[B01D 27/00] Cartridge filters of the throw-away type -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[B01D 27/005] Making filter elements not provided for elsewhere -- B01D 27/005 covers the manufacturing of disposable cartridge filters, detailing processes for forming, assembling and treating filter media that are not specified in other subclasses. It includes steps such as shaping the housing, inserting filter material, sealing and finishing the cartridge for use in liquid or gas separation operations.
[B01D 27/02] with cartridges made from a mass of loose material -- The B01D 27/02 CPC subclass covers disposable cartridge filters whose cartridges are filled with a bulk, loose-material filter medium such as granular activated carbon, sand or similar particles. These throw-away cartridges are designed for a single use, after which the whole unit is replaced rather than regenerated. This classification falls under the broader groups for performing operations, separating and separation.
[B01D 27/04] with cartridges made of a piece of unitary material, e.g. filter paper -- This subclass concerns disposable cartridge filters whose filter element is formed from a single piece of material such as filter paper. The cartridge is a throw-away type and does not contain separate supporting media.
[B01D 27/06] with corrugated, folded or wound material -- B01D 27/06 classifies disposable cartridge filters that incorporate a filtering medium formed from corrugated, folded or wound material. The code covers throw-away filter units where the structured media provide a high surface area for separating particulates from liquids or gases.
[B01D 27/07] having a coaxial stream through the filtering element -- This subclass relates to disposable cartridge filters in which the fluid to be cleaned passes coaxially through or around the filtering element, creating an axial stream within the cartridge. It falls under separation operations that employ throw-away filter cartridges.
[B01D 27/08] Construction of the casing -- B01D 27/08 covers the design and manufacture of the outer housing that holds the filtering media in single-use cartridge filters. It includes the selection of materials, shapes, seals and assembly methods for constructing disposable filter casings used in separation operations.
[B01D 27/10] Safety devices, e.g. by-passes -- This subclass covers safety devices such as bypass mechanisms incorporated in disposable cartridge filters to prevent over-pressure or blockage and to divert or shut off fluid flow. It relates to protective features used during the separation operation of liquids or gases.
[B01D 27/101] Filter condition indicators -- Filter condition indicators are devices that monitor the performance or remaining life of disposable cartridge filters, typically by measuring pressure drop, flow rate or other parameters that signal clogging or degradation. They are used in systems where cartridge filters of the throw-away type separate solids from liquids or gases, helping operators decide when to replace the filter to maintain efficiency.
[B01D 27/103] Bypass or safety valves -- The CPC subclass B01D 27/103 encompasses bypass or safety valves used with disposable cartridge filters. These devices provide pressure relief or alternate flow paths to prevent filter blockage or over-pressure in filtration systems. They are applied in operations where separating solids from liquids or gases is performed with throw-away filter cartridges.
[B01D 27/105] Bidirectional working filters -- Bidirectional working filters are disposable cartridge filters that separate particles from a fluid by allowing flow through the filter media in both directions, enhancing cleaning or processing efficiency. They fall under the B01D 27/105 code in the Cooperative Patent Classification, positioned within the hierarchy of performing operations, separating, and separation.
[B01D 27/106] Anti-leakage or anti-return valves -- This code covers anti-leakage or check valves incorporated in disposable cartridge filters used for separating solids from liquids or gases. The valves prevent fluid backflow or leakage when the filter is removed or when pressure reverses.
[B01D 27/108] Flow control valves; Damping or calibrated passages -- B01D 27/108 covers flow control valves that include damping or calibrated passages within disposable cartridge filters used for separating fluids. These valves regulate fluid movement to maintain a consistent pressure drop and enhance separation efficiency in single-use filtration systems, and the class falls under performing operations, separating, and separation of throw-away cartridge filters.
[B01D 27/14] having more than one filtering element -- B01D 27/14 pertains to disposable cartridge filters that contain two or more filtering elements in a single unit. The multiple elements can be arranged in series or parallel to enhance separation efficiency for liquids or gases, and it sits under the broader categories of performing operations, separating and separation.
[B01D 27/142] connected in parallel -- Cartridge filters of the throw-away type that are linked together in a parallel arrangement to increase flow capacity or provide redundancy during separation processes. This configuration allows multiple disposable filter cartridges to operate simultaneously, sharing the feed stream while maintaining the same pressure drop across each unit. It is used in chemical and industrial processes where continuous filtration is required without interrupting operation for filter change.
[B01D 27/144] arranged concentrically or coaxially -- The entry B01D 27/144 covers disposable cartridge filters whose filtering media are organized in concentric or coaxial layers, forming a cylindrical filter element that can be removed and replaced after use. This design falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and separation in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[B01D 27/146] connected in series -- B01D 27/146 covers arrangements in which disposable cartridge filters are linked one after another so that the feed passes through multiple filter media in series, increasing contaminant removal or extending service life. This subclass is used for describing filtration units where separate, replaceable cartridges are installed sequentially within a single system.
[B01D 27/148] arranged concentrically or coaxially -- The CPC subclass B01D 27/148 covers disposable cartridge filters whose filter elements are positioned concentrically or coaxially, meaning one filter medium is placed inside another along the same central axis. This arrangement enables staged separation of solids or liquids while maintaining a compact, replaceable unit.
[B01D 29/00] Filters with filtering elements stationary during filtration, e.g. pressure or suction filters, not covered by groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[B01D 29/0093] Making filtering elements not provided for elsewhere -- The subclass B01D 29/0093 covers methods for producing filter elements that are not otherwise specified, such as pressure- or suction-type filters where the filtering media remains stationary during operation. It includes manufacturing steps like forming, shaping, or assembling filter media and housings that are not described in other groups.
[B01D 29/0095] Flat filtering elements  B01D25/12 ,  B01D25/26  take precedence -- This CPC entry B01D 29/0095 covers flat filtering elements used in pressure or suction filtration where the filtering media remain stationary during operation, a category not addressed by other groups. The more specific subclasses B01D25/12 and B01D25/26 take precedence over this entry.
[B01D 29/0097] Curved filtering elements, e.g. concave filtering elements -- B01D 29/0097 covers filters that employ stationary, curved or concave filtering elements-such as concave plates, baskets or panels-where the fluid passes through the element by pressure or suction. These elements remain fixed in place during operation and are not classified in other more specific groups.
[B01D 29/01] with flat filtering elements -- Filters that use flat, stationary filtering elements such as sheets, plates or membranes during pressure or suction filtration, where the filtering media do not move relative to the housing and are not covered by other more specific groups.
[B01D 29/012] Making filtering elements  making bag, cage, hose, tube, sleeve or like filtering elements  B01D29/111 -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry B01D 29/012 pertains to the manufacturing of specific filtering elements such as bags, cages, hoses, tubes, and sleeves used in filtration systems where the filter remains stationary during operation. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with separation processes and equipment.
[B01D 29/014] with curved filtering elements  construction  B01D29/035 ,  B01D29/071 -- B01D 29/014 classifies filtration devices in which the filtering elements are curved and remain stationary during operation, such as pressure- or suction-type filters whose media is fixed in shape. It falls under the hierarchy Performing operations → Separating → Separation and covers arrangements not included in more specific groups.
[B01D 29/016] with corrugated, folded or wound filtering elements -- The subclass B01D 29/016 covers pressure or suction filters whose filtering elements stay stationary during operation and are made from corrugated, folded or wound media. Such designs use shaped or rolled filter sheets to increase surface area while the element itself does not move.
[B01D 29/018] ring shaped -- B01D 29/018 pertains to filters whose filtering elements remain stationary during operation, such as pressure- or suction-driven filters, and are fashioned in a ring-shaped configuration. These devices fall under the CPC hierarchy of Performing Operations → Separating → Separation.
[B01D 29/03] self-supporting -- A self-supporting filter in which the filtering medium stays stationary during operation, such as pressure or suction filters that are not covered by other groups. It is classified under performing operations → separating → separation.
[B01D 29/031] with corrugated, folded filtering elements -- B01D 29/031 covers filters that use stationary, corrugated or folded filtering elements during operation, such as pressure- or suction-type filters. The classification applies to devices where the filtering media remains fixed while the fluid passes through it.
[B01D 29/035] with curved filtering elements -- B01D 29/035 covers filtration devices that employ curved filtering elements while the filtering medium remains stationary during operation, such as pressure or suction filters not classified elsewhere. The curved shape is used to improve flow distribution or contaminant capture within the filter housing.
[B01D 29/036] ring shaped -- B01D 29/036 concerns ring-shaped filters whose filtering elements stay stationary during pressure or suction filtration, such as circular or annular filter components used in separation processes and not covered by other groups.
[B01D 29/038] with corrugated, folded filtering elements -- Filters that employ stationary filtering elements shaped into corrugated or folded forms to increase surface area, typically used in pressure or suction filtration systems where the filtering medium does not move during operation.
[B01D 29/05] supported -- B01D 29/05 covers filters whose filtering elements are held in a support structure while the fluid passes through, such as pressure-type or suction filters where the filter medium remains stationary. This subgroup is for supported filtration arrangements not classified in other more specific groups.
[B01D 29/055] ring shaped -- B01D 29/055 relates to ring-shaped filters in which the filtering element remains stationary during operation, such as pressure or suction filtration devices that are not covered by other groups. The classification addresses the design and use of these circular filter elements for separating solids from liquids or gases. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and separation.
[B01D 29/07] with corrugated, folded or wound filtering sheets -- Filters that employ stationary filtering elements formed from corrugated, folded or wound sheets, used in pressure or suction filtration where the sheet structure remains fixed while the fluid passes through. This subgroup covers designs not otherwise classified in other groups of B01D 29.
[B01D 29/071] with curved filtering elements  B01D29/072 ,  B01D29/073  take precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Ring shaped -> B01D29/072 - With wound filtering sheets -> B01D29/073
[B01D 29/072] ring shaped -- Ring-shaped filters in which the filtering media remain stationary during operation, typically employed in pressure or suction filtration processes that are not covered by more specific groups. The design provides a circular flow path for liquids or gases, allowing efficient separation while the filter element itself stays fixed.
[B01D 29/073] with wound filtering sheets -- B01D 29/073 covers filters that employ wound filtering sheets as stationary filtering elements, such as pressure or suction filters where the media does not move during operation. The classification applies to filtration devices using these wound sheets and not covered by other groups within the separation hierarchy.
[B01D 29/075] located in a closed housing and comprising scrapers or agitators on the cake side of the filtering elements, e.g. Nutsche- or Rosenmund-type filters for performing multiple step operations such as chemical reactions, filtering and cake treatment -- If the subject matter classified in this group also contains relevant information covered by other subgroups of group B01D29/00 , it is also classified in the other appropriate subgroups of group B01D29/00 .
[B01D 29/085] Funnel filters -- Funnel filters are stationary-element filters that operate under pressure or suction, using a funnel-shaped housing to collect solid particles from liquids. They fall within B01D 29/085, the class for filters with fixed media not covered by other groups, under the broader categories of performing operations, separating and separation.
[B01D 29/09] with filtering bands, e.g. movable between filtering operations -- B01D 29/09 refers to filtration devices that use movable filtering bands while the filter support stays stationary, typical of pressure or suction filters not classified elsewhere. The subclass falls under Performing Operations - Separating - Separation and addresses systems where the band can be shifted between filtration cycles.
[B01D 29/093] combined with means to fasten the opposite edges of the filtering band together, e.g. Zipper -- This subclass covers filtration devices in which the filter media is a band whose opposite edges are fastened together, typically by a zipper or similar fastening means, forming a continuous sealed filter element. It applies to pressure or suction filters where the filtering band remains stationary during operation.
[B01D 29/096] Construction of filtering bands or supporting belts, e.g. devices for centering, mounting or sealing the filtering bands or the supporting belts -- B01D 29/096 covers the construction of filtering bands or supporting belts, including devices for centering, mounting or sealing these elements in stationary filtration systems such as pressure or suction filters.
[B01D 29/11] with bag, cage, hose, tube, sleeve or like filtering elements -- B01D 29/11 covers filtration apparatus where the filtering material is a bag, cage, hose, tube, sleeve or similar element that remains stationary while the liquid or gas passes through, typical of pressure- or suction-type filters not classified elsewhere.
[B01D 29/111] Making filtering elements -- The subclass covers processes for producing stationary filtering elements used in pressure or suction filters, such as filter media, cartridges, or frames. It includes shaping, assembling, bonding, impregnating or coating the media to create functional filter elements, but excludes methods already classified in other groups.
[B01D 29/112] Ring shaped filters wherein both opposite axial sides are opened and the axial length is shorter than the diameter, e.g. as used in hydraulic transmission systems -- Ring-shaped filters with both axial sides opened and an axial length shorter than the diameter, such as those used in hydraulic transmission systems, are classified under B01D 29/112. This entry falls within the CPC hierarchy for performing operations, separating, and stationary filtration elements not covered by other groups.
[B01D 29/114] arranged for inward flow filtration  B01D29/15 ,  B01D29/33  take precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Arranged for inward filtration -> B01D29/15 - Arranged for inward filtration -> B01D29/33
[B01D 29/115] open-ended, the arrival of the mixture to be filtered and the discharge of the concentrated mixture are situated on both opposite sides of the filtering element -- B01D 29/115 defines open-ended filtration apparatus in which the incoming mixture and the discharged concentrated stream are positioned on opposite sides of a stationary filtering element, typical of pressure or suction filters not covered by other groups.
[B01D 29/117] arranged for outward flow filtration  B01D29/23 ,  B01D29/35  take precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Arranged for outward filtration -> B01D29/23 - Arranged for outward filtration -> B01D29/35
[B01D 29/118] open-ended -- Open-ended pressure or suction filters in which the filtering elements remain stationary during operation, used for separating solids from liquids or gases, and not falling within any more specific sub-groups of the B01D 29 series. This covers designs where the filter housing is open at one end to receive material, with filtration driven by external pressure or vacuum.
[B01D 29/13] Supported filter elements -- This subclass covers filter arrangements where the filtering medium stays stationary while fluid passes through, such as pressure-driven or suction-driven filters that use a support structure to hold the filter element and are not classified elsewhere.
[B01D 29/15] arranged for inward flow filtration -- This subgroup covers filters whose filtering elements remain stationary while the material to be filtered is drawn inward across them, as in pressure or suction filters that pull fluid into the filter medium. It falls under performing operations, separating, separation.
[B01D 29/17] open-ended -- B01D 29/17 open-ended refers to filters in which the filtering element remains stationary during operation, such as pressure or suction filters, that are not classified in more specific groups. These devices have an open-ended configuration allowing material to pass through the fixed filter medium.
[B01D 29/19] on solid frames with surface grooves or the like -- B01D 29/19 covers filters whose stationary filtering elements are mounted on solid frames that feature surface grooves, channels or similar patterns. The grooved frame assists fluid flow, distribution or support of the media in pressure or suction filtration systems not otherwise classified.
[B01D 29/21] with corrugated, folded or wound sheets -- This subgroup covers filters that use stationary corrugated, folded or wound sheet materials as the filtering element, typically employed in pressure or suction filtration where the media remains fixed in place. It falls under B01D 29, which addresses filters with filtering elements stationary during filtration.
[B01D 29/213] having a concertina shape -- The code B01D 29/213 designates filters whose filtering elements are stationary during operation-such as pressure or suction filters-and are formed with a concertina, or folded, shape to increase surface area within a compact volume.
[B01D 29/216] with wound sheets -- Filters in which the filtering medium consists of a continuous sheet that has been wound into a cylindrical or spiral form, remaining stationary while fluid is forced through by pressure or suction. This subclass covers pressure or vacuum filters that employ wound sheet media not otherwise classified.
[B01D 29/23] arranged for outward flow filtration -- B01D 29/23 covers filtration devices in which the filter media remains fixed while the fluid is forced outward through it, such as pressure-driven or suction filters that are not classified elsewhere. These arrangements are used for separating solids from liquids or gases by directing the flow from the interior of the filter toward the exterior.
[B01D 29/232] with corrugated, folded or wound sheets -- This subclass covers filter devices in which the filtering medium consists of corrugated, folded or wound sheets that remain fixed while fluid is forced through, such as pressure or suction filters. The sheets serve as stationary filtering elements and are not included in other more specific groups within the separation hierarchy.
[B01D 29/235] having a concertina shape -- Filters whose stationary filtering elements are shaped like a concertina, i.e., a series of parallel folds that expand the effective filtration area while keeping the element fixed in the filter housing. This form is used in pressure or suction filters that are not otherwise covered by more specific CPC groups.
[B01D 29/237] with wound sheets -- B01D 29/237 covers filtration arrangements in which the filtering elements stay stationary while the fluid is forced through, such as pressure- or suction-driven filters, and the media consists of wound sheets. It applies to filter designs that are not captured by more specific groups within the separation hierarchy.
[B01D 29/25] open-ended -- Open-ended filters in which the filtering element remains stationary while fluid is forced through by pressure or suction, such as pressure-driven or suction-driven filters, are covered by this class. It pertains to filtration arrangements that do not fall within more specific groups of the CPC hierarchy.
[B01D 29/27] Filter bags -- B01D 29/27 pertains to filter bags that serve as stationary filtering elements in pressure or suction filtration systems, retaining solids while allowing fluid to pass through. They are used in separation operations not covered by more specific CPC groups.
[B01D 29/31] Self-supporting filtering elements -- Self-supporting filtering elements are filtration media that retain their shape and position without a supporting frame while fluid passes through, typically used in pressure or suction filters where the filter material itself forms a stable structure. These elements are designed for stationary operation and are not covered by other subgroups of the separation category.
[B01D 29/33] arranged for inward flow filtration -- B01D 29/33 covers filters in which the filtration medium remains stationary while the fluid to be cleaned flows inwardly toward the filtering surface, typical of pressure- or suction-driven devices that are not classified elsewhere. This falls under Performing Operations → Separating → Separation and pertains to filtration arrangements where the filtering elements do not move during operation.
[B01D 29/333] with corrugated, folded filtering elements -- This subclass covers filters whose filtering elements stay stationary during operation, such as pressure- or suction-type filters, and specifically those that use corrugated or folded filter media to increase surface area and improve flow. It applies where the arrangement is not covered by other, more specific groups.
[B01D 29/336] open-ended, the arrival of the mixture to be filtered and the discharge of the concentrated mixture are situated on both opposite sides of the filtering element -- This group covers filtration devices in which the feed mixture enters from one side of a stationary filter element and the concentrated filtrate exits from the opposite side, creating an open-ended flow path. It applies to pressure or suction filters not classified elsewhere.
[B01D 29/35] arranged for outward flow filtration -- A filter type in which the filtering elements remain stationary while the fluid is forced outward, such as pressure-driven or suction filters that are not covered by other groups, classified under performing operations, separating, separation.
[B01D 29/353] with corrugated, folded filtering elements -- B01D 29/353 relates to stationary filters that employ corrugated or folded filtering elements to increase surface area, such as pressure- or suction-type filters, within the broader separation category of performing operations. This subclass covers designs where the filter media are shaped into folds or corrugations but remain fixed during the filtration process.
[B01D 29/356] open-ended, the arrival of the mixture to be filtered and the discharge of the concentrated mixture are situated on both opposite sides of the filtering element -- The code B01D 29/356 refers to filtration arrangements in which the incoming mixture and the discharged concentrate flow on opposite sides of a stationary filtering element, such as pressure or suction filters, that are not otherwise classified in more specific groups. This open-ended configuration is used for continuous separation processes.
[B01D 29/39] with hollow discs side by side on, or around, one or more tubes, e.g. of the leaf type -- The B01D 29/39 subclass covers stationary filtration devices in which hollow disc elements are arranged side-by-side on, or around, one or more tubes, typically of the leaf-type design. These filters operate under pressure or suction and are distinguished from other groups by the use of hollow disc filtering media positioned adjacent to tubular supports.
[B01D 29/395] mounted axially on the tube -- This classification covers filters in which the filtering element is mounted axially on the tube, typical of pressure or suction filters where the media remains stationary during operation and which are not specified in other groups.
[B01D 29/41] mounted transversely on the tube -- The B01D 29/41 subgroup concerns filters whose filtering elements stay stationary while the fluid passes through, such as pressure- or suction-type filters, and specifies a configuration where the filter tube is mounted transversely across the flow path.
[B01D 29/413] divided in sectors -- This subclass defines filters in which the filtering elements stay stationary during operation, such as pressure or suction filters, that are not covered by other groups. It specifically addresses devices whose filter housing is divided into separate sectors, allowing distinct filtering media or zones within the same unit.
[B01D 29/416] Filtering tables -- Filtering tables are stationary devices used in pressure or suction filtration where the filtering elements remain fixed during operation, supporting a filter medium that receives slurry or liquid. They provide a flat, rigid surface that holds the filter cloth or membrane and facilitate the collection of filtrate, typically in laboratory or small-scale industrial settings.
[B01D 29/43] mounted otherwise than transversely on the tube -- B01D 29/43 concerns filters in which the filtering element is attached to the tube at an angle other than across it, such as longitudinal or angled mounting, typically used in pressure- or suction-type filtration systems. This subclass falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, separating, separation.
[B01D 29/44] Edge filtering elements, i.e. using contiguous impervious surfaces -- Edge filtering elements employ continuous, impervious surfaces that run along the periphery of a filter to direct flow and retain particles, typically forming a sealed barrier that prevents bypass. These elements are stationary during operation and are used in pressure or suction filtration systems where conventional filter media are unsuitable. The design improves separation efficiency by ensuring all fluid passes through the defined edge barrier.
[B01D 29/445] Bar screens -- Bar screens are stationary filtration devices comprising parallel bars that form openings through which liquids pass, allowing suspended solids larger than the bar spacing to be retained. They are employed in water-treatment and wastewater-management systems to remove debris before further processing, falling under CPC group B01D 29/445 for filters with stationary elements.
[B01D 29/46] of flat, stacked bodies -- This subclass covers filters that use flat, stacked bodies as the stationary filtering medium, where the elements remain fixed during operation. It applies to pressure or suction filtration devices whose elements are arranged in a flat, layered stack and are not classified in other specific groups.
[B01D 29/48] of spirally or helically wound bodies -- The CPC subclass B01D 29/48 covers filters whose stationary filtering elements are formed from spirally or helically wound bodies, such as coiled or twisted mats that act as the filtering medium. It applies to pressure- or suction-type filters where the winding provides a continuous porous structure not otherwise classified.
[B01D 29/50] with multiple filtering elements, characterised by their mutual disposition -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - With hollow discs side by side on, or around , one or more tubes -> B01D29/39
[B01D 29/52] in parallel connection -- B01D 29/52 covers filtration devices in which the filtering elements remain stationary during operation, such as pressure- or suction-driven filters, and are arranged in parallel connections. This configuration allows multiple filter units to operate simultaneously, increasing flow capacity or providing redundant filtration without moving parts.
[B01D 29/54] arranged concentrically or coaxially -- B01D 29/54 refers to filters whose filtering elements remain stationary during operation and are arranged concentrically or coaxially, such as pressure or suction filters, used for separating mixtures. This subclass falls under the hierarchy of Performing Operations → Separating → Separation in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[B01D 29/56] in series connection -- B01D 29/56 pertains to filters with stationary filtering elements arranged in series connection, typically used for multi-stage filtration processes where each filter stage contributes to the overall purification or separation of substances. This classification is part of a broader category that includes various methods and apparatuses for separating materials through filtration.
[B01D 29/58] arranged concentrically or coaxially -- This subclass covers filters whose stationary filtering elements are positioned in a concentric or coaxial arrangement, such as cylindrical housings or tubular media surrounding a central conduit. The configuration is used in pressure or suction filtration where the flow passes radially between the inner and outer surfaces.
[B01D 29/60] integrally combined with devices for controlling the filtration -- This subclass covers filtration units in which the filtering elements remain stationary while the apparatus includes built-in components that regulate the filtration, such as valves, pumps, pressure or suction controllers, and sensors, providing integrated control of the filtration process.
[B01D 29/601] by clearness or turbidity measuring -- B01D 29/601 pertains to filters that use stationary filtering elements, such as pressure or suction filters, and includes methods for assessing the clarity or turbidity of the filtrate during operation.
[B01D 29/603] by flow measuring -- This subclass relates to filtration processes that employ stationary filtering media, such as pressure or suction filters, where the separation is monitored or controlled by measuring the fluid flow through the filter. It includes methods that use flow-rate data to assess filter performance or to regulate the operation of the filtration step.
[B01D 29/605] by level measuring -- B01D 29/605 covers filtration processes that employ stationary filter media and use level-measuring techniques to control the operation, such as adjusting pressure or suction according to the liquid level in the vessel. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and separation for filters not covered by other specific groups.
[B01D 29/606] by pressure measuring -- This subclass relates to filtration devices where the filter medium remains stationary and the process is monitored or controlled by measuring the pressure applied or developed across the filter, such as pressure or suction filters that are not covered by more specific groups.
[B01D 29/608] by temperature measuring -- This group includes filtration devices that use temperature measurement to control or monitor the separation process, such as pressure or suction filters with stationary filtering elements where temperature sensors adjust operating conditions.
[B01D 29/62] Regenerating the filter material in the filter -- The subclass B01D 29/62 deals with processes and devices that restore or clean filter media while it remains in the filter, such as in-situ backwashing, chemical regeneration or mechanical dislodging of trapped solids, applied to stationary pressure or suction filters not covered by other groups.
[B01D 29/64] by scrapers, brushes, , or the like, acting on the cake side of the filtering element -- B01D 29/64 pertains to filtration methods using stationary filters where the cleaning mechanism involves scrapers, brushes, or similar tools that act directly on the side of the filter cake. This classification is specific to mechanical means for removing accumulated solids from the filtering surface during the filtration process.
[B01D 29/6407] brushes -- Brushes used as stationary filtering elements in pressure or suction filtration devices, where the brush structure retains or guides the material to be separated. The classification covers brushes that form part of filters not otherwise included in more specific CPC groups.
[B01D 29/6415] with a rotary movement with respect to the filtering element -- This subgroup covers filtration devices in which the filter element remains fixed while another component such as the filter housing or supporting frame rotates relative to it, providing continuous or intermittent cleaning or media movement. The rotary motion may be driven mechanically or by fluid flow and is used in pressure or suction filters not otherwise classified.
[B01D 29/6423] with a translational movement with respect to the filtering element -- This subclass concerns filtration processes in which the filtering element remains fixed while the material to be filtered moves translationally across it, such as in pressure or suction filters that are not covered by other groups. It falls under PERFORMING OPERATIONS → SEPARATING → SEPARATION.
[B01D 29/643] with a combination of movements with respect to the filtering elements -- This subclass covers filtration apparatus where the filter medium stays fixed while the surrounding structure or fluid moves, employing two or more types of motion such as rotation combined with reciprocation or oscillation relative to the filter elements, and includes pressure-or suction-driven filters not otherwise classified.
[B01D 29/6438] nozzles -- B01D 29/6438 pertains to nozzles employed in filtration systems where the filtering medium remains stationary, such as pressure-driven or suction filters. The code covers nozzle designs that direct fluid flow into or out of the filter but are not classified in other more specific sub-groups. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and separation.
[B01D 29/6446] with a rotary movement with respect to the filtering element -- Filters in which the filtering element stays fixed while the material to be filtered is moved in a rotary manner relative to that element, such as drum or rotary pressure filters, are covered by this subclass. It includes designs where rotation is the primary means of conveying the feed across the stationary filter surface.
[B01D 29/6453] with a translational movement with respect to the filtering element -- This subgroup covers filtration devices where the filtering element stays stationary and the system or fluid moves linearly relative to it, such as pressure or suction filters that translate with respect to a fixed filter medium.
[B01D 29/6461] with a combination of movements with respect to the filtering elements -- The B01D 29/6461 subclass covers pressure or suction filters whose filtering elements remain fixed while the filtration process involves combined movements of the filter housing, fluid stream, or supporting structures relative to those elements, enabling enhanced separation performance.
[B01D 29/6469] scrapers -- Scrapers are mechanical or pneumatic devices that move across stationary filtering elements, such as pressure or suction filters, to remove accumulated solids or cake layers and restore flow. They are employed where the filter media remains fixed during operation, preventing blockage and extending filter life.
[B01D 29/6476] with a rotary movement with respect to the filtering element -- B01D 29/6476 refers to filtration apparatus in which the filter housing or supporting structure rotates while the filtering element stays stationary, typical of rotary drum or rotating-cartridge filters used with pressure or suction filtration. It is a sub-group of separating-separation technologies for filters whose filtering elements do not move during operation.
[B01D 29/6484] with a translatory movement with respect to the filtering element -- Filters in which the filtering element remains fixed while the material to be filtered moves linearly across it, such as pressure or suction filters that use a translatory motion of the feed relative to a stationary filter element.
[B01D 29/6492] with a combination of movements with respect to the filtering elements -- This CPC subclass covers stationary filtration media, such as pressure or suction filters, that are operated by a combination of motions applied to the feed or the filter housing rather than to the filtering elements themselves. It addresses configurations where the filtering elements remain fixed while the material or the device moves in more than one way during separation.
[B01D 29/66] by flushing, e.g. counter-current air-bumps -- This subgroup concerns stationary filters where the filter cake is cleared by flushing it with a moving fluid or gas, for example using counter-current air bursts to dislodge retained particles and maintain flow. It applies to pressure or suction filtration systems that employ such flushing actions without moving the filtering elements.
[B01D 29/661] by using gas-bumps -- B01D 29/661 refers to filtration apparatus in which the filtering elements stay stationary while a gas-bump-a sudden burst of pressurised gas-is employed to dislodge and expel the retained material, typical of pressure or suction filters not covered by other groups.
[B01D 29/663] by using membranes -- B01D 29/663 covers filtration processes that employ stationary membrane elements to separate substances, typically using pressure or suction to drive the flow. The membranes act as the fixed filtering medium, allowing passage of certain particles or fluids while retaining others, and this subgroup includes designs not specifically covered by other more detailed membrane-based filtration groups.
[B01D 29/665] by using pistons -- This group covers filtration processes that employ pistons to generate pressure or suction and drive liquid or gas through a filter whose filtering elements remain fixed in position. It includes pressure and suction filters where pistons act as the driving means, distinct from other mechanisms.
[B01D 29/666] by a stirrer placed on the filtrate side of the filtering element -- The B01D 29/666 sub-class covers filtration processes that keep the filtering element stationary while a stirrer is positioned on the filtrate side to agitate the liquid flow, improve drainage or prevent cake buildup, and is used in pressure- or suction-type filters not covered by other groups.
[B01D 29/668] with valves, e.g. rotating valves for coaxially placed filtering elements -- This group includes filters whose flow is controlled by valves-commonly rotating valves positioned coaxially with the stationary filtering element-to open, close or modulate the passage of fluid or gas during filtration. The valve mechanism operates without moving the filter medium itself.
[B01D 29/68] with backwash arms, shoes or nozzles -- This subclass covers filters in which the filtering medium remains stationary during operation, such as pressure or suction filters, that are not classified elsewhere, and that incorporate backwash mechanisms like arms, shoes or nozzles for cleaning the filter surface.
[B01D 29/682] with a rotary movement with respect to the filtering element -- B01D 29/682 covers filtration devices in which the filtering element remains stationary while the surrounding filter housing or support rotates relative to it, a rotary movement intended to aid cake removal, cleaning or continuous operation of pressure or suction filters.
[B01D 29/684] with a translatory movement with respect to the filtering element -- Filters in which the filtering element remains stationary while the supporting structure moves linearly relative to it, such as a basket or plate that slides or is pushed through the filter media during operation. This translatory movement assists in cake removal, cleaning or continuous processing and is distinct from rotary or reciprocating motions.
[B01D 29/686] with a combination of movements with respect to the filtering elements -- This subclass deals with pressure or suction filters whose filtering elements stay stationary while the material to be filtered is moved by a combination of actions such as vibration, rotation or oscillation relative to the filter media.
[B01D 29/688] with backwash arms or shoes acting on the cake side -- B01D 29/688 covers filtration devices where the filter media stay stationary during operation, such as pressure or suction filters, and that include backwash arms or shoes acting on the cake side to aid cleaning. It addresses configurations not classified elsewhere in the separating hierarchy.
[B01D 29/70] by forces created by movement of the filter element -- Filters in which the filtering medium is moved to generate the driving force for separation, such as rotating or oscillating filter elements that create pressure differentials or suction without stationary support structures. This subclass covers devices that rely on the motion of the filter element itself to effect filtration.
[B01D 29/705] by compression of compressible filter medium, e.g. foam -- Filters with stationary filtering elements that separate by compressing a compressible medium such as foam, typically used in pressure or suction filtration systems not covered in other groups. The method relies on the medium's ability to change thickness under load, providing a variable pore structure for particle capture.
[B01D 29/72] involving vibrations -- B01D 29/72 covers filter arrangements in which the filter or its housing is subjected to vibration during operation to prevent clogging, loosen retained particles and enhance fluid flow. The vibrations may be generated mechanically, ultrasonically or by other means and are applied to stationary filtering elements such as pressure or suction filters that are not otherwise classified. This subclass is used for vibrating-assisted filtration devices.
[B01D 29/74] involving centrifugal force -- B01D 29/74 classifies filtration arrangements where the filter medium remains stationary and separation is achieved by centrifugal force, such as rotating drum or disc filters that fling liquid outward to leave solids on the fixed filter element. This subgroup is distinct from pressure or suction filters and covers devices that use rotation-induced centrifugal action to separate particulates from fluids.
[B01D 29/76] Handling the filter cake in the filter for purposes other than for regenerating -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - For discharging the filter cake -> B01D29/94
[B01D 29/78] for washing -- B01D 29/78 covers stationary filter devices used for washing operations, where the filter medium remains fixed while liquids are forced through by pressure or suction, excluding other filter types covered elsewhere.
[B01D 29/80] for drying -- This subclass covers filtration apparatus in which the filtering elements remain stationary while fluid is forced through by pressure or suction, and the device is specifically adapted for drying the filtrate. It is distinct from other B01D 29 groups that address different filter configurations.
[B01D 29/82] by compression -- Filters that retain solid particles while the filtering elements remain stationary, employing compression to facilitate separation, such as pressure or suction filters that are not classified in other groups. This entry falls under the separation subclass of performing operations.
[B01D 29/822] using membranes -- B01D 29/822 covers the use of membranes as stationary filtering elements in pressure or suction filtration processes, where the membrane remains fixed while fluid passes through. It falls under Performing operations - Separating - Separation in the CPC scheme.
[B01D 29/824] using pistons -- The B01D 29/824 subclass covers filtration processes that employ pistons to pressurise or manipulate stationary filtering elements, such as pressure or suction filters, where the filter media remain fixed during operation. It includes piston-driven methods that are not otherwise classified in the related groups.
[B01D 29/826] using rollers -- B01D 29/826 relates to filtration processes that employ rollers to move material across stationary filtering elements such as pressure or suction filters. The rollers facilitate the conveyance of the feed while the filter media remains fixed, enabling continuous separation of solids from liquids.
[B01D 29/828] using screws  B01D29/6476  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - With a rotary movement with respect to the filtering element -> B01D29/6476
[B01D 29/84] by gases or by heating -- B01D 29/84 denotes filtration processes where the filter medium stays stationary and separation is effected by passing a gas stream over or through the filter or by heating the filter to alter its properties, thereby facilitating removal of solids or liquids not covered by more specific groups.
[B01D 29/843] by direct contact with a fluid -- B01D 29/843 covers filtration processes in which the filter medium stays fixed while the fluid to be cleaned passes directly through it, such as pressure-driven or suction filters that separate solids from liquids or gases.
[B01D 29/846] by indirect heat-exchange -- This subgroup concerns filters whose filtering elements stay stationary while the material being filtered passes through, and where the filter is warmed by indirect heat-exchange, such as heated walls or a surrounding fluid, rather than by direct contact with a heating element.
[B01D 29/86] Retarding cake deposition on the filter during the filtration period, e.g. using stirrers -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Provoking a tangential stream -> B01D29/908
[B01D 29/865] by vibration of the liquid -- This subclass covers filtration processes in which the liquid is vibrated while flowing through stationary filter media, such as pressure or suction filters, to enhance separation efficiency. The vibration-mechanical, acoustic or ultrasonic-is applied to the liquid rather than to the filter elements.
[B01D 29/88] having feed or discharge devices -- The subclass B01D 29/88 includes stationary-element filters that are equipped with mechanisms for introducing material into the filter or removing filtrate, such as inlet or outlet arrangements integrated into the housing. It applies to pressure-driven or suction filters where the feed or discharge devices are an essential part of the filtration apparatus.
[B01D 29/885] with internal recirculation through the filtering element  B01D37/02  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Precoating the filter medium ; addition of filter aids to the liquid being filtered -> B01D37/02
[B01D 29/90] for feeding -- B01D 29/90 relates to feeding mechanisms for filters that have stationary filtering elements, such as pressure or suction filters, where the method of introducing the material into the filter is not covered by other more specific groups. It includes devices that direct feed streams into the filter housing, ensuring uniform distribution before filtration.
[B01D 29/902] containing fixed liquid displacement elements or cores -- This subclass includes filters that use fixed liquid-displacement elements or cores to retain the filtering medium while the liquid is forced through by pressure, suction or other means. The filtering elements remain stationary during operation, and the design is not covered by any other CPC groups for pressure or suction filters.
[B01D 29/904] directing the mixture to be filtered on the filtering element in a manner to clean the filter continuously  B01D29/115 ,  B01D29/118 ,  B01D29/17 ,  B01D29/25 ,  B01D29/336 ,  B01D29/356 ,  B01D29/902 ,  B01D29/908  take precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Open-ended, the arrival of the mixture to be filtered and the discharge of the concentrated mixture are situated on both opposite sides of the filtering element -> B01D29/115 - Open-ended -> B01D29/118 - Open-ended, the arrival of the mixture to be filtered and the discharge of the concentrated mixture are situated on both opposite sides of the filtering element -> B01D29/17 - Open-ended, the arrival of the mixture to be filtered and the discharge of the concentrated mixture are situated on both opposite sides of the filtering element -> B01D29/25 - Open-ended, the arrival of the mixture to be filtered and the discharge of the concentrated mixture are situated on both opposite sides of the filtering element -> B01D29/336 - Open-ended, the arrival of the mixture to be filtered and the discharge of the concentrated mixture are situated on both opposite sides of the filtering element -> B01D29/356 - Containing liquid displacement elements or cores -> B01D29/902 - Provoking a tangential stream -> B01D29/908
[B01D 29/906] Special treatment of the feed stream before contacting the filtering element, e.g. cutting  B01D35/24 ,  B01D37/02 ,  B01D37/03  take precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Providing loose granular material to scratch the filters clean -> B01D35/24 - Precoating the filter medium; Addition of filter aids to the liquid being filtered -> B01D37/02 - Using flocculating agents -> B01D37/03
[B01D 29/908] provoking a tangential stream -- B01D 29/908 covers techniques for initiating a tangential fluid stream across a stationary filter medium, such as pressure- or suction-driven filters, to enhance particle removal, reduce fouling or aid back-washing.
[B01D 29/92] for discharging filtrate -- This CPC subgroup concerns filters whose filtering elements stay stationary during operation, such as pressure or suction filters, and deals with the means for removing the filtered liquid from the filter. It specifically addresses arrangements for discharging the filtrate.
[B01D 29/925] containing liquid displacement elements or cores -- B01D 29/925 refers to filters whose filtering elements stay stationary during operation, typically pressure- or suction-type filters, and that incorporate liquid-displacement elements or cores to assist the filtration process, covering designs not classified elsewhere in the hierarchy.
[B01D 29/94] for discharging the filter cake, e.g. chutes -- B01D 29/94 covers stationary-element pressure or suction filters that are not assigned to other groups, specifically addressing means for removing the accumulated filter cake, for example by using chutes.
[B01D 29/945] for continuously discharging concentrated liquid -- B01D 29/945 pertains to filtration systems where the filtering elements remain stationary while the filtrate is continuously removed, typically using pressure or suction to discharge a concentrated liquid. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of performing operations, separating, and separation, and covers filters not assigned to other more specific groups.
[B01D 29/96] in which the filtering elements are moved between filtering operations -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - With filtering bands, e.g. movable between filtering operations -> B01D29/09 - By forces created by movement of the filter element -> B01D29/70
[B01D 29/965] Device for changing the inclination of the filtering element -- B01D 29/965 relates to devices that adjust the tilt of a stationary filtering element in pressure or suction filters, enabling the inclination of the filter medium to be varied during operation to improve flow, separation efficiency or cleaning.
[B01D 3/00] Distillation or related exchange processes in which liquids are contacted with gaseous media, e.g. stripping -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This group concerns distillation in general. Specific application should classified in the relevant group.
[B01D 3/001] Processes specially adapted for distillation or rectification of fermented solutions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In B01D3/001 are classified the documents dealing with alcohol batch distillation.
[B01D 3/002] by continuous methods -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In B01D3/001 are classified the documents dealing with alcohol continuous distillation.
[B01D 3/003] Rectification of spirit -- B01D 3/003 falls under Performing Operations → Separating → Separation, covering distillation or related exchange processes where liquids meet a gaseous phase such as stripping. The entry specifically addresses the rectification of spirit, i.e., the purification of alcoholic liquids by repeated vaporisation and condensation to remove impurities.
[B01D 3/004] by continuous methods -- B01D 3/004 refers to continuous distillation or stripping processes in which a liquid is brought into contact with a gaseous medium for separation. It covers apparatus and methods that operate continuously, such as columns or packed beds, to effect the exchange of components between the phases.
[B01D 3/005] Combined distillation and rectification -- B01D 3/005 covers processes that integrate distillation and rectification in a single unit or sequence, allowing simultaneous vapor-liquid contact to separate components of a mixture. The combined operation improves efficiency by using the vapor from the distillation step as reflux for rectification, or vice versa, and is used in chemical or petroleum refining where close separation of close-boiling fractions is required.
[B01D 3/006] by vibration -- The B01D 3/006 subclass includes distillation, stripping or similar liquid-gas contact processes that employ vibration to improve mass-transfer efficiency. Techniques may involve ultrasonic, acoustic or mechanical oscillation of the liquid, the gas or the contacting interface to promote faster separation of components.
[B01D 3/007] Energy recuperation; Heat pumps -- B01D 3/007 relates to energy-recuperation methods within liquid-gas separation processes, specifically the use of heat-pump cycles or heat-exchange arrangements to capture and reuse thermal energy, thereby improving the efficiency of operations such as distillation or stripping.
[B01D 3/008] Liquid distribution -- B01D 3/008 covers devices and techniques for distributing a liquid phase within a column or reactor where it contacts a gaseous phase, such as in distillation, stripping or similar exchange operations. It includes trays, spray nozzles, distributors and other arrangements that ensure uniform liquid spreading for efficient mass transfer.
[B01D 3/009] in combination with chemical reactions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The chemical reaction is inside the distillation vessel.  This subgroup does not cover:  The chemical reaction only for assisting in the separation by distillation of a pre-existing
[B01D 3/02] in boilers or stills -- The CPC subclass B01D 3/02 - in boilers or stills - relates to separation operations where a liquid is brought into contact with a gaseous medium for distillation, stripping or similar exchange processes carried out in equipment such as boilers or stills.
[B01D 3/04] pipe stills -- Pipe stills are apparatus where a liquid feed flows through a heated pipe and is contacted by a counter-flowing gas to effect distillation or stripping, separating more volatile components from the liquid. The design relies on heat transfer and mass exchange within the pipe to achieve the desired separation.
[B01D 3/06] Flash distillation -- Flash distillation is a rapid separation technique in which a liquid stream is instantly introduced into a heated zone or contacted with a gaseous medium, causing immediate vaporisation and separation of components. It falls under CPC B01D 3/06 within the hierarchy of performing operations, separating, separation, and distillation or related exchange processes.
[B01D 3/065] Multiple-effect flash distillation (more than two traps) -- A multiple-effect flash distillation system uses a series of flash chambers, each operating at progressively lower pressure, to vaporize and condense a liquid in successive stages, improving energy efficiency. The entry covers configurations with more than two effects, where the vapor from one chamber serves as the heating medium for the next, allowing continuous separation of components with limited external heat input.
[B01D 3/08] in rotating vessels -- B01D 3/08 covers distillation, stripping or similar separation operations in which the liquid is contacted with a gas inside a rotating vessel. The rotation creates mixing or enhanced mass transfer, improving separation efficiency. It is a subclass of separating processes within the performing operations hierarchy.
[B01D 3/085] using a rotary evaporator -- The B01D 3/085 entry covers the use of a rotary evaporator to carry out distillation-type separations in which a liquid is contacted with a gaseous medium, often under reduced pressure, to remove or recover volatile components. It pertains to operations where the rotating flask and heated bath facilitate efficient solvent evaporation and collection.
[B01D 3/10] Vacuum distillation -- Vacuum distillation is a separation method that reduces the pressure above a liquid mixture to lower boiling points, allowing volatile components to be removed at temperatures below their normal boiling points. This technique is especially useful for heat-sensitive substances or when energy efficiency is desired, and it falls under the CPC class B01D 3/10 for distillation processes involving gaseous media.
[B01D 3/101] Recirculation of the fluid used as fluid working medium in a vacuum creating device -- This subclass covers methods where the vapor or gas used to generate vacuum is continuously recycled within the apparatus during distillation or stripping, improving efficiency and reducing waste. It includes looped vacuum pumps, condensers, and fluid handling that return the working medium to the contact zone.
[B01D 3/103] by using a barometric column -- B01D 3/103 refers to separation techniques that use a barometric column for distillation or stripping, where a liquid is brought into contact with a gas phase to effect mass transfer. The barometric column operates under a pressure gradient to facilitate vapor-liquid contact and enhance separation efficiency.
[B01D 3/105] with the use of an ejector for creating the vacuum, the ejector being placed between evaporator or distillation devices -- This subclass covers distillation or stripping operations that employ an ejector to generate a vacuum, with the ejector positioned between the evaporator or distillation unit and the condenser or other downstream equipment. It is used to enhance separation efficiency by reducing pressure in the vapor-liquid contact zone.
[B01D 3/106] with the use of a pump for creating vacuum and for removing the distillate -- The entry covers distillation or similar liquid-gas contacting processes that employ a pump to generate a vacuum and to extract the distilled product. It falls under CPC code B01D 3/106 within the hierarchy of performing operations, separating, separation.
[B01D 3/108] using a vacuum lock for removing the concentrate during distillation -- The code B01D 3/108 covers distillation or similar liquid-gas exchange processes where a vacuum lock is employed to extract the concentrated product while the operation continues. It relates to systems that isolate the removed stream from the main vessel, maintaining pressure differentials and preventing back-flow during the separation. This facilitates continuous or semi-continuous operation without interrupting the distillation.
[B01D 3/12] Molecular distillation -- B01D 3/12 (Molecular distillation) refers to distillation or related exchange processes where a liquid is brought into contact with a gaseous medium, typically under high vacuum, to separate components based on differences in vapor pressure without significant heating. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating and separation.
[B01D 3/14] Fractional distillation -- B01D 3/14 covers fractional distillation, a process where a liquid mixture is separated into components by repeated vaporization and condensation, often involving contact with a gaseous stream such as stripping.
[B01D 3/141] where at least one distillation column contains at least one dividing wall -- The subclass covers distillation or stripping operations in which a single column incorporates a dividing wall that creates two separate sections, allowing simultaneous counter-current contact of liquid and vapor streams. The wall enables internal split-flow or side-by-side separation without needing separate columns, improving energy efficiency and product purity.
[B01D 3/143] by two or more of a fractionation, separation or rectification step -- This CPC subclass covers processes that combine at least two operations such as fractionation, separation or rectification to achieve distillation or related liquid-gas exchange. It includes methods where a liquid stream is contacted with a gaseous phase for stripping or rectifying, employing multiple steps in sequence or simultaneously.
[B01D 3/145] One step being separation by permeation -- The B01D 3/145 entry covers a single-step separation in which a liquid mixture is contacted with a gaseous medium and components are removed by permeation through a membrane or porous barrier, often as part of a distillation-type exchange process such as stripping. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations → separating → separation.
[B01D 3/146] Multiple effect distillation -- Multiple-effect distillation is a separation technique where the vapor generated in one effect serves as the heating medium for the next, allowing several stages to operate in series and improving energy efficiency. It is used for distilling liquids by contacting them with gaseous media, such as in stripping operations, and is classified under B01D 3/146 in the CPC.
[B01D 3/148] in combination with at least one evaporator -- A process in which a liquid-gas contacting operation such as distillation or stripping is carried out together with at least one evaporator, allowing simultaneous removal of volatile components and concentration of the remaining liquid. This combined arrangement improves efficiency by integrating the evaporative step within the separation stage.
[B01D 3/16] Fractionating columns in which vapour bubbles through liquid -- The CPC subclass B01D 3/16 covers fractionating columns in which a vapor phase rises through a liquid phase, producing a dispersed bubble flow that enhances mass transfer. Such designs are used in distillation, stripping and related separation processes where intimate contact between vapor bubbles and liquid improves component separation.
[B01D 3/163] Plates with valves -- This subclass covers apparatus where individual plates in a distillation or stripping column incorporate one or more valves to control the flow of liquid or gas across the plate. The valves may be used to regulate distribution, bypass or shut off sections, improving separation efficiency or enabling staged operation.
[B01D 3/166] Heating and/or cooling of plates -- The code B01D 3/166 covers techniques for heating or cooling the plates used in distillation-type columns where a liquid contacts a gas, such as stripping operations. It includes methods and devices that regulate plate temperature to control mass-transfer rates and improve separation efficiency.
[B01D 3/18] with horizontal bubble plates -- B01D 3/18 covers distillation or related gas-liquid contacting processes that employ horizontal bubble plates. The plates create a series of chambers where gas rises through liquid, enhancing mass transfer and separation efficiency in stripping or similar operations. This subcategory falls under separating, separation, distillation with gaseous media.
[B01D 3/20] Bubble caps -- Bubble caps are internal tray devices used in distillation columns where liquid flows over a perforated plate and rising vapor passes through caps, creating a bubbling action that enhances mass transfer between the liquid and gaseous phases.
[B01D 3/205] Bubble caps -- Bubble caps are tray fittings used in distillation columns to promote intimate contact between rising vapour and descending liquid. Each cap contains openings that force the vapour to bubble through the liquid layer on the tray, enhancing mass-transfer efficiency in stripping and related separation operations. This design is classified under CPC B01D 3/205.
[B01D 3/22] with horizontal sieve plates or grids -- The B01D 3/22 subclass covers distillation or related gas-liquid contacting processes, such as stripping, that employ horizontal sieve plates or grids within the column. These plates provide structured internals to enhance mass transfer and improve separation efficiency. It falls under the broader CPC hierarchy of performing operations → separating → separation.
[B01D 3/225] Dual-flow sieve trays -- Dual-flow sieve trays are a type of internal column device used in distillation or stripping operations where a perforated sieve plate allows liquid to flow downward through the holes while vapor rises upward through the same openings, promoting intimate liquid-gas contact and efficient mass transfer.
[B01D 3/24] with sloping plates or elements mounted stepwise -- A CPC subclass for distillation or related liquid-gas contacting processes, such as stripping, where the operation employs sloping plates or elements that are mounted in a stepwise arrangement to enhance mass transfer. This design falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and separation.
[B01D 3/26] Fractionating columns in which vapour and liquid flow past each other, or in which the fluid is sprayed into the vapour, or in which a two-phase mixture is passed in one direction -- The code B01D 3/26 covers fractionating columns used in distillation or related processes where vapour and liquid streams move past each other, are sprayed together, or a two-phase mixture travels in a single direction. Such columns are designed to separate liquid components by repeated contacting with a gaseous phase or by internal spray devices.
[B01D 3/28] Fractionating columns with surface contact and vertical guides, e.g. film action -- B01D 3/28, under Performing Operations → Separating → Separation, relates to fractionating columns that employ surface-contact configurations with vertical guides to create a thin liquid film, improving contact with a gaseous phase in distillation or stripping operations.
[B01D 3/30] Fractionating columns with movable parts or in which centrifugal movement is caused -- This subclass covers fractionating columns used in distillation or related liquid-gas contact processes where the column contains moving components, such as rotating trays, mechanically agitated packing, or designs that generate centrifugal forces to enhance separation.
[B01D 33/00] Filters with filtering elements which move during the filtering operation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Filters comprising loose filtering material moving or fluidised during filtration -> B01D24/28 - B01D24/36 - Centrifuges -> B04B
