[B01D 46/2466] of the adhesive layers, i.e. joints between segments -- This CPC subclass concerns filters or filtering methods that employ adhesive layers, i.e., the joints between filter segments, which are specially adapted for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapours.
[B01D 46/247] of the cells -- B01D 46/247 pertains to filters or filtering processes specifically adapted for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapours, focusing on modifications related to the structure and function of the cells within these filtration systems.
[B01D 46/2474] of the walls along the length of the honeycomb -- This subclass covers filters or filtering processes that are specially adapted to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours by using honeycomb structures whose walls run longitudinally along the length of the honeycomb, providing a high-surface-area pathway for particle capture.
[B01D 46/2476] Monolithic structures -- Monolithic structures are solid, often ceramic or polymeric bodies formed as a single piece that contain a network of pores or channels for filtering dispersed particles from gases or vapours. They are produced by shaping and sintering techniques, providing high mechanical strength and uniform flow in filtration applications.
[B01D 46/2478] Structures comprising honeycomb segments -- Honeycomb-segment structures are filter bodies formed from a series of adjoining cells that resemble a honeycomb, providing a large surface area with low resistance to gas flow. They are designed for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapours, often using ceramic, metal or polymeric materials that can be coated or impregnated with filtration media. The configuration improves particle capture while maintaining efficient throughput.
[B01D 46/248] Structures comprising laminated bodies or discs -- This subclass covers filter structures that consist of a series of laminated bodies or disc-shaped elements arranged to create a porous medium for separating solid particles from gases or vapours. The design enhances filtration efficiency by providing multiple layers and flow paths within a compact unit.
[B01D 46/2482] Thickness, height, width, length or diameter -- B01D 46/2482 pertains to filters or filtering processes designed for the separation of dispersed particles from gases or vapours, specifically addressing variations in physical dimensions such as thickness, height, width, length, or diameter.
[B01D 46/2484] Cell density, area or aspect ratio -- B01D 46/2484 relates to filters or filtering processes specially adapted for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapours, where the filter's design or performance is defined by the cell density, area or aspect ratio of its internal structure.
[B01D 46/2486] characterised by the shapes or configurations -- This subclass covers filters or filtering processes specially modified for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapours where the distinguishing feature is the shape or configuration of the filter medium or apparatus. It is indexed under CPC code B01D 46/2486 within the separating group of performing-operations categories.
[B01D 46/2488] Triangular -- Triangular refers to filters or filtering processes that employ a triangular-shaped element or arrangement specially modified to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours. This subclass is coded B01D 46/2488 within the separation operations group.
[B01D 46/249] Quadrangular e.g. square or diamond -- Quadrangular filters-such as square or diamond-shaped devices-are specialized filtering elements designed to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours, typically used in processes where a defined geometric shape improves flow distribution or cleaning efficiency.
[B01D 46/2492] Hexagonal -- The hexagonal entry refers to filters or filtering processes that employ a hexagonal-shaped geometry or structure to improve the separation of dispersed particles from gases or vapours. The specific shape creates a regular, tessellated network of channels that enhances flow uniformity and particle capture efficiency compared with conventional designs. This classification is used for inventions that modify filter media, housings or flow paths into a hexagonal configuration for improved separation performance.
[B01D 46/2494] Octagonal -- Octagonal denotes filters or filtering processes that use an eight-sided configuration specially adapted for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapours. The geometric shape can influence flow distribution, surface area and pressure drop, enhancing separation performance in industrial or laboratory applications.
[B01D 46/2496] Circular -- The B01D 46/2496 subclass relates to circular-shaped filters or filtering processes that have been specially adapted to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours. It includes designs where the filter media or housing forms a round geometry to enhance particle capture efficiency in gas-phase separations.
[B01D 46/2498] The honeycomb filter being defined by mathematical relationships -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This group covers specific ratios with numerical values and mathematical equations or inequations.
[B01D 46/26] rotatable -- The B01D 46/26 "rotatable" subgroup includes filter or filtering devices that use a rotating component to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours, such as rotary drums, rotating discs or cyclone separators, where the motion of the filter element enhances particle capture or transport.
[B01D 46/28] Particle separators, e.g. dust precipitators, using filter brushes -- A particle separator employing filter-brush elements to capture solid or liquid particles from a gas or vapor stream, commonly used in dust precipitators. The brush-type filter provides a high-surface-area medium that continuously sweeps particles from the flow, enabling efficient removal and collection.
[B01D 46/30] Particle separators, e.g. dust precipitators, using loose filtering material -- The B01D 46/30 entry covers particle-separator devices, such as dust precipitators, that employ a loose filtering medium to capture dispersed solid particles from gases or vapours. These systems are specifically designed for separating particulate matter using filter materials that are not fixed in a rigid matrix.
[B01D 46/32] the material moving during filtering -- This CPC subclass covers filters or filtering processes specially adapted to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours, focusing on the material that moves through the filter during operation. It falls under the B01D 46/32 code within the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and separation.
[B01D 46/34] not horizontally, e.g. using shoots -- This subclass covers filter or filtering processes that separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours in a non-horizontal arrangement, for example using vertical or inclined configurations such as shoots or columns. It includes designs where gravity or flow direction is exploited without a flat, horizontal filter surface.
[B01D 46/36] as a substantially horizontal layer, e.g. on rotary tables, drums, conveyor belts -- This subclass covers filter arrangements where the filtering medium is presented as a substantially horizontal layer, for example on rotary tables, drums or conveyor belts, and is used to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours.
[B01D 46/38] as fluidised bed -- A fluidised-bed filter uses an upward flow of gas or vapor to keep a bed of solid particles suspended, creating a highly turbulent medium that traps dispersed particles as the gas passes through. This method enhances separation efficiency by maintaining constant contact between the fluid and the filter media while allowing continuous operation.
[B01D 46/40] Particle separators, e.g. dust precipitators, using edge filters, i.e. using contiguous impervious surfaces -- Particle separators such as dust precipitators that employ edge filters use continuous, impervious surfaces to create a filtering edge, directing a gas stream across the surface so that dispersed particles are captured on the edge and removed from the flow. This approach separates particles from gases or vapours by leveraging the barrier effect of the contiguous impermeable surface.
[B01D 46/403] of helically or spirally wound bodies -- B01D 46/403 covers filters or filtering processes that employ helically or spirally wound bodies to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours. It falls under the separation of particles using specially modified filter structures within the performing operations hierarchy.
[B01D 46/406] of stacked bodies -- B01D 46/406 covers filters constructed from a series of stacked bodies such as plates, meshes or foils that are arranged to create successive passages for gases or vapours, enabling the removal of dispersed particles. The configuration relies on the physical arrangement of the bodies rather than chemical action to achieve separation.
[B01D 46/42] Auxiliary equipment or operation thereof -- B01D 46/42 pertains to auxiliary devices or operations that support filters or filtering processes specially adapted to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours. It includes supplementary equipment, cleaning mechanisms, control steps or other assistive features that enhance the primary separation function.
[B01D 46/4209] Prevention of static charge, e.g. by grounding -- The entry B01D 46/4209 covers filters or filtering processes that are specially adapted to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours while preventing the buildup of static electricity, for example by grounding the equipment. This falls within the hierarchy of performing operations, separating, and separation.
[B01D 46/4218] Influencing the heat transfer which act passively, e.g. isolations, heat sinks, cooling ribs -- The class covers passive heat-transfer modifications applied to filter or filtration devices that separate particles from gases or vapours, such as insulating layers, heat sinks or cooling ribs that manage temperature without active energy input.
[B01D 46/4227] Manipulating filters or filter elements, e.g. handles or extracting tools -- The subclass B01D 46/4227 covers devices or accessories used to manipulate filter media during gas- or vapour-filtration operations, such as handles, gripping mechanisms, or tools for extracting and replacing filter elements. These items facilitate safe and efficient removal, positioning, or maintenance of filters that separate dispersed particles from a fluid stream.
[B01D 46/4236] Reducing noise or vibration emissions -- The CPC subclass B01D 46/4236 concerns filters or filtering processes for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapours that incorporate features to reduce noise or vibration emissions, improving operational quietness and mechanical stability.
[B01D 46/4245] Means for power supply or devices using electrical power in filters or filter elements -- This subclass covers any electrical power source or powered component incorporated into a filter or filter element designed to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapors. It includes motors, heaters, electrostatic or electromagnetic devices that enhance the filtration process, such as vibrating or pulsing elements, and any integrated power-control circuitry.
[B01D 46/4254] Allowing or improving visual supervision, e.g. lamps, transparent parts, windows -- This subclass covers features that enable or enhance visual monitoring of filtration systems that separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours, such as illuminated or transparent components, viewing windows, or other means that allow operators to observe the filter's condition and performance.
[B01D 46/4263] Means for active heating or cooling -- This subclass covers components that actively heat or cool a filter or filtering system designed to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours. The means may be integrated into the filter structure to control temperature and improve separation efficiency.
[B01D 46/4272] Special valve constructions adapted to filters or filter elements -- Special valve constructions adapted to filters or filter elements are valve designs that are integrated with or mounted on filtration devices to regulate the passage of gases or vapours while allowing cleaning, back-flushing or isolation of the filter. They enable controlled flow, safe removal of collected particles and maintenance without dismantling the filter assembly.
[B01D 46/4281] Venturi's or systems showing a venturi effect -- Venturi devices or arrangements that create a venturi effect are employed in filtration systems to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours, using a pressure differential to accelerate flow and enhance particle capture. This subclass covers such specially modified venturi-based filters within the broader category of separating operations.
[B01D 46/429] Means for wireless communication -- B01D 46/429 covers the incorporation of wireless communication elements in filtration systems used for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapours. It includes transmitters, receivers, antennas and related control modules that enable remote monitoring or operation of such filters.
[B01D 46/44] controlling filtration -- This subclass pertains to devices, methods or control systems that regulate the operation of filters specially adapted to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours. It includes adjustable flow, pressure, temperature or other parameters used to optimise particle capture and maintain desired filtration performance.
[B01D 46/442] by measuring the concentration of particles -- This CPC subclass B01D 46/442 covers filter or filtration methods that are specially adapted to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours by monitoring the particle concentration. The measurement of concentration is used to control or adjust the filtering process, ensuring effective removal of particulates.
[B01D 46/444] by flow measuring -- This subclass covers filtration techniques that use flow measurement to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapors. The process involves monitoring or controlling the rate of gas or vapor passing through a filter to assess or improve particle removal. It falls under B01D 46/444 in the CPC, focusing on filters modified for particle separation.
[B01D 46/446] by pressure measuring -- B01D 46/446 relates to filters or filtering processes specially adapted for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapours, where pressure measuring is employed to monitor, control or optimize the separation operation.
[B01D 46/448] by temperature measuring -- This sub-group covers filtration or separation processes for gases or vapours that employ temperature measurement to control or improve particle removal. It includes systems where temperature sensing guides filter operation, adjusts flow conditions, or signals filter performance.
[B01D 46/46] automatic -- B01D 46/46 - automatic - refers to filters or filtering processes specially modified to automatically separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours. The class is situated in the hierarchy performing operations, separating, separation.
[B01D 46/48] Removing dust other than cleaning filters -- This subclass covers methods and devices that remove dust from gas or vapor streams by means of specially modified filters or filtering processes, without involving the cleaning of the filter itself. It includes any arrangement designed to separate dispersed particles from the flow rather than to restore filter performance.
[B01D 46/50] Means for discharging electrostatic potential -- A means for discharging electrostatic potential refers to any device or method that removes static charge from filter media or the flow of gases and vapors during particle-separation operations, such as conductive discharge electrodes, grounding brushes, or ionizing plates incorporated into the filter system. This helps prevent buildup of electrostatic forces that could impede filtration efficiency or cause spark hazards.
[B01D 46/52] Particle separators, e.g. dust precipitators, using filters embodying folded material -- This subclass covers particle-separating devices such as dust precipitators that employ filters made from folded material to capture dispersed particles from gases or vapours. The folded filter structure increases surface area and improves collection efficiency. These systems are used in air cleaning, industrial exhaust treatment, and similar applications.
[B01D 46/521] using folded, pleated material -- This CPC entry (B01D 46/521) relates to filters or filtering processes specially adapted for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapours, specifically when the filtration medium is a folded, pleated material.
[B01D 46/522] with specific folds, e.g. having different lengths -- This subclass covers filters that incorporate specially shaped folds of varying lengths to enhance the removal of particles from gases or vapours. The design improves efficiency by creating tortuous flow paths and increasing surface area.
[B01D 46/523] with means for maintaining spacing between the pleats or folds -- This subclass covers filters or filtering processes specially adapted for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapours, featuring mechanisms that keep the pleats or folds of the filter element spaced apart during operation.
[B01D 46/525] which comprises flutes -- B01D 46/525 covers filters or filtering processes specially modified for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapours by using fluted structures. The flutes provide channels that enhance particle capture and airflow control.
[B01D 46/526] in stacked arrangement -- A stacked arrangement refers to a filter configuration where multiple filtering elements are placed one on top of another, forming a layered assembly that enhances the separation of dispersed particles from gases or vapours. This design allows sequential filtration stages within a compact unit, improving overall efficiency and particle capture.
[B01D 46/527] in wound arrangement -- A filter or filtering process built as a wound (spiral) assembly that separates dispersed particles from gases or vapours. The wound configuration creates a compact, high-surface-area medium that enhances particle capture efficiency in air-purification or industrial gas-cleaning applications.
[B01D 46/528] using wound sheets  B01D46/527  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Particle separators using folded material comprising flutes in wound arrangement -> B01D46/527
[B01D 46/54] Particle separators, e.g. dust precipitators, using ultra-fine filter sheets or diaphragms -- Devices that separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours by passing the stream through ultra-fine filter sheets or diaphragms, such as dust precipitators, are covered by CPC B01D 46/54. These systems are specially modified for fine-particle capture and removal from airborne streams.
[B01D 46/543] using membranes -- B01D 46/543 covers the use of membranes in filtration or filtering processes specially adapted to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours. It is nested under Performing Operations - Separating - Separation.
[B01D 46/546] using nano- or microfibres -- This subclass covers filtration methods that employ nano- or micro-fibres to capture dispersed particles from gases or vapours, typically in filters specially-modified for enhanced separation performance. It is CPC entry B01D 46/546 under separating operations.
[B01D 46/56] with multiple filtering elements, characterised by their mutual disposition -- This subclass covers filters or filtering processes specially modified for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapours that use multiple filtering elements whose mutual disposition - such as series, parallel, staggered or other geometric arrangements - is a defining feature of the apparatus. The relative positioning of the elements is intended to enhance particle capture or flow characteristics.
[B01D 46/58] connected in parallel -- This subclass describes filter systems in which two or more filter units are linked side-by-side so that the gas or vapour stream passes through them simultaneously, rather than sequentially, enhancing capacity, redundancy or efficiency. The arrangement is specifically adapted for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapours and is termed "connected in parallel."
[B01D 46/60] arranged concentrically or coaxially -- B01D 46/60 refers to filters or filtering processes specially adapted to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours where the filtering elements are arranged concentrically or coaxially, creating annular flow paths that enhance contact between the gas stream and the filter medium. This configuration is used to improve separation efficiency in applications such as air cleaning, emissions control, and aerosol filtration.
[B01D 46/62] connected in series -- In this subclass the filter units are linked one after another so that the gas or vapour passes through a sequence of filtering stages. The series arrangement allows successive removal of dispersed particles, often increasing overall efficiency compared with a single filter stage.
[B01D 46/64] arranged concentrically or coaxially -- B01D 46/64 covers filtering devices or processes in which the filter elements are positioned concentrically or coaxially to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours. This arrangement places the inlet, filter media and outlet along a common axis, allowing flow to pass through successive cylindrical layers. Such designs are used to improve separation efficiency in industrial gas cleaning.
[B01D 46/645] Protecting screens at filter inlet or outlet -- The B01D 46/645 entry covers protective measures applied to the screens located at the inlet or outlet of filters that are specially modified for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapours. It addresses designs that shield the mesh from damage, fouling or bypass, ensuring reliable filtration performance.
[B01D 46/66] Regeneration of the filtering material or filter elements inside the filter -- The entry concerns techniques for restoring the filtering medium or filter elements while they remain in place, such as cleaning, re-activating, or otherwise renewing the material that has become loaded with particles. It applies to filtering processes used to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapors.
[B01D 46/68] by means acting on the cake side involving movement with regard to the filter elements -- Groups B01D46/68 - B01D46/78 are only for dry processes.
[B01D 46/681] by scrapers, brushes or the like -- This subclass covers filter or filtering processes that employ mechanical elements such as scrapers, brushes or similar devices to clean the filter medium and remove dispersed particles from gases or vapours during separation. It falls under the broader categories of separating and separation in the CPC.
[B01D 46/682] by nozzles -- This subclass covers filtering or filtration processes that employ nozzles to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours, using the nozzle-induced acceleration of the gas stream to achieve aerodynamic particle separation.
[B01D 46/69] by means acting on the cake side without movement with respect to the filter elements, e.g. fixed nozzles -- This subclass covers filtration operations where the cleaning medium is applied to the filter cake without moving the filter elements, such as using fixed nozzles that direct fluid onto the cake side. The technique relies on static actuation to dislodge or transport particles, avoiding any relative motion between the cake and the filter structure.
[B01D 46/70] by acting counter-currently on the filtering surface, e.g. by flushing on the non-cake side of the filter -- This subclass covers filtration methods in which a counter-current flow is applied to the filter surface, such as flushing the side opposite the accumulated cake, to enhance removal of dispersed particles from gases or vapours. It is a refinement of B01D 46/00 dealing with specially modified filters.
[B01D 46/71] with pressurised gas, e.g. pulsed air -- This subclass covers filters or filtering processes specially modified to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours by employing a pressurised gas, for example pulsed air.
[B01D 46/715] Using pressurized gas at supersonic velocities -- This subclass covers filter systems that employ a pressurized gas stream moving at supersonic speeds to separate suspended particles from a gas or vapor stream. The high-velocity flow creates shock waves and aerodynamic forces that enhance particle capture and removal.
[B01D 46/72] with backwash arms, shoes or nozzles -- This subclass covers filter units equipped with backwash arms, shoes or nozzles that periodically clean the filter media during operation. The backwash mechanisms enable removal of dispersed particles from gases or vapours by reversing flow or spraying cleaning fluid, improving filter performance and longevity.
[B01D 46/74] by forces created by movement of the filter element -- This subclass covers filtration or filtering processes that separate dispersed particles from gases or vapors by using the forces generated when the filter element itself moves, such as vibration, oscillation, or reciprocating motion, rather than external mechanical or fluid forces.
[B01D 46/76] involving vibrations -- This CPC subclass covers filter or filtration systems that are adapted to separate suspended solid particles from gases or vapors by applying vibrations. The vibration can be mechanical or ultrasonic to enhance particle removal. It falls under B01D 46/76 within the separation section.
[B01D 46/762] involving sonic or ultrasonic waves -- This subclass concerns filters or filtering processes that employ sonic or ultrasonic waves to aid the separation of dispersed particles from gases or vapours, using acoustic energy to agitate or mobilize particles and improve collection efficiency.
[B01D 46/78] involving centrifugal forces -- B01D 46/78 covers filtering apparatus or processes that separate solid or liquid particles from gases or vapours by employing centrifugal forces, such as cyclonic separators or centrifuge-augmented filters. The classification is used for inventions where the filter is specially modified to enhance particle removal through rotation.
[B01D 46/785] by electrical means, e.g. for the generation of electrostatic forces in order to reject particles -- This subclass covers filters or filtering processes that are specially adapted to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours using electrical means, such as generating electrostatic forces to repel or capture the particles.
[B01D 46/79] by liquid process -- This subclass covers filtering or filtering processes that employ a liquid medium to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours. It includes methods where a liquid film, curtain or cascade acts as the capturing phase, facilitating removal of solid or liquid particulates from an airstream.
[B01D 46/80] Chemical processes for the removal of the retained particles, e.g. by burning -- B01D 46/80 covers chemical methods, such as combustion, used to destroy or eliminate particles that have been captured in filters or filtering systems designed for separating dispersed solids from gases or vapours. The processes are applied after filtration to clean or regenerate the filter media by burning off the retained material.
[B01D 46/82] with catalysts -- B01D 46/82 covers filters or filtering processes that are specifically adapted to separate dispersed solid particles from gases or vapours by employing catalysts, often to promote reaction or capture of the particles during filtration. This subclass falls under the broader hierarchy of performing operations, separating, and separation.
[B01D 46/84] by heating only -- A subclass of filters or filtering processes that are specially adapted to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours using heat as the sole means of separation, without any mechanical or chemical assistance.
[B01D 46/86] Cleaning the filter surface by interrupting suction so that the filter cake falls by gravity -- This subclass deals with cleaning a filter surface by momentarily stopping suction, allowing the filter cake to detach and fall by gravity. The method is used in gas or vapor filtration systems where the filter is specially modified to separate dispersed particles.
[B01D 46/88] Replacing filter elements -- B01D 46/88 covers techniques and devices for replacing filter elements in filtration units that separate particulate matter from gases or vapors. The entry includes mechanisms that allow quick removal, cleaning or swapping of the filter media without dismantling the whole system, improving maintenance efficiency in industrial or environmental applications.
[B01D 46/90] Devices for taking out of action one or more units of multi-unit filters, e.g. for regeneration or maintenance -- Devices for removing or disabling individual filter units within a multi-unit filter system, allowing those sections to be regenerated, serviced or replaced without dismantling the entire assembly. This facilitates maintenance of filters used to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours.
[B01D 47/00] Separating dispersed particles from gases, air or vapours by liquid as separating agent -- B01D 47/00 covers processes that remove solid particles suspended in gases, air or vapours by contacting them with a liquid that captures the particles and separates them from the gas stream. The method typically involves scrubbing, wet impaction or spray-based techniques where the liquid entrains the dispersed solids for collection.
[B01D 47/02] by passing the gas or air or vapour over or through a liquid bath -- The subclass B01D 47/02 defines a separation technique in which a gas, air or vapour containing dispersed solid particles is passed over or through a liquid bath that acts as the separating agent. The liquid captures the particles, allowing them to be removed from the gas stream, a method commonly used in scrubbing, wet-filtration and similar purification processes.
[B01D 47/021] by bubbling the gas through a liquid bath -- The B01D 47/021 subclass covers methods for removing solid particles dispersed in gases, air, or vapours by passing the gas through a liquid bath, where bubbling the gas creates contact with the liquid that captures the particles. This technique falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and separation, specifically using a liquid as the separating agent.
[B01D 47/022] by using a liquid curtain  B01D47/06  takes precedence -- B01D 47/022 pertains to the separation of dispersed particles from gases, air, or vapours using a liquid as the separating agent, specifically through the method of employing a liquid curtain. However, if the process involves specific aspects covered by B01D 47/06, that classification takes precedence.
[B01D 47/024] by impinging the gas to be cleaned essentially in a perpendicular direction onto the liquid surface -- B01D 47/024 covers a gas-cleaning technique in which a contaminated gas stream is directed essentially at right angles onto a liquid surface, causing dispersed particles to be captured by the liquid. The method is used for separating solids, liquids or droplets from gases, air or vapours by impinging the gas onto the separating liquid.
[B01D 47/025] by contacting gas and liquid with a static flow mixer  B01D47/14  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass covers the removal of solid particles from streams of gas, air or vapour by washing them with a liquid, where the gas and liquid are brought together in a static-flow mixer that creates a fixed turbulence pattern. The mixer provides continuous contact without moving parts, enabling efficient particle capture and downstream separation.
[B01D 47/027] by directing the gas to be cleaned essentially tangential to the liquid surface -- B01D 47/027 pertains to a method of cleaning gases by directing them tangentially across a liquid surface, facilitating the separation of dispersed particles from the gas stream. This technique is used in environmental and industrial processes for air purification.
[B01D 47/028] by directing the gas through a wetted wire mesh or a perforated plate  B01D47/14  takes precedence -- The code B01D47/028 describes a gas cleaning method that removes dispersed particles by directing the gas through a wetted wire mesh or a perforated plate, where a liquid film captures the particles. This technique is part of the broader class of liquid-based separation processes.
[B01D 47/04] by passing the gas or air or vapour through foam -- The B01D 47/04 entry covers processes that clean gases, air or vapours by passing them through a liquid foam, which acts as the separating agent to capture dispersed particles. The foam traps the particles as the gas stream flows through, allowing the cleaned gas to exit while the contaminated foam is removed or regenerated.
[B01D 47/05] by condensation of the separating agent -- This subgroup covers processes that remove dispersed solid particles from gases, air or vapours by introducing a liquid separating agent which is then condensed, causing the droplets to capture and collect the particles so they can be separated from the gas phase.
[B01D 47/06] Spray cleaning -- B01D 47/06, titled Spray cleaning, covers methods that remove dispersed particles from gases, air or vapours by directing a liquid spray onto the stream, causing the particles to be captured and separated. It falls under the CPC hierarchy Performing Operations - Separating - Separation and applies to processes using a liquid as the separating agent.
[B01D 47/063] with two or more jets impinging against each other -- B01D 47/063 covers separation of dispersed particles from gases, air or vapours by a liquid agent using two or more jets that impinge against each other. The method falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and separation.
[B01D 47/066] with nozzles using mechanical vibrations -- The B01D 47/066 subclass covers separation of dispersed particles from gases, air or vapours by means of a liquid where the liquid is delivered through nozzles that are excited by mechanical vibrations. The vibrations improve atomisation and the contact between the liquid film and the particle-laden stream, enhancing collection efficiency.
[B01D 47/08] with rotary nozzles -- This subclass covers methods or apparatus that use rotating nozzles to spray a liquid into a gas stream to capture and remove suspended particles. The rotary nozzles create a swirl that enhances liquid-gas contact, improving separation efficiency in gas cleaning, dust removal, or vapor scrubbing.
[B01D 47/085] with nozzles which are partly immersed in the washing fluid -- A method for removing dispersed particles from gases, air or vapours by contacting them with a liquid washing fluid, wherein the washing fluid is supplied through nozzles that are at least partly immersed in the fluid. The partially immersed nozzles create a spray or jet that enhances mixing and mass transfer, improving the efficiency of the liquid-based separation process. This approach is classified under CPC B01D 47/085.
[B01D 47/10] Venturi scrubbers -- Venturi scrubbers are devices that use a high-velocity gas stream through a narrowed throat to atomize a liquid and capture particulate matter from gases, air or vapours. They fall under CPC class B01D 47/10, which covers separating dispersed particles from gases by liquid as the separating agent.
[B01D 47/12] Washers with plural different washing sections -- Washers that contain two or more distinct washing sections, each employing liquid as the separating medium, are used to remove dispersed particles from gases, air or vapours. The multiple sections allow staged or differential cleaning, improving removal efficiency for a variety of contaminants. This design falls under the CPC class B01D 47/12 for separation processes that employ liquid washing.
[B01D 47/14] Packed scrubbers -- Packed scrubbers are devices that clean gases, air or vapours by passing them through a column filled with packing material that holds a liquid film, allowing dispersed particles to be captured and removed. The liquid acts as a separating agent, enhancing contact between the gas stream and the scrubbing medium.
[B01D 47/16] Apparatus having rotary means, other than rotatable nozzles, for atomising the cleaning liquid -- This CPC subclass covers equipment that uses rotating components-excluding rotatable nozzles-to break a cleaning liquid into a fine spray for removing solid particles from gases, air or vapours. The rotary atomising action creates a liquid mist that contacts the gas stream, facilitating particle separation.
[B01D 47/18] with horizontally-arranged shafts -- B01D 47/18 covers methods of separating dispersed particles from gases, air or vapours by using a liquid as the separating agent in apparatus that employs horizontally-arranged shafts, and is classified under Separation within Performing Operations.
[B01D 49/00] Separating dispersed particles from gases, air or vapours by other methods -- B01D 49/00 covers technologies for removing solid particles that are suspended in gases, air or vapours using methods other than conventional filtration, cyclone or electrostatic techniques. It includes processes such as inertial impaction, thermophoretic collection, acoustic or magnetic separation, and any other unconventional means of particle capture from a gas stream. This subclass falls under the broader hierarchy of performing operations, separating, and separation.
[B01D 49/003] by sedimentation -- Separating dispersed particles from gases, air or vapours by allowing them to settle out under gravity, i.e., sedimentation, is classified under CPC code B01D 49/003 within the broader groups of performing operations, separating and separation. This method relies on particle weight and fluid resistance to cause particles to collect at the bottom of a vessel or chamber.
[B01D 49/006] by sonic or ultrasonic techniques -- This subgroup covers the use of sound waves, including ultrasonic frequencies, to separate dispersed solid particles from gases, air or vapours. Acoustic energy creates pressure variations or cavitation that cause particles to be removed from the gas stream, falling under the broader CPC class B01D 49/006.
[B01D 49/02] by thermal repulsion -- B01D 49/02 pertains to techniques that separate dispersed particles from gases, air or vapours by employing thermal repulsion, meaning the particles are driven away from the gas stream through heated surfaces or zones. It falls under the broader category of separating operations in the CPC system.
[B01D 5/00] Condensation of vapours -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - condensers -> F28B
[B01D 50/00] Combinations of methods or devices for separating particles from gases or vapours -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[B01D 5/0003] by using heat-exchange surfaces for indirect contact between gases or vapours and the cooling medium -- The subclass B01D 5/0003 covers methods for condensing vapours in which the vapour is brought into indirect contact with a cooling medium via heat-exchange surfaces, allowing heat to be transferred without mixing the two streams. It is part of the broader separation operations hierarchy that deals with vapor condensation using external heat-exchange equipment.
[B01D 5/0006] Coils or serpentines -- B01D 5/0006 relates to the use of coils or serpentines as heat-exchange elements for condensing vapours in separation processes. The coils provide a surface where vapour can release heat and turn into liquid, aiding the separation step within the broader category of separating operations.
[B01D 5/0009] Horizontal tubes -- Horizontal tubes are conduits arranged in a level orientation within condensation equipment, allowing vapour to flow across their length and release heat to the tube walls, producing liquid condensate. This configuration is used in condensers and heat exchangers where gravity assists liquid removal and enhances heat transfer efficiency.
[B01D 5/0012] Vertical tubes -- B01D 5/0012 pertains to vertical tubes employed in processes that condense vapours, a sub-category within separation operations. The classification covers the design, arrangement and use of upright tubular equipment that facilitates vapor condensation and phase change.
[B01D 5/0015] Plates -- B01D 5/0015 - Plates defines plate-type equipment used for the condensation of vapours, such as plate condensers, tray condensers or stacked plate heat exchangers, where the vapor contacts a series of flat surfaces to transfer heat and cause phase change.
[B01D 5/0018] Dome shaped  B01D5/0066  takes precedence -- B01D 5/0018 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to dome-shaped structures used in the condensation of vapours, with a note that B01D 5/0066 takes precedence when applicable. This classification is part of a detailed system for categorizing inventions related to performing operations and separating processes.
[B01D 5/0021] Vortex -- The B01D 5/0021 "Vortex" class defines apparatus or processes that create a vortex flow to assist the condensation of vapours, thereby facilitating separation of condensed liquid from gas. Such vortex techniques are employed in dryers, condensers and similar equipment to improve efficiency of vapour-to-liquid conversion.
[B01D 5/0024] Rotating vessels or vessels containing movable parts -- B01D 5/0024 covers rotating vessels or vessels that contain movable parts used in processes involving condensation of vapours, classified under the separation operations hierarchy. These devices promote efficient phase contact and aid in separating condensed liquids from vapor streams.
[B01D 5/0027] by direct contact between vapours or gases and the cooling medium -- This subclass covers processes that condense vapours or gases by bringing them into direct contact with a cooling medium, allowing heat transfer without an intervening barrier. It applies to equipment such as condensers, scrubbers and cooling towers where the vapor mixes directly with the coolant to achieve condensation.
[B01D 5/003] within column(s) -- The CPC class B01D 5/003 relates to processes where vapours are condensed inside one or more columns, such as packed or tray-filled columns, to achieve separation of components. It covers designs and operating conditions that promote condensation within the column structure for efficient material recovery.
[B01D 5/0033] Other features -- B01D 5/0033 refers to miscellaneous or additional aspects of vapour-condensation separation processes that are not covered by more specific sub-categories. It includes a range of auxiliary features such as particular apparatus arrangements, control mechanisms, heating or cooling provisions, and other functional elements that aid the condensation step in separating mixtures. This heading gathers those "other features" of condensation of vapours within the broader performing-operations, separating, separation hierarchy.
[B01D 5/0036] Multiple-effect condensation; Fractional condensation -- The CPC entry B01D 5/0036 falls under performing operations - separating - separation - condensation of vapours and covers technologies that use multiple-effect or fractional condensation to recover heat and separate vapor mixtures. It includes arrangements where condensation occurs in successive stages or where condensate is split into fractions based on temperature or composition.
[B01D 5/0039] Recuperation of heat, e.g. use of heat pump(s), compression -- The B01D 5/0039 class deals with methods that recover heat from vapour streams during separation, typically by employing heat-pump or compression techniques to condense the vapour and reuse the thermal energy. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and condensation of vapours.
[B01D 5/0042] Thermo-electric condensing; using Peltier-effect -- This subclass includes apparatus that condense vapour by means of thermoelectric cooling using the Peltier effect, where an electric current across dissimilar conductors creates a temperature gradient that removes heat and forces the vapour to change to liquid.
[B01D 5/0045] Vacuum condensation -- Vacuum condensation refers to the process of condensing vapour streams while they are under reduced pressure, typically using a vacuum pump or low-pressure environment to lower the boiling point of the components. This technique is employed in separation operations to recover liquids from gases more efficiently and with lower energy consumption.
[B01D 5/0048] Barometric condensation -- Barometric condensation is a separation technique in which a vapor is condensed by lowering the surrounding pressure, causing the vapor to change to liquid without cooling. The process exploits the pressure-dependent saturation temperature of the vapor and is used in laboratory and industrial settings to isolate low-volatility components.
[B01D 5/0051] Regulation processes; Control systems, e.g. valves -- B01D 5/0051 relates to regulation processes and control systems used in the condensation of vapours, such as valves or other devices that adjust pressure, flow or temperature to maintain desired operating conditions. The entry covers methods and apparatus for controlling the separation of vapour into liquid during condensation.
[B01D 5/0054] General arrangements, e.g. flow sheets -- B01D 5/0054 pertains to the overall layout of apparatus and process flow diagrams used in vapor-condensation separation operations. It includes general arrangements such as the positioning of condensers, heat exchangers, and associated piping within a system. The classification covers how these elements are organized to achieve efficient condensation of vapors.
[B01D 5/0057] in combination with other processes -- The CPC subclass B01D 5/0057 covers methods where condensation of vapors is performed together with other processing steps. It falls under the hierarchy Performing Operations - Separating - Separation, indicating that the condensation is part of a broader separation operation integrated with additional procedures.
[B01D 5/006] with evaporation or distillation -- The CPC class B01D 5/006 covers separation processes that rely on condensing vapours, specifically when the vapour is first evaporated or distilled and then condensed to recover or purify components of a mixture. It includes methods and apparatus for the condensation step in such vapor-phase separations.
[B01D 5/0063] Reflux condensation -- The CPC subclass B01D 5/0063 covers reflux condensation, a technique in separation processes where vapour that rises in a column is cooled and condensed to flow back down, enhancing the efficiency of distillation or other vapor-liquid operations. It falls under the hierarchy of Performing Operations → Separating → Separation → Condensation of vapours.
[B01D 5/0066] Dome shaped condensation -- A dome-shaped condensation refers to a configuration in which condensed vapour collects on a curved, hemispherical surface, forming a dome that facilitates uniform heat removal and liquid collection. This geometry is employed in condensers and related equipment to enhance separation efficiency by promoting even film flow and preventing droplet buildup.
[B01D 5/0069] with degasification or deaeration -- B01D 5/0069 is a CPC subclass under Performing Operations → Separating → Separation → Condensation of vapours and covers processes that incorporate degasification or deaeration, i.e., the removal of dissolved gases from a vapor as it condenses.
[B01D 5/0072] with filtration -- This subgroup covers methods of separating condensed vapour by passing it through a filter to remove solid particles or liquid droplets, often used in drying or purification steps. It lies under performing operations - separating - separation - condensation of vapours in the CPC scheme, with code B01D 5/0072 and title with filtration.
[B01D 5/0075] with heat exchanging  B01D5/0039  takes precedence -- B01D 5/0075 defines separation processes that condense vapours by employing a heat-exchanging arrangement, where thermal energy is transferred between the vapour stream and another fluid to facilitate condensation. This subclass falls under B01D 5/0039, which provides the broader context for heat-exchange-assisted condensation and therefore has precedence.
[B01D 5/0078] characterised by auxiliary systems or arrangements -- The CPC subclass B01D 5/0078 covers processes for condensing vapours in which the main operation is assisted by additional equipment or auxiliary systems, such as heat exchangers, pumps, or control devices, that support the condensation but are not the primary separation element. This subclass falls under the hierarchy of Performing Operations → Separating → Separation → Condensation of vapours.
[B01D 5/0081] Feeding the steam or the vapours -- The B01D 5/0081 classification covers devices or processes that supply steam or vapour to a condenser in separation operations, ensuring effective condensation of vapours during the separation step. It includes feeding arrangements, distribution systems and control mechanisms for delivering the condensable medium to the condensation zone.
[B01D 5/0084] Feeding or collecting the cooling medium  B01D5/0087  takes precedence -- B01D5/0084 relates to the provision or recovery of the cooling medium in systems that condense vapours. It includes feeding the coolant to a condenser or collecting it after cooling, and is positioned under separation operations, specifically condensation of vapours. The subclass B01D5/0087 is treated as higher in precedence.
[B01D 5/0087] Recirculating of the cooling medium -- The code covers processes in which the cooling fluid used during vapour condensation is continuously reused, typically within condensers, to improve energy efficiency, reduce waste and maintain temperature control. It falls under the broader category of separating operations, specifically the condensation of vapours.
[B01D 5/009] Collecting, removing and/or treatment of the condensate -- The CPC subclass B01D 5/009 covers techniques for handling the liquid condensate that forms when vapours are condensed in separation operations. It includes methods and devices for collecting, removing, draining, recovering or otherwise treating the condensate to prevent buildup or to enable further use.
[B01D 5/0093] Removing and treatment of non condensable gases -- B01D 5/0093 covers processes for eliminating or conditioning gases that remain in the vapor phase during condensation, such as air, carbon dioxide or inert gases. It includes techniques like scrubbing, adsorption, membrane separation or pressure swing adsorption used to improve product purity or process efficiency.
[B01D 5/0096] Cleaning  cleaning in general  B08B -- B01D 5/0096 pertains to separation processes that use condensation of vapors as a cleaning step, encompassing methods that remove contaminants by cooling vapors to a liquid state and collecting the condensate. It falls under Performing Operations → Separating → Separation → Condensation of vapours and is generally described as cleaning in general (B08B).
[B01D 50/10] Combinations of devices covered by groups -- This CPC subclass covers combinations of devices that fall within the groups for separating particles from gases or vapours, as part of the broader "Separating" and "Separation" operations. It pertains to integrated or paired equipment that jointly performs particle-removal functions, rather than single-device solutions. The classification is used for patents describing such combined apparatuses.
[B01D 50/20] Combinations of devices covered by groups -- B01D 50/20 covers integrated arrangements that combine two or more separation devices from the related groups for removing particles from gases or vapours, such as filters coupled with cyclones or electrostatic precipitators. The subclass is used for patents describing combined or hybrid apparatuses that perform the separation operation in a single system.
[B01D 50/40] Combinations of devices covered by groups -- B01D 50/40 covers integrated systems that combine two or more separation devices-such as filters, cyclones, scrubbers or electrostatic precipitators-to remove particles from gases or vapours. The subclass groups arrangements that exploit complementary principles to enhance collection efficiency, reduce pressure drop or enable staged cleaning in a single apparatus. It applies to any combination of devices defined in the surrounding CPC groups for gas-solid separation.
[B01D 50/60] Combinations of devices covered by groups -- B01D 50/60 covers combined methods or apparatuses that separate solid particles from gases or vapours, integrating two or more devices described in the preceding groups. It includes hybrid systems that use, for example, filtration together with cyclonic separation or electrostatic precipitation in a single unit.
[B01D 51/00] Auxiliary pretreatment of gases or vapours to be cleaned -- B01D 51/00 covers auxiliary pretreatment steps applied to gases or vapours before the main cleaning operation, such as conditioning, pre-scrubbing, adsorption, or chemical modification to improve subsequent removal of contaminants. It includes methods that alter physical or chemical properties of the stream to enhance efficiency of the primary separation process.
[B01D 51/02] Amassing the particles, e.g. by flocculation -- The subgroup addresses techniques for gathering particles present in a gas or vapor stream, typically by inducing agglomeration or floc formation so that the solid matter can be removed more easily. Such amassing methods are used as an auxiliary pretreatment step before primary separation processes like filtration or cyclonic cleaning.
[B01D 51/04] by seeding, e.g. by adding particles -- This subclass covers methods that introduce particles into a gas or vapour stream to create nucleation sites, thereby aiding the removal of contaminants during auxiliary pretreatment steps such as scrubbing, filtration, or condensation.
[B01D 51/06] by varying the pressure of the gas or vapour -- This subclass covers auxiliary pretreatment of gases or vapours to be cleaned by varying their pressure, such as compression, expansion or pressure pulsation, to aid subsequent separation or contaminant removal. It is placed under the separation hierarchy of performing operations.
[B01D 51/08] by sound or ultrasound -- The subclass B01D 51/08 covers auxiliary pretreatment of gases or vapours that are to be cleaned, where the pretreatment is performed using sound or ultrasonic energy. It includes methods that apply acoustic waves to the gas stream to promote particle agglomeration, enhance dissolving of contaminants, or otherwise improve subsequent separation or cleaning steps.
[B01D 51/10] Conditioning the gas to be cleaned -- The B01D 51/10 class covers methods that prepare a gas or vapour before it undergoes a cleaning step. It includes auxiliary pretreatments such as heating, cooling, humidifying, de-humidifying, or adding chemicals to modify the gas composition or physical state, thereby improving the efficiency of the subsequent separation or purification process.
[B01D 53/00] Separation of gases or vapours -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Catalytic treatment of gases in B01D53/00 is only for waste gases, i.e. catalytic treatment or synthesis of other gases such as commercially relevant gases (e.g. synthesis or natural gas) is not classified in B01D53/00 ).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Catalytic treatment of exhaust gases from engine exhaust gases (a waste gas) -> B01D53/94 - B01D53/9495
[B01D 53/002] by condensation -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Condensation of water vapour -> B01D53/265 - Solidifying carbon dioxide -> C01B32/55 - Other gas mixtures -> F25J1/00
[B01D 53/005] by heat treatment -- The CPC subclass B01D 53/005 covers processes that separate gases or vapours through the application of heat, such as thermal diffusion, condensation, or temperature-gradient techniques. It includes methods where temperature differences drive the separation of components in a gaseous mixture.
[B01D 53/007] by irradiation -- B01D 53/007 covers methods for separating gases or vapours that rely on irradiation, such as photolysis, UV or other radiative energy to induce differential uptake or reaction, enabling the components to be separated. This sub-group lies within the broader category of performing operations for gas separation.
[B01D 53/02] by adsorption, e.g. preparative gas chromatography -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Only specific solid adsorbent materials, i.e. not for documents covering general aspects of adsorption.
[B01D 53/025] with wetted adsorbents; Chromatography  analytical chromatography  G01N30/00  -  G01N30/96 ; for liquids  B01D15/08 -- The CPC subclass B01D 53/025 covers separation of gases or vapours using wetted adsorbents, primarily in analytical chromatography. It pertains to liquid-phase chromatography methods linked to the G01N30 series and the related B01D15/08 subcategory.
[B01D 53/04] with stationary adsorbents -- **Definition:** This place covers:  General adsorption process aspects.  This group is often used for (general) regeneration aspects and new combinations of known adsorbents for specific gases.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Adsorption with wetted adsorbents; Chromatography -> B01D53/025 - Controlling processes and pressure & temperature swing adsorption processes -> B01D53/0454 , B01D53/047 - B01D53/053
[B01D 53/0407] Constructional details of adsorbing systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus (mechanical) related concepts (as opposed to process related inventions classified in B01D53/04 ).
[B01D 53/0415] Beds in cartridges -- B01D 53/0415 covers cartridges that contain a packed bed of material used to separate gases or vapours, generally by adsorption, absorption or catalytic reaction within the cartridge.
[B01D 53/0423] Beds in columns -- This CPC subclass covers arrangements where the separation of gases or vapours is performed using packed or fluidised beds arranged in vertical columns, focusing on the design, operation and related aspects of columnar bed structures for gas-phase separation processes.
[B01D 53/0431] Beds with radial gas flow -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Concepts with an annular bed in form of a hollow cylinder.
[B01D 53/0438] Cooling or heating systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mainly regeneration systems.
[B01D 53/0446] Means for feeding or distributing gases -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Concepts focussed on ducts, manifolds, flow dividers, pumps, valves, etc.
[B01D 53/0454] Controlling adsorption  controlling temperature swing adsorption  B01D53/0462 , controlling pressure swing adsorption  B01D53/047 -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Documents relating to temperature swing adsorption (TSA) -> B01D53/0462 - Documents relating to pressure swing adsorption (PSA) -> B01D53/047
[B01D 53/0462] Temperature swing adsorption -- The CPC subclass B01D 53/0462 covers temperature-swing adsorption processes used to separate gases or vapours. It falls under the broader hierarchy of performing operations, separating, and separation of gases or vapours, and describes systems that adsorb a mixture at one temperature and release the desired component by changing the temperature.
[B01D 53/047] Pressure swing adsorption -- **Definition:** This place covers:  PSA processes characterised by a sequences of cycle steps
[B01D 53/0473] Rapid pressure swing adsorption -- **Definition:** This place covers:  RPSA processes characterised by a sequences of cycle steps
[B01D 53/0476] Vacuum pressure swing adsorption -- **Definition:** This place covers:  VPSA processes characterised by a sequences of cycle steps.
[B01D 53/053] with storage or buffer vessel -- The CPC subclass B01D 53/053 classifies methods and apparatus for separating gases or vapours in which the separated stream is collected or held in a storage or buffer vessel, covering designs that incorporate tanks or reservoirs as part of the separation process. This entry falls under the hierarchy of Performing Operations → Separating → Separation → Separation of gases or vapours.
[B01D 53/06] with moving adsorbents, e.g. rotating beds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Moving (e.g. rotating or oscillating) adsorbent structures or units, e.g. rotating beds.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Separation of gases or vapours with wetted absorbents -> B01D53/025
[B01D 53/08] according to the "moving bed" method -- **Definition:** This place covers: Concepts where adsorbent particles travel (mainly by gravity) through a reactor and are often, after regeneration, reintroduced.  **Glossary:** In this place, the following terms or expressions are used with the meaning indicated: In the patent documents the following expressions/words "Fliessbett, Wanderbett" (German) ,"fluidized bed" and "moving bed" are often used as synonyms.
[B01D 53/10] with dispersed adsorbents -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Concepts where (loose) adsorbent particles are dispersed into a gas stream for a short residence (contact) time.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Concepts with loose particles being held for a longer residence time in a fluidized state -> B01D53/12
[B01D 53/12] according to the "fluidised technique" -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Concepts with loose particles being held for a longer residence time in a fluidized state.
[B01D 53/14] by absorption -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Concepts using liquid physical and chemical solvents
[B01D 53/1406] Multiple stage absorption -- A multiple-stage absorption process separates gases or vapours by passing the mixture through two or more absorption zones, each using a liquid absorbent that preferentially captures one component. The staged arrangement enhances overall removal efficiency and allows finer control of temperature and concentration gradients compared with a single-stage system. This method is used in industrial gas purification, CO₂ capture and similar applications.
[B01D 53/1412] Controlling the absorption process -- B01D 53/1412 relates to techniques for regulating the absorption step in gas or vapour separation processes. It includes control of operating variables such as temperature, pressure, flow rate or the use of sensors and feedback systems to maintain a desired absorption efficiency. The classification falls under Performing Operations - Separating - Separation of gases or vapours.
[B01D 53/1418] Recovery of products -- B01D 53/1418 covers technologies for recovering useful products from mixtures of gases or vapours. It includes processes such as condensation, adsorption, membrane separation or other methods that isolate and collect target components after a gas-phase separation step. The classification is used for equipment and procedures that retrieve valuable substances from gaseous streams.
[B01D 53/1425] Regeneration of liquid absorbents -- The CPC class B01D 53/1425 covers processes for regenerating liquid absorbents that have been used to capture gases or vapours, including techniques such as heating, pressure changes, stripping or chemical treatment to restore their absorbing capacity. It falls under the broader hierarchy of performing operations, separating, and separation of gases or vapours.
[B01D 53/1431] Pretreatment by other processes -- B01D 53/1431 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to methods for pretreating gases or vapours before separation, involving processes that are not chemical reactions. These may include physical treatments like cooling, heating, or pressure changes to prepare the gas or vapour for more effective separation.
[B01D 53/1437] Pretreatment by adsorption -- B01D 53/1437 covers methods that pre-treat a gas or vapour by passing it over an adsorbent to remove selected components before subsequent separation or processing steps. The adsorption step can eliminate moisture, trace contaminants or specific gases, improving the efficiency of later separation operations.
[B01D 53/1443] Pretreatment by diffusion -- B01D 53/1443 covers methods that precondition a gas or vapour mixture by allowing its components to diffuse through a membrane, porous layer or other selective barrier before the main separation step. The pretreatment can adjust concentrations, remove impurities or enrich a target component, thereby enhancing the efficiency of subsequent separation processes.
[B01D 53/145] Pretreatment by separation of solid or liquid material -- The CPC code B01D 53/145 refers to pretreatment steps that separate solid or liquid material before the main gas or vapour separation operation. It covers methods such as filtration, centrifugation, sedimentation or other physical means used to remove impurities, recover valuable fractions, or adjust the composition of the feed prior to downstream processing. This subclass falls under the hierarchy of Performing Operations → Separating → Separation → Separation of gases or vapours.
[B01D 53/1456] Removing acid components -- This CPC subclass covers processes for separating gases or vapours in which acidic constituents are removed from a mixture, including scrubbing, absorption or chemical neutralisation techniques. It is applied to patents describing equipment or methods that extract acid components during gas-separation operations.
[B01D 53/1462] Removing mixtures of hydrogen sulfide and carbon dioxide -- The subclass B01D 53/1462 covers methods for separating a gas mixture that contains hydrogen sulfide and carbon dioxide, focusing on processes that remove both components simultaneously. Techniques may include absorption, adsorption, membrane separation or chemical conversion to treat the mixture and recover the purified gases.
[B01D 53/1468] Removing hydrogen sulfide -- A process within the gas-separation group that specifically removes hydrogen sulfide from gas or vapor streams. It includes methods such as absorption, adsorption, chemical reaction, or membrane techniques to eliminate H₂S, thereby purifying the remaining gases for industrial or environmental applications.
[B01D 53/1475] Removing carbon dioxide -- B01D 53/1475 covers technologies for extracting carbon dioxide from gas or vapor mixtures. It includes chemical absorption, physical adsorption, membrane separation, cryogenic condensation and related processes designed to reduce CO₂ content in industrial or emission streams.
[B01D 53/1481] Removing sulfur dioxide or sulfur trioxide -- This CPC subclass covers processes for removing sulfur dioxide or sulfur trioxide from gas streams, typically by absorption, adsorption or catalytic conversion, and is used in industrial gas cleaning and environmental control. It falls under the broader category of gas separation within performing operations.
[B01D 53/1487] Removing organic compounds -- B01D 53/1487 relates to processes that eliminate organic substances from gaseous or vapor mixtures. It covers techniques such as adsorption, absorption, condensation and membrane separation used to purify air, exhaust streams or process gases by selectively removing hydrocarbons, solvents or other organic contaminants.
[B01D 53/1493] Selection of liquid materials for use as absorbents -- The CPC subclass B01D 53/1493 falls under the hierarchy of Performing Operations → Separating → Separation → Separation of gases or vapours. It covers the choice of liquid materials to be used as absorbents in processes that remove or capture gases or vapour streams.
[B01D 53/18] Absorbing units -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus (mechanical) aspects of absorption columns or units.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Fractionating columns -> B01D3/16 , B01D3/26 , B01D3/30
[B01D 53/185] Liquid distributors -- Liquid distributors are apparatuses that spread or introduce a liquid phase uniformly within a separation system, often used to enhance contact between liquids and gases or vapours. They include spray nozzles, atomisers, and perforated plates that create droplets or films to improve mass-transfer efficiency in processes such as absorption, stripping, or drying.
[B01D 53/22] by diffusion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The groups B01D53/22 - B01D53/229 cover gas separation concepts using membranes.
[B01D 53/225] Multiple stage diffusion -- Multiple-stage diffusion refers to a separation method where gases or vapours pass sequentially through two or more diffusion zones, each stage further enriching the desired component. By arranging several diffusion steps in series, the overall separation efficiency is increased compared with a single stage, and the process is used in membrane, porous-media, or similar apparatuses for gas-vapour purification.
[B01D 53/226] in serial connexion -- The subclass B01D 53/226 pertains to separation of gases or vapours using a series connection, where the product of one separation stage is fed directly into the next stage. It covers methods and apparatus that employ successive separation units to increase purity, recovery or efficiency of the overall process.
[B01D 53/227] in parallel connexion -- This subclass covers separation of gases or vapours using multiple parallel units or pathways, where the individual separating elements are connected side-by-side to operate concurrently. The parallel configuration allows higher throughput or simultaneous processing of separate streams within a single overall system.
[B01D 53/228] characterised by specific membranes -- B01D 53/228 relates to methods for separating gases or vapours that are distinguished by the use of specific membrane materials, where the membrane's composition, structure or functional coating is essential to the separation process. It includes designs where the membrane selectively permits certain components to pass while retaining others, enabling efficient gas or vapour purification.
[B01D 53/229] Integrated processes (Diffusion and at least one other process, e.g. adsorption, absorption) -- B01D 53/229 defines integrated separation processes that combine diffusion with at least one additional operation such as adsorption or absorption, used for separating gases or vapors. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, separating, and separation of gases or vapours.
[B01D 53/24] by centrifugal force -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Centrifugal separation of particles or aerosols from a gas stream -> B01D45/00
[B01D 53/26] Drying gases or vapours -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Concepts for removing water vapour from a gas.
[B01D 53/261] by adsorption -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Use of solid sorbents as drying agents.
[B01D 53/263] by absorption -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gas drying concepts using a liquid drying agent (solvent).
[B01D 53/265] by refrigeration (condensation) -- B01D 53/265 covers processes that separate gases or vapours by cooling them until one or more components condense, allowing the condensed phase to be withdrawn from the remaining gas stream.
[B01D 53/266] by filtration -- B01D 53/266 pertains to the separation of gases or vapours by filtration, describing processes that pass a gas stream through a porous medium or filter element to remove particulate matter, droplets or condensates, thereby achieving a cleaner or purified gas output.
[B01D 53/268] by diffusion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gas drying concepts using membranes.
[B01D 53/28] Selection of materials for use as drying agents -- B01D 53/28 concerns the choice of substances employed as drying agents in processes that separate gases or vapours. It covers criteria for selecting materials that effectively absorb moisture to facilitate gas or vapour purification within the broader field of separating operations.
[B01D 53/30] Controlling by gas-analysis apparatus -- B01D 53/30 covers devices that regulate gas or vapour separation processes by analysing the gas composition and using that information to adjust operating conditions. Such control systems may include sensors, analysers and feedback mechanisms that maintain product quality or efficiency during separation.
[B01D 53/32] by electrical effects other than those provided for in group -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Concepts using electric discharge or corona discharge, e.g. a plasma treatment.
[B01D 53/323] by electrostatic effects or by high-voltage electric fields -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Concepts using electric fields where no discharge takes place.
[B01D 53/326] in electrochemical cells -- B01D 53/326 covers methods for separating gases or vapours using electrochemical cells, such as electrolytic generation of gas streams or ion-exchange membranes that selectively transport specific gases.
[B01D 53/34] Chemical or biological purification of waste gases -- B01D 53/34 covers methods that use chemical reactions or biological agents to cleanse waste gases, removing contaminants such as pollutants or harmful vapours. It falls under the broader category of gas separation techniques, focusing on purification processes that convert the waste stream into cleaner emissions.
[B01D 53/343] Heat recovery -- The CPC entry B01D 53/343 covers methods and apparatuses that recover heat generated during the separation of gases or vapours, typically by using heat exchangers, regenerative cooling, or other thermodynamic techniques to improve energy efficiency of the separation process. This includes systems that capture and reuse thermal energy from compression, expansion, condensation or adsorption steps within gas-vapor separation operations.
[B01D 53/346] Controlling the process -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling concepts for chemical and biological waste gas treatments.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Controlling catalytic processes -> B01D53/8696 - Controlling catalytic processes involving engine exhaust gases -> B01D53/9495

[B01D 53/501] by treating the gases with a solution or a suspension of an alkali or earth-alkali or ammonium compound -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Removing sulfur dioxide or sulfur trioxide from gases other than waste gases by absorption with solvents -> B01D53/1481
[B01D 53/502] characterised by a specific solution or suspension -- Subclass B01D 53/502 deals with separation of gases or vapours that is achieved by using a particular liquid solution or suspension as the separating medium, such as absorption, scrubbing or liquid-phase extraction. The method relies on the specific chemical or physical properties of the chosen solution to selectively capture or dissolve target components from the gas stream.
[B01D 53/504] characterised by a specific device -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Constructional (mechanical) concepts related to desulfurization.
[B01D 53/505] in a spray drying process -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Inter alia concepts involving semi-solid reagents such as slurries.
[B01D 53/507] by treating the gases with other liquids -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Removing sulfur dioxide or sulfur trioxide from gases other than waste gases by absorption with solvents -> B01D53/1481
[B01D 53/508] by treating the gases with solids -- B01D 53/508 classifies processes that separate gases or vapours by contacting them with solid materials, such as adsorbents, absorbents or solid catalysts, to remove or purify specific components. The solid phase may capture, react with or otherwise treat the gas mixture to achieve the desired separation.
[B01D 53/52] Hydrogen sulfide -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Removing hydrogen sulfide from gases other than waste gases by absorption with solvents -> B01D53/1468 - Corresponding group for catalytic treatments -> B01D53/8612
