[B01F 21/30] Workflow diagrams or layout of plants, e.g. flow charts; Details of workflow diagrams or layout of plants, e.g. controlling means -- B01F 21/30 covers patents that describe the overall layout and workflow diagrams of plants used for dissolving or related mixing operations, including flow charts and the control means that coordinate the sequence of unit operations. It pertains to the arrangement and management of equipment in separating and mixing processes.
[B01F 21/40] characterised by the state of the material being dissolved -- B01F 21/40 relates to mixing operations where the substance is already in a dissolved state, focusing on methods that maintain or exploit that state during processing. It includes techniques that use solvents, temperature, or pressure to keep the material dissolved while performing further actions.
[B01F 21/401] Molten solids -- Covers processes where solid materials are melted to form a fluid phase for subsequent mixing, dissolution, emulsification or dispersion. It is used for melting metals, polymers, ceramics or other solids before operations such as alloying, compounding or slurry preparation.
[B01F 21/402] characterised by the configuration, form or shape of the solid material, e.g. in the form of tablets or blocks -- This CPC subclass B01F 21/402 addresses dissolution processes in which the configuration, form or shape of the solid feedstock - such as tablets, pellets or blocks - is a defining characteristic of the operation. It covers methods where the geometry of the solid material influences how it dissolves.
[B01F 21/4021] in the form of tablets stored in containers, canisters or receptacles -- This subclass deals with methods of dissolving solid substances that are supplied as tablets kept in containers, canisters or other receptacles, focusing on the preparation of solutions from pre-packaged tablet forms.
[B01F 21/403] Solid carbon dioxide or dry ice -- B01F 21/403 covers processes that employ solid carbon dioxide, or dry ice, as a cooling or freezing medium, a blowing agent, or a reagent in operations such as mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing substances. It is situated within the broader categories of separating and mixing, specifically under dissolving.
[B01F 21/50] Elements used for separating or keeping undissolved material in the mixer -- The CPC code B01F 21/50 relates to elements placed in mixers to keep undissolved material separate from the liquid phase, such as baffles, screens, filters or other retaining devices. These components assist in separating solids during mixing operations like dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 21/501] Tablet canisters provided with perforated walls, sieves, grids or filters -- B01F 21/501 covers tablet canisters that incorporate perforated walls, sieves, grids or filters to facilitate the dissolution of solid dosage forms. Such containers are used in mixing or separating operations where the solid material is broken down and dispersed into a liquid medium. The design enables controlled-release and efficient separation of dissolved components.
[B01F 21/502] Baffles -- Baffles are stationary plates or ribs mounted inside a vessel or tank to disrupt fluid flow, increase turbulence and prevent vortex formation during mixing, thereby improving the efficiency of dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing processes. They are commonly used in stirred-tank reactors, batch mixers and other equipment where enhanced mixing performance is required.
[B01F 21/503] Filters -- Filters are devices that separate solid particles from liquids or gases by forcing the mixture through a porous medium, allowing the fluid to pass while retaining the solids. The entry covers various filter types, materials and configurations used in mixing and separating operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 21/504] Sieves, i.e. perforated plates or walls -- Sieves are perforated plates or walls that separate solids from liquids or gases during processes such as dissolving, mixing, or emulsifying. The perforations allow fluid to pass while retaining particles, and the devices may be stationary or rotating to enhance separation efficiency. This entry covers various sieve designs used in chemical and mechanical operations.
[B01F 2215/00] Auxiliary or complementary information in relation with mixing -- The code B01F 2215/00 covers auxiliary or complementary information used during mixing operations, such as process data, sensor readings, control signals, or visual indicators that help monitor, adjust, or document the mixing, dispersing, emulsifying or dissolving of materials. It includes information that supports the primary mixing function but is not part of the mechanical equipment itself.
[B01F 2215/04] Technical information in relation with mixing -- This CPC subclass B01F 2215/04 pertains to auxiliary or complementary technical information-such as data, instructions, or documentation-that supports or enhances mixing operations, including dissolution, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 2215/0404] theories or general explanations of phenomena associated with mixing or generalizations of a concept by comparison of equivalent methods -- The code B01F 2215/0404 covers theoretical or general explanations of phenomena linked to mixing, including comparative generalizations of concepts across equivalent methods, and auxiliary information related to mixing processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 2215/0409] Relationships between different variables defining features or parameters of the apparatus or process -- This CPC subclass (B01F 2215/0409) covers auxiliary or complementary information for mixing operations, focusing on the relationships among variables that define the features or parameters of the mixing apparatus or process, such as speed, temperature, geometry, residence time, or other operating conditions.
[B01F 2215/0413] Numerical information -- B01F 2215/0413 covers the provision of numerical data that describe or support mixing operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing, including measurements, calculations or statistical parameters related to the process. This auxiliary information helps quantify performance, efficiency or product characteristics in separation and mixing technologies.
[B01F 2215/0418] Geometrical information -- B01F 2215/0418 covers the provision of geometrical information that supports or complements mixing operations, such as data on the shape, size, arrangement or spatial configuration of mixers, vessels, baffles, agitators or other components involved in separating, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing processes. It is aimed at facilitating the design, analysis or optimization of mixing equipment by supplying dimensional or layout details.
[B01F 2215/0422] Numerical values of angles -- The subclass covers the disclosure of specific angle measurements that define the geometry of mixing devices or the orientation of flow in separation operations. It includes numerical values for inlet, outlet, blade or vortex angles used to characterize mixing actions such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. These angle data serve as auxiliary information for the design or control of mixing processes.
[B01F 2215/0427] Numerical distance values, e.g. separation, position -- This subclass concerns the provision of numerical data that quantify distances, separations or relative positions of components during mixing or separating operations, such as the spacing between particles, layers or phases. It serves as auxiliary information for monitoring, modelling or controlling processes like dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 2215/0431] Numerical size values, e.g. diameter of a hole or conduit, area, volume, length, width, or ratios thereof -- B01F 2215/0431 covers the provision of quantitative dimensional data that accompany mixing or separating operations, such as specified diameters, areas, volumes, lengths, widths or their ratios. This auxiliary information helps define the geometry of reactors, conduits or dispersion zones involved in the process.
[B01F 2215/0436] Operational information -- This subclass contains information that details how a mixing operation is performed, specifying parameters such as speed, temperature, time and other operating conditions required for dissolution, emulsification or dispersion. It includes procedural guidance, process data and control instructions that enable effective execution of the mixing step.
[B01F 2215/044] Numerical composition values of components or mixtures, e.g. percentage of components -- This subclass provides auxiliary information on the quantitative composition of mixtures, such as the percentage of each component, used to support mixing operations like dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It is intended for documentation, quality control, or process optimisation.
[B01F 2215/0445] Numerical electrical values, e.g. intensity, voltage -- The CPC subclass B01F 2215/0445 covers the provision of numerical electrical parameters such as current or voltage as auxiliary or complementary information for mixing-related operations, including separating, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing, to aid control or monitoring of the process.
[B01F 2215/045] Numerical flow-rate values -- The subclass B01F 2215/045 covers the provision of numerical flow-rate data as auxiliary or complementary information for mixing operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It pertains to quantitative values that describe the rate at which fluids are moved through a mixer, helping to define process conditions and performance.
[B01F 2215/0454] Numerical frequency values -- The subclass B01F 2215/0454 covers the provision of numerical frequency values that are used to control or describe mixing operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It includes data on pulse, oscillation or rotation frequencies, timing parameters and other quantitative frequency information that assist in planning, monitoring or optimizing the mixing process.
[B01F 2215/0459] Numerical values of dimensionless numbers, i.e. Re, Pr, Nu, transfer coefficients -- The entry concerns auxiliary data for mixing operations, specifically the provision of numerical values for dimensionless groups such as Reynolds, Prandtl, Nusselt and related transfer coefficients. These values support the analysis and design of processes involving dissolution, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 2215/0463] Numerical power values -- The CPC entry B01F 2215/0463 covers auxiliary or complementary data that provide numerical power values for mixing operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing, within the broader class of separating processes. It specifies how power measurements are reported or used to characterize the performance of mixers and related equipment.
[B01F 2215/0468] Numerical pressure values -- B01F 2215/0468 designates auxiliary or complementary information concerning mixing processes, specifically the provision of numerical pressure values used to monitor, control or improve operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. The subclass falls under performing operations, separating, and mixing, and covers methods of measuring, reporting or applying pressure data in mixing equipment.
[B01F 2215/0472] Numerical temperature values -- The subclass B01F 2215/0472 concerns the provision of numerical temperature data that support mixing operations such as dissolution, emulsification or dispersion. It includes temperature values, profiles, set points or measurement methods used to monitor or control the temperature during the mixing process.
[B01F 2215/0477] Numerical time values -- The CPC entry B01F 2215/0477 provides auxiliary information concerning mixing operations, specifically the numerical time values such as durations, time intervals or timing sequences that are used to control or describe processes like dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It falls under PERFORMING OPERATIONS → SEPARATING → MIXING, e.g. DISSOLVING, EMULSIFYING OR DISPERSING.
[B01F 2215/0481] Numerical speed values -- B01F 2215/0481 supplies numerical speed values - for example revolutions per minute, shear rate or other quantitative speed parameters - that describe the conditions of a mixing operation such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing, and are presented as auxiliary or complementary information to characterize the performance of the process.
[B01F 2215/0486] Material property information -- B01F 2215/0486 covers the provision of material property data that support mixing operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It includes auxiliary information like viscosity, density, surface tension or particle size that influence how substances behave during mixing and help optimize process parameters.
[B01F 2215/049] Numerical values of density of substances -- The code B01F 2215/049 concerns the provision of numerical density data for substances that are mixed or dispersed in a process. It is used to classify documents that report measured or calculated densities of liquids, solids, or gases which affect separating, mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing operations. This auxiliary information supports the design and control of mixing equipment and formulations.
[B01F 2215/0495] Numerical values of viscosity of substances -- This subclass records the provision of quantitative viscosity data for substances involved in mixing operations, such as liquids, suspensions or emulsions, and includes measured values, tables or charts that describe the flow resistance of the material. It supports the design, control or analysis of mixing processes by supplying the numerical viscosity parameters needed for such applications.
[B01F 23/00] Mixing according to the phases to be mixed, e.g. dispersing or emulsifying -- In this group, the following term is used with the meaning indicated: "gases" covers also vapours.
[B01F 23/02] Maintaining the aggregation state of the mixed materials -- B01F 23/02 relates to techniques for preserving the aggregation state of materials after they have been mixed, dispersed or emulsified, including measures to keep particles or droplets stable, prevent coalescence or sedimentation and maintain desired dispersion or emulsion characteristics during or after processing.
[B01F 23/021] Maintaining mixed ingredients in movement to prevent crystalisation of the ingredients after mixing -- The subclass B01F 23/021 deals with processes that maintain a mixed composition in motion after the mixing operation-such as dispersing or emulsifying-to inhibit crystallisation of the ingredients and preserve a uniform state. It is applied where continuous agitation is required to keep the product from solidifying after the initial blend.
[B01F 23/022] Preventing precipitation of solid ingredients during or after mixing by adding a solvent -- B01F 23/022 covers techniques for preventing the precipitation of solid components while mixing, by introducing a solvent either during or after the mixing step. The approach is used to keep solids in suspension or dissolved, improving stability of mixtures such as dispersions or emulsions.
[B01F 23/023] Preventing sedimentation, conglomeration or agglomeration of solid ingredients during or after mixing by maintaining mixed ingredients in movement -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus that keep solid particles dispersed while mixing by continuously moving the mixture, thereby avoiding settling, clumping or agglomeration of the solids during or after the mixing step. It includes techniques such as agitation, recirculation or controlled flow to maintain a homogeneous suspension.
[B01F 23/024] Maintaining mixed ingredients in movement to prevent separation of the ingredients after mixing -- This CPC subclass covers processes that keep a mixture in motion after it has been combined so that the components do not separate. It includes techniques such as continuous agitation, recirculation or transport that maintain dispersion during handling.
[B01F 23/04] Specific aggregation state of one or more of the phases to be mixed -- B01F 23/04 covers mixing operations in which the aggregation state - solid, liquid or gas - of one or more material phases to be combined is specified, such as dispersing a liquid in a solid matrix or forming an emulsion with a gaseous component. The code applies to processes that require particular phase states to achieve the intended mixing effect.
[B01F 23/041] Mixing ingredients in more than two different agglomeration states, phases -- The code B01F 23/041 covers methods for mixing components that are present in more than two different agglomeration states, such as solids, liquids and gases combined in a single operation. It includes techniques that simultaneously emulsify, disperse and dissolve to create composite mixtures where each phase retains its distinct structure.
[B01F 23/042] Mixing cryogenic aerosols, i.e. mixtures of gas with solid particles in cryogenic condition, with other ingredients -- B01F 23/042 covers processes for mixing cryogenic aerosols - mixtures of gas and solid particles kept at cryogenic temperatures - with additional ingredients. The mixing may involve dispersing, emulsifying or otherwise combining the cold aerosol with liquids, powders or other substances to produce a homogeneous product. It falls under the broader categories of separating, mixing and performing operations.
[B01F 23/043] Mixing fluids or with fluids in a supercritical state, in supercritical conditions or variable density fluids -- This subclass covers processes where fluids are mixed while in a supercritical state or under supercritical conditions, including variable-density fluids. It addresses techniques for dispersing, dissolving or emulsifying components when pressures and temperatures exceed the critical point, where the fluid exhibits both liquid-like and gas-like properties.
[B01F 23/06] Mixing phases by adding a very small quantity of one of the phases or microdosing -- B01F 23/06 covers mixing two phases by adding only a trace amount of one phase to the other, a technique known as microdosing that promotes dispersing or emulsifying without bulk addition. It is used when a very small quantity of a liquid, solid or gas can initiate the formation of a stable mixture, reducing energy input and improving control over phase distribution.
[B01F 23/061] Adding a small quantity or concentration of an additional phase in a main phase, e.g. acting as a carrier phase -- The subclass covers mixing processes where a minor secondary phase is introduced into a primary phase to act as a carrier or to aid dispersion, such as adding a small amount of liquid to a solid slurry or a surfactant to an emulsion.
[B01F 23/062] Mixing ingredients in very small quantity, adding microingredients or microconcentration, e.g. adding vitamins, minerals, proteins, enzymes, hormones, antibiotics or worm medicines -- B01F 23/062 covers processes for mixing very small quantities of microingredients or microconcentrations into a material, such as adding vitamins, minerals, proteins, enzymes, hormones, antibiotics or worm medicines. The classification falls under mixing according to the phases to be mixed, specifically dispersing or emulsifying operations.
[B01F 23/09] Mixing systems, i.e. flow charts or diagrams for components having more than two different of undetermined agglomeration states, e.g. supercritical states -- B01F 23/09 covers mixing systems illustrated by flow charts or diagrams that involve more than two components with variable agglomeration states, including supercritical conditions, and are used for processes such as dispersing, emulsifying or other phase-based mixing.
[B01F 23/10] Mixing gases with gases -- B01F 23/10 covers processes that combine one gas with another, including techniques for dispersing, emulsifying or otherwise homogenising gaseous streams. It is applied to equipment and procedures whose primary purpose is to achieve a uniform gas mixture, such as blending, compressing, or agitation methods used in chemical or industrial gas production.
[B01F 23/12] with vaporisation of a liquid  disinfection, sterilisation or deodorisation of air  A61L9/00 -- The B01F 23/12 subclass covers processes that mix a liquid with a gas phase in order to vaporise the liquid while performing disinfection, sterilisation, or deodorisation of air. It is situated under mixing operations defined by the phases to be mixed, specifically for air-treatment applications such as sanitising or odor removal.
[B01F 23/14] with moving mixing elements, e.g. with liquid seal -- The subclass B01F 23/14 covers mixing apparatus that employs moving mixing elements, such as rotors, paddles or agitators, which operate while being sealed by a surrounding liquid to prevent material loss. These devices are used to disperse, emulsify or otherwise combine substances in processes where a liquid barrier is required.
[B01F 23/19] Mixing systems, i.e. flow charts or diagrams; Arrangements, e.g. comprising controlling means -- B01F 23/19 covers mixing systems shown as flow charts or diagrams and the related arrangements that incorporate controlling means for managing mixing operations, such as phase-specific dispersing or emulsifying processes.
[B01F 23/191] characterised by the construction of the controlling means -- This subclass covers mixers where the control unit - such as a speed regulator, torque limiter or feedback system - is defined by a specific structural arrangement that governs the mixing of separate phases, including dispersion and emulsification. It includes devices in which the controlling means are integral to the mixing apparatus to achieve precise phase interaction.
[B01F 23/20] Mixing gases with liquids -- The CPC subclass B01F 23/20 covers processes that blend a gas phase with a liquid, such as aeration, dissolution, or gas-liquid emulsification. It includes apparatus and methods that introduce and disperse gases into liquids using impellers, spargers, or similar devices to achieve uniform mixing.
[B01F 23/21] by introducing liquids into gaseous media -- This CPC subclass concerns mixing operations in which a liquid is introduced into a gaseous medium, such as atomising, spraying or forming aerosols to achieve dispersion, dissolution or emulsification of the liquid within the gas.
[B01F 23/211] Methods -- B01F 23/211 concerns methods for mixing substances according to the phases to be mixed, such as dispersing or emulsifying, within separations operations. The classification includes any technique-mechanical, chemical, ultrasonic or otherwise-that creates a stable mixture of immiscible or partially miscible phases for subsequent processing.
[B01F 23/213] by spraying or atomising of the liquids -- This subclass covers mixing processes where liquids are combined by spraying or atomising, typically to achieve dispersion, emulsification or dissolution. It is part of the mixing-according-to-phases group within the separating operations of the CPC.
[B01F 23/2131] using rotating elements, e.g. rolls or brushes -- This CPC subclass covers mixing processes that employ rotating components such as rolls or brushes to disperse, emulsify, or dissolve materials, typically within a mixing device where different phases are combined. It falls under mixing according to the phases to be mixed, within separating operations of performing operations.
[B01F 23/21311] for spraying the liquid radially by centrifugal force -- The code B01F 23/21311 covers apparatus or processes that spray a liquid outward in a radial direction by means of centrifugal force, typically to achieve dispersion, emulsification or other mixing actions. It falls within the CPC subclass for mixing according to the phases to be mixed, under separating operations within the performing operations section.
[B01F 23/21312] with additional rotating elements mounted on the same axis, e.g. fans, for moving the gas -- A subclass for mixing processes that employ extra rotating elements mounted on the same axis-such as fans-to move gas and assist dispersion or emulsification. It belongs to the mixing-by-phase category within the broader separating and performing-operations section.
[B01F 23/2132] using nozzles -- B01F 23/2132 covers mixing operations in which nozzles are employed to introduce, atomize or spray fluids, creating shear and turbulence that disperse, emulsify or dissolve separate phases. It relates to processes that use nozzle geometry or pressure-driven jets to achieve intimate mixing of liquids, gases or solids, typically within reactors, spray-dryers or similar equipment. This falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and mixing according to the phases to be mixed.
[B01F 23/21321] High pressure atomization, i.e. the liquid is atomized and sprayed by a jet at high pressure -- This subclass deals with high-pressure atomization, where a liquid is forced through a nozzle at high pressure to produce a fine spray that mixes, disperses or emulsifies substances. It falls under performing operations - separating - mixing (phases) - mixing according to the phases to be mixed.
[B01F 23/21322] Internal mixer atomization, i.e. liquid and gas are mixed and atomized in a jet nozzle before spraying -- The entry B01F 23/21322 covers processes in which a liquid and a gas are combined and atomised within a jet nozzle to form a spray, facilitating rapid mixing, dispersing or emulsifying of the phases. It relates to internal-mixer atomisation techniques used for producing fine droplets while simultaneously mixing the components.
[B01F 23/2133] using electric, sonic or ultrasonic energy -- The subclass B01F 23/2133 covers mixing operations in which electric, sonic or ultrasonic energy is employed to disperse, dissolve, emulsify or otherwise combine separate phases. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating and mixing according to the phases to be mixed.
[B01F 23/214] using a gas-liquid mixing column or tower -- B01F 23/214 covers processes that employ a gas-liquid mixing column or tower to achieve mixing of immiscible phases, such as dispersing or emulsifying liquids with a gas stream. The method uses the column's contacting stages to promote mass transfer and uniform distribution of one phase within the other.
[B01F 23/215] by forcing the gas through absorbent pads containing the liquid -- This subclass covers processes in which a gas stream is forced through absorbent pads that contain a liquid, causing the gas and liquid phases to mix, dissolve, emulsify or disperse. The technique uses a porous medium to retain the liquid while the gas passes, facilitating efficient mass-transfer and phase interaction.
[B01F 23/216] by using liquefied or cryogenic gases as liquid component -- This subclass covers mixing processes where a liquid component is supplied as a liquefied or cryogenic gas. It includes operations such as dissolving, emulsifying, or dispersing where the gas is introduced in liquid form to achieve the mixing.
[B01F 23/23] by introducing gases into liquid media, e.g. for producing aerated liquids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mixing by introducing the gas into the liquid media using a particular technique, e.g. to obtain a homogenised mixture including appropriate bubble sizes or to produce aerated fluids.
[B01F 23/231] by bubbling -- B01F 23/231 classifies mixing processes that use bubbling, where gas bubbles are introduced into a liquid or other phase to disperse, dissolve, or emulsify components. The method includes aeration, sparging and bubble-column techniques that achieve mixing by the motion of the bubbles.
[B01F 23/23105] Arrangement or manipulation of the gas bubbling devices -- B01F 23/23105 covers the configuration and control of devices that introduce gas bubbles into a liquid or solid medium to achieve mixing, dissolution, dispersion or emulsification. It includes the geometric arrangement, positioning, movement or adjustment of spargers, diffusers, nozzles and related components to influence bubble distribution and mixing efficiency. This subclass focuses on how the gas-bubbling apparatus is designed and manipulated to attain the desired phase-mixing outcome.
[B01F 23/2311] Mounting the bubbling devices or the diffusers -- This subclass covers the installation of bubbling devices or diffusers that introduce gas into a medium during mixing operations aimed at dispersing, dissolving, emulsifying or otherwise homogenising phases. It details how such equipment is mounted to facilitate the creation of bubbles or aeration within the mixture, supporting processes such as emulsification or dispersion.
[B01F 23/23112] comprising the use of flow guiding elements adjacent or above the gas stream -- The subclass B01F 23/23112 addresses mixing operations that employ flow-guiding elements placed adjacent to or above a gas stream to improve dispersion, emulsification or dissolution of separate phases. It belongs to the hierarchy of performing operations, separating and mixing according to the phases to be mixed.
[B01F 23/231121] the flow guiding elements being baffles, tubes or walls -- B01F 23/231121 covers mixing operations in which the fluid flow is directed by internal elements such as baffles, tubes or walls to promote dispersion or emulsification of phases. It falls under the broader category of separating processes that involve mixing according to the phases to be mixed.
[B01F 23/231122] the flow guiding elements being dome-shaped elements, i.e. for trapping air, e.g. cap-, umbrella- or inversed cone-shaped -- This classification entry pertains to mixing processes where the equipment includes specific flow guiding elements that are dome-shaped, such as caps, umbrellas, or inverted cones, designed primarily to trap air during the mixing of different phases.
[B01F 23/23113] characterised by the disposition of the bubbling elements in particular configurations, patterns or arrays -- The subclass B01F 23/23113 covers mixing apparatus in which the bubbling or gas-introducing elements are positioned in specific configurations, patterns or arrays to influence the dispersing or emulsifying action. These arranged elements are intended to improve mass transfer and achieve a uniform mixture during the process.
[B01F 23/23114] characterised by the way in which the different elements of the bubbling installation are mounted -- This subclass concerns bubbling installations in which the distinguishing feature is the way the individual components-such as diffusers, reactors, gas distributors and supporting structures-are mounted or arranged. The focus is on how this mounting influences mixing, separating and phase-dispersing operations like dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing within the process.
[B01F 23/231141] Mounting auxiliary devices, e.g. pumps or compressors in a particular place on the bubbling installation, e.g. under water -- The CPC code B01F 23/231141 relates to the placement of auxiliary equipment such as pumps or compressors at designated positions on bubbling installations, for example beneath the liquid surface. It falls under mixing operations where phases are dispersed or emulsified, and addresses how the devices are mounted to improve gas-liquid contact and separation efficiency.
[B01F 23/231142] Mounting the gas transporting elements, i.e. connections between conduits -- The CPC subclass B01F 23/231142 covers the mounting of gas-transporting components, such as pipes, tubes or channels, within mixing equipment, including fittings, clamps, flanges and other joining arrangements that connect conduits to enable gas flow during mixing operations. It addresses the mechanical securing of these gas lines for processes like dispersing, emulsifying or dissolving.
[B01F 23/231143] Mounting the bubbling elements or diffusors, e.g. on conduits, using connecting elements; Connections therefor -- This CPC subclass concerns the way bubbling elements or diffusers are attached to conduits in mixing equipment, including the use of brackets, clamps or other connecting parts to secure them. It applies to processes where gases are introduced into liquids for dispersing, emulsifying or dissolving.
[B01F 23/23115] characterised by the way in which the bubbling devices are mounted within the receptacle -- The entry B01F 23/23115 covers processes in which the manner of mounting bubbling devices-such as spargers, nozzles or diffusers-inside a receptacle is the defining feature. It relates to mixing operations that rely on gas-induced agitation for dispersing, emulsifying or separating phases, and specifies how the mounting influences the effectiveness of the bubbling and the resulting mixture.
[B01F 23/231151] the bubbling devices being fixed or anchored in the bottom -- This subclass covers mixing equipment that uses gas bubbles introduced from devices fixed or anchored to the bottom of the vessel to disperse or emulsify liquids. The bottom-mounted bubbling units create upward flow that promotes phase contact and homogenization.
[B01F 23/231152] the bubbling devices being supported, e.g. on cables or laying on the bottom -- This CPC subclass covers mixing equipment in which bubble-generating devices are mounted on supports such as cables or positioned on the bottom of a vessel, used for processes like dispersing, emulsifying or dissolving.
[B01F 23/231153] the bubbling devices being suspended on a supporting construction, i.e. not on a floating construction -- This subclass pertains to mixing or emulsifying equipment where the gas-producing bubbling devices are mounted on a rigid supporting construction rather than being free-floating. The fixed arrangement enables controlled dispersion of bubbles within a liquid phase to improve dissolution, emulsification or dispersion. It is used in processes that require stable, positioned gas injection for efficient phase mixing.
[B01F 23/231154] the bubbling devices being provided with ballast to keep them floating under the surface, i.e. when the bubbling devices are lighter than the liquid -- This CPC subclass covers bubbling devices equipped with ballast that allow them to remain submerged in a liquid when the device itself is lighter than the liquid. Such devices are used for mixing processes such as dispersing, emulsifying or dissolving by introducing gas bubbles while being held at depth.
[B01F 23/231155] the bubbling devices floating and having a pendulum movement, going to and from or moving in alternating directions -- This CPC subclass covers mixing apparatus that use floating bubbling elements that swing like a pendulum, moving back and forth or in alternating directions to promote dispersion or emulsification of phases. The pendular motion creates agitation in the liquid, enhancing the intermixing of immiscible components and is used in processes such as dissolving, dispersing or emulsifying where gentle continuous mixing is required.
[B01F 23/231156] the bubbling devices floating and having a rotating movement around a central vertical axis -- This CPC subclass covers apparatus where bubbles are generated by devices that float in the liquid and rotate around a central-vertical axis, providing mixing, dispersion or emulsification of immiscible phases. The rotating bubbling action creates a turbulent flow that enhances mass transfer.
[B01F 23/23116] Means for manipulating the bubbling constructions or elements, e.g. for raising or lowering them -- The entry covers devices or components that adjust the position or movement of bubbles or other gas-filled structures during mixing, dispersing, dissolving or emulsifying operations, such as mechanisms that raise or lower the bubbles to control the dispersion or phase interaction.
[B01F 23/2312] Diffusers -- This subclass covers diffusers that introduce fluids or gases into a medium to promote mixing, dispersion or emulsification, typically by creating fine bubbles or jets that enhance contact between phases. Such devices are used in operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing within the broader category of separating and performing operations.
[B01F 23/23121] having injection means, e.g. nozzles with circumferential outlet -- The B01F 23/23121 subclass covers mixing operations that employ injection means, such as nozzles with a circumferential outlet, to introduce one material into another for dispersing, emulsifying or otherwise blending phases. It focuses on the mechanical design of the injector used to achieve uniform mixing.
[B01F 23/23122] having elements opening under air pressure, e.g. valves -- This CPC subclass deals with mixing processes that employ elements which open under air pressure, such as pneumatic valves that admit gases or liquids into a mixture. It covers apparatus and methods using air-pressured openings to achieve dispersion, emulsification, or dissolution within the mixing operation.
[B01F 23/23123] consisting of rigid porous or perforated material -- This subclass covers mixing operations where the mixing medium is a rigid porous or perforated structure, such as screens, meshes, or sintered plates that promote dispersion or emulsification of different phases. The device relies on fluid flow through the openings to achieve intimate contact between components. It is used in processes that separate and blend liquids or solids without moving parts.
[B01F 23/231231] the outlets being in the form of perforations -- The CPC subclass B01F 23/231231 belongs to the mixing group that focuses on the way phases are combined, such as for dispersion or emulsification, and specifies devices whose outlet is formed by perforations. It covers mixers that discharge material through a perforated plate or similar arrangement, enabling controlled release of the mixed product. This detail refines the broader category of mixing operations by describing the shape of the outlet.
[B01F 23/231232] in the form of slits or cut-out openings -- B01F 23/231232 relates to mixing operations-such as dispersing, dissolving, emulsifying or other phase-to-phase interactions-where the mixing is achieved by passing materials through slits or cut-out openings that generate the necessary shear and contact.
[B01F 23/231233] comprising foam-like gas outlets -- This subclass covers mixing apparatus that introduce gas through foam-like outlets to create fine bubbles that aid dispersion or emulsification of phases. The design uses a porous or foam structure to release gas into the material being mixed, enhancing the uniformity of the resulting mixture.
[B01F 23/23124] consisting of flexible porous or perforated material, e.g. fabric -- This subclass relates to mixing devices that employ a flexible porous or perforated material, such as a fabric sheet, to facilitate dispersion or emulsification of separate phases. The porous structure permits fluid flow while delivering shear forces that promote effective mixing of liquids, suspensions or immiscible components.
[B01F 23/231241] the outlets being in the form of perforations -- B01F 23/231241 relates to mixers in which the discharge is achieved through perforated outlets such as plates, screens or nozzles, allowing the mixed phases to exit while continuing to be dispersed or emulsified. The design is used to enhance uniform distribution and reduce dead zones.
[B01F 23/231242] in the form of slits or cut-out openings -- The CPC entry B01F 23/231242 covers mixing operations performed with devices that incorporate slits or cut-out openings to facilitate dispersion or emulsification of separate phases. It falls under the hierarchy of Performing Operations → Separating → Mixing, specifically mixing according to the phases to be mixed. This subclass describes apparatus designs that use narrow openings to enhance the intimate contact of liquids or solids during the mixing process.
[B01F 23/231243] comprising foam-like gas outlets -- The code B01F 23/231243 designates mixing or dispersing processes that incorporate foam-like gas outlets to introduce gas into the material, aiding the formation of emulsions or dispersions. It falls within the CPC class for mixing according to the phases to be mixed, particularly where gas bubbles improve mixing efficiency.
[B01F 23/231244] Dissolving, hollow fiber membranes -- B01F 23/231244 is a CPC entry under Performing Operations → Separating → Mixing (including dispersing and emulsifying) that specifically addresses dissolving processes using hollow-fiber membranes. It covers methods where hollow-fiber membrane modules facilitate dissolution of substances as part of mixing, dispersing or emulsifying operations.
[B01F 23/231245] Fabric in the form of woven, knitted, braided, non-woven or flocculated fibers or filaments -- The CPC entry B01F 23/231245 is situated in the mixing-operations subclass for separating and covers fabrics made of woven, knitted, braided, non-woven or flocculated fibers or filaments that are employed in processes such as mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 23/23125] characterised by the way in which they are assembled or mounted; Fabricating the parts of the diffusers -- This CPC subgroup concerns mixing equipment in which the diffuser components are defined by their assembly or mounting method, covering the fabrication of the diffuser parts themselves. It falls under mixing operations aimed at dispersing or emulsifying substances within the broader separating processes hierarchy.
[B01F 23/23126] characterised by the shape of the diffuser element -- The CPC subclass B01F 23/23126 relates to mixing operations-such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing-where the distinguishing factor is the shape of the diffuser element used to blend the phases. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, separating, mixing according to the phases to be mixed, and specifically addresses diffuser-element geometry.
[B01F 23/231261] having a box- or block-shape, being in the form of aeration stones -- The code refers to mixing equipment shaped as a box or block that functions as an aeration stone, being introduced into a liquid to inject gas and promote dispersion or emulsification of phases.
[B01F 23/231262] having disc shape -- The subclass B01F 23/231262 pertains to mixing operations in which the mixing elements are disc-shaped. It is used for processes such as dispersing, emulsifying or dissolving where a disc-type rotor or blade effects the blend of two or more phases. The classification falls under mixing according to the phases to be mixed within the broader separating and performing operations categories.
[B01F 23/231263] having dome-, cap- or inversed cone-shape -- This subclass covers mixing operations in which the material to be dispersed or emulsified is introduced into a mixing zone shaped like a dome, a cap, or an inverted cone, using the geometry to promote phase contact and efficient dispersion. The shape facilitates continuous flow and controlled residence time, improving the effectiveness of dissolving, emulsifying, or dispersing processes.
[B01F 23/231264] being in the form of plates, flat beams, flat membranes or films -- This subclass covers mixing operations such as dispersing or emulsifying applied to substances presented as plates, flat beams, membranes or films. It relates to processes where the material's geometry is essentially flat, requiring specific handling to achieve uniform phase distribution. The classification is used for patents describing mixing of thin or planar forms.
[B01F 23/231265] being tubes, tubular elements, cylindrical elements or set of tubes -- B01F 23/231265 covers processes in which tubes, tubular or cylindrical elements, or sets of tubes are employed as the mixing medium or vessel during operations such as separating, mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It relates specifically to the handling of these tubular components while mixing different phases.
[B01F 23/231266] being in the form of rings or annular elements -- The CPC code B01F 23/231266 refers to mixing operations in which the material is handled as rings or annular elements, a subtype of mixing that involves dispersing or emulsifying phases. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating and mixing, and specifies the geometry of the material being processed.
[B01F 23/231267] being axially stacked discs, rings or plates -- B01F 23/231267 covers mixing operations where the material consists of axially stacked flat components such as discs, rings or plates. The class addresses processes that disperse or emulsify these stacked elements, typically by rotating or otherwise moving them relative to each other to achieve a uniform mixture.
[B01F 23/231268] being helically wound, coiled and joined bands or wires -- B01F 23/231268 classifies mixing operations in which the materials are combined by helically winding, coiling and joining bands or wires, a technique used to achieve dispersion or emulsification of different phases. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of Performing Operations - Separating - Mixing, e.g. dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 23/231269] being spirally wound, coiled tubes or spirally wound, coiled and joined bands or wires -- The subclass covers mixing operations that employ spirally wound, coiled tubes, bands or wires to disperse or emulsify separate phases. By arranging the material in a helical or coiled configuration, the device creates intensive shear and contact, enhancing dissolution, emulsification or dispersion within a compact geometry. This approach is typical in heat-exchange or reactor designs where efficient phase mixing is required.
[B01F 23/23127] Screens, nets, grades or grids -- This subclass, B01F 23/23127, covers screens, nets, grades or grids used in mixing operations to separate or disperse phases, such as in emulsifying or dispersing processes. It includes perforated plates, meshes, or graded openings that control particle size or phase distribution while mixing.
[B01F 23/23128] having specific properties or elements attached thereto -- This CPC subclass covers mixing operations in which the material being dispersed or emulsified carries defined functional groups, particles or other attached elements that confer particular properties to the final mixture. It applies to processes that deliberately introduce these specific features during the phase-mixing step, such as attaching surfactants, catalysts or magnetic particles to droplets or particles before or during emulsification.
[B01F 23/231281] made of or comprising a biocide -- This CPC subclass B01F 23/231281 covers processes in which a biocidal substance is incorporated during mixing operations that involve phase dispersion or emulsification. It pertains to the preparation of mixtures where the biocide is a component of the product, typically for antimicrobial or preservative purposes. The subclass falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and mixing.
[B01F 23/231282] made of or comprising a material able to store a gas which is released when water flows through it -- The CPC code B01F 23/231282 is nested under Performing Operations - Separating - Mixing, e.g. dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing, specifically mixing according to the phases to be mixed such as dispersing or emulsifying. It concerns devices or compositions made of a material that can store a gas and release it when water flows through, enabling controlled aeration or foam generation.
[B01F 23/231283] having elements to protect the parts of the diffusers, e.g. from clogging when not in use -- The subclass addresses mixing apparatus equipped with protective elements for diffuser parts, such as screens, caps or filters that prevent clogging when the equipment is not in use. These features are intended to keep the diffuser openings clear and maintain performance during idle periods.
[B01F 23/2319] Methods of introducing gases into liquid media -- The CPC subclass B01F 23/2319 addresses methods for introducing gases into liquid media, typically used in processes that involve mixing phases such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It is classified under performing operations, separating, mixing, specifically for gas-liquid incorporation techniques.
[B01F 23/232] using flow-mixing means for introducing the gases, e.g. baffles -- This CPC subclass concerns mixing operations in which gases are introduced into a liquid or slurry by flow-mixing devices such as baffles, creating turbulence that disperses or emulsifies the phases. It falls under mixing according to the phases to be mixed, specifically using flow-mixing means to incorporate gases.
[B01F 23/2321] by moving liquid and gas in counter current -- B01F 23/2321 covers mixing operations in which a liquid and a gaseous phase are moved in opposite directions to achieve dispersion, emulsification or dissolution. The counter-current flow creates shear and interfacial contact that enhances mixing efficiency, and it is classified under separating-type operations for phase-to-phase mixing.
[B01F 23/23211] the liquid flowing in a thin film to absorb the gas -- This subclass deals with mixing operations in which a liquid is spread as a thin flowing film that contacts a gas phase to absorb it, such as in gas-scrubbing or liquid-film reactors. The method relies on intimate phase contact to dissolve, emulsify or disperse the gas in the liquid.
[B01F 23/232111] the liquid film or layer flowing over a horizontal or inclined surface, e.g. perforated -- This subclass concerns mixing processes that use a liquid film or layer moving across a horizontal or inclined surface, often over a perforated plate or screen, to achieve dispersion, dissolution, or emulsification. The flowing film creates shear and interfacial contact that breaks up particles or droplets, enabling uniform mixing of the phases involved.
[B01F 23/232112] the liquid film or layer flowing over a vertical surface, e.g. a mesh -- The code B01F 23/232112 covers mixing operations in which a liquid film or layer moves down a vertical surface, such as a mesh, to achieve dispersion, emulsification or dissolution. It is used for processes that rely on gravity-driven film flow to promote intimate contact between phases, often in coating, washing or thin-film reactors.
[B01F 23/2322] using columns, e.g. multi-staged columns -- B01F 23/2322 concerns mixing operations carried out in columnar apparatus, such as multi-staged columns, where distinct phases are combined to achieve dispersion, emulsification or dissolution. The classification applies to processes that separate and mix components by passing them through sequential column sections, enabling controlled phase interaction and product formation.
[B01F 23/2323] by circulating the flow in guiding constructions or conduits -- B01F 23/2323 covers mixing operations-such as dispersing, dissolving or emulsifying-performed by moving the material through guiding constructions or conduits so that the flow circulates and creates the desired mixture. This method falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and mixing according to the phases to be combined.
[B01F 23/23231] being at least partially immersed in the liquid, e.g. in a closed circuit -- B01F 23/23231 covers mixing operations in which the material to be mixed is at least partially immersed in a liquid, such as in a closed-circuit system, and pertains to processes that disperse, dissolve or emulsify substances by contacting them with the liquid phase. This falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and mixing according to the phases to be mixed.
[B01F 23/232311] the conduits being vertical draft pipes with a lower intake end and an upper exit end -- This CPC subclass B01F 23/232311 covers mixing operations that employ vertical draft pipes as conduits, where the pipe has a lower intake end and an upper exit end to draw fluid upward, facilitating phase dispersion, emulsification or dissolution. It falls under mixing according to the phases to be mixed within the broader separating and performing operations categories.
[B01F 23/232312] the guiding constructions being baffles for guiding the flow up-and-down or from left-to-right -- The subclass B01F 23/232312 defines devices that use baffles as guiding constructions to steer the flow of liquids up-and-down or side-to-side during mixing operations such as dispersing or emulsifying, improving phase interaction. It belongs to the CPC hierarchy under Performing Operations → Separating → Mixing (e.g., dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing) → Mixing according to the phases to be mixed.
[B01F 23/2326] adding the flowing main component by suction means, e.g. using an ejector -- The code B01F 23/2326 covers a mixing operation in which the main fluid is introduced into the system by suction, typically using an ejector or similar device, rather than by direct pump feed. This method is employed in processes that require dispersing, emulsifying or otherwise mixing phases and is classified under separating, mixing and dissolving operations.
[B01F 23/233] using driven stirrers with completely immersed stirring elements -- The class covers mixing operations where the material is blended by mechanically driven stirrers whose stirring elements are completely immersed in the medium. It applies to processes such as dissolution, dispersion or emulsification that rely on fully-immersed agitators.
[B01F 23/2331] characterised by the introduction of the gas along the axis of the stirrer or along the stirrer elements -- The subclass covers mixing processes where gas is fed coaxially with the stirring shaft or directly onto the stirrer blades, facilitating dispersion or emulsification in liquid media. It applies to devices or methods that introduce gas along the axis of the stirrer or its elements to enhance mass transfer.
[B01F 23/23311] through a hollow stirrer axis -- B01F 23/23311 covers mixing processes in which the material passes through a hollow stirrer axis, allowing the rotor's interior to act as a conduit for the substance while it is being agitated. This method is employed for dispersing, emulsifying or otherwise blending phases as the stream moves through the hollow shaft.
[B01F 23/23312] through a conduit surrounding the stirrer axis -- The CPC code B01F 23/23312 covers mixing apparatus in which the material to be mixed is conveyed through a pipe that encloses the stirrer shaft. The surrounding conduit creates a shearing flow that aids dispersing, emulsifying or dissolving by simultaneous stirring and conduit-induced turbulence.
[B01F 23/23313] through a separate conduit substantially parallel with the stirrer axis -- This subclass covers mixing processes in which a separate conduit delivers material substantially parallel to the stirrer axis, allowing feed to be introduced alongside the rotating impeller during dispersing or emulsifying operations.
[B01F 23/23314] through a hollow stirrer element -- The CPC subclass B01F 23/23314 covers mixing operations in which the material is forced through a hollow stirrer element, creating a flow that promotes intimate contact between phases. This technique is used for dispersing or emulsifying substances by pulling the mixture through the internal cavity of the agitator, enhancing shear and mixing efficiency.
[B01F 23/23315] through a hollow guide surrounding the stirrer element -- This CPC subclass describes mixing operations in which the stirrer element is encircled by a hollow guide that directs the material flow, allowing phases to be dispersed, emulsified or otherwise combined as they pass through the guide. The configuration improves contact between components and can be used for dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing processes.
[B01F 23/23316] through a separate hollow guide substantially parallel with the stirrer element -- The entry B01F 23/23316 covers a mixing technique where a hollow guide runs substantially parallel to the stirrer element, directing fluid flow to improve dispersion or emulsification. This arrangement is used in processes that separate components by mixing, such as dissolving or emulsifying, by creating a controlled shear field.
[B01F 23/2332] the stirrer rotating about a horizontal axis; Stirrers therefor -- This CPC subclass B01F 23/2332 covers stirrers whose rotating element turns about a horizontal axis, together with related stirrer designs. It falls under mixing operations such as dispersing or emulsifying, where the horizontal rotation aids fluid movement.
[B01F 23/2333] Single stirrer-drive aerating units, e.g. with the stirrer-head pivoting around an horizontal axis -- B01F 23/2333 covers single-stirrer-drive aerating units in which the stirrer head pivots around a horizontal axis to introduce gas while mixing, suited for processes such as dispersing, emulsifying or dissolving. The design combines agitation and aeration in a compact, mechanically simple arrangement for liquid-phase operations.
[B01F 23/2334] provided with stationary guiding means surrounding at least partially the stirrer -- In the CPC hierarchy for performing operations, separating, and mixing, class B01F 23/2334 covers mixing apparatus that incorporates stationary guiding means that surround at least part of the stirrer. The guiding structures channel the material flow around the agitator, enhancing dispersion, emulsification or dissolution by controlling the mixing zones. This subclass refines broader mixing categories by specifying the presence of partially enclosing, fixed guides around the stirring element.
[B01F 23/23341] with tubes surrounding the stirrer -- This subclass pertains to mixing apparatuses in which the stirring element is surrounded by one or more tubes that convey material past the agitator, allowing simultaneous conveyance and intimate contact of different phases for dispersion or emulsification. It covers configurations where the tube(s) form a sheath around the stirrer to improve mixing efficiency in processes such as dissolving or dispersing solids into liquids.
[B01F 23/23342] the stirrer being of the centrifugal type, e.g. with a surrounding stator -- B01F 23/23342 refers to centrifugal stirrers that incorporate a surrounding stator, designed for mixing operations such as dispersing or emulsifying by generating a centrifugal flow that blends different phases. The arrangement promotes efficient phase interaction while minimizing separation, making it suitable for processes that require fine dispersion of liquids or solids.
[B01F 23/2335] characterised by the direction of introduction of the gas relative to the stirrer -- The subclass B01F 23/2335 covers mixing operations in which a gas is introduced into the liquid phase and the key distinguishing feature is the direction of the gas inlet relative to the stirrer, such as feeding gas from above, below, or opposite the agitator to achieve dispersion or emulsification.
[B01F 23/23351] the gas moving along the axis of rotation -- The entry B01F 23/23351 covers mixing operations in which a gas stream is introduced and moves parallel to the axis of a rotating device, such as axial-flow mixers or reactors, to aid dispersion, emulsification or dissolution of immiscible phases. It focuses on the flow of the gas along the rotor's axis rather than radially, influencing the mixing efficiency and product uniformity.
[B01F 23/23352] the gas moving perpendicular to the axis of rotation -- This subclass covers mixing apparatus in which the gas stream is introduced transverse to the rotor axis, creating turbulence that promotes dispersion or emulsification of immiscible phases. It is used in processes where gas aids mixing, such as aerated reactors or high-shear mixers.
[B01F 23/23353] the gas being sucked towards the rotating stirrer -- This subclass covers processes where a rotating agitator creates a low-pressure zone that draws gas into the liquid, enhancing dispersion or emulsification. It applies to mixers in which the gas is pulled toward the stirrer rather than injected externally.
[B01F 23/23354] the gas being driven away from the rotating stirrer -- The entry B01F 23/23354 covers mixing processes in which a rotating stirrer drives gas out of the liquid phase, typically by creating a vortex that releases entrained air or other gases during dispersing, emulsifying or dissolving operations. This technique improves contact between phases and enhances overall mixing efficiency.
[B01F 23/2336] characterised by the location of the place of introduction of the gas relative to the stirrer -- The B01F 23/2336 entry covers mixing processes in which a gas is introduced at a specific position relative to the stirrer, such as upstream, downstream, or adjacent to the impeller. This classification distinguishes the effect of gas location on dispersing, emulsifying or dissolving operations during the mixing of liquids.
[B01F 23/23361] the gas being introduced in a guide tube surrounding at least partially the axis of the stirrer -- This CPC subclass covers mixing processes in which a gas is fed through a guide tube that partially encloses the stirrer shaft, allowing the gas to be introduced along the stirrer's axis. The arrangement is used to enhance dispersion, emulsification, or dissolution during phase mixing.
[B01F 23/23362] the gas being introduced under the stirrer -- In CPC subclass B01F 23/23362, which lies within mixing according to the phases to be mixed, the entry covers processes where a gas is injected beneath the stirrer to assist dissolution, dispersion or emulsification of materials. Introducing gas under the agitator creates bubbles or turbulence that improves mixing efficiency and phase interaction.
[B01F 23/23363] the gas being introduced above the stirrer -- The CPC entry B01F 23/23363 relates to mixing operations where a gas stream is supplied above the stirrer to enhance dispersion, emulsification or dissolution of liquid phases. It specifies the introduction of the gas into the mixing zone from a position over the agitator, influencing mass transfer and bubble distribution.
[B01F 23/23364] the gas being introduced between the stirrer elements -- This subclass concerns mixing operations in which a gas is introduced into the material between the stirrer elements, typically to improve dispersion, emulsification or dissolution. It applies to devices that inject gas directly in the mixing zone to enhance mass-transfer efficiency.
[B01F 23/233641] at the stirrer axis -- The entry B01F 23/233641 covers mixing operations that are performed at the stirrer axis, meaning the dispersion, emulsification or other phase-mixing actions occur directly on or around the central shaft of the agitator. It pertains to equipment where the stirring element itself provides the mixing zone.
[B01F 23/233642] at the stirrer elements -- The CPC entry B01F 23/233642 pertains to mixing processes that follow the phase of the material, such as dispersing or emulsifying, and specifies that the mixing takes place directly at the stirrer elements. It is situated under Performing Operations → Separating → Mixing, e.g. dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 23/23365] the gas being introduced at the radial periphery of the stirrer -- This CPC entry covers mixing operations where gas is injected at the outer, radial edge of the stirrer, using the peripheral region to create bubbles that improve dispersion or emulsification of liquid phases. It is used to enhance mass transfer and homogenisation in processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing mixtures.
[B01F 23/23366] the gas being introduced in front of the stirrer -- The entry B01F 23/23366 covers mixing operations where a gas stream is fed in front of the stirrer to aid dispersing, emulsifying or dissolving phases. Introducing the gas upstream of the agitation element improves contact between gas and liquid, enhancing the formation of fine dispersions.
[B01F 23/23367] the gas being introduced behind the stirrer -- The CPC subclass B01F 23/23367 covers mixing operations in which a gas is fed behind the stirrer to aid dispersion, emulsification or dissolution of immiscible phases. It applies to processes that inject the gas directly at the impeller region to enhance agitation and inter-phase contact during separation or blending steps.
[B01F 23/234] Surface aerating -- B01F 23/234 relates to surface aerating, i.e. techniques that introduce a gas, usually air, at the surface of a liquid to promote mixing, dispersion or emulsification of phases. The classification covers devices such as aerators, diffusers or spargers that generate bubbles or jets at the liquid interface, used in processes like water treatment, fermentation or chemical synthesis.
[B01F 23/2341] by cascading, spraying or projecting a liquid into a gaseous atmosphere  B01F23/2342  takes precedence -- B01F 23/2341 in the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to mixing processes where a liquid is cascaded, sprayed, or projected into a gaseous atmosphere. This entry is specific to techniques involving the dispersion of liquids into gases, with B01F 23/2342 taking precedence for more detailed aspects.
[B01F 23/23411] by cascading the liquid -- B01F 23/23411 describes mixing by cascading a liquid through a series of successive stages, allowing drops or streams to intermix and promote dispersion or emulsification. The cascade creates shear and repeated contact between phases, achieving uniform mixing without direct mechanical agitation.
[B01F 23/23412] using liquid falling from orifices in a gaseous atmosphere, the orifices being exits from perforations, tubes or chimneys -- The code B01F 23/23412 covers processes in which a liquid is discharged from perforations, tubes or chimneys into a surrounding gas, using the falling stream to achieve mixing, emulsifying or dispersing of phases. It relates specifically to the use of orifice-generated liquid jets in a gaseous atmosphere for such operations.
[B01F 23/23413] using nozzles for projecting the liquid into the gas atmosphere -- This CPC subclass covers methods that employ nozzles to inject a liquid into a surrounding gas in order to mix, disperse or emulsify the phases. The jet created by the nozzle generates turbulence and droplets that promote rapid contact between liquid and gas, typical of atomisation, spray drying or aerosol generation. It falls under B01F 23/23 relating to mixing according to the phases to be mixed.
[B01F 23/2342] with stirrers near to the liquid surface, e.g. partially immersed, for spraying the liquid in the gas or for sucking gas into the liquid, e.g. using stirrers rotating around a horizontal axis or using centrifugal force -- This subclass covers mixing operations where stirrers are placed near the liquid surface, partially immersed, to spray the liquid into a gas stream or to draw gas into the liquid, using stirrers that rotate around a horizontal axis or rely on centrifugal force.
[B01F 23/23421] the stirrers rotating about a vertical axis -- B01F 23/23421 refers to mixing devices whose stirrers rotate about a vertical axis, used to disperse or emulsify substances during separation operations. It is situated under performing operations, separating, mixing according to the phases to be mixed.
[B01F 23/234211] Stirrers thereof -- The entry B01F 23/234211 covers stirrers designed for use in mixing operations that involve phase separation, such as dispersing or emulsifying mixtures. It includes the geometry, drive mechanisms and material characteristics of devices that impart agitation to liquids or suspensions during processing.
[B01F 23/235] for making foam -- The B01F 23/235 entry covers processes and apparatus for producing foam during mixing operations, where a gas phase is dispersed into a liquid or semi-solid to create a stable cellular structure. It includes methods for generating, controlling and stabilising foam in chemical, food, cosmetic or material-processing contexts, typically by mechanical agitation, aeration or the use of surfactants.
[B01F 23/2351] using driven stirrers -- The code B01F 23/2351 covers processes that employ powered stirrers to mix two or more phases, such as liquids, solids or gases, for purposes like dispersion, dissolution, emulsification or other phase-mixing operations. It includes any driven agitation device that creates the necessary shear or turbulence to achieve a uniform mixture.
[B01F 23/236] specially adapted for aerating or carbonating beverages -- B01F 23/236 covers mixing devices or processes specially adapted to aerate or carbonate beverages, such as systems that introduce gas into liquid streams to create foam or dissolve carbon dioxide during preparation or dispensing.
[B01F 23/2361] within small containers, e.g. within bottles -- This subclass covers mixing operations-such as dispersing or emulsifying-performed inside small containers like bottles. It specifies that the mixing follows the phases to be mixed, addressing processes where the material is combined within confined vessels. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of performing operations, separating, mixing.
[B01F 23/23611] Portable appliances comprising a gas cartridge -- The code B01F 23/23611 covers portable devices that contain a replaceable gas cartridge to supply fuel for mixing operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. These appliances are designed for on-site or mobile use where a self-contained gas source provides the energy needed to agitate or combine materials.
[B01F 23/2362] for aerating or carbonating within receptacles or tanks, e.g. distribution machines  B01F23/2361  takes precedence -- B01F 23/2362 classifies processes that aerate or carbonate a liquid inside a receptacle or tank, typically employed in distribution or dispensing machines. The method involves mixing the gas with the liquid to form a uniform carbonated product within the container.
[B01F 23/2363] Mixing systems, i.e. flow charts or diagrams; Arrangements, e.g. comprising controlling means -- The CPC entry B01F 23/2363 relates to mixing systems presented as flow charts or diagrams and to the arrangement of those systems, including any controlling means. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and mixing (such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing) where the mixing is defined by the phases to be combined. This code is used by the EPO and USPTO to classify documentation of mixing process designs.
[B01F 23/2364] using security elements, e.g. valves, for relieving overpressure -- This subclass concerns mixing operations, especially dispersing or emulsifying, that incorporate safety components such as pressure-relief valves to control or release excess pressure during the process. It falls within the broader CPC group for separating and mixing.
[B01F 23/2366] Parts; Accessories -- The B01F 23/2366 entry relates to parts and accessories that are employed in mixing operations where distinct phases are combined, such as dispersing or emulsifying, within equipment used for separating processes. It covers components like agitators, baffles, mixers and other elements that facilitate phase-to-phase interaction during the mixing step.
[B01F 23/2368] Mixing receptacles, e.g. tanks, vessels or reactors, being completely closed, e.g. hermetically closed -- B01F 23/2368 concerns mixing equipment such as tanks, vessels or reactors that are fully sealed, often hermetically, to prevent any material exchange with the environment. These closed receptacles are used for processes that involve dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing components while maintaining a controlled internal atmosphere. The classification falls under Performing Operations → Separating → Mixing, specifically for mixing according to the phases to be mixed.
[B01F 23/237] characterised by the physical or chemical properties of gases or vapours introduced in the liquid media -- This subgroup covers mixing operations in which gases or vapours are introduced into a liquid medium and the process is defined by the physical or chemical properties of the introduced phase, such as solubility, reactivity, pressure or volatility, which affect dissolution, dispersion or emulsification.
[B01F 23/2373] for obtaining fine bubbles, i.e. bubbles with a size below 100 µm -- This subclass covers mixing operations that intentionally create very small gas bubbles-typically under 100 µm-to enhance dispersion, dissolution, emulsification or other contacting processes. The techniques are used where fine-bubble generation improves the efficiency of mixing separate phases.
[B01F 23/2375] for obtaining bubbles with a size below 1 µm -- B01F 23/2375 covers processes that generate sub-micron bubbles, specifically bubbles smaller than 1 µm, during mixing operations such as dispersing or emulsifying. The classification is used for technologies that create fine gas-filled cavities to improve material properties, stabilize emulsions, or enhance chemical reactions.
[B01F 23/2376] characterised by the gas being introduced -- The subclass B01F 23/2376 covers mixing operations in which a gas is introduced into the material being processed to achieve dispersion, dissolution or emulsification, often by aeration, bubbling or gas-injection while the medium is stirred. It applies to devices and methods that combine gas delivery with mechanical mixing to form fine dispersions, emulsions or solutions.
[B01F 23/23761] Aerating, i.e. introducing oxygen containing gas in liquids -- The CPC entry B01F 23/23761 covers processes that aerate liquids by introducing an oxygen-containing gas. It is applied in mixing operations where the gas is dispersed to enhance dissolution, oxidation or reaction of the liquid phase, such as in dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing tasks.
[B01F 23/237611] Air -- The CPC subclass B01F 23/237611 concerns mixing operations in which air is employed as the medium to disperse, emulsify or dissolve separate phases. It includes equipment or methods that introduce air to achieve mixing of immiscible liquids or solid-liquid suspensions.
[B01F 23/237612] Oxygen -- The entry covers methods for introducing oxygen into a mixture as part of a mixing operation, including dispersing, emulsifying or dissolving oxygen in another phase. It relates to processes where oxygen is the substance being mixed, blended or distributed within liquids, gases or solids to achieve the desired composition.
[B01F 23/237613] Ozone -- The CPC entry B01F 23/237613 covers mixing processes in which ozone is introduced to disperse, dissolve or emulsify one or more phases, often to promote oxidation or create stable emulsions. It pertains to methods that employ ozone as a reactive mixing agent in liquid- or gas-phase systems.
[B01F 23/23762] Carbon dioxide -- The CPC entry B01F 23/23762 falls under Performing Operations → Separating → Mixing (e.g., dissolving, emulsifying, dispersing) and pertains specifically to mixing processes that employ carbon dioxide as the mixing medium, such as using CO₂ to disperse or emulsify phases in chemical or industrial applications.
[B01F 23/237621] in beverages -- The code B01F 23/237621 covers mixing operations applied to beverages, including dissolving, dispersing or emulsifying the constituent phases to achieve a uniform drink. It specifies techniques for blending liquids, solids or immiscible phases during beverage production.
[B01F 23/23763] Chlorine or chlorine containing gases -- B01F 23/23763 covers mixing operations performed on chlorine or chlorine-containing gases, where the gases are dispersed, dissolved, emulsified or otherwise combined with other phases. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations → separating → mixing, specifying techniques that treat chlorine-based streams in processes such as dispersing or emulsifying.
[B01F 23/23764] Hydrogen -- B01F 23/23764 covers mixing operations in which hydrogen is employed as a component or medium to facilitate the dispersion, dissolution, or emulsification of materials. It includes processes where hydrogen gas aids the blending of immiscible phases or acts as a reactant to improve mixing efficiency.
[B01F 23/23765] Nitrogen -- The code B01F 23/23765 covers the use of nitrogen in mixing processes where it acts as a dispersing or emulsifying medium. It includes methods where nitrogen is introduced to create fine droplets, bubbles or to dissolve substances during the preparation of mixtures such as emulsions, suspensions or solutions. This falls under the broader category of mixing operations involving phase interaction.
[B01F 23/23766] Sulphur containing gas -- This subclass covers the use of a sulphur-containing gas as the mixing medium in processes that disperse, dissolve or emulsify other phases, typically in chemical or industrial operations where the gas facilitates the blending of immiscible components. It falls under performing operations → separating → mixing according to the phases to be mixed.
[B01F 23/23767] Introducing steam or damp in liquids -- The B01F 23/23767 entry covers processes that introduce steam, vapor or damp into a liquid medium during mixing operations. It applies to methods where added moisture or steam helps disperse, dissolve, emulsify or otherwise combine components in the liquid phase.
[B01F 23/238] using vibrations, electrical or magnetic energy, radiations -- The B01F 23/238 entry covers mixing operations where the phases are combined by applying vibrations, electrical or magnetic energy, or radiation, such as for dispersing, emulsifying or dissolving substances. It applies to processes that use these energy forms to achieve the required mixing.
[B01F 23/29] Mixing systems, i.e. flow charts or diagrams -- B01F 23/29 relates to graphical representations of mixing processes, such as flow charts or diagrams that show the arrangement and operation of equipment used for dispersing, emulsifying or dissolving substances. These schematics aid in the design, analysis and optimisation of mixing systems in chemical and process engineering.
[B01F 23/291] for obtaining foams or aerosols -- B01F 23/291 is a CPC subclass within the mixing category of the performing-operations section, covering processes that deliberately create foams or aerosols. It relates to the mixing of phases to produce a dispersed gas-in-liquid or liquid-in-gas system, such as emulsifying or dispersing steps that generate a stable foam or aerosol product.
[B01F 23/30] Mixing gases with solids -- B01F 23/30 covers processes where gases are combined with solid materials to achieve mixing, dispersion or emulsification. It includes methods such as spraying gas through solid powders, fluidised-bed mixing, or contacting gases with solids for reactions or blending.
[B01F 23/32] by introducing solids in gas volumes -- B01F 23/32 covers mixing operations in which solid material is introduced directly into a gas volume to achieve dispersion, emulsification or other phase-mixing effects. The method is used to distribute solids uniformly throughout the gas stream for reactions, drying, coating or similar processing steps. This subclass falls under the broader category of separating and mixing operations that involve phase-specific techniques.
[B01F 23/34] by introducing gases in solid materials, e.g. in masses of powder or particles -- This subclass covers processes where a gas is injected into a solid bulk such as powders or particles to achieve mixing, dispersion or emulsification. The gas bubbles help separate and distribute solid components, often used for aeration, fluidization, or to improve homogeneity.
