[B01F 23/341] by introducing steam, e.g. for wetting the solids -- This subclass concerns mixing operations where steam is introduced to wet solid particles, aiding their dispersion or emulsification. The steam provides moisture that improves contact between phases and enhances the mixing efficiency.
[B01F 23/36] by mixing in fluidised bed state -- B01F 23/36 covers processes that achieve mixing by creating a fluidised-bed state, where solid particles are suspended in a flowing gas or liquid to promote intimate contact and dispersion of different phases. This technique is applied for dissolving, emulsifying or otherwise dispersing materials within the broader category of separating operations.
[B01F 23/39] Mixing systems, i.e. flow charts or diagrams -- B01F 23/39 covers graphical representations such as flow charts or diagrams of mixing systems used to combine materials of different phases, including processes that disperse, emulsify or dissolve substances. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and mixing according to the phases to be mixed.
[B01F 23/40] Mixing liquids with liquids -- This subclass covers processes in which one liquid is combined with another liquid to achieve mixing, including dispersion, emulsification, or dissolution, by direct contact or using devices that promote intimate contact. It includes methods that rely on the physical interaction of the two liquids to form a homogeneous or stable multiphase mixture.
[B01F 23/405] Methods of mixing liquids with liquids  B01F23/4105  takes precedence -- B01F 23/405 in the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to methods for mixing liquids with other liquids, specifically when one method does not take precedence over another. However, if a more specific method is applicable, such as that covered under B01F 23/4105, it should be used instead.
[B01F 23/41] Emulsifying -- The CPC subclass B01F 23/41 covers processes and apparatus for emulsifying, i.e., mixing two immiscible liquid phases to form a stable emulsion. It includes methods that disperse one liquid into another by mechanical, chemical or combined means, used in chemical, pharmaceutical, food and related industries.
[B01F 23/4105] Methods of emulsifying -- The CPC subclass B01F 23/4105 defines methods for emulsifying, i.e. forming stable mixtures of two immiscible liquids by dispersing one phase within the other. It includes mechanical, chemical or ultrasonic techniques that achieve emulsification as part of broader mixing operations.
[B01F 23/411] using electrical or magnetic fields, heat or vibrations -- B01F 23/411 covers mixing processes that employ electrical or magnetic fields, heat, or mechanical vibrations to achieve dispersion, emulsification, or dissolution of substances. It falls under mixing according to the phases to be mixed, specifically the methods that use energy inputs such as electromagnetic or thermal energy to promote intimate contact between components.
[B01F 23/4111] using vibrations -- The code B01F 23/4111 refers to mixing methods that use vibratory energy to disperse, emulsify or otherwise combine different phases, such as liquids, solids or gases. It is classified under performing operations → separating → mixing according to the phases to be mixed.
[B01F 23/413] Homogenising a raw emulsion or making monodisperse or fine emulsions -- B01F 23/413 pertains to processes that homogenise a raw emulsion or produce monodisperse or fine emulsions, focusing on mixing operations where phases are dispersed or emulsified. It covers methods such as dissolving, emulsifying, or dispersing to achieve uniform droplet size, and falls under the broader category of separating operations in the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[B01F 23/414] characterised by the internal structure of the emulsion -- B01F 23/414 falls under mixing operations, specifically the sub-category dealing with separating and mixing phases such as dispersing or emulsifying. It pertains to processes where the internal structure of the resulting emulsion is a defining characteristic of the method.
[B01F 23/4141] High internal phase ratio [HIPR] emulsions, e.g. having high percentage of internal phase, e.g. higher than 60-90 % of water in oil [W/O] -- B01F 23/4141 classifies high internal phase ratio emulsions, where the dispersed water phase makes up more than 60-90 % of the total in a water-in-oil system, and which are produced by mixing techniques such as dispersing or emulsifying.
[B01F 23/4142] Inversed-type emulsions -- B01F 23/4142 covers methods for producing inverse emulsions, where the internal phase is dispersed in a continuous external phase, typically water droplets dispersed in oil. The entry addresses mixing techniques that achieve the required phase distribution, such as high-shear or low-shear agitation, and may include additives that stabilize the inverse emulsion.
[B01F 23/4143] Microemulsions -- B01F 23/4143 concerns methods for producing microemulsions, thermodynamically stable mixtures of oil, water and surfactants that form nanoscale droplets for use in pharmaceuticals, cosmetics and other applications. It falls under the mixing operations hierarchy, specifically the subclass dealing with mixing according to the phases to be mixed, such as dispersing or emulsifying. The entry addresses the preparation and control of these fine-scale emulsions.
[B01F 23/4144] Multiple emulsions, in particular double emulsions, e.g. water in oil in water; Three-phase emulsions -- B01F 23/4144 covers processes for producing multiple emulsions, especially double emulsions such as water-in-oil-in-water systems, and three-phase emulsions. It is classified under mixing operations for separating and dispersing phases.
[B01F 23/4145] Emulsions of oils, e.g. fuel, and water -- B01F 23/4145 covers emulsions formed from oil phases such as fuels and water, and the methods for preparing or handling these mixtures, including their stabilization and properties. It falls under mixing operations where the oil and water are dispersed to create a stable emulsion, relevant to fuel additives, lubricants, and related applications.
[B01F 23/4146] Emulsions including solid particles, e.g. as solution or dispersion, i.e. molten material or material dissolved in a solvent or dispersed in a liquid -- The CPC subclass B01F 23/4146 covers mixing operations that create emulsions containing solid particles, where the solids may be dissolved, melted, or dispersed in a liquid medium. It falls within the broader categories of separating and mixing, specifically addressing emulsifying or dispersing processes that involve solid-phase material.
[B01F 23/43] using driven stirrers -- B01F 23/43 covers processes that employ powered stirrers to mix substances, especially when the mixing is defined by the phases to be combined such as dispersion or emulsification. The classification falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and mixing, and addresses the mechanical action of driven agitators to achieve uniform mixtures.
[B01F 23/431] the liquids being introduced from the outside through or along the axis of a rotating stirrer, e.g. the stirrer rotating due to the reaction of the introduced liquid -- B01F 23/431 covers mixing processes in which liquids are supplied from the outside through or alongside the axis of a rotating stirrer, the stirrer being driven by the reaction or torque generated by the introduced fluid. This arrangement enables simultaneous feeding and dispersion or emulsification of the liquids during operation.
[B01F 23/45] using flow mixing -- B01F 23/45 covers the use of flow-induced mixing techniques to combine materials, especially when the goal is to disperse, emulsify or otherwise mix different phases. It pertains to operations where the relative motion of fluids creates shear or turbulence that achieves the desired homogenisation.
[B01F 23/451] by injecting one liquid into another -- Methods that achieve mixing, dispersing or emulsifying by forcing a liquid jet or stream into a second liquid, causing turbulent interaction and breakup of droplets. Used for rapid dispersion, emulsion formation or dissolution in chemical and process engineering.
[B01F 23/452] by uniting flows taken from different parts of a receptacle or silo; Sandglass-type mixing  for particulate material  B01F25/80 -- B01F 23/452 pertains to methods and apparatus for mixing materials, specifically focusing on techniques that involve uniting flows from different sections of a container or using sandglass-type mechanisms for particulate matter. This classification is part of the broader category of operations involving separating and mixing processes.
[B01F 23/453] by moving the liquids in countercurrent -- B01F 23/453 concerns mixing liquids by moving them in opposite directions, i.e., countercurrent flow, to promote dispersion, emulsification or dissolution. The method uses two liquid streams that travel against each other to improve mixing efficiency.
[B01F 23/454] by injecting a mixture of liquid and gas -- B01F 23/454 pertains to mixing processes where a mixture of liquid and gas is injected, typically for dispersing or emulsifying purposes. This classification covers methods and apparatuses designed to introduce and combine these phases effectively in various industrial applications.
[B01F 23/47] involving high-viscosity liquids, e.g. asphalt -- **Glossary:** - High viscosity liquids: A high viscosity fluid is generally considered one with a viscosity above 2000 centipoise, such as the viscosity of bitumen measured at 25 deg.C.
[B01F 23/471] using a very viscous liquid and a liquid of low viscosity -- This CPC subclass covers mixing operations in which a highly viscous liquid is combined with a liquid of much lower viscosity, such as when dispersing or emulsifying two phases with markedly different flow properties. It is classified under performing operations, separating, mixing according to the phases to be mixed.
[B01F 23/48] characterised by the nature of the liquids  B01F23/47  takes precedence -- B01F 23/48 refers to mixing operations that are defined primarily by the nature of the liquids being processed, such as their polarity, viscosity or miscibility, especially in dispersing or emulsifying applications. It falls under the broader group of mixing according to the phases to be mixed and is subordinated to B01F 23/47, which has priority.
[B01F 23/481] using liquefied or cryogenic gases -- This subclass covers mixing processes that employ liquefied or cryogenic gases-such as liquid nitrogen, carbon dioxide, or other compressed, low-temperature fluids-to disperse, dissolve, or emulsify materials, often by rapid expansion, cooling, or phase change. It includes techniques where the gas itself acts as the mixing medium or carrier.
[B01F 23/482] using molten solids -- The subclass B01F 23/482 covers mixing operations where molten solid materials are employed to aid dispersion, dissolution or emulsification of other phases. It includes processes that use solids melted above their melting point to facilitate the formation of homogeneous mixtures or emulsions.
[B01F 23/483] using water for diluting a liquid ingredient, obtaining a predetermined concentration or making an aqueous solution of a concentrate -- This CPC subclass B01F 23/483 concerns processes that use water to dilute a liquid ingredient, achieve a predetermined concentration, or prepare an aqueous solution from a concentrate. It falls under mixing according to the phases to be mixed, such as dispersing or emulsifying, within the broader categories of performing operations and separating.
[B01F 23/49] Mixing systems, i.e. flow charts or diagrams -- B01F 23/49 covers mixing systems represented by flow charts or diagrams that illustrate the sequence and interaction of mixing operations such as dispersing, emulsifying or dissolving, especially when the system is organized according to the phases to be mixed. It is used to classify documentation that details the logical or physical layout of mixing processes in industrial or laboratory settings.
[B01F 23/50] Mixing liquids with solids -- B01F 23/50 covers processes for mixing liquids with solid materials, including techniques for dispersing or emulsifying the liquid phase within a solid matrix. It is applied in chemical, pharmaceutical and food production where uniform distribution of liquids in powders or granules is required.
[B01F 23/51] Methods thereof -- B01F 23/51 covers methods for mixing where the specific phases to be combined are defined, including processes such as dispersing or emulsifying. It falls under performing operations - separating - mixing and addresses techniques that achieve the desired blend by acting on the designated phases.
[B01F 23/511] characterised by the composition of the liquids or solids -- B01F 23/511 covers mixing processes where the defining factor is the specific composition of the liquids or solids being combined, such as particular solvents, polymers, or particulate additives. The classification is used for methods that rely on the material makeup to achieve dispersion, emulsification, or dissolution during the mixing operation.
[B01F 23/53] using driven stirrers -- B01F 23/53 covers mixing operations in which mechanically driven stirrers such as impellers, paddles or agitators are used to combine material phases, creating the shear and turbulence needed for dispersion, emulsification or dissolution. The subclass includes devices and methods that employ continuous rotary motion to achieve these phase-mixing effects.
[B01F 23/54] wetting solids -- B01F 23/54 covers methods for wetting solid particles with a liquid to initiate dispersion, dissolution or emulsification. It includes techniques such as spraying, dipping or agitation that promote intimate contact between the solid surface and the liquid phase, facilitating subsequent mixing operations.
[B01F 23/55] the mixture being submitted to electrical, sonic or similar energy -- B01F 23/55 refers to mixing processes in which the material is subjected to electrical, sonic or comparable energy to promote dispersion, emulsification or dissolution. It belongs to the hierarchy of performing operations, separating, and mixing according to the phases to be mixed.
[B01F 23/551] using vibrations -- B01F 23/551 covers mixing operations in which mechanical vibrations are applied to achieve dispersion, dissolution, or emulsification of components, particularly when the materials to be mixed belong to different phases. The method uses vibratory energy to enhance particle contact and break up agglomerates, facilitating rapid and uniform blending. This falls under the broader category of separating and mixing processes in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[B01F 23/56] by introducing solids in liquids, e.g. dispersing or dissolving -- The CPC subclass B01F 23/56 covers processes where solid materials are added to a liquid medium to achieve mixing, such as dispersing or dissolving particles. This method is used in solid-liquid operations that separate or combine phases, typically in chemical or material preparation.
[B01F 23/565] by introducing liquids in solid material, e.g. to obtain slurries -- This CPC subclass relates to mixing operations in which a liquid is introduced into a solid material to create a slurry, such as wetting particles to achieve dispersion or emulsification. It covers methods that combine the liquid and solid phases to form a homogeneous mixture.
[B01F 23/566] by introducing liquids in a fluidised bed -- In the CPC subclass B01F 23/566 the mixing operation is achieved by introducing liquids into a fluidised bed, a method used to disperse or emulsify separate phases. It belongs to the hierarchy performing operations - separating - mixing (e.g., dissolving, emulsifying, dispersing).
[B01F 23/57] Mixing high-viscosity liquids with solids -- B01F 23/57 covers processes for mixing high-viscosity liquids with solid material, including dispersing or emulsifying actions when the liquid phase is very thick. It is a sub-class of "Mixing according to the phases to be mixed" within the broader "Separating" group of Performing Operations.
[B01F 23/58] characterised by the nature of the liquid  B01F23/57  takes precedence -- B01F 23/58 concerns mixing operations in which the defining factor is the nature of the liquid involved, such as its polarity, viscosity, or miscibility, and includes methods tailored specifically for aqueous, organic, polar or non-polar phases. This subclass falls under mixing according to the phases to be mixed, where the liquid's characteristics dictate the approach to dispersing or emulsifying.
[B01F 23/581] Mixing liquids with solids, slurries or sludge, for obtaining a diluted slurry -- The subclass B01F 23/581 covers operations in which a liquid is mixed with solid particles, slurries or sludge to produce a more dilute slurry, emphasizing mixing methods based on the phases involved such as dispersing or emulsifying. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and mixing (e.g., dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing).
[B01F 23/582] Mixing foam with solids -- B01F 23/582 covers processes in which a foam phase is combined with solid material, typically by dispersing or emulsifying the foam into the solids to achieve a uniform mixture. The method is used where the foam acts as a carrier or medium for the solids, such as in the production of lightweight composites, foamed concrete, or aerated food products.
[B01F 23/59] Mixing systems, i.e. flow charts or diagrams -- B01F 23/59 pertains to mixing systems represented by flow charts or diagrams that illustrate how different phases-such as liquids, solids, or gases-are combined, dispersed, or emulsified. The classification covers schematic representations of processes used to achieve thorough mixing according to the specific phases involved.
[B01F 23/60] Mixing solids with solids -- B01F 23/60 covers processes for mixing solid particles with other solid particles, such as dry blending, granulating or coating where no liquid phase is involved. It includes methods that disperse, emulsify or otherwise combine powders, granules or pellets using mechanical agitation, tumbling, kneading or high-shear forces to achieve a uniform mixture. This subclass falls under mixing operations that separate phases by handling solids alone.
[B01F 23/62] using a receptacle with a bottom discharge with oscillating or vibrating opening and closing elements -- The subclass B01F 23/62 covers mixing operations that employ a receptacle with a bottom discharge whose opening and closing are controlled by oscillating or vibrating elements, a setup commonly used for dispersing, emulsifying or otherwise combining different phases.
[B01F 23/64] using rotatable mixing elements at the lower end of discharge hoppers -- B01F 23/64 covers mixing operations that employ rotatable mixing elements positioned at the lower part of a discharge hopper, typically to disperse or emulsify materials as they exit the hopper. The rotating elements create shear and turbulence, improving homogeneity of the product before it leaves the equipment.
[B01F 23/66] by evaporating or liquefying at least one of the components; using a fluid which is evaporated after mixing -- This subclass covers mixing processes in which at least one component is turned into a vapour or liquid during the operation, or a fluid is added for mixing and then evaporated afterward, such as in evaporative or liquefaction-assisted dispersing, emulsifying, or dissolution procedures.
[B01F 23/69] Mixing systems, i.e. flow charts or diagrams; Arrangements, e.g. comprising controlling means -- B01F 23/69 covers the documentation and layout of mixing processes, including flow charts, diagrammatic representations, and system arrangements that incorporate control mechanisms for operations such as dispersing, emulsifying, or otherwise mixing separate phases.
[B01F 23/70] Pre-treatment of the materials to be mixed -- B01F 23/70 covers processes that condition or modify materials before they are mixed, including steps such as heating, grinding, dissolving, or adding surfactants to facilitate subsequent dispersing or emulsifying. It falls under the mixing operations of the CPC, specifically dealing with pre-treatment of the substances to be combined.
[B01F 23/701] Coating solid materials -- B01F 23/701 covers processes for coating solid materials in which a coating composition is first prepared by mixing, dispersing or emulsifying the constituent phases and then applied as a uniform layer to a solid substrate, typically using mixers or similar equipment to achieve the desired coating quality.
[B01F 23/702] Cooling materials -- B01F 23/702 relates to processes that mix two or more phases specifically to cool a material, often by dispersing or emulsifying a hot stream into a cooler medium. The subclass is part of the performing-operations hierarchy under separating and mixing operations.
[B01F 23/703] Degassing or de-aerating materials; Replacing one gas within the materials by another gas -- The subclass B01F 23/703 covers processes that remove gas from a material or replace one gas with another, such as degassing, de-aerating, or gas-exchange steps carried out during mixing operations. It is situated under mixing / dispersing procedures within the broader category of separating.
[B01F 23/704] Drying materials, e.g. in order to mix them in solid state -- B01F 23/704 covers methods for removing moisture from substances so that they can be blended while still in the solid phase. The subclass includes drying techniques such as evaporation, spray-drying, freeze-drying, or other moisture-removal operations performed before or during solid-state mixing.
[B01F 23/705] Submitting materials to electrical energy fields to charge or ionize them -- The classification B01F 23/705 covers processes in which materials are introduced into electrical energy fields to become charged or ionized as part of a mixing operation, such as dispersing, emulsifying or dissolving substances. It addresses the use of electric fields to modify the physical state of the components during the mixing stage.
[B01F 23/706] Evaporating solvents or dispersion liquids, e.g. water, at least partially -- B01F 23/706 covers mixing operations-such as dispersing or emulsifying-where a portion of the solvent or dispersion liquid, often water, is removed by evaporation. The partial evaporation reduces the liquid phase while the mixture continues to be processed, facilitating concentration or solid formation.
[B01F 23/707] Extracting materials to be mixed from a stream of fluid or from a solid containing them, e.g. by adsorption, absorption or distillation -- B01F 23/707 covers processes that remove or isolate components that will later be mixed, taking them from a flowing fluid stream or from a solid-containing matrix by methods such as adsorption, absorption or distillation. The extraction step prepares the material for subsequent dispersing, emulsifying or other mixing operations.
[B01F 23/708] Filtering materials -- B01F 23/708 concerns filtering materials as a step following mixing operations such as dispersion or emulsification. It includes methods for separating solid, liquid or immiscible phases by passing the mixture through a filter medium, and the selection or conditioning of the filter material.
[B01F 23/709] Freezing materials, e.g. to mix them in solid state -- B01F 23/709 covers the preparation of mixtures by first freezing the constituent materials so they can be combined while solid, enabling processes such as solid-state blending, grinding or milling of frozen components. This approach is used when the desired product requires intimate contact of components that are difficult to mix in the liquid phase, for example to preserve heat-sensitive ingredients or to obtain fine dispersions after subsequent thawing.
[B01F 23/71] Grinding materials -- B01F 23/71 covers processes that grind solid or semi-solid substances as part of a mixing operation, typically to reduce particle size before or during dispersion, emulsification or other phase-mixing steps. It includes mechanical grinding devices such as mills, crushers or bead mills used in combination with mixers to achieve finer particles for subsequent processing.
[B01F 23/711] Heating materials, e.g. melting -- B01F 23/711 concerns mixing operations in which heat is applied to the materials, such as melting, to aid dissolution, emulsification or dispersion. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, separating and mixing, and addresses processes that deliberately raise temperature to improve the mixing of different phases.
[B01F 23/712] Irradiating materials -- Subclass B01F 23/712 covers mixing operations in which the material to be combined is subjected to irradiation, such as ultraviolet, gamma, or laser radiation, to promote dissolution, dispersion, emulsification or other chemical changes during the mixing step. It includes processes that use irradiation to modify the physical or chemical state of the components as part of the overall mixing operation.
[B01F 23/713] Sieving materials -- The subclass B01F 23/713 covers processes and apparatus for sieving solid materials, where particles are separated by passing a bulk through a screen or mesh to retain larger pieces and allow finer ones to pass. It includes methods for size-based separation in mixing operations such as dispersing or emulsifying, and the design of sieves, vibratory screens, and related equipment used to classify or refine particulate feedstocks.
[B01F 23/80] After-treatment of the mixture -- B01F 23/80 concerns after-treatment of mixtures in the mixing domain, covering steps applied after dispersing or emulsifying such as heating, cooling, settling, filtration or other processes that stabilise, modify or complete the product.
[B01F 23/801] Coating the solid mixture -- B01F 23/801 covers methods for applying a coating to a solid mixture that has been prepared by mixing distinct phases, such as dispersing or emulsifying components. The process typically follows the mixing step described in the preceding CPC groups and is used to modify surface properties, protect particles, or enable controlled release.
[B01F 23/802] Cooling the mixture -- The B01F 23/802 class covers processes in which a mixture being mixed-such as a dispersion or emulsion-is cooled to control temperature, improve stability, or facilitate phase formation. It includes methods for removing heat during mixing, using external cooling media, refrigeration, or other thermal management techniques applied to the combined phases. This sub-category falls under mixing operations that separate components while managing their thermal conditions.
[B01F 23/803] Venting, degassing or ventilating of gases, fumes or toxic vapours from the mixture -- B01F 23/803 covers methods and apparatus for removing gases, fumes or toxic vapours from a mixture during mixing operations, such as venting, degassing or ventilating. It is applied in processes where phases are dispersed, emulsified or dissolved, ensuring safe and efficient handling of volatile components.
[B01F 23/804] Drying the mixture -- B01F 23/804 covers processes that dry a mixture after it has been combined in a liquid or semi-liquid state, typically following dispersing or emulsifying operations. The classification addresses techniques for removing moisture from the blended material, such as evaporation, spray-drying, freeze-drying or other drying methods, to obtain a solid product. It is situated under mixing operations that separate phases, specifically those aimed at dispersing or emulsifying substances.
[B01F 23/805] Submitting a mixture to electrical energy fields, e.g. corona discharge -- The subclass B01F 23/805 covers processes that apply electrical energy fields, such as corona discharge, to a mixture in order to promote mixing, dispersion, or emulsification of the phases, thereby achieving the desired separation or blending effect.
[B01F 23/806] Evaporating a carrier, e.g. liquid carbon dioxide used to dissolve, disperse, emulsify or other components that are difficult to be mixed; Evaporating liquid components -- B01F 23/806 covers processes that use a volatile carrier-commonly liquid carbon dioxide-to dissolve, disperse, emulsify or otherwise mix components that are hard to combine, followed by evaporation of the carrier or other liquid ingredients to isolate the final mixture. This falls under separating operations within the broader mixing class.
[B01F 23/807] Extracting components from the mixture, e.g. by adsorption, absorption or distillation -- This subclass covers methods for recovering individual constituents from a previously mixed system by means such as adsorption, absorption, or distillation. It is applied after mixing operations like dispersing or emulsifying and focuses on the extraction phase that isolates desired components from the bulk mixture.
[B01F 23/808] Filtering the mixture -- B01F 23/808 covers processes in which a mixture that has been created by mixing, dispersing, emulsifying or otherwise combining components is subsequently passed through a filter to separate solid or liquid phases. The classification applies to operations that remove unwanted particles, recover product, or clarify the mixture after the mixing stage.
[B01F 23/809] Freezing the mixture -- B01F 23/809 relates to processes where a mixture is frozen as a step in a separating or mixing operation, such as solidifying a dispersed or emulsified phase to aid subsequent separation or further processing.
[B01F 23/81] Grinding the mixture -- B01F 23/81 covers the step of grinding a mixture as part of a mixing operation where the phases are combined, such as in dispersing or emulsifying. The grinding reduces particle size to promote uniform distribution and interaction between components, often preceding or accompanying dissolution or dispersion.
[B01F 23/811] Heating the mixture -- B01F 23/811 relates to methods where a mixture is heated while being mixed, typically to facilitate dissolution, dispersion or emulsification of the components. The heating step can be applied to any phase combination during the mixing operation to improve homogeneity or reaction rates.
[B01F 23/812] Irradiating the mixture -- The subclass covers processes in which a mixture is exposed to radiation such as ultraviolet, gamma or electron-beam energy to effect mixing, curing, polymerisation or other chemical changes. It is part of the broader category of separating and mixing operations that treat mixtures according to their phases.
[B01F 25/00] Mixers -- The CPC class B01F 25/00 covers mixers used in processes of separating, mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing materials. It includes devices that agitate or blend substances to achieve uniform composition, typically in chemical, pharmaceutical, food or material processing. These mixers can be static or dynamic, employing various mechanisms such as blades, paddles or impellers.
[B01F 25/10] Mixing by creating a vortex flow, e.g. by tangential introduction of flow components -- Mixing by creating a vortex flow uses tangentially introduced fluid streams that swirl to form a vortex, promoting rapid dispersion, dissolution or emulsification. The vortex-induced motion enhances homogeneity in mixers without relying on direct mechanical agitation.
[B01F 25/101] wherein the vortex flows in a spherical shaped receptacle or chamber -- B01F 25/101 pertains to mixers where the mixing process involves a vortex flow specifically occurring within a spherical shaped receptacle or chamber, enhancing the mixing efficiency through the unique geometry of the container.
[B01F 25/102] wherein the vortex is created by two or more jets introduced tangentially in separate mixing chambers or consecutively in the same mixing chamber -- B01F 25/102 pertains to mixers that generate a vortex by directing two or more jets tangentially, either into separate mixing chambers or sequentially into the same chamber, to achieve actions such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. This subclass falls under the broader categories of separating and mixing operations within the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[B01F 25/103] with additional mixing means other than vortex mixers, e.g. the vortex chamber being positioned in another mixing chamber -- B01F 25/103 defines mixers that employ additional mixing mechanisms beyond a simple vortex mixer, such as integrating the vortex chamber within a separate mixing chamber to improve agitation and dispersion. It is a sub-category of mixing operations within separating processes.
[B01F 25/104] characterised by the arrangement of the discharge opening -- B01F 25/104 covers mixing apparatus whose defining feature is the specific arrangement of the discharge opening, which determines how the processed material exits the device. This subclass falls under mixers used for separating and mixing operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 25/1041] the mixing chamber being vertical with the outlet tube at its upper side -- B01F 25/1041 covers mixers in which the mixing chamber is arranged vertically and the discharge tube is positioned at the top of the chamber, allowing mixed material to exit upward. This design is used in processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing where gravity assists product removal. It falls under CPC class B01F for mixing equipment.
[B01F 25/1042] the mixing chamber being vertical and having an outlet tube at its bottom whose inlet is at a higher level than the inlet of the vortex creating jet, e.g. the jet being introduced at the bottom of the mixing chamber -- This subclass covers mixers where the mixing chamber is oriented vertically and the outlet tube is located at the bottom, while the chamber inlet is positioned higher than the inlet that creates a vortex-producing jet, for example when the jet is introduced at the chamber base.
[B01F 25/105] Mixing heads, i.e. compact mixing units or modules, using mixing valves for feeding and mixing at least two components -- Mixing heads are compact units or modules that incorporate mixing valves to feed and combine at least two components, enabling rapid dissolution, emulsifying or dispersing within a single device. They are used in processes where precise, on-demand mixing is required.
[B01F 25/1051] of the mixing valve type -- The code B01F 25/1051 refers to mixing devices that employ a mixing valve, allowing one fluid stream to be introduced into another while providing agitation. Such valves are used to achieve dissolution, emulsification or dispersion in chemical and industrial processes.
[B01F 25/12] Interdigital mixers, i.e. the substances to be mixed are divided in sub-streams which are rearranged in an interdigital or interspersed manner  micromixers using interdigital streams  B01F33/3012 -- B01F 25/12 covers mixers in which the feed streams are split into parallel sub-streams that are interleaved or rearranged in an interdigital pattern, typically used in micro-scale mixing devices. Such interdigital mixers create intimate contact between fluids by alternating the streams side-by-side.
[B01F 25/14] Mixing drops, droplets or bodies of liquid which flow together or contact each other  micromixers using where the materials to be mixed are in the form of droplets  B01F33/302 -- B01F 25/14 covers devices and processes that mix drops, droplets or liquid bodies that flow together or come into contact, typically employing micromixer designs where the components to be combined exist as discrete droplets. The class includes technologies that achieve dissolution, emulsification, or dispersion by bringing separate liquid phases into intimate contact within micro-scale channels or reactors.
[B01F 25/20] Jet mixers, i.e. mixers using high-speed fluid streams -- Jet mixers are devices that create a high-speed fluid jet to generate turbulence and shear, rapidly mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing substances within a vessel. The jet's momentum entrains surrounding material, achieving efficient blending without moving mechanical parts. These mixers are employed in chemical, pharmaceutical and food-processing operations where quick, uniform mixing is required.
[B01F 25/21] with submerged injectors, e.g. nozzles, for injecting high-pressure jets into a large volume or into mixing chambers -- B01F 25/21 covers mixers that employ submerged injectors such as nozzles to introduce high-pressure jets directly into a bulk liquid or a mixing chamber, enhancing rapid dispersion, dissolution or emulsification. The design allows the jet to be generated within the material being processed, improving mixing efficiency for large-volume or continuous operations.
[B01F 25/211] the injectors being surrounded by guiding tubes -- B01F 25/211 covers mixers in which the injection devices are enclosed within guiding tubes that direct the introduced fluid into the mixing zone. This arrangement improves control of spray or jet dispersion for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 25/212] the injectors being movable, e.g. rotating -- B01F 25/212 covers mixers in which the injection elements are capable of movement, such as rotation, to improve the distribution and dispersion of fluids or solids during the mixing process. This mobility enables more efficient blending, dissolution, emulsification or dispersion of components.
[B01F 25/2121] Pivoting or oscillating in a multidirectional way during jetting -- The code B01F 25/2121 covers mixers that produce fluid jets which are pivoted or oscillated in several directions during operation, providing multidirectional jetting to enhance dissolution, emulsification or dispersion. Such devices use moving nozzles or adjustable flow paths to vary jet direction while the fluid is being mixed.
[B01F 25/2122] Rotating during jetting -- B01F 25/2122 covers mixers that achieve mixing by rotating the material while it is being jet-ejected through a nozzle or similar opening. The rotation may be applied to the jet or to the vessel, facilitating dissolution, emulsifying or dispersing of components. It resides under the hierarchy of performing operations, separating and mixing.
[B01F 25/2123] being vertically moved to bring the injector in or out of operative position -- The B01F 25/2123 subclass refers to mixers in which the injector is positioned or withdrawn by a vertical motion, allowing it to be placed into or out of the operative zone of the mixing chamber. This vertical movement mechanism is used to initiate, control or stop the injection of liquids, gases or solids during processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 25/2124] being moved or transported between different locations during jetting -- The subclass concerns processes in which the material to be mixed, dispersed, dissolved or emulsified is relocated while a jet of fluid is applied, such as moving the product through pipes, between vessels or along a conveyor during jetting operations. It includes equipment and methods that transport the mixture between different locations as the jet performs the separating or mixing action.
[B01F 25/2125] Moving to adjust the direction of jetting, the injectors being fixed during operation -- Code B01F 25/2125 covers mixers in which the direction of a jet is altered by moving the mixing element while the injectors stay fixed during operation, enabling on-line adjustment of flow paths for processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 25/23] Mixing by intersecting jets -- B01F 25/23 pertains to mixing processes that rely on intersecting fluid jets to achieve blending, dissolution, emulsification or dispersion. The method directs two or more streams so they collide, generating turbulent flow that rapidly mixes the contents. It is used in mixers, reactors and spray devices where jet interaction is the primary means of mixing.
[B01F 25/231] the intersecting jets having the configuration of sheets, cylinders or cones -- B01F 25/231 covers mixers in which opposing fluid jets intersect to form sheet-, cylinder- or cone-shaped flow patterns, creating intense shear for separating, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing materials. The configuration enhances mixing efficiency by promoting rapid interfacial contact and turbulent breakdown of particles or droplets.
[B01F 25/25] Mixing by jets impinging against collision plates -- B01F 25/25 covers mixing processes in which one or more fluid jets are projected onto collision plates, causing the jets to impinge and create intense turbulence that disperses, dissolves or emulsifies the material. The technique is employed in reactors, mixers and processing lines where rapid and uniform blending is required.
[B01F 25/27] Mixing by jetting components into a conduit for agitating its contents -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mixing whereby the high-speed jetting components form a fluid stream which is used to agitate the contents flowing in a conduit, e.g. by directing the jet flow against each other.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[B01F 25/28] characterised by the specific design of the jet injector -- B01F 25/28 covers mixers that employ a jet injector, where the mixing action is achieved by a high-velocity fluid jet directed into the material to be mixed. The subclass is defined by particular design features of the injector such as nozzle geometry, pressure generation, or feeding arrangement that enhance dispersion, emulsification or dissolution.
[B01F 25/281] the jet injector being of the explosive rapid expansion of supercritical solutions [RESS] or fluid injection of molecular spray [FIMS] type, i.e. the liquid is jetted in an environment (gas or liquid) by nozzles, in conditions of significant pressure drop, with the possible generation of shock waves -- B01F 25/281 covers jet injectors that create a rapid expansion of super-critical solutions (RESS) or employ fluid-injection-of-molecular-spray (FIMS) techniques, where a liquid is forced through a nozzle into a gas or liquid medium under a large pressure drop, often generating shock waves to enhance mixing, dissolution, emulsification or dispersion. This technology is classified under mixers within separating operations of the CPC.
[B01F 25/282] the jet injector being of Coanda-type, i.e. having a surface to attract the jet for adjusting its direction -- B01F 25/282 falls under the mixing subgroup of the Performing Operations-Separating hierarchy and covers jet injectors that employ the Coanda effect, using a shaped surface to draw the fluid jet toward it and thereby steer its direction. Such devices are used to enhance mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing processes.
[B01F 25/30] Injector mixers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mixers wherein additional components are injected to a flowing main component without jetting the additional component.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Mixing by creating a vortex flow, e.g. by tangential introduction of flow components -> B01F25/10
[B01F 25/305] the additional component being axially fed and radially discharged through a circumferential outlet -- B01F 25/305 covers mixers in which an additional component is fed along the axis of the mixing chamber and discharged radially through a circumferential outlet, allowing continuous introduction and removal of material during the mixing operation.
[B01F 25/31] in conduits or tubes through which the main component flows -- B01F 25/31 covers mixers that are installed directly inside conduits or tubes through which the main component flows, enabling mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing to occur within the pipeline during transport. This subclass falls under Performing Operations → Separating → Mixing, e.g. Dissolving, Emulsifying or Dispersing → Mixers.
[B01F 25/311] for mixing more than two components; Devices specially adapted for generating foam -- B01F 25/311 covers mixers that combine more than two ingredients and are specifically designed to produce foam, such as foaming agents or aerated products. These devices are used in processes where simultaneous mixing and foam generation are required.
[B01F 25/3111] Devices specially adapted for generating foam, e.g. air foam -- B01F 25/3111 covers devices that are specifically designed to produce foam, typically by injecting air or gas into a liquid to create a stable aerated mixture. These foam-generating machines are used in applications such as firefighting, food processing, or cosmetics where a lightweight, uniform foam is required, and they fall within the broader class of mixers in the separating-mixing hierarchy.
[B01F 25/31112] with additional mixing means other than injector mixers, e.g. screen or baffles  B01F25/31113  takes precedence -- B01F 25/31112 covers mixers that employ secondary mixing elements other than injector mixers, such as screens, baffles or similar structures, to enhance dispersion, dissolution or emulsification within the mixing chamber. This subclass is superseded by B01F 25/31113.
[B01F 25/31113] with rotating elements, e.g. driven by one of the components for feeding or by the resulting mixture for additional mixing -- This subclass covers mixers that use rotating elements whose motion is produced either by a dedicated drive component for feeding the material or by the kinetic energy of the mixture itself to provide additional agitation. It applies to equipment where the rotation serves both feeding and secondary mixing functions.
[B01F 25/31114] with means for introducing an additional component, e.g. in predetermined proportion or in the main component -- This subclass pertains to mixers that incorporate a mechanism for adding a secondary component into the primary material, allowing the additional substance to be introduced in a predetermined proportion or at a controlled point within the main mixture, such as through feed ports, dosing pumps or dedicated addition chambers.
[B01F 25/312] with Venturi elements -- B01F 25/312 pertains to mixers that incorporate a Venturi element, using a constricted flow passage to generate a high-velocity jet that enhances mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing of substances. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, separating, mixing, and specifically mixers.
[B01F 25/3121] with additional mixing means other than injector mixers, e.g. screens, baffles or rotating elements -- B01F 25/3121 covers mixing devices that include extra mixing elements besides injector mixers, such as screens, baffles or rotating parts, to improve dispersion or dissolution. These additional components enhance the interaction of materials within the mixer, facilitating more uniform blending or emulsifying processes.
[B01F 25/3122] the material flowing at a supersonic velocity thereby creating shock waves -- This subclass covers mixers in which the material is accelerated to supersonic velocity, creating shock waves that enhance mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. The high-speed flow and resulting shock fronts improve energy transfer and particle size reduction.
[B01F 25/3123] with two or more Venturi elements -- This CPC subclass describes mixers that incorporate two or more Venturi elements to generate turbulence and improve the blending of liquids, such as for dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. The Venturi effect accelerates flow through each element, drawing in additional streams and enhancing mass-transfer efficiency in chemical, food or other processing operations.
[B01F 25/31231] used alternatively -- The code B01F 25/31231 classifies mixers that are employed alternately in processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing within separating operations. It falls under the Performing Operations hierarchy, specifically the Mixing subgroup, and covers equipment designed to be used as an alternative mixing means.
[B01F 25/31232] used simultaneously -- B01F 25/31232 designates mixers that perform mixing actions such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing while at the same time carrying out a separating operation, for example simultaneous agitation and filtration or centrifugation within a single apparatus. This subclass covers integrated equipment that combines both functions in one process step.
[B01F 25/31233] used successively -- The CPC entry B01F 25/31233 (used successively) covers mixers in which the material undergoes a series of distinct mixing actions performed one after another, rather than simultaneously. It applies to equipment designed to effect successive steps such as dissolution, emulsification or dispersion within a single processing sequence.
[B01F 25/3124] characterised by the place of introduction of the main flow -- B01F 25/3124 pertains to mixers whose defining feature is the location at which the main material flow is introduced into the mixing chamber. The classification distinguishes devices based on whether the feed enters at the inlet, through a side port, or directly onto the agitator, affecting flow patterns and mixing performance.
[B01F 25/31241] the main flow being injected in the circumferential area of the venturi, creating an aspiration in the central part of the conduit -- This CPC subclass covers mixers where the main fluid stream is injected around the circumference of a venturi, creating a low-pressure zone that aspirates material into the central part of the conduit.
[B01F 25/31242] the main flow being injected in the central area of the venturi, creating an aspiration in the circumferential part of the conduit  B01F25/31243  takes precedence -- This CPC entry (B01F 25/31242) covers mixers in which the primary fluid stream is introduced into the centre of a venturi, generating a low-pressure zone that draws secondary streams from the surrounding conduit wall. The arrangement is used for dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing materials. The more specific subclass B01F 25/31243 supersedes it.
[B01F 25/31243] Eductor or eductor-type venturi, i.e. the main flow being injected through the venturi with high speed in the form of a jet -- An eductor-type venturi mixer uses a high-speed jet of the main fluid passing through a venturi to create suction that draws in and rapidly blends secondary streams, enabling efficient mixing, dissolution or emulsification of liquids.
[B01F 25/3125] characteristics of the Venturi parts -- This subclass covers technical features of Venturi components used in mixers for separating and mixing processes, such as geometry, dimensions and flow characteristics that influence pressure drop and mixing efficiency. It lies within the broader group concerning mixers in mixing and separating operations.
[B01F 25/31251] Throats -- Throats are the narrowed passageways within a mixing device that convey material from the feed inlet to the mixing zone. They regulate flow rate, create shear and pressure differentials, and can be shaped to enhance dispersion or dissolution. The classification covers designs, features and arrangements of such throats in mixers.
[B01F 25/312511] Adjustable Venturi throat -- An adjustable venturi throat in a mixer is a controllable narrowing that varies the flow cross-section to regulate pressure drop and fluid velocity for optimized mixing, dissolution, or emulsification. By modifying the throat geometry the device can fine-tune residence time and shear conditions to improve process efficiency.
[B01F 25/312512] Profiled, grooved, ribbed throat, or being provided with baffles -- This CPC subclass (B01F 25/312512) covers mixers whose flow passages are shaped with profiles, grooves, ribs or equipped with baffles to improve mixing efficiency. Such features are used in equipment for dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing materials during separation processes.
[B01F 25/31252] Nozzles -- B01F 25/31252 covers nozzles employed in mixing apparatuses for processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It pertains to devices that introduce, spray or atomize fluids into the mixing zone to enhance interaction between phases. The classification falls under Performing Operations - Separating - Mixing - Mixers.
[B01F 25/312521] Adjustable Venturi nozzle -- The B01F 25/312521 entry defines an adjustable-venturi nozzle used in mixers that perform separating operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It comprises a converging-diverging passage whose throat size or geometry can be varied to control fluid velocity and pressure drop, allowing precise adjustment of mixing intensity and residence time in batch or continuous processes.
[B01F 25/312522] Profiled, grooved, ribbed nozzle, or being provided with baffles -- This CPC subclass covers mixers that employ a profiled, grooved or ribbed nozzle, or incorporate baffles, to create turbulence and improve dissolution, emulsification or dispersion of materials. It resides under performing operations, separating and mixing, and applies to devices that enhance fluid agitation in batch or continuous processes.
[B01F 25/31253] Discharge -- The entry covers devices, components or methods for removing the mixed material from a mixer, such as outlets, valves, ports or other discharge arrangements that allow the product to exit the mixing chamber. It includes designs that facilitate controlled or timed release of the mixture after operations like dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing have been completed.
[B01F 25/312531] Adjustable discharge conduit or barrel, e.g. adjustable in width -- The CPC entry B01F 25/312531 covers mixers that incorporate a discharge conduit or barrel whose dimensions can be altered, typically by adjusting the width, to control the rate or pattern of material exit during mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing operations. Such adjustable features enable fine tuning of residence time and shear conditions to optimise product quality.
[B01F 25/312532] Profiled, grooved, ribbed discharge conduit, or being provided with baffles -- This CPC subclass covers mixers that incorporate a discharge conduit shaped with profiles, grooves, ribs or equipped with baffles to boost flow turbulence and improve mixing efficiency. The design aids separation processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing by promoting better distribution of materials.
[B01F 25/312533] Constructional characteristics of the diverging discharge conduit or barrel, e.g. with zones of changing conicity -- This subclass covers the design of the diverging discharge conduit or barrel in mixers, focusing on features such as zones with varying conicity. It addresses how changes in the shape of the outlet passage affect fluid flow, separation, and mixing efficiency.
[B01F 25/313] wherein additional components are introduced in the centre of the conduit -- This subclass covers mixing devices that introduce extra components directly into the centre of the conduit during operation, enabling efficient blending, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing of materials. It belongs to the broader group of separating and mixing processes and specifies the method of central addition of additives within the mixing apparatus.
[B01F 25/3131] with additional mixing means other than injector mixers, e.g. screens, baffles or rotating elements -- The subclass B01F 25/3131 covers mixers that use additional mixing devices other than injector mixers, such as screens, baffles or rotating elements, to improve dissolution, emulsifying or dispersing actions. It applies to equipment where these auxiliary elements create extra turbulence or shear during the mixing operation.
[B01F 25/3132] by using two or more injector devices -- The subclass B01F 25/3132 covers mixing or dispersing operations in which two or more injector devices are used simultaneously to introduce liquids, gases or solids into a vessel or stream, facilitating dissolution, emulsification or dispersion of the components. This approach is applied in processes that require rapid or uniform distribution of additives during separation or blending steps.
[B01F 25/31321] used alternatively -- B01F 25/31321 covers mixers in which the mixing elements are employed alternately rather than simultaneously, such as devices that switch between different agitation mechanisms during operation. This subclass falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, separating, and mixing processes.
[B01F 25/31322] used simultaneously -- B01F 25/31322 refers to mixers where the mixing elements are used simultaneously, indicating a process or device that employs multiple mixing actions at the same time to achieve efficient blending or dispersion.
[B01F 25/31323] used successively -- B01F 25/31323 refers to mixers that are employed successively in a process, where each mixing step follows another to achieve functions such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. The classification falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and mixing.
[B01F 25/31324] arranged concentrically -- The entry B01F 25/31324 defines mixers whose mixing elements are positioned concentrically, with one or more rotating or stationary parts placed inside one another to create radial flow. This arrangement promotes efficient dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing of materials by generating shear forces throughout the volume of the substance. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and mixing.
[B01F 25/3133] characterised by the specific design of the injector -- B01F 25/3133 is a CPC subclass for mixers that incorporate a specifically designed injector. The injector's geometry or operation is intended to enhance the efficiency of mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing substances during processing. This subclass is applied to devices where the injector design is a defining technical feature.
[B01F 25/31331] Perforated, multi-opening, with a plurality of holes -- The CPC subclass B01F 25/31331 covers mixers that incorporate a perforated, multi-opening design featuring a plurality of holes to promote fluid flow and enhance mixing or dispersion. Such devices may be used in processes like dissolving, emulsifying or separating particulate phases.
[B01F 25/313311] Porous injectors -- Porous injectors are devices that introduce liquids or gases into a material through a fine-porous structure to create rapid mixing, dissolution, emulsification or dispersion. By forcing the fluid through the pores, they generate fine droplets or bubbles that improve mass transfer and uniformity within the process stream, and are commonly incorporated into mixer systems for efficient blending of immiscible phases.
[B01F 25/31332] Ring, torus, toroidal or coiled configurations -- The B01F 25/31332 subclass covers mixers that employ a ring, torus, toroidal or coiled geometry to achieve mixing, dispersion or dissolution. Such devices use the continuous curved pathway to create shear and turbulence, enhancing the distribution of solids or liquids within the material being processed.
[B01F 25/31333] Rotatable injectors -- B01F 25/31333 covers rotatable injectors that introduce liquids or gases into a mixing-zone to achieve dissolution, emulsification or dispersion. The devices are classified as mixers within the separating subgroup of the Performing Operations section of the CPC.
[B01F 25/31334] the opening for introducing the supplementary stream being a slit -- The entry covers a slit-type opening used to introduce an auxiliary fluid stream into a mixer, allowing precise feeding of a supplementary stream during mixing operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. This feature is part of devices classified under mixers within the broader categories of separating and performing operations.
[B01F 25/314] wherein additional components are introduced at the circumference of the conduit -- The B01F 25/314 subclass covers mixing processes in which one or more auxiliary substances are fed into the flow channel from the outer perimeter of the conduit, rather than centrally. This arrangement enables continuous introduction of additives while the primary material is being mixed, improving dispersion, dissolution or emulsification along the pipe wall. It is used in mixers where precise side-wall feeding of reagents is required.
[B01F 25/3141] with additional mixing means other than injector mixers -- B01F 25/3141 covers mixers that incorporate additional mixing devices apart from injector mixers, such as agitators, stators or baffles, to improve the dispersion, dissolution or emulsification of substances during a separating operation. The subclass specifies the use of these auxiliary mixing elements to enhance the primary mixing action.
[B01F 25/3142] the conduit having a plurality of openings in the axial direction or in the circumferential direction -- B01F 25/3142 pertains to mixers where the mixing conduit features multiple openings along its length or around its circumference, enhancing fluid interaction and dispersion.
[B01F 25/31421] the conduit being porous -- The B01F 25/31421 entry covers mixers in which the conduit-such as a pipe, tube or channel-is made porous to permit fluid to pass through the walls during mixing, separating, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing operations. This porous conduit enables intimate contact between phases and can improve mass transfer or removal of solids while the material is being mixed.
[B01F 25/31422] with a plurality of perforations in the axial direction only -- B01F 25/31422 designates mixers that incorporate a series of holes arranged only along the axial direction of the mixing device. The axial perforations allow fluid to pass through the impeller or shaft, enhancing dissolution, emulsification or dispersion while maintaining structural integrity. This subclass falls within the CPC hierarchy of mixing operations under separating and performing operations.
[B01F 25/31423] with a plurality of perforations in the circumferential direction only and covering the whole circumference -- This CPC subgroup relates to mixers that feature a series of perforations running solely in the circumferential direction and extending around the entire circumference of the rotor or sleeve, enhancing fluid flow for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 25/314231] the perforations being a complete cut-out in the circumferential direction covering the whole diameter of the tube, i.e. having two consecutive tubes placed consecutively, the additional component being introduced between them -- This CPC subgroup deals with mixers where the tube or pipe has a full-circumferential perforation that completely cuts the tube wall, creating two adjoining tube sections. The design permits an extra component or material to be placed in the gap between the sections while the tubes remain aligned for continuous operation. It is used in processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing where precise introduction of additives is required.
[B01F 25/31424] with a plurality of perforations aligned in a row perpendicular to the flow direction -- This subclass defines mixers that incorporate a series of perforations arranged in a single row that is oriented perpendicular to the direction of fluid flow, the aligned holes facilitating enhanced mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing actions within the stream.
[B01F 25/31425] with a plurality of perforations in the axial and circumferential direction covering the whole surface -- A mixer whose working surface is pierced with many holes arranged both axially and circumferentially across the entire area, allowing fluid to pass through the rotor or stator during operation. This perforated design enhances turbulence and promotes more efficient dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing of materials.
[B01F 25/3143] characterised by the specific design of the injector -- B01F 25/3143 covers mixers that are distinguished by a specially designed injector, intended to enhance the introduction and distribution of fluids or gases into a medium for dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. The injector's shape, size or flow channel is optimized to promote efficient mixing and separation within the device.
[B01F 25/31431] being a slit extending in the longitudinal direction only -- The code B01F 25/31431 refers to mixers in which the mixing element includes a slit that runs only along the length of the device, providing longitudinal flow or displacement without transverse interruptions. This design is used to achieve gentle, continuous mixing such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing in processes where minimal shear is desired.
[B01F 25/31432] being a slit extending in the circumferential direction only -- This CPC subclass (B01F 25/31432) identifies mixers that feature a slit extending only in the circumferential direction of a rotating element, allowing material to flow around the shaft and enhance mixing efficiency without a radial opening.
[B01F 25/31433] being rotatable, e.g. placed on a rotatable housing or conduit -- The CPC subclass B01F 25/31433 covers mixers in which the mixing element or its housing is rotatable, for example mounted on a rotating barrel or conduit, enabling continuous agitation or dispersion of the material as the entire assembly turns.
[B01F 25/31434] being a bundle of similar tubes, each of them having feedings on the circumferential wall, e.g. as mixer for a reactor -- The CPC entry B01F 25/31434 defines a mixer made of a bundle of identical tubes, each tube having feed openings on its circumferential wall so that fluid is introduced along the tube length to promote dispersion and mixing within a reactor. It falls under the performing-operations class, specifically separating and mixing processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 25/315] wherein a difference of pressure at different points of the conduit causes introduction of the additional component into the main component -- B01F 25/315 pertains to mixers where the introduction of an additional component into the main flow is driven by a pressure differential within the conduit. This method facilitates the mixing process without external mechanical agitation, relying instead on the fluid dynamics created by varying pressures.
[B01F 25/316] with containers for additional components fixed to the conduit -- This CPC subclass covers mixers in which containers holding additional components are permanently fixed to the mixing conduit, enabling substances to be introduced directly into the flow during operation.
[B01F 25/32] wherein the additional components are added in a by-pass of the main flow -- This subclass of the CPC covers mixing operations in which supplemental ingredients are introduced through a side-stream that bypasses the main material flow, allowing continuous processing without disturbing the primary stream. It is used in devices such as mixers and reactors where the by-pass addition improves control of dosage, dispersion or reaction conditions.
[B01F 25/40] Static mixers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mixers for the continuous mixing of material without moving components and is either by a change of direction of the flow or by splitting and recombining of the stream.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Crushing, pulverizing or disintegrating, e.g. colloid mills -> B02C - Multiple-way valves, e.g. mixing valves; Pipe fittings incorporating such valves; Arrangement of valves and flow lines specially adapted for mixing fluid -> F16K11/00
[B01F 25/41] Mixers of the fractal type -- B01F 25/41 covers mixers that employ fractal-shaped elements to create complex flow patterns and enhance the contact between substances during mixing operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. The fractal geometry increases surface area and induces turbulence at multiple scales, improving efficiency in processes that require thorough homogenisation.
[B01F 25/42] in which the mixing is affected by moving the components jointly in changing directions, e.g. in tubes provided with baffles or obstructions -- B01F 25/42 covers mixing processes in which the components are moved jointly while their direction of travel is altered, such as in tubular mixers that contain baffles, obstacles or other internal structures to continually change flow paths and promote dissolution, emulsification or dispersion.
[B01F 25/421] by moving the components in a convoluted or labyrinthine path -- The subclass B01F 25/421 covers mixers that achieve mixing by directing the material through a convoluted or labyrinthine flow path, causing repeated changes in direction and turbulence. This approach enhances dissolution, emulsifying or dispersing by continuously re-orienting the components as they travel within the apparatus.
[B01F 25/422] between stacked plates, e.g. grooved or perforated plates -- B01F 25/422 covers mixers that achieve blending by passing material between stacked plates, often grooved or perforated, to promote separation, dissolution, emulsification or dispersion. The design creates shear and turbulence as the feed moves through the plate gaps, enabling efficient mixing in batch or continuous processes.
[B01F 25/423] by means of elements placed in the receptacle for moving or guiding the components -- B01F 25/423 covers mixing or dispersing apparatus that incorporates movable or guiding elements placed directly inside the receptacle, such as paddles, pins, blades, beads or other internal fixtures, to agitate, direct or subdivide the components during processes like dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 25/4231] using baffles -- B01F 25/4231 relates to mixing operations that employ baffles to intensify agitation, disrupt flow and improve the dispersion, dissolution or emulsification of substances. The presence of baffles-internal or external obstructions-enhances the efficiency of mixers used for separating and homogenising material blends.
[B01F 25/4232] using dams -- The CPC subclass B01F 25/4232 covers mixers that employ a dam-an internal barrier or baffle-to enhance mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing actions during separating operations.
[B01F 25/4233] using plates with holes, the holes being displaced from one plate to the next one to force the flow to make a bending movement -- This subclass covers mixing devices that use a stack of plates with holes whose positions are offset from one plate to the next, causing the material to follow a curved path and generate intense turbulence for dissolution, emulsification or dispersion. The displaced holes force the flow to make a bending movement, improving mixing efficiency.
[B01F 25/43] Mixing tubes, e.g. wherein the material is moved in a radial or partly reversed direction -- B01F 25/43 covers mixing tubes in which the material flows radially outward or partially reverses direction to enhance blending, such as in dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing processes. These devices use a tubular geometry to create shear and circulation, improving homogeneity of liquids, suspensions or pastes.
[B01F 25/431] Straight mixing tubes with baffles or obstructions that do not cause substantial pressure drop -- B01F 25/431 refers to straight mixing tubes that contain baffles or other internal obstructions designed to promote mixing such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing while maintaining a low pressure drop along the flow path. These devices are typically used in process streams where efficient mixing is needed without significant energy loss.
[B01F 25/4311] the baffles being adjustable -- B01F 25/4311 covers mixers whose internal baffles can be repositioned or rotated to alter flow patterns and enhance separating, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing actions. Adjustable baffles let operators fine-tune turbulence and residence time to suit different batch processes.
[B01F 25/4312] having different kinds of baffles, e.g. plates alternating with screens -- The entry B01F 25/4312 classifies mixers that incorporate different kinds of baffles, such as plates alternating with screens, to enhance the agitation and dispersion of materials during processes like dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and mixing within the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[B01F 25/4313] comprising a plurality of stacked ducts having their axes parallel to the tube axis -- B01F 25/4313 covers mixing devices in which several ducts are stacked side-by-side inside a tube so that each duct runs parallel to the tube's longitudinal axis. The arrangement creates flow paths that promote dispersion, dissolution, or emulsification of materials as they pass through the stacked ducts.
[B01F 25/4314] with helical baffles -- This subclass deals with mixers that employ helical baffles to generate a swirling flow and enhance the mixing action, often used for dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing substances. The helical baffles create a helical motion that improves energy efficiency and uniformity of the blend.
[B01F 25/43141] composed of consecutive sections of helical formed elements -- The CPC entry B01F 25/43141 relates to mixers built from a series of adjoining helical-shaped sections that form the mixing element. Such devices are used in operations that involve separating by mixing, including dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing substances.
[B01F 25/4315] the baffles being deformed flat pieces of material  B01F25/4314  takes precedence -- This subclass relates to mixing devices that employ baffles formed from deformed flat pieces of material, distinguishing them from earlier baffle designs covered by B01F25/4314. It falls under the CPC group for mixers used in separating and dispersing operations. The classification specifies the geometry and deformation of the baffles as the defining feature.
[B01F 25/43151] composed of consecutive sections of deformed flat pieces of material -- The B01F 25/43151 mixer is built from a series of adjacent, deformed flat pieces of material that together form the mixing element. These consecutive sections create a textured surface that agitates, blends or disperses the medium as it passes through, improving mixing efficiency in batch or continuous operations.
[B01F 25/4316] the baffles being flat pieces of material, e.g. intermeshing, fixed to the wall or fixed on a central rod -- The CPC entry B01F 25/4316 covers mixers that employ flat baffles, either intermeshing or mounted on a central rod or the vessel wall, to create turbulent flow and improve mixing efficiency. These baffles are fixed structures that enhance dispersion, dissolution or emulsification in the mixing process.
[B01F 25/43161] composed of consecutive sections of flat pieces of material -- This CPC entry B01F 25/43161 falls under mixers in the performing operations > separating > mixing group and defines mixing devices that consist of a series of flat material sections placed consecutively, forming a channel or chamber through which the material to be mixed is conveyed and agitated.
[B01F 25/43162] Assembled flat elements -- B01F 25/43162 refers to mixers that use assembled flat elements such as plates, paddles or sheets arranged to generate shear or laminar flow for separating, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing substances, enhancing mixing efficiency through the interaction of these flat components.
[B01F 25/43163] in the form of small flat plate-like elements -- This CPC subclass covers mixers that use small, flat, plate-like elements to perform mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing operations. The thin plates are arranged to generate shear and turbulence, enabling efficient separation and blending of materials in chemical or mechanical processes.
[B01F 25/4317] Profiled elements, e.g. profiled blades, bars, pillars, columns or chevrons -- B01F 25/4317 classifies mixers that employ profiled elements such as specially shaped blades, bars, pillars, columns or chevron forms to create the shear and flow patterns needed for separating operations like mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. The profile of these components is designed to enhance fluid movement and improve mixing efficiency.
[B01F 25/43171] Profiled blades, wings, wedges, i.e. plate-like element having one side or part thicker than the other -- The code B01F 25/43171 refers to plate-like mixing elements whose cross-section is asymmetrical, such as profiled blades, wings or wedges that have one side or part thicker than the other. These shaped components are used in mixers to improve dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing actions by creating varied flow patterns and shear forces within the material being processed.
[B01F 25/43172] Profiles, pillars, chevrons, i.e. long elements having a polygonal cross-section -- B01F 25/43172 pertains to specific internal structures within mixers, such as profiles, pillars, and chevrons, characterized by their long, polygonal cross-sections designed to enhance mixing efficiency.
[B01F 25/4318] Ring-shaped blades or strips -- B01F 25/4318 designates mixing devices that employ ring-shaped blades or strips as the agitators. These elements are arranged to create rotational shear or turbulence, aiding in separating, dissolving, emulsifying or otherwise dispersing materials within a vessel. The code falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and mixing.
[B01F 25/4319] Tubular elements -- B01F 25/4319 concerns tubular elements employed in mixers for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing substances. It includes tubes, pipes or similar hollow components that convey, agitate or provide heat transfer within mixing equipment.
[B01F 25/43195] Wires or coils -- B01F 25/43195 covers mixers that use wires or coiled elements as the agitation means, typically employed to dissolve, emulsify or disperse substances within a liquid medium.
[B01F 25/431951] Spirally-shaped baffle -- The CPC subclass B01F 25/431951 designates spirally-shaped baffles incorporated in mixing devices that aid separation, dissolution, emulsification or dispersion of materials. These baffles create a helical flow pattern within the mixer, promoting efficient mixing and enhancing the interaction of solid, liquid or gaseous phases. They are used in a variety of industrial processes where controlled agitation and phase contact are required.
[B01F 25/431952] Conical or pyramidal elements -- The code B01F 25/431952 refers to mixers that employ conical or pyramidal elements to enhance mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing of materials. These shaped components create localized shear and promote material flow within the mixing chamber, improving homogeneity in batch or continuous processes.
[B01F 25/43197] characterised by the mounting of the baffles or obstructions -- This subclass concerns mixers in which the design is defined by how internal baffles or other flow-obstructing elements are mounted, specifying their positions, attachment methods and orientation within the mixing chamber to influence flow patterns and mixing efficiency.
[B01F 25/431971] Mounted on the wall -- A wall-mounted mixer designed for separating, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing materials, where the mixing unit is fixed to a vertical surface to save floor space and provide stable operation. This arrangement is used in processes that require continuous or batch mixing while keeping the equipment compact and accessible.
