[B01F 25/431972] Mounted on an axial support member, e.g. a rod or bar -- The subclass B01F 25/431972 covers mixers that are attached to an axial support member such as a rod or bar, enabling the mixing device to be positioned or rotated along the support. This arrangement is used for operations like dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing within the broader category of separating and performing mixing processes.
[B01F 25/431973] Mounted on a support member extending transversally through the mixing tube -- The CPC subclass B01F 25/431973 covers mixers in which the mixing element is mounted on a support that extends transversely through the mixing tube. The support member spans across the tube to hold the mixing device in place, enabling dissolution, emulsification or dispersion of materials within the tube.
[B01F 25/431974] Support members, e.g. tubular collars, with projecting baffles fitted inside the mixing tube or adjacent to the inner wall -- The entry refers to mixing apparatus that incorporates support members such as tubular collars fitted with projecting baffles inside the mixing tube or against its inner wall. These baffles act as internal obstacles to improve agitation, promote dissolution or dispersion, and provide structural support during the mixing operation.
[B01F 25/432] with means for dividing the material flow into separate sub-flows and for repositioning and recombining these sub-flows -- B01F 25/432 covers mixing devices that include mechanisms to split the material flow into separate sub-flows, reposition or treat those streams, and then recombine them, allowing precise control of dissolution, emulsification or dispersion. Such equipment is used where handling distinct phases before merging improves product uniformity.
[B01F 25/4321] the subflows consisting of at least two flat layers which are recombined, e.g. using means having restriction or expansion zones -- The entry B01F 25/4321 covers mixers that form at least two flat sub-layers of material and then recombine them, using zones that restrict or expand the flow to enhance mixing. This sub-flow recombination technique is applied in separating and dissolving operations.
[B01F 25/43211] using a simple by-pass for separating and recombining the flow, e.g. by using branches of different length -- The entry describes a technique in which a fluid stream is diverted through a simple bypass and later recombined, using branches of different length to achieve separation and recombination within a mixer. This method controls residence time or splits and merges streams without requiring complex equipment.
[B01F 25/4322] essentially composed of stacks of sheets, e.g. corrugated sheets -- B01F 25/4322 covers mixing devices whose active zone is formed by a stack of thin sheets, such as corrugated plates, that create a series of channels or cavities through which material passes. This arrangement uses the sheet geometry to induce turbulence, shear and dispersion, enabling operations like dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing while keeping the overall construction simple and compact.
[B01F 25/4323] using elements provided with a plurality of channels or using a plurality of tubes which can either be placed between common spaces or collectors -- Subclass B01F 25/4323 relates to mixers that achieve mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing by using elements that contain a plurality of parallel channels or a bundle of tubes positioned between common spaces or collector zones, directing fluid flow through these passages to promote intimate contact of components.
[B01F 25/43231] the channels or tubes crossing each other several times -- This CPC subgroup covers mixers in which the fluid passages or tubes are arranged to intersect repeatedly, creating multiple crossing points that promote intensive mixing, dissolution, emulsification or dispersion of components. The intersecting channels increase shear and turbulence, improving homogeneity in processes such as chemical, pharmaceutical or food production. The classification is used for devices where the crossing geometry is a key mixing principle.
[B01F 25/433] Mixing tubes wherein the shape of the tube influences the mixing, e.g. mixing tubes with varying cross-section or provided with inwardly extending profiles -- B01F 25/433 covers mixing tubes whose geometry is designed to enhance mixing, the tube may have a varying cross-section or inwardly extending profiles that promote dissolution, emulsification or dispersion of substances.
[B01F 25/4331] Mixers with bended, curved, coiled, wounded mixing tubes or comprising elements for bending the flow -- This subclass covers mixers that employ bent, curved, coiled or wound mixing tubes, or that include elements specifically designed to bend the flow of material. The geometry creates additional turbulence and residence time, improving operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 25/4332] Mixers with a strong change of direction in the conduit for homogenizing the flow -- This subclass covers devices that use abrupt bends, elbows or loops in the pipe to create turbulent flow and improve mixing of liquids or suspensions. The strong directional change forces the stream to split and recombine, promoting homogenization without moving parts. It is employed in processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 25/4333] Mixers with scallop-shaped tubes or surfaces facing each other -- B01F 25/4333 covers mixers that employ scallop-shaped tubes or opposite facing surfaces to create turbulent flow for separating and mixing actions such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing materials. The design uses the scalloped geometry to enhance shear and improve homogeneity in liquid or slurry processing.
[B01F 25/4334] Mixers with a converging cross-section -- Mixers whose internal passage narrows along the flow direction, creating a converging cross-section that accelerates the material and enhances shear forces for efficient mixing, blending, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing of liquids, pastes or powders. This design falls under the B01F 25/4334 subgroup of mixing equipment.
[B01F 25/4335] Mixers with a converging-diverging cross-section -- Mixers with a converging-diverging cross-section (CPC B01F 25/4335) are devices in which the fluid stream passes through a passage that first narrows and then expands. The change in cross-section creates acceleration, turbulence and shear that intensify mixing, dissolution, emulsification or dispersion of phases within the conduit. This design is classified under performing operations, separating, mixing.
[B01F 25/4336] Mixers with a diverging cross-section -- B01F 25/4336 covers mixers whose internal passage or chamber widens along its length, creating a diverging cross-section that promotes fluid flow expansion and enhanced mixing. The design typically reduces velocity, encourages turbulence and improves the dispersion of components, useful in processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing liquids and solid particles. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of performing operations, separating and mixing.
[B01F 25/4337] Mixers with a diverging-converging cross-section -- B01F 25/4337 covers mixers whose working chamber has a cross-section that first diverges and then converges, creating a flow that accelerates and decelerates to improve dispersion of liquids or solids. Such geometry is employed in chemical, pharmaceutical and food processes to achieve efficient dissolution, emulsification or particle-size reduction.
[B01F 25/4338] Mixers with a succession of converging-diverging cross-sections, i.e. undulating cross-section -- Mixers whose conduit or chamber walls feature a series of alternating converging and diverging cross-sections, creating an undulating flow path that promotes shear, turbulence and rapid dispersion of fluids or particles. The geometry repeatedly squeezes and expands the material, enhancing mixing efficiency in processes such as dissolution, emulsification or dispersion.
[B01F 25/434] Mixing tubes comprising cylindrical or conical inserts provided with grooves or protrusions -- B01F 25/434 covers mixing tubes that contain cylindrical or conical inserts which are equipped with grooves, ribs or other protrusions to enhance agitation and improve the dispersion, dissolution or emulsification of materials flowing through the tube. The design creates turbulence and promotes more efficient mixing of liquids or suspensions within the tube.
[B01F 25/4341] the insert being provided with helical grooves -- A B01F 25/4341 mixer design uses an insert that carries helical grooves to create a swirling flow and improve the dispersion of liquids or solids. The helical geometry promotes axial and radial movement, enhancing dissolution, emulsifying or other mixing actions in processing equipment.
[B01F 25/4342] the insert being provided with a labyrinth of grooves or a distribution of protrusions -- The B01F 25/4342 entry covers mixer inserts that are engineered with a network of grooves or a pattern of protrusions, forming a labyrinth-like surface to enhance fluid flow, improve dispersion and promote more uniform mixing within the vessel.
[B01F 25/435] Mixing tubes composed of concentric tubular members -- B01F 25/435 covers mixing tubes formed by at least two tubular members arranged concentrically, where the inner and outer tubes create a flow path that promotes agitation, dissolution, emulsification or dispersion of material passing through. The design enables efficient mixing by forcing fluids to circulate between the tubes.
[B01F 25/438] with movable slits formed between reciprocating surfaces -- The class B01F 25/438 covers mixers that create mixing action by means of movable slits formed between surfaces that reciprocate relative to each other. The slits open and close as the surfaces move, forcing material through the gaps to achieve dispersion, dissolution or emulsification.
[B01F 25/44] Mixers in which the components are pressed through slits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mixers in which the pressing of the material through the orifices or interstitial spaces causes shear forces to mix the components.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[B01F 25/441] characterised by the configuration of the surfaces forming the slits -- B01F 25/441 covers mixers in which the mixing action is defined by the shape and arrangement of the surfaces that form the slits through which material passes. The design of these slit surfaces influences the degree of separation, dissolution, emulsification or dispersion achieved during operation.
[B01F 25/4412] the slits being formed between opposed planar surfaces, e.g. pushed again each other by springs -- This subclass covers mixers that form slits between opposing planar surfaces, usually by pressing them together with springs, to promote mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing substances.
[B01F 25/44121] with a plurality of parallel slits, e.g. formed between stacked plates -- B01F 25/44121 covers mixers that incorporate a series of parallel slits, typically formed by arranging stacked plates, to promote intensive mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing of materials. The design creates fine shear zones that enhance separation and homogeneity.
[B01F 25/4413] the slits being formed between opposed conical or cylindrical surfaces -- B01F 25/4413 relates to mixers that form slits between opposed conical or cylindrical surfaces to improve the mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing of substances.
[B01F 25/4414] the slits being formed between the balls and the seats of a bearing-like construction -- B01F 25/4414 covers mixing devices that create slits between spherical elements and their bearing seats to generate shear and turbulence, enhancing dissolution, emulsification or dispersion of materials. The design uses bearing-like constructions where the gaps act as flow channels for efficient mixing.
[B01F 25/4415] the slits being formed between the helical windings of a spring-like construction or by deforming a spring -- B01F 25/4415 covers mixers that create slits between the helical windings of a spring-like structure or that form slits by deforming a spring, using these openings to improve the shearing, dispersing or emulsifying action during mixing operations. This technique falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating and mixing.
[B01F 25/4416] the opposed surfaces being provided with grooves -- B01F 25/4416 covers mixers in which the confronting surfaces are provided with grooves that create localized turbulence and shear, aiding processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing substances. The grooved geometry increases contact area and promotes efficient mixing in batch or continuous equipment.
[B01F 25/44161] Axial grooves formed on opposed surfaces, e.g. on cylinders or cones -- The CPC subclass B01F 25/44161 relates to mixers that have axial grooves on opposed surfaces such as cylinders or cones. The grooves generate helical flow and shear that improve dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing actions. It falls under performing operations → separating → mixing → mixers.
[B01F 25/44162] Circumferential grooves formed on opposed surfaces, e.g. on planar surfaces or on cylinders or cones -- This CPC subclass covers mixers that employ circumferential grooves on opposing surfaces, such as planar, cylindrical or conical elements, to create shear and turbulence that improve fluid motion. The grooved geometry enhances contact between phases, aiding separating and mixing operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 25/44163] Helical grooves formed on opposed surfaces, e.g. on cylinders or cones -- It covers mixers that employ helical grooves on opposing surfaces such as cylinders or cones to generate shear flow and promote mixing, dissolution, emulsifying or dispersing of materials. The grooved geometry creates continuous relative motion and enhances fluid displacement, improving separation and homogenisation of components.
[B01F 25/44164] Crossing sets of grooves forming a labyrinth formed on opposed surfaces, e.g. on planar surfaces or on cylinders or cones -- The CPC subclass B01F 25/44164 refers to mixers that use intersecting grooved patterns on opposing planar or cylindrical surfaces to form a labyrinth channel. The labyrinth forces fluid to follow a tortuous path, improving mixing, dissolution, emulsifying or dispersing actions. It is classified under performing operations, separating and mixing.
[B01F 25/44165] Radial grooves formed on opposed surfaces, e.g. on planar surfaces -- The subclass B01F 25/44165 covers mixers whose opposing surfaces are provided with radial grooves, such as grooves machined or stamped into planar plates that face each other. These grooves create turbulent flow patterns that enhance separation, dissolution, emulsification or dispersion of materials during mixing operations.
[B01F 25/44166] Spiral grooves formed on opposed surfaces, e.g. on planar surfaces -- The B01F 25/44166 subclass covers mixers that create spiral grooves on facing surfaces, such as flat plates, to promote fluid movement and enhance separating or mixing actions like dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. The grooves generate a helical flow pattern that improves the efficiency of material processing.
[B01F 25/44167] the grooves being formed on the outer surface of the cylindrical or conical core of the slits -- This subclass relates to mixers that incorporate a cylindrical or conical core with slits whose outer surface is provided with grooves. The grooved geometry creates additional turbulence and shear, improving the dispersion, dissolution or emulsification of materials during the mixing operation. It falls under the CPC group B01F 25/44 for mixers with specially shaped cores.
[B01F 25/44168] the grooves being formed on the inner surface of the cylindrical or conical housing of the slits -- The entry B01F 25/44168 covers mixers that create grooves on the inner surface of a cylindrical or conical housing, forming slits that generate turbulence and improve the dispersion, dissolution or emulsification of materials during mixing operations.
[B01F 25/442] characterised by the relative position of the surfaces during operation -- B01F 25/442 refers to mixers whose design is defined by the relative position of the interacting surfaces during operation, such as coaxial, concentric or intersecting arrangements. The classification captures devices where the motion of one surface relative to another is a key feature for achieving dissolution, emulsification or dispersion. It lies within the broader group of separating operations in the CPC.
[B01F 25/4421] the surfaces being maintained in a fixed position, spaced from each other, therefore maintaining the slit always open -- This entry covers mixers that use stationary surfaces positioned at a fixed distance so the slit between them stays continuously open, allowing material to flow through while being sheared or dispersed. It falls under the sub-category of static mixing devices that maintain an open channel for effective mixing.
[B01F 25/4422] the surfaces being maintained in a fixed but adjustable position, spaced from each other, therefore allowing the slit spacing to be varied  B01F25/4423  takes precedence -- B01F 25/4422 covers mixers in which the working surfaces are held in a fixed yet adjustable arrangement, allowing the distance between them - the slit spacing - to be altered during operation. This feature enables control of flow or particle distribution while retaining structural stability. The more specific subclass B01F 25/4423 supersedes this entry.
[B01F 25/4423] the surfaces being part of a valve construction, formed by opposed members in contact, e.g. automatic positioning caused by spring pressure -- This subclass deals with mixers whose mixing surfaces also serve as a valve, formed by two opposing members that meet in contact. The valve action is achieved by automatic positioning of the surfaces, often using spring pressure to open or close the flow path during operation. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of performing operations, separating, and mixing such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 25/45] Mixers in which the materials to be mixed are pressed together through orifices or interstitial spaces, e.g. between beads -- B01F 25/45 classifies mixers that achieve mixing by pressing the materials together as they pass through orifices or interstitial spaces, for example between beads, using pressure to create the desired dispersion. This subclass falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and mixing (including dissolving, emulsifying, or dispersing).
[B01F 25/451] characterised by means for moving the materials to be mixed or the mixture -- The CPC subclass B01F 25/451 covers mixers that incorporate means for moving the material to be mixed or the mixture itself. It is situated under performing operations, separating and mixing such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing, and is applied to devices where agitation is achieved by internal transport or conveyance of the contents.
[B01F 25/4511] with a rotor surrounded by a stator provided with orifices -- This CPC subclass covers mixers that have a rotor enclosed by a stator equipped with openings, allowing material to flow through the stationary housing as it is agitated. The configuration creates high-shear conditions that enhance dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing of liquids and fine particles.
[B01F 25/4512] with reciprocating pistons -- The CPC subclass B01F 25/4512 covers mixing devices that employ reciprocating pistons to agitate materials, such as for dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing substances. These mixers use back-and-forth piston motion to generate shear and turbulence, enabling efficient blending of liquids, pastes or powders.
[B01F 25/452] characterised by elements provided with orifices or interstitial spaces -- B01F 25/452 covers mixers in which the mixing elements are provided with openings, perforations or interstitial spaces that allow material to pass through, be redirected and repeatedly contacted for improved dispersion. These perforated plates, screens, grids or similar members may be stationary or movable and create turbulence, shear or flow disruption in liquids, powders or suspensions.
[B01F 25/4521] the components being pressed through orifices in elements, e.g. flat plates or cylinders, which obstruct the whole diameter of the tube -- This CPC subclass B01F 25/4521 covers mixers in which the material to be processed is forced through apertures that span the full cross-section of a flat plate, cylinder or similar element, thereby creating shear and turbulence for separating, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing the components.
[B01F 25/45211] the elements being cylinders or cones which obstruct the whole diameter of the tube, the flow changing from axial in radial and again in axial -- This sub-group covers mixing devices whose internal elements are full-diameter cylinders or cones that block the tube's cross-section, forcing the material to change from axial flow to radial flow and back to axial, thereby creating vigorous mixing through repeated flow reorientation.
[B01F 25/45212] the elements comprising means for adjusting the orifices -- This subclass deals with the individual parts that make up adjustment mechanisms for the openings in mixing equipment, such as adjustable plates, valves, inserts or shims that regulate flow through orifices. It applies to mixers used in dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing processes where precise control of the orifice size is required.
[B01F 25/4522] the components being pressed through porous bodies, e.g. flat plates, blocks or cylinders, which obstruct the whole diameter of the tube  B01F25/45243  takes precedence -- B01F 25/4522 covers mixers in which the material to be mixed is forced through a porous element such as a flat plate, block or cylinder that blocks the full tube diameter, aiding dissolution, emulsification or dispersion. This sub-group lies under Performing operations - Separating - Mixing (e.g., dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing) - Mixers. The preceding group B01F 25/45243 takes precedence.
[B01F 25/45221] the porous bodies being cylinders or cones which obstruct the whole diameter of the tube, the flow changing from axial in radial and again in axial -- B01F 25/45221 covers mixers that use porous cylindrical or conical bodies filling the whole tube diameter, forcing the fluid to flow axially, then radially outward through the pores, and back to axial flow, thereby achieving mixing, dissolution, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 25/4523] the components being pressed through sieves, screens or meshes which obstruct the whole diameter of the tube -- The CPC entry B01F 25/4523 falls under performing operations → separating → mixing, mixers, and covers the situation where material is forced through sieves, screens or meshes that completely block the tube's diameter, describing the components that are pressed through these full-diameter obstructions.
[B01F 25/45231] the sieves, screens or meshes being cylinders or cones which obstruct the whole diameter of the tube, the flow changing from axial in radial and again in axial -- This subclass pertains to cylindrical or conical sieves, screens or meshes that occupy the full diameter of a mixing tube, forcing the material to flow axially, then radially, and back to axial as it is processed.
[B01F 25/4524] the components being pressed through foam-like inserts or through a bed of loose bodies, e.g. balls -- B01F 25/4524 covers mixing operations in which the material to be combined is forced through a foam-like insert or a packed bed of loose media such as balls, using the pressure of the feed to achieve dispersion, dissolution or emulsification. This sub-category falls under mixers within the broader groups of separating and performing operations.
[B01F 25/45241] through a bed of balls -- This subclass covers mixers that achieve mixing by forcing material to flow through a packed bed of spherical particles, using the interstitial gaps to create turbulence and shear. It applies to processes such as dissolution, emulsification, dispersion or fine grinding where the ball bed serves as a dynamic mixing medium.
[B01F 25/45242] through a bed of fibres, steel wool or wood chips -- The entry defines mixing or dispersing operations in which the material is forced through a packed bed of fibres, steel wool or wood chips, using the bed as a mechanical agitation medium to promote dissolution, emulsification or dispersion. Such a bed provides high surface area and turbulence, enabling efficient contact between phases in processes such as liquid-solid blending or catalytic treatment.
[B01F 25/45243] through a foam or expanded material body -- B01F 25/45243 covers mixers that effect separation or mixing by passing the material through a foam or other expanded medium, using the porous structure of the foam to disperse, dissolve, emulsify or otherwise treat the feed.
[B01F 25/46] Homogenising or emulsifying nozzles -- B01F 25/46 covers nozzles designed to produce fine drops or streams that create intense shear, facilitating homogenisation or emulsification during mixing operations. These devices are used to break up immiscible phases into uniform dispersions, often in food, pharmaceutical or chemical processing. They are a specialized type of mixer within the separating and mixing hierarchy.
[B01F 25/50] Circulation mixers, e.g. wherein at least part of the mixture is discharged from and reintroduced into a receptacle -- The CPC subclass B01F 25/50 covers circulation mixers used in separating and mixing operations, where at least part of the material is withdrawn from a vessel and fed back in. This continuous discharge-and-re-introduction enables actions such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing within the same receptacle.
[B01F 25/51] in which the mixture is circulated through a set of tubes, e.g. with gradual introduction of a component into the circulating flow -- B01F 25/51 pertains to mixing processes where the material is continuously circulated through a network of tubes, enabling a component to be introduced gradually into the flowing stream. This tube-circulation method facilitates dissolution, emulsification or dispersion while maintaining a uniform mixture.
[B01F 25/52] with a rotary stirrer in the recirculation tube -- B01F 25/52 covers mixing apparatus that incorporate a rotary stirrer positioned inside a recirculation tube, enabling continuous circulation of material for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. The design promotes uniform mixing by forcing the fluid through the tube where rotation provides agitation. This subclass falls under the broader category of separating and mixing processes in the CPC.
[B01F 25/53] in which the mixture is discharged from and reintroduced into a receptacle through a recirculation tube, into which an additional component is introduced -- This subclass covers mixers that discharge the material from a vessel, pass it through a recirculation tube where an additional ingredient is added, and then return the mixture to the same receptacle, enabling continuous blending, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing of the combined stream.
[B01F 25/54] provided with a pump inside the receptacle to recirculate the material within the receptacle -- This subclass covers mixers that incorporate an internal pump to draw material from the vessel and return it, enabling continuous recirculation for improved mixing, dissolution, emulsification, or dispersion. It pertains to equipment where the pump is located inside the mixing receptacle.
[B01F 25/60] Pump mixers, i.e. mixing within a pump -- The CPC subclass B01F 25/60 covers devices that perform mixing inside a pump, where the pump's flow creates shear or turbulence to dissolve, emulsify or disperse components while conveying the fluid. These pump mixers are used in chemical, food, and pharmaceutical processes to blend ingredients in a single unit.
[B01F 25/62] of the gear type -- B01F 25/62 covers mixers that employ gear mechanisms to convey and blend materials, using intermeshing or internal gears to generate shear and agitation. These gear-type mixers are used for separating, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing substances in chemical and industrial processes.
[B01F 25/621] Wankel pump -- A Wankel pump is a rotary positive-displacement pump that uses the epitrochoidal chamber of a Wankel engine to move fluid, providing continuous flow with low pulsation. It is classified under mixers because the rotating motion can also create dispersion or mixing of liquids during pumping.
[B01F 25/64] of the centrifugal-pump type, i.e. turbo-mixers -- B01F 25/64 covers mixers that operate on the centrifugal-pump principle, commonly called turbo-mixers. These devices use a rapidly rotating impeller to create high-speed liquid flow, achieving efficient dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing of substances. The classification falls under performing operations, separating, and mixing within the CPC system.
[B01F 25/641] Multi-staged turbo-mixers -- B01F 25/641 covers multi-staged turbo-mixers designed for high-shear mixing operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing within separation processes. The devices combine sequential mixing zones driven by a turbo-type impeller to achieve rapid and uniform dispersion of liquids or solids. They are used in chemical, pharmaceutical and food industries where efficient continuous mixing is required.
[B01F 25/642] consisting of a stator-rotor system with intermeshing teeth or cages -- B01F 25/642 designates mixers that employ a stator-rotor arrangement with intermeshing teeth or cages, such as gear-type or lobed rotor mixers. The interlocked teeth or cage structures transmit motion and create shear forces that dissolve, emulsify or disperse materials during mixing operations. This subclass falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and mixing.
[B01F 25/643] with axial access to the mixing device at both its sides -- B01F 25/643 covers mixers that provide axial access to the mixing chamber from both ends of the device, allowing material to be introduced, removed or monitored along the axis of rotation. This design is useful for processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing where continuous feeding or sampling is required.
[B01F 25/70] Spray-mixers, e.g. for mixing intersecting sheets of material -- B01F 25/70 refers to spray-mixers designed to blend intersecting sheets of material by directing a spray onto the contact area, achieving rapid mixing, dispersion or dissolution across the sheet interface. These devices are used in processes such as coating, polymer lamination or emulsification where two or more layers must be combined efficiently. They fall under the CPC hierarchy of performing operations, separating, and mixing.
[B01F 25/72] with nozzles -- B01F 25/72 pertains to mixers that employ one or more nozzles to inject fluids, gases or powders into the mixing zone, using jets or sprays to improve dissolution, dispersion, emulsification or other mixing actions. The nozzle-based feeding enhances contact between phases and is typical in chemical, food-processing or pharmaceutical equipment designed for efficient blending.
[B01F 25/721] for spraying a fluid on falling particles or on a liquid curtain -- The entry covers devices or methods that spray a fluid onto particles falling through air or onto a moving liquid curtain, enabling rapid coating, dispersion or dissolution. It is part of the mixing operations family, specifically addressing fluid application to enhance particle treatment or liquid-liquid interaction.
[B01F 25/74] with rotating parts, e.g. discs -- B01F 25/74 covers mixing devices that employ rotating components, such as discs, to achieve separation, dissolution, emulsification or dispersion of materials. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and mixing, and is used to classify equipment where the motion of rotating parts effects the desired mixing action.
[B01F 25/741] with a disc or a set of discs mounted on a shaft rotating about a vertical axis, on top of which the material to be thrown outwardly is fed -- The subclass covers mixers that employ a disc or a set of discs mounted on a shaft rotating about a vertical axis, where material is fed onto the top of the disc(s) and thrown outwardly by centrifugal force for mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. This arrangement is suited for continuous feeding of the feedstock onto the rotating surface.
[B01F 25/7411] with repeated action, i.e. the material thrown outwardly being guided, by means provided on the surrounding casing or on top of the next lower disc -- B01F 25/7411 covers mixers that use a repeated throwing-out action where the material is directed outwardly and then guided either by features on the surrounding casing or by a disc positioned above the next lower rotating element, enhancing dispersion or blending during continuous operation.
[B01F 25/742] for spraying a liquid on falling particles or on a liquid curtain  B01F25/7411  takes precedence -- B01F 25/742 covers apparatus or methods that spray a liquid onto particles that are falling through the air or onto a flowing liquid curtain, typically to achieve wetting, coating, or reaction as the particles pass. It falls within the mixers sub-group of separating operations in the CPC.
[B01F 25/743] the material being fed on both sides of a part rotating about a vertical axis -- The B01F 25/743 entry covers mixers in which the feed material is introduced on both sides of a component that rotates about a vertical axis, allowing simultaneous feeding from opposite directions into the mixing zone. This configuration is used for processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing where uniform distribution of the material is required.
[B01F 25/744] the rotating part being composed of at least two cooperating members rotating independently about the same vertical axis -- This subgroup addresses mixers whose rotating part consists of at least two separate members that turn independently around the same vertical axis, creating distinct shear zones and flow patterns to enhance operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 25/80] Falling particle mixers, e.g. with repeated agitation along a vertical axis -- This subclass covers mixers in which particles fall under gravity while being repeatedly agitated along a vertical axis, often using rotating blades or impellers to promote mixing, dissolution, emulsifying or dispersing. They are employed in chemical, pharmaceutical and food processes where continuous vertical motion improves contact between phases.
[B01F 25/82] uniting flows of material taken from different parts of a receptacle or from a set of different receptacles -- B01F 25/82 covers mixers that unite material flows drawn from different points within a single container or from multiple containers, directing them together for combined processing. The arrangement enables uniform blending, dispersion or emulsification by merging streams before or during mixing.
[B01F 25/821] by means of conduits having inlet openings at different levels -- B01F 25/821 relates to mixers that introduce substances through conduits with inlet openings positioned at different levels, allowing staged feeding into the mixing zone to enhance separating actions such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. This arrangement improves material distribution and process efficiency.
[B01F 25/8211] by means of a central conduit or central set of conduits -- The code refers to mixers where the material to be mixed is introduced through a central pipe or group of pipes that run through the mixing chamber, enabling dissolution, emulsification or dispersion. This arrangement is used in equipment that performs separating and mixing operations.
[B01F 25/822] the receptacle being divided into compartments for receiving or storing the different components -- B01F 25/822 covers mixers whose receptacle is internally divided into separate compartments that hold different ingredients before or during the mixing process, allowing distinct components to be stored or introduced separately within a single device. This design facilitates controlled interaction of the materials while maintaining separation until the desired point of mixing.
[B01F 25/823] Flow collectors therefor -- B01F 25/823 covers devices that incorporate flow collectors within mixers to direct and gather the moving material, enhancing uniformity during operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. These collectors may be channels, ducts or baffles designed to guide the fluid stream toward the mixing zone.
[B01F 25/83] with receptacles provided with fixed guiding elements therein, e.g. baffles -- B01F 25/83 covers mixers that incorporate receptacles containing fixed guiding components such as baffles. These internal elements direct the movement of material during operations like dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing, improving the efficiency of the mixing process. The classification applies to devices where the guiding structures are integral and non-adjustable.
[B01F 25/84] Falling-particle mixers comprising superimposed receptacles, the material flowing from one to the other, e.g. of the sandglass type -- A falling-particle mixer with superimposed receptacles uses gravity to move material from an upper chamber to a lower one, creating mixing as the particles cascade, similar to a sand-glass. The design relies on the sequential flow of solids to achieve dispersion, dissolution or emulsification without mechanical agitation. It is classified under B01F 25/84 for mixers where the material passes from one compartment to another.
[B01F 25/85] wherein the particles fall onto a film that flows along the inner wall of a mixer -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Falling particle mixers whereby the particles are dropped on a flowing film of liquid which is created on the wall of a receptacle, e.g. to mix or avoid particles sticking to the wall.
[B01F 25/90] with moving or vibrating means, e.g. stirrers, for enhancing the mixing -- B01F 25/90 covers mixers that employ moving or vibrating components, such as stirrers, to boost the mixing process. The classification includes equipment that uses mechanical agitation or vibration to dissolve, emulsify or disperse materials more efficiently. Devices falling under this code are typically used in chemical, pharmaceutical or food processing where enhanced blending is required.
[B01F 25/901] using one central conveyor or several separate conveyors, e.g. belt, screw conveyors or vibrating tables, for discharging flows from receptacles, e.g. in layers -- B01F 25/901 covers methods that employ a central conveyor or several separate conveyors such as belts, screw conveyors or vibrating tables to remove material from vessels, typically delivering the flow in layers during mixing or separating processes.
[B01F 27/00] Mixers with rotary stirring devices in fixed receptacles -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Magnetic mixers -> B01F33/45
[B01F 27/05] Stirrers -- B01F 27/05 covers stirrers that use a rotary stirring element placed inside a stationary vessel to agitate liquids or suspensions. The devices typically consist of a rotating shaft, impeller or blade mounted in a fixed receptacle, providing mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing actions without moving the container itself.
[B01F 27/051] characterised by their elements, materials or mechanical properties -- This CPC subclass pertains to mixing equipment that employs a rotary stirring device mounted in a fixed receptacle, where the invention is defined by the construction, materials or mechanical characteristics of the stirrer, container or related components. The classification is used when these element- or material-based features are the distinguishing aspects of the device.
[B01F 27/052] Stirrers with replaceable wearing elements -- Stirrers with replaceable wearing elements are rotary mixing devices mounted in a fixed vessel that perform separating, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing operations. Their design allows the wear parts, such as blades or paddles, to be swapped out for maintenance or to adapt to different materials.
[B01F 27/053] characterised by their materials -- B01F 27/053 comprises mixers that employ rotary stirring devices within a fixed vessel and are classified according to the material from which the stirring elements, housing or other components are made, such as metals, ceramics, polymers or composites. This subclass narrows the broader mixing category to identify equipment where material choice influences performance, durability or suitability for specific media.
[B01F 27/0531] with particular surface characteristics, e.g. coated or rough -- This subclass covers mixers that use a rotary stirrer inside a stationary container where the stirrer or container surfaces have special finishes such as coatings or rough textures to affect the mixing process. It is part of the CPC group B01F 27/05 for mixers with rotary devices, focusing on surface treatment.
[B01F 27/054] Deformable stirrers, e.g. deformed by a centrifugal force applied during operation -- B01F 27/054 covers stirrers that change shape during use, such as blades that are bent by centrifugal forces while rotating in a fixed mixing vessel. The deformable design improves mixing efficiency by adapting to fluid dynamics and reducing dead zones.
[B01F 27/0541] with mechanical means to alter the position of the stirring elements -- This CPC subclass defines rotary mixers in stationary vessels that incorporate a mechanical mechanism to change the location or orientation of the stirring elements while operating. Examples include impellers that can be lifted, rotated, or pivoted to vary shear, flow pattern or mixing intensity during a batch process.
[B01F 27/0542] deformable by centrifugal force -- This CPC subclass covers mixers that use a rotary stirring element placed in a stationary vessel, where the mixing element or its components are designed to change shape under centrifugal forces generated during operation, aiding dissolution, emulsification or dispersion.
[B01F 27/0543] the position of the stirring elements depending on the direction of rotation of the stirrer -- The entry B01F 27/0543 relates to mixers that have rotary stirring devices in a fixed vessel, where the position or orientation of the stir-ring elements is selected according to the direction of rotation, enabling different flow patterns or processing actions when the stirrer turns clockwise versus counter-clockwise.
[B01F 27/06] Stirrers made by deforming a plate -- This subgroup includes stirrers that are formed by deforming a flat plate into a bent or stamped shape that acts as an impeller in rotary mixers with stationary containers. The plate-derived devices generate fluid flow for operations such as blending, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 27/07] characterised by their mounting on the shaft -- This subgroup covers mixing devices where the rotary stirring element is fixed to and rotates with the drive shaft of the equipment, typically used in stationary vessels for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 27/071] Fixing of the stirrer to the shaft -- B01F 27/071 concerns the means by which a stirring element is attached to the rotating shaft inside a fixed mixing vessel, including couplings, clamping, welding, pins, or other securing mechanisms that transmit torque from the shaft to the stirrer.
[B01F 27/072] characterised by the disposition of the stirrers with respect to the rotating axis -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Stirrers or stirring elements mounted to the shaft and their arrangement or position in relation to the rotating axis of the main shaft of the stirrer, e.g. oblique, parallel, perpendicular, directly mounted on the shaft or mounted on a rod connected to the shaft.
[B01F 27/0721] parallel with respect to the rotating axis -- Mixers that employ rotary stirring devices within a stationary container where the stirring elements are positioned parallel to the axis of rotation, enabling efficient dissolution, emulsification or dispersion of materials in the vessel. This arrangement falls under CPC B01F 27/0721 and is used for performing mixing operations in fixed receptacles.
[B01F 27/0722] perpendicular with respect to the rotating axis -- The B01F 27/0722 entry covers mixers in which the stirring element is mounted so that it lies perpendicular to the rotor's axis of rotation, typically within a fixed receptacle. This arrangement is used for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing where a cross-wise flow pattern improves the contact between components. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating and mixing.
[B01F 27/0723] oblique with respect to the rotating axis -- This subclass covers mixers that use a rotary stirring element positioned at an oblique angle to the shaft, allowing the impeller to sweep across the vessel rather than rotate strictly around a horizontal or vertical axis. The arrangement improves flow patterns and may aid dissolution or dispersion.
[B01F 27/0724] directly mounted on the rotating axis -- This CPC subclass (B01F 27/0724) concerns mixers where the rotary stirring element is attached directly to the rotating axis of the machine, rather than being driven by belts, gears or other transmission parts. It applies to fixed-receptacle devices used for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing, and is located under performing operations - separating - mixing, e.g. dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing - mixers with rotary stirring devices in fixed receptacles.
[B01F 27/0725] on the free end of the rotating axis -- This subclass covers mixers where the stirring element is mounted at the free end of a rotating shaft that protrudes from the motor, typically used in fixed vessels for processes such as dissolving, emulsifying, or dispersing. The design allows the stirrer to be positioned directly in the material without a surrounding housing.
[B01F 27/0726] having stirring elements connected to the stirrer shaft each by a single radial rod, other than open frameworks -- B01F 27/0726 is a CPC subgroup for mixers that use rotary stirring devices in fixed containers, where each stirring element is attached to the stirrer shaft by a single radial rod rather than an open framework. It falls under performing operations → separating → mixing (e.g., dissolving, emulsifying, dispersing).
[B01F 27/07261] of the anchor type, i.e. the stirring elements being connected to the rods by one end and extending parallel to the shaft axis -- The subgroup B01F 27/07261 pertains to mixers that employ anchor-type stirring elements, which are fixed to rods at one end and extend parallel to the shaft axis within a stationary vessel. This design is used for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing materials in a fixed receptacle.
[B01F 27/0727] having stirring elements connected to the stirrer shaft each by two or more radial rods, e.g. the shaft being interrupted between the rods, or of crankshaft type -- The B01F 27/0727 subclass covers mixers that use rotary stirring devices mounted in a fixed container where the stirring elements are linked to the shaft by two or more radial rods. The design may feature a shaft interrupted between the rods or a crankshaft arrangement to transmit motion to the stirrers.
[B01F 27/073] with stirring elements moving with respect to the stirrer shaft, e.g. floating or comprising contracting chambers -- The CPC subclass B01F 27/073 covers mixers that use rotary stirring devices in a stationary container where the agitators are not rigidly attached to the shaft but can move independently, such as floating paddles or elements housed in chambers that expand and contract during operation. This allows enhanced mixing actions like dissolution, emulsification or dispersion.
[B01F 27/074] having two or more mixing elements being concentrically mounted on the same shaft -- The B01F 27/074 subclass defines mixers that have two or more mixing elements arranged concentrically on a common shaft within a fixed receptacle. Such devices use stacked impellers or similar elements to achieve compound-agitation, dissolution, emulsification or dispersion.
[B01F 27/09] characterised by the mounting of the stirrers with respect to the receptacle -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mounting of stirrers and their arrangement or position with regard to the receptacle.
[B01F 27/091] with elements co-operating with receptacle wall or bottom, e.g. for scraping the receptacle wall -- B01F 27/091 designates rotary mixers in fixed containers that incorporate elements such as scrapers, blades or fingers that cooperate with the receptacle wall or bottom to remove material adherent to the surface. This sub-class is used for devices that actively engage the container interior during mixing, dissolution, emulsifying or dispersing operations.
[B01F 27/092] occupying substantially the whole interior space of the receptacle -- B01F 27/092 covers mixing devices in which the rotary stirring element fills essentially the entire interior of a fixed receptacle, so that the whole volume of the container is occupied by the mover. This design is used for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing where thorough agitation of the material throughout the vessel is required.
[B01F 27/093] eccentrically arranged -- B01F 27/093 covers mixers that use a rotary stirring device mounted off-center inside a fixed receptacle, creating an eccentric motion that enhances the mixing of liquids for purposes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. The arrangement improves flow patterns and shear within the vessel.
[B01F 27/11] characterised by the configuration of the stirrers -- This subclass pertains to mixers that use a rotary stirring device mounted inside a fixed vessel, where the invention is defined by the particular shape, arrangement or geometry of the stirrer itself. It falls under the performing-operations hierarchy for separating and mixing processes.
[B01F 27/111] Centrifugal stirrers, i.e. stirrers with radial outlets -- B01F 27/111 covers centrifugal stirrers, mixing devices that generate a radial flow of liquid by a rotating impeller and channel the fluid through outward-directed outlets. They are employed in fixed receptacles for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing where continuous removal of product from the mixing zone is required.
[B01F 27/1111] with a flat disc or with a disc-like element equipped with blades, e.g. Rushton turbine -- B01F 27/1111 covers mixers that use a flat disc or disc-like element equipped with blades, such as a Rushton turbine, to provide rotary stirring in a fixed receptacle. It is classified under mixing operations for processes like dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 27/112] with arms, paddles, vanes or blades -- B01F 27/112 covers mixers that employ a rotary stirring device located in a fixed container, where the agitation is provided by arms, paddles, vanes or blades. This subclass falls under the broader category of mixing operations, specifically for equipment that dissolves, emulsifies or disperses materials by mechanical agitation.
[B01F 27/1121] pin-shaped -- This subclass covers mixing apparatus that uses a pin-shaped rotary stirring element within a fixed receptacle. The pin agitator creates localized shear for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing, and is grouped under performing operations, separating and mixing.
[B01F 27/1122] anchor-shaped -- The entry B01F 27/1122 defines anchor-shaped mixers, which are rotary stirring devices whose blades are shaped like an anchor and mounted in a stationary vessel to provide efficient bulk mixing, dissolution, emulsifying or dispersing of fluids or solids. The design improves shear and flow in fixed receptacles.
[B01F 27/1123] sickle-shaped, i.e. curved in at least one direction -- This CPC sub-group covers mixers that use rotary stirring devices mounted in a fixed receptacle where the stirring element is sickle-shaped, meaning it is curved in at least one direction. It falls under performing operations → separating → mixing → mixers with rotary stirring devices in fixed receptacles.
[B01F 27/1124] rake-shaped or grid-shaped -- The B01F 27/1124 subclass covers mixers that employ rotary stirring devices shaped like a rake or a grid within a fixed receptacle. These stirrers are used to disperse, dissolve, emulsify or otherwise mix materials by sweeping or interlacing the medium as the vessel rotates. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and mixing.
[B01F 27/1125] with vanes or blades extending parallel or oblique to the stirrer axis -- This subclass covers mixers that employ a rotary stirrer mounted in a stationary vessel, where the stirring element includes vanes or blades that run parallel or at an oblique angle to the stirrer axis. Such designs are used for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying, or dispersing materials in fixed receptacles.
[B01F 27/11251] having holes in the surface -- The subclass B01F 27/11251 pertains to mixers that use a rotary stirring device within a fixed vessel whose walls or bottom contain holes or perforations, allowing fluid passage, gas exchange, or discharge of material during mixing operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 27/11252] paddle wheels -- Paddle-wheel mixers are devices that rotate a set of flat or slightly curved blades mounted on a shaft within a stationary vessel to generate bulk flow and shear for processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. They are classified under CPC B01F 27/11252, which covers mixers with rotary stirring devices in fixed receptacles.
[B01F 27/11253] the blades extending oblique to the stirrer axis -- The CPC subclass B01F 27/11253 covers mixers in which the rotary stirrer is fitted with blades that are set at an oblique angle to the stirrer's longitudinal axis, providing non-axial flow and enhanced shearing within a fixed vessel.
[B01F 27/1126] the stirrer being a bent rod supported at one end only -- This subclass covers mixers that employ a single-ended, bent-rod stirrer confined to a fixed vessel. The rod is supported only at one end while it rotates to provide agitation for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It falls under mixing devices with rotary stirring elements in stationary receptacles.
[B01F 27/1127] spoon-shaped -- The CPC code B01F 27/1127 covers spoon-shaped mixing tools that rotate within a fixed receptacle to stir, dissolve, emulsify or disperse materials. It is a subcategory of mixers with rotary stirring devices in stationary containers, belonging to the broader mixing operations segment of the performing-operations hierarchy.
[B01F 27/113] Propeller-shaped stirrers for producing an axial flow, e.g. shaped like a ship or aircraft propeller -- The CPC subclass B01F 27/113 covers propeller-shaped stirrers designed to generate an axial flow within a fixed mixing vessel, often resembling marine or aircraft propellers. These devices are used in mixers with rotary stirring elements to improve dispersion, dissolution or emulsification by directing fluid along the axis of rotation.
[B01F 27/1131] with holes in the propeller blade surface -- A mixer that uses a rotary stirring device mounted in a stationary vessel, where the propeller blades are perforated with holes to allow fluid to pass through the blade surface during operation, improving flow distribution and mixing efficiency.
[B01F 27/1132] with guiding tubes or tubular segments fixed to and surrounding the tips of the propeller blades, e.g. for supplementary mixing -- This subclass covers mixers that use rotary stirring devices in fixed containers and incorporate guiding tubes or tubular segments attached around the propeller blade tips to promote additional mixing action. The tubes direct fluid flow and improve dispersion, dissolution or emulsification within the vessel.
[B01F 27/1133] the impeller being of airfoil or aerofoil type -- B01F 27/1133 designates mixers that employ a rotary impeller shaped like an airfoil or aerofoil. The airfoil profile provides lift-type forces that enhance fluid movement and mixing efficiency within a fixed receptacle, useful for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 27/1134] the impeller being of hydrofoil type -- B01F 27/1134 classifies mixers that employ a rotary impeller shaped as a hydrofoil inside a fixed vessel. The hydrofoil impeller generates a directed flow pattern to enhance dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing of materials, and is used in stationary mixing equipment.
[B01F 27/114] Helically shaped stirrers, i.e. stirrers comprising a helically shaped band or helically shaped band sections -- A helically shaped stirrer is a mixing element formed from a continuous or segmented helical band that rotates within a fixed vessel to create axial and radial flow, enhancing dissolution, emulsifying or dispersing actions. It is used in rotary stirring devices where the helix drives fluid movement and improves contact between phases during the mixing process.
[B01F 27/1141] having holes in the surface -- B01F 27/1141 covers mixers that use a rotary stirring device inside a fixed vessel whose walls or base contain holes or perforations, allowing material to be introduced, removed, or to interact with external streams while mixing. The perforated surface facilitates continuous processing, drainage, or gas-liquid contact during operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 27/1142] of the corkscrew type -- B01F 27/1142 refers to mixers that employ a corkscrew-type rotary stirring element within a stationary vessel. These devices are used for separating processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing mixtures.
[B01F 27/1143] screw-shaped, e.g. worms -- B01F 27/1143 covers mixers that incorporate a screw-shaped or worm-type stirring element rotating within a stationary container. The screw geometry conveys material while providing shear and distributive mixing, suitable for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. These devices belong to the broader class of rotary mixers in fixed receptacles.
[B01F 27/1144] with a plurality of blades following a helical path on a shaft or a blade support -- This subclass covers mixers that use a rotary stirring device in a fixed receptacle and feature a plurality of blades arranged to follow a helical path on a shaft or blade support, providing continuous agitation for processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 27/1145] ribbon shaped with an open space between the helical ribbon flight and the rotating axis -- This subclass covers mixers that employ a helical ribbon stirrer in a stationary container where the ribbon is shaped so that a gap remains between the ribbon flight and the central rotating shaft, allowing fluid to flow through the open space while the ribbon rotates. The design is intended to improve mixing efficiency for dissolution, emulsification, or dispersion processes.
[B01F 27/11451] forming open frameworks or cages -- B01F 27/11451 covers the use of mixers equipped with rotary stirrers in stationary vessels to produce open-framework or cage structures, such as porous materials, by dispersing and reacting constituent components. The classification applies to processes that form these three-dimensional networks during mixing operations.
[B01F 27/115] comprising discs or disc-like elements essentially perpendicular to the stirrer shaft axis -- A mixer in a fixed vessel that employs a rotary shaft fitted with discs or disc-like elements arranged essentially perpendicular to the shaft axis. This configuration provides shear and turbulent flow for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying, or dispersing substances within the liquid.
[B01F 27/1151] with holes on the surface -- B01F 27/1151 covers mixers that employ a rotary stirring device mounted in a stationary vessel and feature holes on the vessel's surface to enable material passage during operations such as dissolution, emulsification or dispersion.
[B01F 27/1152] with separate elements other than discs fixed on the discs, e.g. vanes fixed on the discs -- B01F 27/1152 covers mixers that use rotary stirring devices in a fixed receptacle and incorporate separate elements attached to the rotating discs, such as fixed vanes, rather than only disc-shaped parts.
[B01F 27/1153] the discs being made by deforming flat discs -- The code covers mixers that use rotary stirring devices in a stationary vessel where the stirring elements are disc-shaped and are manufactured by deforming initially flat metal discs into the required geometry. This method is used to create robust, uniformly shaped discs for efficient mixing, dispersing, or emulsifying operations.
[B01F 27/1154] the discs being cup shaped, e.g. semi sphere -- B01F 27/1154 covers mixers that use rotary stirring devices within a stationary container where the stirring elements are cup-shaped or semi-spherical discs. The design creates a localized, high-shear zone that promotes effective dissolution, emulsification or dispersion of materials. This subclass falls under mixing operations within the broader separating category of the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[B01F 27/1155] with interconnected discs, forming open frameworks or cages -- The code B01F 27/1155 designates mixers that employ a set of interlinked discs arranged as open frameworks or cage-like structures to stir material in a fixed vessel. The disc network creates a three-dimensional lattice that agitates liquids or suspensions for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 27/116] Stirrers shaped as cylinders, balls or rollers -- The code B01F 27/116 covers stirrers that are cylindrical, spherical or roller-type elements used in mixers with rotary stirring devices in fixed receptacles. These shaped stirrers are employed to improve mixing, dispersion or emulsification of liquids or suspensions.
[B01F 27/1161] having holes in the surface -- This CPC subclass B01F 27/1161, within mixing operations that use rotary stirrers in fixed receptacles, covers mixers that have holes in their surface to permit material flow, introduce liquids or gases, or enable discharge during mixing.
[B01F 27/1162] Balls -- This CPC subclass covers the use of balls as mixing media within rotary-stirred mixers that are mounted in fixed receptacles, where the balls assist in separating, mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing materials. It pertains to devices that employ a rotating agitator and stationary vessel, with the balls providing the mechanical action needed for fine dispersion.
[B01F 27/1163] Rollers -- The CPC subclass B01F 27/1163 covers rollers employed as the rotating mixing elements inside a stationary vessel. It pertains to mixers in which rotary stirring devices are fixed in the receptacle and the rollers impart shear and agitation to dissolve, emulsify or disperse materials. This subclass falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and mixing.
[B01F 27/11631] comprising paddles fixed thereon, e.g. with a total a diameter close to that of the surrounding receptacle -- The subclass B01F 27/11631 covers rotary mixers in fixed vessels that are equipped with paddles permanently attached to the rotor, where the combined paddle width approaches the internal diameter of the surrounding receptacle, providing thorough agitation for dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing operations.
[B01F 27/117] Stirrers provided with conical-shaped elements, e.g. funnel-shaped -- This subclass covers mixers that employ rotary stirring devices in fixed receptacles and incorporate conical-shaped or funnel-shaped elements to guide flow, enhance blending and aid in separating operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating and mixing.
[B01F 27/1171] having holes in the surface -- The subclass B01F 27/1171 refers to mixers that include holes in the surface of the rotary stirring device or the fixed receptacle. These perforations are used to aid the introduction, removal or distribution of liquids, powders or other materials during mixing, separating or dissolving operations. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, separating, and mixing such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 27/118] Stirrers in the form of brushes, sieves, grids, chains or springs -- B01F 27/118 covers stirrers formed as brushes, sieves, grids, chains or springs that are used in mixers with rotary stirring devices inside fixed receptacles, providing agitation for separating, mixing or dispersing operations.
[B01F 27/119] Stirrers with rigid wires or flexible rods -- The B01F 27/119 subclass defines rotary mixers that employ rigid wires or flexible rods as the stirring elements inside a fixed receptacle, used for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It covers devices where the stirrer itself consists of wire or rod bundles rather than paddles or blades.
[B01F 27/1191] with a bent rod of non-helical configuration supported at one end -- B01F 27/1191 covers mixers that use a bent, non-helical stirring rod anchored at one end inside a stationary vessel. The rod rotates to generate flow for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing materials, providing agitation without a helical screw.
[B01F 27/13] Openwork frame or cage stirrers not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- B01F 27/13 covers mixers that use an openwork frame or cage-type stirrer positioned in a fixed receptacle, where the stirrer's geometry is not covered by other groups in the subclass. These devices create agitation by rotating the frame or cage to disperse, dissolve or emulsify materials within the vessel.
[B01F 27/15] Stirrers with tubes for guiding the material -- The code B01F 27/15 covers stirrers that incorporate one or more tubes to guide material into, through, or out of the mixing zone, typically used in rotary stirrers mounted in fixed vessels where the tubes control feed direction, residence time or discharge of the product.
[B01F 27/17] Stirrers with additional elements mounted on the stirrer, for purposes other than mixing -- B01F 27/17 pertains to mixers that use rotary stirring devices within a fixed container, specifically focusing on stirrers equipped with extra components designed for functions beyond the primary mixing process.
[B01F 27/171] for disintegrating, e.g. for milling -- B01F 27/171 covers mixers that use a rotary stirring device within a fixed vessel and are specifically adapted for disintegrating materials, such as by milling. The classification includes equipment where the rotor agitates the contents to break down solids or particles while the vessel remains stationary, facilitating processes like dissolution, emulsification or dispersion.
[B01F 27/172] for cutting, e.g. with knives -- The code refers to mixers that incorporate rotary stirring elements which also cut or shear the material, such as knife-type stirrers, within a stationary vessel. It is used for processes that require simultaneous mixing and cutting, like chopping solids while dispersing them in a liquid.
[B01F 27/19] Stirrers with two or more mixing elements mounted in sequence on the same axis -- Stirrers with two or more mixing elements mounted in sequence on the same axis are devices that use a single rotating shaft to drive several successive agitators, such as stators, paddles or turbines, within a fixed container, enabling efficient mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing of fluids.
[B01F 27/191] with similar elements -- Mixers that use rotary stirring devices mounted inside a stationary container, where the mixing elements are of the same type or design, such as identical blades, paddles or pins, are classified under B01F 27/191. This subclass covers equipment that achieves mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing by rotating similar elements within a fixed receptacle.
[B01F 27/192] with dissimilar elements -- B01F 27/192 concerns mixing devices that incorporate rotary stirring components installed in a fixed vessel, where the stirring elements are of differing forms or materials. The dissimilar elements are intended to improve the handling of immiscible or heterogeneous mixtures by providing varied flow patterns and shear actions.
[B01F 27/1921] comprising helical elements and paddles -- B01F 27/1921 covers mixers that operate in a fixed vessel and employ rotary stirring devices incorporating both helical elements and paddles. The helical components create a screw-type flow while the paddles add axial agitation, enabling efficient dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing of media.
[B01F 27/21] characterised by their rotating shafts -- B01F 27/21 relates to mixers that use a rotating shaft to stir material in a stationary container, a type of rotary stirring device employed for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. The subclass is nested under B01F 27/00 for mixers with rotary stirring devices in fixed receptacles, within the broader CPC group for performing operations, separating, mixing.
[B01F 27/211] characterised by the material of the shaft -- B01F 27/211 is a sub-class of mixers with rotary stirring devices in fixed receptacles, focusing on the material from which the shaft is made. It includes inventions where the shaft's composition-such as stainless steel, ceramic, polymer or composite-is a defining feature for durability, chemical resistance or magnetic properties in mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing operations.
[B01F 27/2111] Flexible shafts -- Flexible shafts are components that transmit rotary motion while bending to accommodate misalignment between a motor and a stirring device in mixers using fixed receptacles, enabling operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. They are used to connect the drive unit to the agitator without requiring a rigid shaft, allowing compact designs and easier maintenance.
[B01F 27/212] Construction of the shaft  B01F27/2121 ,  B01F27/2122 ,  B01F27/2123 ,  B01F27/2124  take precedence -- B01F 27/212 covers the structural design of the shaft in rotary mixers that operate in fixed vessels, including its mounting, bearing arrangements, drive couplings, sealing, and other features that affect rotation and durability. The sub-entries B01F 27/2121-2124 detail specific aspects such as shaft geometry, support mechanisms, power transmission elements, and protective measures for the shaft assembly. This subclass is used to classify patents dealing with how the shaft is constructed and integrated in mixers for separating or dispersing processes.
[B01F 27/2121] composed of interconnected parts -- B01F 27/2121 covers mixers that are constructed from several connected components forming a single unit, such as a rotary stirrer, drive shaft, bearings and fixed receptacle that are mechanically linked. The subclass includes designs where these parts are assembled together rather than being separate, interchangeable pieces, enabling stable operation for dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing mixtures.
[B01F 27/2122] Hollow shafts -- B01F 27/2122 refers to hollow shafts employed in mixers that use rotary stirring devices within stationary receptacles. These shafts transmit torque while providing a central cavity for fluid passage, ventilation or instrument insertion, aiding processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 27/2123] Shafts with both stirring means and feeding or discharging means -- B01F 27/2123 covers shafts that integrate a stirring element with a feeding or discharging mechanism, enabling simultaneous mixing and material transfer within a fixed-vessel mixer. The design combines rotary agitation and inlet or outlet functions on a single rotating shaft to improve process efficiency and reduce equipment count.
[B01F 27/2124] Shafts with adjustable length, e.g. telescopic shafts -- The CPC subclass B01F 27/2124 covers shafts whose length can be varied, such as telescopic shafts, used in mixers that employ rotary stirring devices in fixed receptacles. These adjustable shafts let the stirring element be positioned at different depths or adapted to varying batch sizes, enhancing mixing efficiency and flexibility.
[B01F 27/213] characterised by the connection with the drive -- Mixers that employ a rotary stirring device mounted inside a fixed receptacle and are distinguished by the manner in which the stirrer is coupled to its drive mechanism. The classification covers designs where the drive connection, such as a shaft, gear or coupling, is an integral feature of the equipment. This entry is used for devices where the drive linkage is a defining element of the mixing apparatus.
[B01F 27/23] characterised by the orientation or disposition of the rotor axis -- B01F 27/23 covers mixers that use a rotary stirring device mounted in a stationary container, where the classification is based on how the rotor axis is oriented or arranged relative to the vessel, such as vertical, horizontal or inclined positions.
[B01F 27/231] with a variable orientation during mixing operation, e.g. with tiltable rotor axis -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Stirrers with a rotating axis which is movable during the mixing operation, e.g. to reach different areas of the mixing receptacle. The orientation of the fixed stirrer can be changed.
[B01F 27/2311] the orientation of the rotating shaft being adjustable in the interior of the receptacle, e.g. by tilting the stirrer shaft during the mixing -- This CPC subgroup covers mixers with a rotary stirring device in a fixed receptacle where the angle of the rotating shaft can be varied inside the vessel, for example by tilting the stirrer shaft during mixing.
[B01F 27/2312] the position of the rotating shaft being adjustable in the interior of the receptacle, e.g. to locate the stirrer in different locations during the mixing -- B01F 27/2312 relates to mixers that have a rotary stirring shaft whose position can be varied inside a fixed receptacle, allowing the stirrer to be placed at different points within the tank during mixing operations. This adjustable-shaft feature is used to improve homogenisation, heat transfer or reaction control in processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 27/232] with two or more rotation axes -- This subclass pertains to mixers that employ rotary stirring devices fixed within a stationary receptacle and that rotate about two or more axes, creating complex flow patterns to aid dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing of substances.
[B01F 27/2321] having different inclinations, e.g. non parallel -- The subclass B01F 27/2321 refers to mixers that employ rotary stirring devices mounted inside a fixed receptacle where the stirrers are positioned at different inclinations rather than being parallel, creating varied flow paths to enhance mixing efficiency during separating operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 27/2322] with parallel axes -- The subclass covers mixers that incorporate rotary stirring devices mounted in a stationary vessel where the stirring shafts are arranged parallel to each other. It applies to equipment used for separating, mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing substances.
[B01F 27/2323] with perpendicular axes -- This subclass covers mixers in which the rotary stirring device is mounted in a fixed vessel and its axis of rotation is perpendicular to the principal axis of the receptacle. Typical examples are horizontal impellers in a vertical tank or vertical stirrers in a horizontal container.
[B01F 27/2324] planetary -- B01F 27/2324 covers planetary mixers that employ a rotary stirring device within a fixed vessel. The device moves the agitator on a planetary orbit while it rotates, providing efficient mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing of materials in the receptacle. This configuration is used where thorough separation and homogenization are required.
[B01F 27/25] Mixers with both stirrer and drive unit submerged in the material being mixed -- This subclass covers mixers in which both the stirrer and its drive unit are submerged in the material being mixed, usually within a fixed receptacle, allowing direct power transmission and efficient mixing of liquids, suspensions or pastes.
[B01F 27/251] Vertical beam constructions therefor -- B01F 27/251 covers vertical-beam constructions used in mixers that have rotary stirring devices mounted in fixed receptacles, providing a framework that supports the agitator shaft and facilitates efficient mixing, dispersing or emulsifying of materials within a stationary container.
[B01F 27/27] Mixers with stator-rotor systems, e.g. with intermeshing teeth or cylinders or having orifices -- B01F 27/27 pertains to mixers that employ a stator-rotor configuration, such as intermeshing teeth, cylinders or similar elements, and may incorporate orifices for material flow. The device uses a rotating component against a stationary counterpart inside a fixed receptacle to achieve mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing of substances.
[B01F 27/271] with means for moving the materials to be mixed radially between the surfaces of the rotor and the stator -- B01F 27/271 covers mixers that use a rotary stirring device inside a stationary vessel and incorporate features that transport the material radially outward or inward between the rotor and stator surfaces, enhancing mixing efficiency and uniformity. This subclass falls under separating operations, specifically mixing processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 27/2711] provided with intermeshing elements -- B01F 27/2711 refers to mixers that use a rotary stirring device housed in a fixed receptacle, where the stirrer incorporates intermeshing elements that engage each other during operation. These intermeshing parts enhance the mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing action within the stationary container.
[B01F 27/2712] provided with ribs, ridges or grooves on one surface -- This subcategory covers mixers that incorporate rotary stirrers housed in a stationary container and whose interior or contact surface features ribs, ridges or grooves to enhance fluid dynamics, improve mixing efficiency, or control turbulence. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, mixing such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 27/2713] the surfaces having a conical shape -- The subclass B01F 27/2713 covers mixers that use a rotary stirring device inside a fixed vessel whose internal surfaces are shaped conically. The conical geometry promotes efficient mixing, dissolution, emulsification or dispersion of the material being processed.
