The supplementary device works together with the primary motion to improve homogeneity in batch or continuous processes.
[B01F 31/831] the vibrations being generated by the rotation of the stirring element -- B01F 31/831 pertains to mixers that produce vibrations as a result of the stirring element's rotation, using the generated shaking, oscillating, or vibrating motion to enhance mixing, dispersing or emulsifying processes.
[B01F 31/84] for material continuously moving through a tube, e.g. by deforming the tube -- This CPC subclass covers mixers that employ shaking, oscillating or vibrating actions for processing material that is continuously conveyed through a tube, particularly when the tube itself is deformed to aid mixing, such as in dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing operations.
[B01F 31/841] with a vibrating element inside the tube -- B01F 31/841 covers mixers that use a vibrating element located inside the tube to create agitation, aiding processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. The vibration may be generated by an eccentric mass, piezoelectric transducer or similar mechanism, providing shaking or oscillatory motion directly within the material container.
[B01F 31/85] with a vibrating element inside the receptacle -- B01F 31/85 relates to mixing apparatus that includes a vibrating element positioned inside the receptacle holding the material to be processed. The internal vibration generates agitation that assists in dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing components, and it is classified under performing operations, separating and mixing.
[B01F 31/86] with vibration of the receptacle or part of it -- This subclass covers mixing devices that achieve their action by vibrating the receptacle or a component of it, such as shaking or oscillating mechanisms that create vibration-driven flow to dissolve, emulsify or disperse materials. It falls under the hierarchy Performing Operations → Separating → Mixing, specifically B01F 31/86.
[B01F 31/861] caused by hitting or striking the receptacle -- B01F 31/861 designates mixers that achieve separation or dispersion by striking or hitting the vessel, using impact-generated motion rather than continuous shaking or vibration. The subclass covers apparatus where impacts create mixing actions such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing within the receptacle.
[B01F 31/87] transmitting the vibratory energy by means of a fluid, e.g. by means of air shock waves -- This subclass covers devices that convey vibratory energy to a mixer by using a fluid medium such as air shock waves, enabling shaking, oscillating or vibrating action for processes like dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 31/89] Methodical aspects; Controlling -- B01F 31/89 covers the methodical aspects and control of mixers that employ shaking, oscillating or vibrating actions for separating, mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It includes regulation of operating conditions, timing, sequencing and feedback systems to achieve the desired material treatment.
[B01F 33/00] Other mixers -- B01F 33/00 relates to mixers that are not otherwise classified in more specific sub-groups, covering devices that combine, disperse or emulsify materials through mechanical agitation. These mixers may operate in batch or continuous mode and can handle liquids, powders or pastes in various sectors such as chemicals, food and pharmaceuticals.
[B01F 33/05] Mixers using radiation, e.g. magnetic fields or microwaves to mix the material -- B01F 33/05 covers mixers that employ radiation such as magnetic fields or microwaves to agitate and combine materials, enabling dissolution, emulsification or dispersion without mechanical contact. These devices use energy transmission to induce mixing at the molecular or particulate level, suitable for processes where conventional mechanical mixers are unsuitable.
[B01F 33/051] the energy being electrical energy working on the ingredients or compositions for mixing them -- B01F 33/051 covers mixers that employ electrical energy to act on the material being processed, such as electromagnetic, ultrasonic or electrically driven stirrers that dissolve, emulsify or disperse components by applying a field or current directly to the mixture. This subclass falls under separating-mixing operations where the energy source is electricity rather than mechanical motion alone.
[B01F 33/052] the energy being electric fields for electrostatically charging of the ingredients or compositions for mixing them -- B01F 33/052 covers mixers that use electric fields to electrostatically charge the materials being mixed, enhancing dispersion and interaction. This technology applies electrostatic forces to improve blending, dissolution or emulsification of ingredients.
[B01F 33/053] the energy being magnetic or electromagnetic energy, radiation working on the ingredients or compositions for or during mixing them -- B01F 33/053 covers mixing operations in which magnetic, electromagnetic or radiative energy is applied to the ingredients or the composition during the mixing process. The energy may be used to heat, stir, disperse or otherwise influence the material while it is being mixed, for example by induction, microwave or other electromagnetic fields. This subclass groups methods that rely on such energy forms rather than purely mechanical agitation.
[B01F 33/054] the energy being in the form of a laser to modify the characteristics or conditions of the products, e.g. for heating -- B01F 33/054 concerns mixing processes in which laser energy is applied to alter the properties or condition of the material being mixed, for example to heat, cure, or otherwise modify the product during dissolution, emulsifying or dispersing operations. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating and mixing.
[B01F 33/055] the energy being particle radiation working on the ingredients or compositions for or during mixing them -- B01F 33/055 covers mixing devices that employ particle radiation, such as electron or ion beams, to act on ingredients or compositions during the mixing process. The radiation energy is used to promote dissolution, emulsification, dispersion or other alterations of the material while it is being mixed.
[B01F 33/12] Mixers in which the mixing of the components is achieved by natural convection -- B01F 33/12 covers mixers that rely on natural convection currents to blend components, without mechanical agitation, typically used in processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing during separation operations.
[B01F 33/25] Mixers with loose mixing elements, e.g. loose balls in a receptacle -- The CPC subclass B01F 33/25 pertains to mixers that employ free-floating elements such as loose balls, beads or pellets inside a receptacle to achieve blending, dispersing or emulsifying of substances. These devices rely on the motion of the loose elements to generate shear and impact forces, rather than fixed impellers.
[B01F 33/251] using balls as loose mixing element -- B01F 33/251 covers mixing processes that employ loose balls as the mixing element. This subclass falls under other mixers in the mixing and separating operations of the CPC, describing the use of spherical particles to promote dissolution, emulsification or dispersion of materials.
[B01F 33/252] using bubbles as loose mixing element -- The code B01F 33/252 covers mixing processes that employ gas bubbles as a loose mixing element, where bubbles are introduced into a liquid, slurry or reactor to promote dispersion, dissolution, emulsification or other separating actions. It applies to mixers that rely on the motion and distribution of bubbles rather than mechanical impellers.
[B01F 33/253] using sliders or cylindrical elements as loose mixing element -- B01F 33/253 covers mixing devices that employ sliders or cylindrical components that move freely within the mixture to create turbulence and improve homogeneity. The loose elements slide or rotate, generating shear forces that aid in dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing substances, and are classified among other non-fixed mixers.
[B01F 33/254] using springs as loose mixing element -- B01F 33/254 relates to mixing operations in which springs are employed as loose mixing elements to agitate or blend materials, supporting processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing within broader separating and mixing activities.
[B01F 33/26] Mixers with an endless belt for transport of the material, e.g. in layers or with mixing means above or at the end of the belt -- The subclass B01F 33/26 covers mixers that employ an endless belt to transport material while mixing, either by forming layers on the belt or by positioning mixing elements above or at the belt's end. It applies to devices where the belt provides continuous conveyance of the product and simultaneous mixing action, commonly used in food, chemical or polymer processing.
[B01F 33/27] Mixers having moving endless chains or belts, e.g. provided with paddles, as mixing elements -- This subclass includes mixers that employ a continuously moving chain or belt, often fitted with paddles or similar mixing elements, to convey and blend materials. The endless moving element provides both agitation and transport of the product, enabling operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing within the mixer.
[B01F 33/30] Micromixers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mixers having transporting fluid channels with sizes in the micrometre range.
[B01F 33/301] using specific means for arranging the streams to be mixed, e.g. channel geometries or dispositions -- B01F 33/301 concerns mixing operations in which the streams are positioned by particular physical features such as channel shapes, layouts or other geometrical configurations that direct the flow before they combine. These specific means are employed to achieve desired mixing, dissolution, emulsification or dispersion.
[B01F 33/3011] using a sheathing stream of a fluid surrounding a central stream of a different fluid, e.g. for reducing the cross-section of the central stream or to produce droplets from the central stream -- This subclass covers processes in which a fluid stream is enveloped by a second sheathing-fluid stream. The surrounding stream is used to narrow the core stream or to fragment it into droplets, enabling controlled mixing, emulsifying, or dispersing operations.
[B01F 33/3012] Interdigital streams, e.g. lamellae -- This subclass covers devices that generate or handle interdigitated fluid streams such as lamellae, used in mixing processes. It includes apparatuses that create alternating parallel streams to improve dissolution, emulsification, or dispersion.
[B01F 33/30121] the interdigital streams being concentric lamellae -- The subclass concerns mixers where fluid streams travel between interdigitated channels that are arranged as concentric lamellae, creating layered flow patterns that repeatedly split and recombine the streams to improve dispersion, emulsification or dissolution. This radial, lamellar configuration is used for fine, high-efficiency mixing.
[B01F 33/3017] Mixing chamber -- A mixing chamber is a component of a mixing device that provides a confined space where fluids or solids are combined and agitated to achieve dissolution, emulsification, or dispersion. It may be shaped or equipped to enhance turbulence, residence time, or contacting efficiency, and is used in processes such as chemical reactors, food processing, or pharmaceutical preparation.
[B01F 33/302] the materials to be mixed flowing in the form of droplets -- The CPC subclass B01F 33/302 covers devices or methods in which the materials to be combined flow as droplets, such as sprays or atomised streams, so that mixing occurs by direct contact of the droplets. It is placed under other mixers within the mixing operations branch of separating processes.
[B01F 33/3021] the components to be mixed being combined in a single independent droplet, e.g. these droplets being divided by a non-miscible fluid or consisting of independent droplets -- B01F 33/3021 covers mixing processes where the substances to be combined exist as a single independent droplet, often surrounded or partitioned by a non-miscible fluid, so that the droplet itself acts as the reaction vessel. This sub-class falls under PERFORMING OPERATIONS → SEPARATING → MIXING (e.g. dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing) → Other mixers. It addresses techniques that isolate each droplet while still allowing the components within it to intermix.
[B01F 33/3022] the components being formed by independent droplets which are alternated, the mixing of the components being achieved by diffusion between droplets -- It covers mixing techniques where separate droplets of different substances are arranged in an alternating sequence and allowed to mix by diffusion across droplet interfaces, typically used for controlled micro-scale mixing without mechanical agitation.
[B01F 33/3031] using electro-hydrodynamic [EHD] or electro-kinetic [EKI] phenomena to mix or move the fluids -- This subclass covers devices or processes that employ electro-hydrodynamic or electro-kinetic effects to induce fluid motion for mixing, dispersion, or transport. Such techniques use electric fields to generate forces within liquids, enabling dissolution, emulsifying, or other mixing actions without mechanical agitators.
[B01F 33/3032] using magneto-hydrodynamic [MHD] phenomena to mix or move the fluids -- The B01F 33/3032 subclass covers mixing or moving fluids by exploiting magneto-hydrodynamic (MHD) effects, whereby magnetic fields interact with electrically conductive liquids to induce flow or dispersion without mechanical agitators. This approach is classified under other mixers within the broader category of separating and mixing operations.
[B01F 33/3033] using heat to mix or move the fluids -- B01F 33/3033 covers processes that employ heat to induce mixing or fluid motion, using thermal energy rather than mechanical agitation to dissolve, emulsify, disperse or otherwise combine substances. The heat creates convection currents or reduces viscosity, facilitating the blending of liquids or suspensions in chemical and material processing.
[B01F 33/3034] using induced convection or movement in the mixture to mix or move the fluids without mechanical means, e.g. thermodynamic instability, strong gradients, etc. -- The subclass B01F 33/3034 refers to mixing or fluid displacement methods that rely on induced convection or other non-mechanical movements within the mixture. It includes processes that generate flow by thermodynamic instability, strong temperature or concentration gradients, buoyancy or similar effects, rather than using rotating or reciprocating equipment.
[B01F 33/3035] using surface tension to mix, move or hold the fluids -- This CPC subclass (B01F 33/3035) covers processes and devices that exploit surface tension to mix, move, or hold fluids without mechanical agitation. It includes methods where capillary or interfacial forces drive the mixing, emulsifying, or dispersing of liquids and related apparatus for such operations.
[B01F 33/30351] using hydrophilic/hydrophobic surfaces -- The code B01F 33/30351 falls under mixing operations where the process employs surfaces that are either water-attracting or water-repelling to aid dissolution, emulsification or dispersion of materials. Such techniques use patterned or coated hydrophilic/hydrophobic areas to control fluid flow, droplet formation or particle distribution during mixing. It is a sub-class of other mixers within the separating and performing operations hierarchy.
[B01F 33/30352] using roughness of the surfaces -- B01F 33/30352 covers mixing processes that exploit the roughness of contacting surfaces to enhance dispersion, dissolution, emulsification or other separating actions. By using textured or uneven surfaces, the technique promotes more efficient breakup of particles or droplets during the mixing operation. This approach falls within the broader class of mixers in the performing-operations hierarchy.
[B01F 33/3036] using a biological motor, i.e. biological molecules which are activated and movement is induced to stir a fluid -- B01F 33/3036 covers mixing processes that employ biological motors-such as motor proteins or other activated biomolecules-to generate movement and stir a fluid. It is classified under performing operations, separating, mixing (e.g., dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing), and specifically the sub-category of other mixers.
[B01F 33/3037] using coupled electrorotation [CER] phenomena to mix or move fluids, or to sense properties of the mixture -- B01F 33/3037 covers techniques that employ coupled electrorotation-simultaneous rotation of particles induced by an electric field-to agitate or transport liquids, or to detect characteristics of the mixture, as a method of mixing, dispersing or sensing within fluid-processing operations.
[B01F 33/3038] using ciliary stirrers to move or stir the fluids -- The code covers processes that employ ciliary-type stirrers-arrays of microscopic, hair-like elements that beat in a coordinated fashion-to create localized flow and mix liquids. These devices mimic biological cilia and are used in micro-reactors, lab-on-a-chip and other small-scale fluid handling systems.
[B01F 33/3039] with mixing achieved by diffusion between layers -- This subclass covers processes or devices where mixing of components occurs primarily through diffusion across adjacent layers rather than mechanical stirring. It is used for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing where two or more liquid or semi-solid layers are brought into contact and the substances intermix by molecular diffusion.
[B01F 33/304] the mixing being performed in a mixing chamber where the products are brought into contact -- B01F 33/304 covers mixing operations carried out in a mixing chamber where the separate product streams are introduced so that they come into direct contact and combine. It pertains to mixers that rely on the chamber's geometry and flow patterns to achieve dissolution, emulsifying or dispersion of the materials. This subclass falls under PERFORMING OPERATIONS → SEPARATING → MIXING, specifically under "Other mixers."
[B01F 33/3045] using turbulence on microscale -- B01F 33/3045 pertains to the use of microscale turbulence to enhance mixing operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing, typically employing specialized mixers that generate turbulent flow at very small dimensions. This approach falls within the broader categories of performing operations, separating and mixing, and is classified under other mixers.
[B01F 33/305] using mixing means not otherwise provided for  B01F25/00 ,  B01F27/00 ,  B01F29/00 ,  B01F31/00 ,  B01F33/301  -  B01F33/3045 ,  B01F33/40  and  B01F33/45  take precedence -- The code B01F 33/305 covers mixing operations in which the apparatus or method is not specified elsewhere in the related subclasses for separating, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It applies to other mixers used in processes that fall under the broader categories B01F25/00, B01F27/00, B01F29/00, B01F31/00, B01F33/301-3045, B01F33/40 and B01F33/45, taking precedence over those more specific entries.
[B01F 33/35] Mixing after turning the mixing vessel upside down -- This class covers apparatus or methods where the mixing container is inverted after initial mixing to cause further agitation, often to improve dispersion or ensure uniformity. It includes devices that automatically flip the vessel or manual procedures that turn the vessel upside down during the mixing cycle, used for powders, liquids, or emulsions where gravity-assisted mixing enhances the process.
[B01F 33/40] Mixers using gas or liquid agitation, e.g. with air supply tubes -- The agitating fluid is not meant to mix with the material.
[B01F 33/401] Methods -- B01F 33/401 covers methods involving the use of miscellaneous mixing equipment not otherwise classified, applied in processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing during separation operations. The subclass addresses procedural aspects of employing such mixers to achieve uniform mixtures or aid material separation.
[B01F 33/402] comprising supplementary stirring elements -- B01F 33/402 covers mixers that incorporate additional stirring components alongside the primary agitator, such as secondary impellers, baffles, or auxiliary blades, to enhance the mixing, dispersing or emulsifying action. These supplementary elements are intended to improve flow patterns, reduce dead zones, and increase efficiency in processes like dissolving or emulsifying.
[B01F 33/4021] the gas being introduced through the shaft of the stirring element -- B01F 33/4021 relates to mixers in which a gas is delivered through the shaft of the stirring element, allowing the gas to be injected directly into the material being agitated. The arrangement is used to improve dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing actions by combining mechanical agitation with simultaneous gas introduction, typical in chemical, food or pharmaceutical processing.
[B01F 33/403] for mixing liquids  B01F33/402 ,  B01F33/405 ,  B01F33/406  take precedence -- B01F 33/403 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to mixers specifically designed for mixing liquids, with a note that more specific codes such as B01F33/402, B01F33/405, and B01F33/406 should be used when applicable.
[B01F 33/404] for mixing material moving continuously therethrough, e.g. using impinging jets -- B01F 33/404 denotes mixers that handle material moving continuously through the unit, typically by directing opposing streams of fluid or solids that collide and mix, such as impinging jet designs.
[B01F 33/405] in receptacles having guiding conduits therein, e.g. for feeding the gas to the bottom of the receptacle -- B01F 33/405 pertains to mixers that work inside receptacles provided with guiding conduits, for example systems that feed gas to the bottom of the container to enhance dissolution, emulsification or dispersion.
[B01F 33/4051] with vertical conduits through which the material is being moved upwardly driven by the fluid -- B01F 33/4051 covers mixers that incorporate vertical conduits through which the material is lifted upward by a flowing fluid, enabling continuous movement and intimate contact for processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. The design uses fluid-driven transport to achieve mixing while the material ascends within the device.
[B01F 33/40511] with a central conduit or a central set of conduits -- B01F 33/40511 covers mixing apparatus that incorporates a central channel or series of channels through which material flows, allowing the substance to be introduced, recirculated, or discharged while mixing occurs. The design typically enables efficient mixing by directing the medium through the core of the vessel, often improving homogenization and heat transfer.
[B01F 33/40512] involving gas diffusers at the bottom -- The subclass B01F 33/40512 covers mixing equipment or methods that employ gas diffusers located at the bottom of a vessel to aid dissolution, emulsification or dispersion of material phases. It pertains to other types of mixers used in separating operations.
[B01F 33/406] in receptacles with gas supply only at the bottom  B01F33/405  takes precedence -- This subclass covers mixing devices used in receptacles where the gas is introduced solely at the bottom, a configuration applied to operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It belongs to the "Other mixers" group and is subordinated to subclass B01F33/405, which takes precedence.
[B01F 33/4061] through orifices arranged around a central cone  B01F33/4062  takes precedence -- B01F 33/4061 covers mixers that create fluid flow by directing streams through multiple openings positioned around a central conical element, producing shear and turbulence for dispersing, dissolving or emulsifying substances. The design uses a central cone surrounded by orifices to enhance mixing efficiency.
[B01F 33/4062] with means for modifying the gas pressure or for supplying gas at different pressures or in different volumes at different parts of the bottom -- This subclass covers mixing devices that incorporate mechanisms to adjust gas pressure or deliver gas at varying pressures or volumes to different areas of the mixing vessel base, allowing better control of the mixing process. Such equipment is used in processes where precise gas distribution enhances dissolution, emulsification or dispersion.
[B01F 33/407] by blowing gas on the material from above -- Mixing processes that use a gas jet directed onto the material from above to agitate, disperse or emulsify it, typically employing air or another gas stream to create turbulence and enhance contact between components. This method is applied when the material is spread in a shallow layer or on a conveyor and the gas flow provides the required mixing action. It falls under the subclass for mixers that operate by blowing gas onto the material.
[B01F 33/408] Controlling -- B01F 33/408 covers mixers that incorporate control mechanisms to regulate their operation, such as speed, torque, temperature or mixing time, enabling precise adjustment of the mixing process for consistent product quality. It applies to equipment where the control system is integral to the mixer rather than an external add-on, and includes feedback and automation features that manage the mixing action.
[B01F 33/409] Parts, e.g. diffusion elements; Accessories -- The CPC subclass B01F 33/409 covers components used with mixers that perform separating operations, such as diffusion elements, baffles, or other accessories that aid in dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing materials. These parts are not the primary mixing elements themselves but support or enhance the mixing process.
[B01F 33/4092] Storing receptacles provided with separate mixing chambers -- B01F 33/4092 covers storage receptacles that incorporate one or more separate mixing chambers, allowing material to be held and mixed within the same container without external equipment. The design is intended for processes that combine storage and mixing, such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing, while keeping the mixed portion isolated from the rest of the contents.
[B01F 33/4094] Plants -- B01F 33/4094 refers to plants in the context of mixing operations, specifically addressing systems or apparatus designed for mixing processes within plant settings. This classification is part of a detailed hierarchy that narrows down from performing operations and separating to specific types of mixers used in industrial plants.
[B01F 33/45] Magnetic mixers -- B01F 33/45 magnetic mixers cover devices that use a magnetic-field to agitate or stir fluids without direct mechanical contact, typically by a rotating magnetic element driven by an external field. They are employed for dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing substances in laboratory, industrial or food-processing operations.
[B01F 33/451] wherein the mixture is directly exposed to an electromagnetic field without use of a stirrer, e.g. for material comprising ferromagnetic particles or for molten metal -- This CPC subclass covers mixing operations in which the mixture is acted on directly by an electromagnetic field, eliminating the need for a mechanical stirrer. It includes processes for materials such as ferromagnetic particle suspensions or molten metal where the field induces motion to achieve dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, separating, mixing, other mixers.
[B01F 33/452] using independent floating stirring elements -- B01F 33/452 covers mixing methods that employ independent stirring elements that float freely in the material being treated rather than being fixed to a shaft or frame. The floating agitators create turbulence to dissolve, emulsify or disperse substances, providing efficient mixing without direct mechanical contact.
[B01F 33/453] using supported or suspended stirring elements -- B01F 33/453 concerns mixing processes that employ stirring elements which are either supported on a fixed frame or suspended within the material being treated, such as paddles, blades or impellers mounted on shafts or hung from rods, and are used for operations like dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 33/4531] using an axis supported in several points for mounting the stirring element -- This subclass covers mixers in which the stirrer is mounted on a shaft that is supported at several points, providing enhanced rigidity and precise positioning during operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. The multi-point support helps maintain consistent agitation and reduces vibration in the mixing vessel.
[B01F 33/4532] using a bearing, tube, opening or gap for internally supporting the stirring element -- B01F 33/4532 covers mixers that employ a bearing, tube, opening or gap to provide internal support for the stirring element. It falls under the mixing subclass for devices where the agitator is held in place by an internal support structure rather than external mounts.
[B01F 33/4533] supporting the stirring element in one point -- This subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification covers mixers where the stirring element is supported at a single point, typically by a shaft bearing or pivot, allowing it to rotate while being held in one location. Such arrangements are used in equipment for dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing media where a simple support reduces mechanical complexity and space requirements.
[B01F 33/4534] using a rod for supporting the stirring element, e.g. stirrer sliding on a rod or mounted on a rod sliding in a tube -- This subclass covers mixers that employ a rod to support and guide the stirring element, allowing the stirrer to slide along the rod or be mounted on a rod that moves within a tube, improving stability and positioning during mixing.
[B01F 33/4535] using a stud for supporting the stirring element -- This subclass concerns mixers in which the stirring element is mounted on a stud that supports and positions the agitator during operation. The stud-supported design helps maintain alignment, transmit torque efficiently, and reduce wear while performing actions such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 33/4536] using a wire for supporting or suspending the stirring element, e.g. stirrer sliding on a wire -- This subclass addresses mixing devices where the agitator is held or hung from a wire, allowing the stirrer to slide along the wire during operation. It covers designs that employ a wire support for the stirring element in processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 33/4537] the stirring element being suspended by one point -- The subclass B01F 33/4537 covers mixers in which the stirring element is supported from a single point, such as a pivot, hinge or hanging attachment, allowing it to swing or rotate while immersed in the material. This design is used to improve agitation, blending or dispersion in processes like dissolving or emulsifying.
[B01F 33/50] Movable or transportable mixing devices or plants -- B01F 33/50 covers mixing equipment that can be moved or transported, including portable agitators, drum-mixers, mobile blenders and other self-contained devices designed for on-site or temporary mixing operations rather than fixed plant installations. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating and mixing such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 33/501] Movable mixing devices, i.e. readily shifted or displaced from one place to another, e.g. portable during use -- The CPC subclass B01F 33/501 covers movable mixing devices, meaning apparatus that can be readily shifted or displaced from one place to another during use, such as portable mixers employed for dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing substances.
[B01F 33/5011] portable during use, e.g. hand-held -- A portable, hand-held mixer designed for use while operating, enabling on-site mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing of materials. It falls under the "Other mixers" subgroup of the separating and mixing operations class.
[B01F 33/50111] Small portable bottles, flasks, vials, e.g. with means for mixing ingredients or for homogenizing their content, e.g. by hand shaking -- B01F 33/50111 covers small portable bottles, flasks or vials that include means for mixing or homogenizing their contents, usually by hand shaking. These containers are intended for convenient on-site blending of liquids, suspensions or powders and belong to the CPC subgroup for other mixers within the mixing and separating operations class.
[B01F 33/50112] of the syringe or cartridge type -- The entry B01F 33/50112 covers mixers that are formed as syringes or cartridges, designed to blend, dissolve, emulsify or disperse materials within a single, often disposable, unit. These devices typically combine a plunger-driven chamber with a mixing element, allowing rapid preparation of homogeneous mixtures directly before use.
[B01F 33/50113] of the pipette type -- B01F 33/50113 designates mixers of the pipette type, used to blend, dissolve, emulsify or disperse very small quantities of liquids or powders. These devices typically employ a narrow, elongated tip to draw and dispense material, enabling precise localized mixing in laboratory or analytical settings.
[B01F 33/50114] of the hand-held gun type -- The B01F 33/50114 subclass covers mixing devices that are of the hand-held gun type. These handheld mixers are used for operations such as separating, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing materials in a localized, manually directed manner. The code falls under the "Other mixers" group within the broader "Mixing, e.g. dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing" category of the CPC.
[B01F 33/50115] Stirring devices adapted to be connected to a standard boring machine or other kind of domestic tool -- B01F 33/50115 covers stirring devices that are built to be fitted onto a standard boring machine or other common domestic tools, turning them into mixers. These devices enable the tool to perform mixing operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing while retaining its original function. The class falls under the "other mixers" subgroup of the CPC hierarchy for mixing and separating operations.
[B01F 33/5012] adapted to be mounted during use on a standard, base or support -- The subclass B01F 33/5012 covers mixing devices that are designed to be mounted on a standard, base or support while they are operating. These mixers are not integral to a fixed installation but can be attached to a separate holder or frame during use, allowing flexible positioning in processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. This entry falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating and mixing.
[B01F 33/5013] movable by mechanical means, e.g. hoisting systems, grippers or lift trucks -- B01F 33/5013 refers to devices that move mixers by mechanical means such as hoisting systems, grippers or lift trucks. It is classified under performing operations → separating → mixing, e.g. dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing → other mixers.
[B01F 33/5014] movable by human force, e.g. kitchen or table devices -- B01F 33/5014 covers manually operated mixing equipment such as hand-cranked or push-type devices used on a kitchen counter or tabletop to blend, dissolve, emulsify or disperse materials. The class falls under Performing Operations, Separating, Mixing, other mixers and includes portable human-force driven mixers.
[B01F 33/502] Vehicle-mounted mixing devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Vehicle mounted mixing devices which cannot be separated from the movable vehicle.
[B01F 33/5021] the vehicle being self-propelled, e.g. truck mounted, provided with a motor, driven by tracks  B01F33/5022  -  B01F33/5027  take precedence -- B01F 33/5021 covers self-propelled mixing devices, such as truck-mounted units that incorporate their own motor and are driven by tracks, used for separating or mixing operations. It is a subcategory of other mixers, and more specific sub-entries B01F 33/5022-33/5027 take precedence over it.
[B01F 33/5022] the vehicle being a carriage moving or driving along fixed or movable beams or bridges -- B01F 33/5022 classifies mixers in which the mixing unit is mounted on a carriage that travels along a fixed or movable beam or bridge, providing linear motion to convey material while mixing, such as continuous or batch mixers that use a rail-guided vehicle for material transport and processing.
[B01F 33/5023] the vehicle being a trailer which is hand moved or coupled to self-propelling vehicles -- B01F 33/5023 covers mixing apparatus whose vehicle is a trailer that can be moved by hand or coupled to a self-propelling vehicle, allowing the mixer to be transported and operated as a mobile unit for processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 33/5024] the vehicle being moved by human force -- B01F 33/5024 pertains to mixing devices where the movement or operation is powered by human effort, such as hand-operated mixers. This classification is part of a broader category that includes various types of mixers and their mechanisms.
[B01F 33/5025] using rails for guiding the mixing installation during moving or displacing -- The subclass B01F 33/5025 covers mixers in which the mixing unit is moved or displaced along rails that act as guides, ensuring precise positioning during operation. It is used for installations where the mixing apparatus is mounted on a rail system to facilitate controlled movement while mixing. This falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and mixing, specifically other mixers.
[B01F 33/5026] using sledges or skids for moving or displacing the mixing installation -- The code B01F 33/5026 covers the use of sledges or skids to shift or reposition a mixing installation, facilitating transport or repositioning of the equipment during operation. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and mixing, specifically within the subclass for other mixers.
[B01F 33/5027] using driven tracks, caterpillars or crawler for moving or displacing the mixing installation -- B01F 33/5027 pertains to mixing installations that utilize driven tracks, such as caterpillars or crawlers, for movement or displacement. This classification is specific to equipment designed for mobility in rough or challenging terrains during mixing operations.
[B01F 33/503] Floating mixing devices -- Floating mixing devices are apparatuses that remain buoyant within a liquid to provide agitation, typically using paddles, propellers, or other impellers that rotate or oscillate while floating. They are employed to promote dispersion, dissolution, emulsification or homogenisation in tanks, ponds, or reactors where submerged mechanical drives are impractical, often powered externally or by the fluid flow itself.
[B01F 33/70] Mixers specially adapted for working at sub- or super-atmospheric pressure, e.g. combined with de-foaming -- B01F 33/70 covers mixers that are specifically designed to operate at pressures below or above atmospheric level, often incorporating de-foaming functions. These devices belong to the CPC subgroup for other mixers within the broader class of mixing operations used in chemical or mechanical processes.
[B01F 33/71] working at super-atmospheric pressure, e.g. in pressurised vessels -- B01F 33/71 covers mixers that operate under super-atmospheric pressure, such as in pressurised vessels, where the elevated pressure aids dissolution, emulsification or dispersion of substances. The classification is applied to equipment and processes that rely on high-pressure environments to enhance mixing efficiency.
[B01F 33/80] Mixing plants -- B01F 33/80 classifies industrial mixing plants that perform bulk mixing operations, such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing, within the broader categories of separating and mixing. It covers large-scale apparatus designed to handle continuous or batch processes for chemicals, foodstuffs, polymers and similar materials, distinct from more specific mixer types.
[B01F 33/805] for granular material -- The CPC subclass B01F 33/805 pertains to mixers that handle granular material, such as powders or solid particles, using mechanisms other than standard rotating or blade mixers. It includes specialised devices that achieve uniform distribution of the granules without involving dissolution, emulsifying or dispersing actions, and is relevant to processes in pharmaceuticals, chemicals, food production and similar fields.
[B01F 33/8051] with several silos arranged in a row or around a central delivery point, e.g. provided with proportioning means -- B01F 33/8051 covers mixers that incorporate multiple silos positioned either in a line or around a central feed point, allowing material to be drawn from several storage units. The arrangement may include proportioning devices to control the relative amounts of each silo's content during mixing.
[B01F 33/80514] the silos being arranged in a circular configuration, i.e. in a circle around a central delivery point -- This subclass covers arrangements where storage silos are positioned in a circular layout around a central delivery point, enabling material to be drawn uniformly from the surrounding silos into the mixing system.
[B01F 33/8052] involving other than mixing operations, e.g. milling, sieving or drying -- In the CPC, B01F 33/8052 covers equipment or processes that perform operations related to mixing but are not mixing per se, such as milling, sieving, or drying of materials. It sits under the hierarchy PERFORMING OPERATIONS - SEPARATING - MIXING, e.g. dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing - Other mixers.
[B01F 33/81] Combinations of similar mixers, e.g. with rotary stirring devices in two or more receptacles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mixers where the material is in a type of mixer, e.g. static mixer, then the material is moved into another similar mixer.
[B01F 33/811] in two or more consecutive, i.e. successive, mixing receptacles or being consecutively arranged -- The B01F 33/811 subgroup covers mixing operations performed in two or more successive mixing receptacles that are arranged one after another, so that the material undergoes consecutive mixing stages as it moves through the series of vessels. This includes equipment where multiple mixing chambers are linked in series to achieve further dispersion or homogenisation.
[B01F 33/812] in two or more alternative mixing receptacles, e.g. mixing in one receptacle and dispensing from another receptacle -- B01F 33/812 pertains to mixing processes that involve the use of two or more separate mixing containers, such as systems where ingredients are mixed in one vessel and then dispensed from a different one for further processing or storage.
[B01F 33/813] mixing simultaneously in two or more mixing receptacles -- B01F 33/813 relates to processes where mixing actions are performed concurrently in two or more separate mixing receptacles, enabling parallel blending such as simultaneous dissolution, emulsifying or dispersing. The code applies to equipment or methods that allow multiple vessels to operate together, increasing throughput or ensuring uniformity across batches.
[B01F 33/82] Combinations of dissimilar mixers -- B01F 33/82 covers apparatus or processes that combine two or more mixers of different types, such as a rotor-stator together with a planetary or a high-shear impeller, to achieve complementary mixing actions. The classification is used for devices where dissimilar mixing mechanisms are integrated to enhance dispersion, dissolution, emulsification or other material handling functions.
[B01F 33/821] with consecutive receptacles -- B01F 33/821 covers mixers that employ a series of consecutive receptacles for processing materials, such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing substances. The classification falls under performing operations, separating, and mixing, and is used for devices where successive containers enable continuous or staged mixing actions.
[B01F 33/8212] with moving and non-moving stirring devices -- B01F 33/8212 classifies mixers that employ a combination of moving and stationary stirring elements to achieve mixing, dissolution, emulsification or dispersion. It covers devices where at least one agitator rotates while another part remains fixed, providing enhanced shear and flow control during the process.
[B01F 33/822] with moving and non-moving stirring devices in the same receptacle -- B01F 33/822 covers mixers that incorporate both moving and non-moving stirring devices in the same receptacle, enabling simultaneous agitation and static interaction for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 33/823] in two or more alternative mixing receptacles, e.g. mixing in one receptacle and dispensing from another receptacle -- This CPC subclass defines devices or methods that mix material in one receptacle while concurrently dispensing the product from a different receptacle, using two or more alternative mixing containers. It covers arrangements that allow continuous or alternating mixing and delivery to improve process efficiency or product control.
[B01F 33/824] mixing simultaneously in two or more mixing receptacles -- B01F 33/824 covers apparatus or processes that mix materials at the same time in two or more separate mixing vessels, allowing parallel blending of different batches or stages. The simultaneous operation improves efficiency in dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing tasks where multiple mixers are employed.
[B01F 33/83] Mixing plants specially adapted for mixing in combination with disintegrating operations -- B01F 33/83 covers mixing plants that are specifically designed to perform mixing together with disintegration processes such as comminution, grinding or crushing. These plants integrate mixers with equipment that breaks down raw material, enabling simultaneous blending and size reduction.
[B01F 33/8305] Devices with one shaft, provided with mixing and milling tools, e.g. using balls or rollers as working tools; Devices with two or more tools rotating about the same axis -- This subclass covers apparatus that use a single shaft equipped with mixing or milling implements such as balls, rollers or similar tools, and also devices where two or more tools are mounted on the same shaft and rotate together. It is applied to mixers and mills that combine agitation and grinding actions on a common axis.
[B01F 33/831] Devices with consecutive working receptacles, e.g. with two intermeshing tools in one of the receptacles  B01F33/8305  takes precedence -- Devices that have a series of working chambers or receptacles where the material passes from one to the next, such as a mixer that contains two intermeshing tools positioned in a single chamber, are covered by CPC entry B01F 33/831. This subclass falls under mixing operations involving consecutive receptacles, allowing sequential processing like dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing within the same equipment. It is subordinate to B01F 33/8305, which has priority.
[B01F 33/833] Devices with several tools rotating about different axis in the same receptacle -- Devices in which two or more mixing elements are mounted so that each rotates around its own axis while sharing a single container, enabling simultaneous actions such as stirring, grinding, or dispersing within the same batch. The arrangement allows complex flow patterns and improves efficiency in processes that require thorough mixing or separation of components.
[B01F 33/834] Mixing in several steps, e.g. successive steps  B01F33/81 ,  B01F33/82  and  B01F33/85  take precedence -- This subclass covers processes that achieve mixing through a series of distinct stages, each possibly involving different actions such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing, and it requires that the preceding steps defined in B01F33/81, B01F33/82 and B01F33/85 be applied in sequence.
[B01F 33/836] combining mixing with other treatments -- B01F 33/836 covers equipment or processes that perform mixing together with another treatment in a single operation, such as simultaneous stirring and heating, cooling, reacting, or phase-changing. It includes mixers that incorporate additional steps like dissolution, emulsification or dispersion combined with further processing. The class is used for integrated mixers that go beyond simple blending.
[B01F 33/8361] with disintegrating -- B01F 33/8361 covers mixers designed to incorporate a disintegration function, wherein the material being mixed is broken down into smaller particles during the mixing process. It applies to equipment that combines mixing actions such as dissolving or emulsifying with mechanical or chemical disintegration, typically used in pharmaceutical, food or chemical processing.
[B01F 33/83611] by cutting -- This subclass covers mixers that create blending by cutting the material with rotating or reciprocating cutting elements such as knives, rotors or blades, rather than relying on shear or turbulence. The cutting action fragments and disperses components to promote dissolution, emulsification or dispersion, and the primary mixing mechanism is the mechanical cutting of the bulk.
[B01F 33/83612] by crushing or breaking -- This subclass covers mixers that achieve blending by crushing or breaking the material, using mechanical impact, shear or fractures to disperse components. It applies to devices where the mixing action relies on breaking particles rather than stirring, tumbling or fluid motion.
[B01F 33/83613] by grinding or milling -- This CPC subclass covers mixers that achieve blending by grinding or milling the material, using abrasive or crushing actions to disperse and combine components. It includes equipment where the mixing action is produced by the relative movement of grinding surfaces, suitable for powders, pastes or liquids that require fine dispersion.
[B01F 33/83614] with irradiating -- A CPC subclass for mixing operations that employ irradiation during the process, covering equipment and methods used to dissolve, emulsify or disperse materials while exposing them to radiation such as UV, microwave or electron beams, classified under other mixers.
[B01F 33/8362] with chemical reactions -- B01F 33/8362 covers apparatus that carries out mixing while a chemical reaction occurs, typically using a mixer that is not otherwise specified. It includes other mixers integrated in reactors where dissolution, emulsification or dispersion takes place concurrently with the reaction.
[B01F 33/8363] with coating -- This subgroup covers mixers that simultaneously apply a coating to the material being mixed, such as equipment that combines polymer, paint or other surface-layer substances with particles during dispersion, emulsification or dissolution. It includes devices designed for one-step coating-while-mixing operations.
[B01F 33/8364] with drying -- The CPC subclass B01F 33/8364 refers to mixing operations that incorporate a drying step, such as mixers equipped with heating, vacuum or air-flow devices that remove moisture from the material being blended. It falls under "Other mixers" in the hierarchy of performing operations, separating, mixing (including dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing). This subclass covers processes where drying is integrated directly into the mixing apparatus.
[B01F 33/84] Mixing plants with mixing receptacles receiving material dispensed from several component receptacles, e.g. paint tins -- A mixing plant in which the main mixing receptacle receives its ingredients directly from several separate component containers, such as multiple paint tins, is covered by this subclass. It pertains to equipment that integrates the dispensing of individual material streams into a single mixing chamber for processes like dissolution, emulsification or dispersion.
[B01F 33/841] with component receptacles fixed in a circular configuration on a horizontal table, e.g. the table being able to be indexed about a vertical axis -- This subclass covers mixers in which the containers for the materials are mounted permanently around a circular layout on a horizontal turntable that can be rotated about a vertical axis during operation. The design enables simultaneous processing of several batches and facilitates uniform mixing such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 33/844] with means for customizing the mixture on the point of sale, e.g. by sensing, receiving or analysing information about the characteristics of the mixture to be made -- A mixer that can tailor a product at the point of sale by detecting, receiving or analyzing data about the target composition, then adjusting the mixing process accordingly. It integrates sensors or communication links to customize the final blend in real time, allowing on-demand formulation of liquids, powders or emulsions.
[B01F 33/8442] using a computer for controlling information and converting it in a formula and a set of operation instructions, e.g. on the point of sale -- B01F 33/8442 pertains to mixers that utilize computers for control, specifically for processing information into formulas and operational instructions, often at the point of sale or use. This classification covers systems where computer technology is integrated to enhance mixing processes through precise control and data conversion.
[B01F 33/846] using stored recipes for determining the composition of the mixture to be produced, i.e. for determining the amounts of the basic components to be dispensed from the component receptacles -- This subclass covers processes in which a previously stored formulation or recipe is consulted to calculate the precise quantities of each basic component to be dispensed from their containers before the mixing operation. It is used for automated or computer-controlled mixers that select ingredient amounts based on the recipe data.
[B01F 33/848] using data, i.e. barcodes, 3D codes or similar type of tagging information, as instruction or identification codes for controlling the dispensing and mixing operations -- This subclass covers mixers that employ encoded data such as barcodes, QR or 3-D codes to direct or identify the dispensing and mixing process. The encoded information serves as a programmable instruction set, enabling automated control of ingredient release, dosage and blending parameters.
[B01F 33/85] Mixing plants with mixing receptacles or mixing tools that can be indexed into different working positions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mixing plants where the receptacles are capable of moving through the plant to a plurality of locations for performing different mixing operations.
[B01F 33/86] Mixing heads comprising a driven stirrer -- B01F 33/86 covers mixing heads that incorporate a driven stirrer, used in processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing within separating equipment. The design places the stirrer directly in the mixing head to enhance mixing efficiency and control.
[B01F 33/862] the stirrer being provided with a surrounding stator -- B01F 33/862 relates to mixers in which the rotating stirrer is surrounded by a stationary stator that forms a fixed flow field around the rotor. The arrangement enhances shear and dispersion for operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating and mixing.
[B01F 33/87] Roll-type mixers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mixers having at least one roller rotating around its longitudinal axis, typically having at least two rollers pressing against each other to homogenize and mix a material.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[B01F 35/00] Accessories for mixers -- B01F 35/00 covers devices that supplement or enhance mixers, including shafts, blades, guides, seals, control or monitoring elements and other ancillary components intended to improve the efficiency, safety or precision of mixing processes such as dissolution, emulsification or dispersion.
[B01F 35/10] Maintenance of mixers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Maintenance of mixers includes any process of preserving the proper functioning of the mixers, e.g. washing, cleaning, repairing or replacing of mixers.
[B01F 35/11] using fluids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Maintenance of mixers by the application of fluids for maintenance purposes, e.g. using oil for lubrication of parts or using cleaning fluids for cleaning of mixers.
[B01F 35/12] using mechanical means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Maintenance of mixers by using mechanical means, e.g. knockers or scrapers to dislodge material that sticks to the surface of the mixer.
[B01F 35/121] using a brush for cleaning out rests of products -- B01F 35/121 pertains to accessories for mixers that employ a brush to clean residual product from the mixing chamber or blades. The brush is designed to scrape or sweep away adhered material after operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing, facilitating maintenance and preventing cross-contamination.
[B01F 35/122] using pushers, i.e. a piston, for pushing out rests of products -- The B01F 35/122 subclass covers accessories for mixers that use a pusher, such as a piston, to expel residual product from the mixing chamber. It relates to equipment designed to improve discharge of material in mixing or separating operations.
[B01F 35/123] using scrapers for cleaning mixers -- The CPC subclass B01F 35/123 covers accessories for mixers that employ scrapers to remove material adhering to the mixing vessel or impeller surfaces. It includes designs and methods for using scraping tools to clean mixers after operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 35/13] using one or more of the components of the mixture to wash-out the mixer -- The subclass B01F 35/13 covers methods where one or more ingredients of a mixture are employed to clean the mixing equipment itself, such as using residual solvent, water, or surfactant in the batch to wash out the mixer during or after operation. This approach integrates cleaning into the processing step, reducing the need for separate rinsing devices or chemicals. It falls under accessories for mixers within the broader category of separating and mixing operations.
[B01F 35/145] Washing or cleaning mixers not provided for in other groups in this subclass; Inhibiting build-up of material on machine parts using other means -- B01F 35/145 covers washing or cleaning mixers that are not covered in other groups of the subclass, as well as methods or devices that prevent material build-up on mixer parts by alternative means.
[B01F 35/1452] using fluids -- This subclass covers mixer accessories that introduce or handle fluids during operation, including devices for feeding liquids, slurries or dissolved substances to aid mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing processes. It falls under mixing, separating, performing operations.
[B01F 35/1453] by means of jets of fluid, e.g. air -- B01F 35/1453 relates to accessories for mixers that employ jets of fluid, such as air, to achieve mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing of materials. The subcategory covers devices that direct fluid streams into the material to create turbulence or shear without mechanical impellers.
[B01F 35/146] Working under sterile conditions; Sterilizing the mixer or parts thereof -- This subclass covers methods and equipment for operating mixers in sterile environments, including sterilization of the mixer or its components, typically for pharmaceutical, food, or biotech processes where contamination must be avoided.
[B01F 35/165] Making mixers or parts thereof -- This class covers devices and components for constructing mixers or their ancillary parts, such as shafts, blades, housings and other accessories that enable mixing, separating, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing operations. It falls under the mixing subgroup of the Performing Operations section of the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[B01F 35/181] Preventing generation of dust or dirt; Sieves; Filters  B01F35/145 ,  B01F35/43  take precedence -- The B01F 35/181 subclass defines accessories mounted on mixers that restrict the release of dust or dirt, typically by incorporating sieves, filters or similar particle-capture elements during mixing operations. It is situated under the broader categories for mixing and dust-prevention and supersedes the more specific subclasses B01F 35/145 and B01F 35/43.
[B01F 35/184] Preventing generation of dust -- B01F 35/184 covers devices or methods attached to mixers that reduce or eliminate dust released during mixing processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. These accessories may include protective enclosures, air filtration systems, or sealed containers to contain particulate matter.
[B01F 35/186] using splash guards in mixers for avoiding dirt or projection of material -- The CPC entry B01F 35/186 covers the use of splash guards attached to mixers as accessories that prevent dirt, spray or material from being expelled during mixing operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It falls within the hierarchy of Performing Operations → Separating → Mixing.
[B01F 35/187] using filters in mixers, e.g. during venting -- B01F 35/187 relates to accessories for mixers that incorporate filtration elements, such as screens or membranes, to separate particles or liquids during the mixing process, particularly when venting is required. The classification covers designs where filters are integral to the mixer to maintain product quality and prevent contamination.
[B01F 35/1872] Filters for micro-living organisms, i.e. filtering of the mixture -- The subclass B01F 35/1872 covers accessories for mixers that filter out microorganisms from a mixture, such as filters designed to retain bacteria, yeast or other micro-living organisms during mixing or dispersion processes. It falls under the mixing-and-separating hierarchy.
[B01F 35/188] using sieves in mixers for purposes other than mixing, e.g. eliminating dust during venting -- This subclass pertains to mixer accessories that employ sieves for functions other than the primary mixing action, such as removing dust or particulate matter when venting the material. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations → separating → mixing (including dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing).
[B01F 35/189] Venting, degassing or ventilating of gases, fumes or toxic vapours during mixing -- This CPC subclass covers methods and devices for removing or venting gases, fumes, or toxic vapours during a mixing operation, such as venting, degassing or ventilating while mixing. It applies to accessories for mixers that provide safe removal of volatile substances.
[B01F 35/20] Measuring -- B01F 35/20 pertains to accessories for mixers that monitor and display operating parameters. These devices measure quantities such as speed, torque, temperature, pressure or viscosity to help control the mixing process.
[B01F 35/21] Measuring -- The B01F 35/21 subclass groups measuring accessories for mixers, covering devices that monitor parameters such as speed, torque, temperature or viscosity while a mixing operation is performed. These instruments are designed to be fitted to or used with mixing equipment to provide real-time data for controlling or optimizing the mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing process.
[B01F 35/211] of the operational parameters  B01F35/212  and  B01F35/213  take precedence -- B01F 35/211 covers accessories for mixers used in processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing, where the control of operational parameters-defined in the related subclasses B01F35/212 and B01F35/213-takes precedence over other design aspects.
[B01F 35/2111] Flow rate -- B01F 35/2111 covers specifications and devices that monitor, regulate or indicate the flow rate of fluids or gases supplied to or removed from mixers, including the speed at which ingredients are added or withdrawn to affect mixing performance.
[B01F 35/21111] Mass flow rate -- The classification B01F 35/21111 covers accessories for mixers that are intended to measure, monitor or control the mass flow rate of material being mixed, dissolved, emulsified or dispersed. It belongs to the mixing-operation group where precise regulation of material per unit time is essential for consistent processing.
[B01F 35/21112] Volumetric flow rate -- This subclass covers accessories for mixers that provide or monitor the volumetric flow rate of material being processed. It includes flow-rate sensors, meters, or control devices used in mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing operations.
[B01F 35/2112] Level of material in a container or the position or shape of the upper surface of the material -- This subclass covers accessories for mixers that detect, indicate or regulate the level, position or shape of the material's upper surface in a container, such as sensors, sight-glasses, level indicators and devices that maintain a desired fill height during mixing.
[B01F 35/2113] Pressure -- The B01F 35/2113 subclass covers accessories for mixers that employ pressure in operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It includes devices that generate, transmit or maintain pressure within mixing equipment, for example pressurised vessels, sealed chambers or pressure-driven feed systems, enabling more efficient separation and mixing processes.
[B01F 35/2114] Speed of feeding material, e.g. bands or strips -- The B01F 35/2114 subclass covers features that control the speed at which material such as bands or strips is fed into a mixing device. It pertains to accessories for mixers that regulate feed rate to ensure consistent mixing performance. This falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and mixing processes.
[B01F 35/2115] Temperature -- The CPC subclass B01F 35/2115 refers to accessories for mixers that deal with temperature, including heating or cooling units, thermostatic controls and temperature sensors used to regulate or monitor the thermal conditions during mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing processes.
[B01F 35/21151] using infrared radiation thermometer or pyrometer or infrared sensors for temperature measurement without contact -- B01F 35/21151 covers accessories for mixers that employ infrared radiation thermometers, pyrometers or infrared sensors to measure temperature without contacting the material. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for performing operations, separating and mixing processes, specifically addressing non-contact temperature monitoring in mixing equipment.
[B01F 35/2116] Volume -- This subclass covers accessories for mixers that deal with the volume of material being processed. It includes devices such as volume indicators, graduated containers or control elements that let the operator set, monitor or adjust the amount of substance mixed.
[B01F 35/2117] Weight -- B01F 35/2117 pertains to accessories for mixers that involve weight, such as devices for measuring, loading or controlling the mass of materials during mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing operations within the broader category of separating and performing operations.
[B01F 35/212] of the driving system data, e.g. torque, speed or power data -- The entry B01F 35/212 covers accessories for mixers that provide data about the driving system, such as torque, speed or power. It includes devices that monitor, record or display these operational parameters to aid control and optimization of mixing processes.
[B01F 35/213] of the properties of the mixtures, e.g. temperature, density or colour -- B01F 35/213 pertains to accessories for mixers that monitor or control mixture properties such as temperature, density, or color during the mixing process.
[B01F 35/2131] Colour or luminescence -- The CPC entry B01F 35/2131 designates accessories for mixers that introduce colour or luminescence into the material being processed, such as dyed, pigmented, fluorescent or phosphorescent additives. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations → separating → mixing, specifically the sub-category of mixer accessories.
[B01F 35/2132] Concentration, pH, pOH, p(ION) or oxygen-demand  B01F35/2133  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass covers accessories attached to mixers that adjust or monitor solution concentration, pH, ion activity or oxygen demand, such as dosing, sampling or detection units. More specific subclass B01F35/2133 takes precedence for detailed implementations.
[B01F 35/2133] Electrical conductivity or dielectric constant of the mixture -- The CPC subclass B01F 35/2133 relates to accessories for mixers that enable measurement of the electrical conductivity or dielectric constant of the mixture being processed. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, separating, and mixing such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing, and covers devices that monitor these electrical properties during the mixing process.
[B01F 35/2134] Density or solids or particle number -- B01F 35/2134 covers accessories for mixers that determine or control the density, solid content, or particle count of the material being processed. It includes devices such as densitometers, solid-content sensors and particle-counting instruments used in mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing operations. These tools help monitor and adjust formulation properties during processing.
[B01F 35/2135] Humidity, e.g. moisture content -- The code B01F 35/2135 covers accessories for mixers that monitor or control humidity or moisture content during mixing, such as humidifiers, dehumidifiers or moisture sensors. It resides in the CPC hierarchy under performing operations, separating and mixing.
[B01F 35/2136] Viscosity -- B01F 35/2136 covers accessories for mixers that are used to measure, control or adjust the viscosity of a material during mixing operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing, including viscometers, viscosity probes and related feedback devices that help maintain proper consistency and performance.
[B01F 35/214] characterised by the means for measuring -- B01F 35/214 falls within the mixing subgroup of the Cooperative Patent Classification, covering accessories for mixers that incorporate means for measuring process variables such as temperature, viscosity or composition. It is intended for devices that combine mixing action with real-time monitoring to optimise dissolution, emulsification or dispersion operations.
[B01F 35/2142] using wireless sensors introduced in the mixture, e.g. transponders or RFID tags, for measuring the parameters of the mixture or components to be mixed -- This subclass covers accessories for mixers that embed wireless sensors such as transponders or RFID tags directly in the mixture, enabling real-time measurement of parameters like temperature, viscosity, composition or component concentration during mixing for process control and quality monitoring.
[B01F 35/2144] using radiation for measuring the parameters of the mixture or components to be mixed -- This subclass covers methods that employ radiation such as infrared, microwave or X-ray to determine properties like composition, concentration, temperature or particle size of materials while they are being mixed. It includes on-line or off-line measurement techniques that monitor the mixture or its components to control the mixing process.
[B01F 35/22] Control or regulation -- The B01F 35/22 subclass covers devices that control or regulate the operation of mixers used for separating or mixing processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It includes accessories that adjust parameters like speed, torque, temperature, pressure or safety interlocks to ensure consistent mixing performance.
[B01F 35/2201] characterised by the type of control technique used -- The CPC subclass B01F 35/2201 groups accessories for mixers that are distinguished primarily by the control technique employed, such as variable-speed drives, electronic or programmable controllers, torque-limiting devices or feedback-based regulation systems, rather than by their mechanical form or mixing function.
[B01F 35/2202] Controlling the mixing process by feed-back, i.e. a measured parameter of the mixture is measured, compared with the set-value and the feed values are corrected -- B01F 35/2202 relates to accessories for mixers that use feedback control to regulate the mixing operation. A parameter of the mixture is measured, compared with a preset value, and the feed inputs are adjusted accordingly to maintain the desired mixing conditions.
[B01F 35/2203] Controlling the mixing process by feed-forward, i.e. a parameter of the components to be mixed is measured and the feed values are calculated -- B01F 35/2203 relates to mixers that employ feed-forward control, measuring a property of the components such as temperature, viscosity or composition during operation and automatically calculating the feed rates to achieve the desired mixing outcome.
[B01F 35/2204] Controlling the mixing process by fuzzy control, i.e. a prescribed fuzzy rule -- B01F 35/2204 covers accessories for mixers that regulate the mixing operation using fuzzy-logic control, wherein a predefined fuzzy rule set adjusts parameters such as speed or torque to achieve desired dissolution, emulsification or dispersion outcomes.
[B01F 35/2205] Controlling the mixing process from a remote server, e.g. by sending commands using radio, telephone, internet, local network, GPS or other means -- B01F 35/2205 covers systems that control mixing equipment from a remote location, using communication methods such as radio, telephone, internet, local networks, GPS or similar links to send operational commands and adjust parameters of mixers or related accessories.
[B01F 35/2206] Use of stored recipes for controlling the computer programs, e.g. for manipulation, handling, production or composition in mixing plants  B01F33/846  takes precedence -- The classification covers accessories for mixers that use stored recipes to control computer programs for manipulating, handling, producing or composing materials in mixing plants, with the more specific B01F33/846 sub-class taking precedence.
[B01F 35/2207] Use of data, i.e. barcodes, 3D codes or similar type of tagging information, as instruction or identification codes for controlling the computer programs, e.g. for manipulation, handling, production or compounding in mixing plants  B01F33/848  takes precedence -- This subclass deals with using encoded data such as barcodes or three-dimensional tags to feed instruction or identification codes to computer programs that control mixers in processes like dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It covers data-driven accessories for mixing plants that guide manipulation, handling, production or compounding, and is subordinate to the broader B01F33/848 class.
[B01F 35/2208] Controlling using ultrasonic waves during the operation -- The B01F 35/2208 subclass covers mixer accessories that employ ultrasonic wave technology to control the mixing process. It includes devices that generate, transmit or modulate ultrasonic energy to influence dissolution, emulsification or dispersion while the mixer is operating, improving product uniformity and process efficiency.
[B01F 35/2209] Controlling the mixing process as a whole, i.e. involving a complete monitoring and controlling of the mixing process during the whole mixing cycle -- B01F 35/2209 covers systems that supervise and adjust the entire mixing operation, providing continuous monitoring and control throughout the mixing cycle. It includes hardware and software that manage parameters such as speed, temperature and time to ensure consistent product quality.
[B01F 35/221] of operational parameters, e.g. level of material in the mixer, temperature or pressure -- B01F 35/221 covers accessories for mixers that monitor or control operational parameters such as material level, temperature or pressure within the mixing chamber. These devices are used to optimise mixing performance and ensure consistent product quality during separating or dispersing processes.
[B01F 35/2211] Amount of delivered fluid during a period -- B01F 35/2211 denotes accessories for mixers that monitor or control the volume of fluid introduced to the mixing chamber during a set time interval, facilitating precise dosing in operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 35/2212] Level of the material in the mixer -- The code B01F 35/2212 designates devices that monitor or control the material level inside a mixer, such as sensors, indicators, or automatic filling mechanisms. It falls under accessories for mixers used in operations like dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing, and relates to ensuring the correct quantity of material is present during mixing.
[B01F 35/2213] Pressure -- The CPC subclass B01F 35/2213 covers accessories for mixers in which pressure is applied, such as pressurised vessels, pipes or agitators used to aid mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing operations.
[B01F 35/2214] Speed during the operation -- B01F 35/2214 defines the speed at which mixers and related accessories operate, specifying optimal ranges for efficient mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It falls within the broader categories of performing operations, separating and mixing, and guides design and selection of mixer accessories.
[B01F 35/22141] Speed of feeding of at least one component to be mixed -- The entry B01F 35/22141 covers accessories for mixers that control the rate at which at least one material is introduced into the mixing chamber. It specifies mechanisms or settings that determine the feeding speed of components during operations such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 35/22142] Speed of the mixing device during the operation -- This CPC subclass covers the speed at which a mixing device operates while performing mixing actions such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing. It pertains to accessories for mixers that control or indicate the operating speed during the process. The code B01F 35/22142 falls under the hierarchy of Performing Operations - Separating - Mixing.
[B01F 35/221421] Linear speed of the tip of a moving stirrer during the operation -- The CPC subclass B01F 35/221421 defines the specification of the linear speed of the tip of a moving stirrer while it is operating, as an accessory parameter for mixers. It falls under Performing Operations - Separating - Mixing (e.g., dissolving, emulsifying, dispersing) - Accessories for mixers.
[B01F 35/221422] Speed of rotation of the mixing axis, stirrer or receptacle during the operation -- The CPC subclass B01F 35/221422 covers technical aspects concerning the speed at which the mixing axis, stirrer or container rotates while the mixing operation is performed. It is applied to devices and accessories for mixers where rotational speed is a key parameter for processes such as dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing.
[B01F 35/2215] Temperature -- B01F 35/2215 designates accessories for mixers that provide temperature control, such as heating or cooling units, thermostats, and temperature-regulating jackets, enabling precise thermal conditions during mixing, dissolving, emulsifying or dispersing processes.
