[B01J 31/2428] with more than one complexing phosphine-P atom -- The CPC subclass B01J 31/2428 falls under Performing Operations → Separating → Chemical or Physical Processes, specifically covering catalysts that are hydrides, coordination complexes or organic compounds and contain more than one phosphine-P atom as a complexing ligand. This entry is used to classify inventions involving such multi-phosphine-P catalytic systems in separation processes.
[B01J 31/2433] comprising aliphatic or saturated rings -- The CPC entry B01J 31/2433 falls under Performing Operations → Separating → Chemical or Physical Processes (e.g., catalysis) and designates catalysts that contain aliphatic or saturated ring structures, including hydride-based, coordination-complex, or organic compounds used to facilitate separation reactions.
[B01J 31/2438] and further hetero atoms as ring members, excluding the positions adjacent to P -- This CPC subgroup deals with catalysts that incorporate hetero atoms as members of ring structures, but specifically excludes those where the hetero atoms are positioned directly adjacent to phosphorus. It covers hydride-based catalysts, coordination-complex catalysts, and organic compounds in which the hetero atoms form part of a ring system away from the phosphorus atom.
[B01J 31/2442] comprising condensed ring systems -- B01J 31/2442 covers catalysts that contain condensed ring systems, such as fused aromatic or heterocyclic structures, used in chemical or physical processes including catalysis and separation. These catalysts may be hydrides, coordination complexes or organic compounds whose activity derives from the fused ring framework.
[B01J 31/2447] and phosphine-P atoms as substituents on a ring of the condensed system or on a further attached ring -- B01J 31/2447 covers catalysts that contain hydride, coordination-complex or organic components where phosphorus atoms of phosphine groups are attached as substituents to a ring within a condensed aromatic system or to an additional linked ring. These catalysts are used in chemical or physical processes such as separation and catalysis.
[B01J 31/2452] with more than one complexing phosphine-P atom -- B01J 31/2452 pertains to catalysts that include coordination complexes with more than one phosphine-P atom as ligands, falling under the broader category of chemical processes involving catalysis. This classification is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office.
[B01J 31/2457] comprising aliphatic or saturated rings, e.g. Xantphos -- This subclass covers catalysts that contain aliphatic or saturated ring systems, such as the bidentate phosphine ligand Xantphos, used in homogeneous catalytic reactions. It is nested under catalysts comprising hydrides, coordination complexes or organic compounds within the separating chemical processes of the CPC.
[B01J 31/2461] and phosphine-P atoms as ring members in the condensed ring system or in a further ring -- The CPC subclass B01J 31/2461 pertains to catalysts that include phosphorus atoms from phosphine groups incorporated as members of a condensed ring system or an additional ring. It addresses hydride, coordination-complex or organic catalysts where the phosphine-P atom is part of a fused or separate cyclic structure used in chemical or physical processes such as catalysis.
[B01J 31/2466] comprising aliphatic or saturated rings -- The CPC subgroup B01J 31/2466 defines catalysts that contain aliphatic or saturated ring structures, typically as part of hydride, coordination-complex or organic catalytic systems used for chemical or physical separation processes.
[B01J 31/2471] with more than one complexing phosphine-P atom -- B01J 31/2471 is a CPC subclass within performing operations - separating - chemical or physical processes, focusing on catalysts that include hydrides, coordination complexes or organic compounds and contain more than one phosphine ligand bound to a phosphorus atom. It covers catalysts where multiple phosphine-P groups act as complexing agents, typically used in catalytic separation or reaction steps. The entry is used to classify inventions involving such multi-phosphine catalytic systems.
[B01J 31/2476] comprising aliphatic or saturated rings -- B01J 31/2476 pertains to catalysts that include aliphatic or saturated ring structures within their composition, falling under the broader category of catalysts made from hydrides, coordination complexes, or organic compounds. This classification is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office.
[B01J 31/248] Bridged ring systems, e.g. 9-phosphabicyclononane -- The subclass B01J 31/248 concerns catalysts that incorporate bridged ring systems, such as 9-phosphabicyclononane, where the fused cyclic framework acts as a scaffold for hydride, coordination-complex or organic catalytic sites used in chemical or physical separation processes.
[B01J 31/2485] Tricyclic systems, e.g. phosphaadamantanes and hetero analogues -- The CPC code B01J 31/2485 classifies catalysts that contain tricyclic structures such as phosphaadamantanes and related hetero-analogues. These compounds are used in chemical or physical processes, including catalysis, within the broader category of separating operations. The entry falls under the hierarchy of performing operations - separating - chemical or physical processes.

It specifically pertains to spiro-condensed ring systems, i.e., molecules where two rings share a single atom, when used as or related to catalytic agents in these processes.
[B01J 31/2495] Ligands comprising a phosphine-P atom and one or more further complexing phosphorus atoms covered by groups  B01J31/1845  -  B01J31/1885 , e.g. phosphine/phosphinate or phospholyl/phosphonate ligands -- B01J 31/2495 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system covers catalysts with ligands that include a phosphine-P atom and one or more additional complexing phosphorus atoms, such as phosphine/phosphinate or phospholyl/phosphonate combinations.

[B01J 31/4084] involving electromagnetic wave energy, e.g. UV or visible light -- This subclass covers catalysts such as hydrides, coordination complexes or organic compounds used in separating operations that rely on chemical or physical processes like catalysis, where the reaction is driven by electromagnetic-wave energy such as UV-visible light.

[B01J 35/36] Mechanical strength -- **Definition:** This place covers:  A catalyst's ability to resist an applied force.
[B01J 35/37] Crush or impact strength -- **Definition:** This place covers:  A catalyst's ability to resist fracture or breakage when force or pressure is applied to the catalyst material in the axial direction or a catalyst's ability to resist the formation of fines, e.g. side or bulk crush strength.
[B01J 35/38] Abrasion or attrition resistance -- **Definition:** This place covers:  A catalyst's ability to resist erosion caused by particle movement and collision between particles.
[B01J 35/39] Photocatalytic properties -- **Definition:** This place covers:  A catalyst's ability to change energy state when it absorbs light, achieves a higher energy level and initiates a reaction by providing the additional, absorbed energy to a reacting substance, e.g. catalysts with a band gap.

It is relevant when the metal's form or other physical attributes influence catalytic performance, fitting within the broader class of separating chemical or physical processes and catalysis.

It includes methods for measuring, specifying or increasing the metal surface area to enhance catalytic activity or related chemical processes.
[B01J 35/393] Metal or metal oxide crystallite size -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Active metal or metal oxide crystallite size, e.g. a metal oxide nanoparticle of 100 nm or less.
[B01J 35/394] Metal dispersion value, e.g. percentage or fraction -- B01J 35/394 covers the quantitative expression of metal dispersion in heterogeneous catalysts, typically given as a percentage or fraction that represents the proportion of metal atoms exposed on the catalyst surface. This metric is used to assess how finely metal particles are distributed on a support, which influences catalytic activity and efficiency. It falls within the class for catalysts characterised by form or physical properties in chemical or physical processing.

[B01J 35/69] bimodal -- B01J 35/69 covers catalysts whose physical form or properties are described as bimodal, meaning they exhibit two distinct size, shape, or pore-distribution populations. [B01J 35/695] polymodal -- Polymodal catalysts are materials whose catalytic activity is defined by multiple physical forms or structures, such as particles, layers, or porous networks, enabling them to facilitate several reaction pathways simultaneously. [B01J 37/00] Processes, in general, for preparing catalysts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for preparing any type of catalysts  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electrocatalysts, electrodes -> H01M
[B01J 37/0009] Use of binding agents; Moulding; Pressing; Powdering; Granulating; Addition of materials ameliorating the mechanical properties of the product catalyst -- B01J 37/0009 covers methods of preparing catalysts that involve binding agents and mechanical-shaping steps such as molding, pressing, powdering or granulating, and the addition of additives that improve the catalyst's mechanical strength or durability.
[B01J 37/0018] Addition of a binding agent or of material, later completely removed among others as result of heat treatment, leaching or washing,(e.g. forming of pores; protective layer, desintegrating by heat) -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Use of pore forming agents including mineral oils, steric acid, polyethylene glycol polymers, carbohydrate polymers, methacrylates, cellulose polymers, and carboxylates which decompose upon being heated.
[B01J 37/0027] Powdering -- Powdering refers to processes that reduce catalyst particles to a fine powder, typically by grinding, milling, jet-grinding or similar mechanical actions, optionally combined with precipitation or spray-drying, to increase surface area and improve catalytic performance. The class covers methods for producing powdered forms of catalysts used in chemical or physical catalytic processes.
[B01J 37/0036] Grinding -- This subclass covers grinding operations employed in catalyst preparation, where solid catalyst material is reduced to finer particles to increase surface area and enhance activity. It falls within the separating and chemical-or-physical processes sections of performing operations.
[B01J 37/0045] Drying a slurry, e.g. spray drying -- The CPC entry B01J 37/0045 covers processes that remove moisture from liquid-solid mixtures, typically by atomising the slurry into a heated gas stream to produce dried particles; spray drying is the principal example. These methods are used in the preparation of catalysts and other fine powders.
[B01J 37/0054] Drying of aerosols -- The CPC subclass B01J 37/0054 covers processes that dry aerosol streams, typically used in catalyst preparation to remove liquids and obtain solid particles. It falls under performing operations, separating, chemical or physical processes, and relates to general methods for preparing catalysts.
[B01J 37/0063] Granulating -- Granulating is the step of converting catalyst powders or fine particles into larger granules or pellets, improving handling, transport, and reactor contact properties. The process can include agglomeration, pelletisation, spray-drying or extrusion to create uniform sized granules suitable for use in catalytic reactors.
[B01J 37/0072] Preparation of particles, e.g. dispersion of droplets in an oil bath -- B01J 37/0072 concerns methods for producing particles, especially by dispersing liquid droplets in an oil bath, a technique used in catalytic preparation. It falls under Performing Operations → Separating → Chemical or Physical Processes, covering general processes for preparing catalysts.
[B01J 37/0081] Preparation by melting -- B01J 37/0081 relates to catalyst-preparation processes in which the constituent materials are melted together to form the active phase, such as alloying metals or fusing oxides, prior to solidification or further treatment.
[B01J 37/009] Preparation by separation, e.g. by filtration, decantation, screening -- B01J 37/009 addresses methods for producing catalysts through separation techniques, such as filtration, decantation or screening, that isolate the catalytic material from a reaction mixture. It belongs to the chemical or physical processes group within the broader separating operations of performing operations.
[B01J 37/02] Impregnation, coating or precipitation -- B01J 37/02 relates to processes for preparing catalysts by impregnating, coating or precipitating active substances onto a support material. It includes methods that deposit catalytic components onto porous carriers to create heterogeneous catalysts for chemical reactions.
[B01J 37/0201] Impregnation -- B01J 37/0201 covers impregnation techniques for introducing active components onto a solid support during catalyst preparation. The methods involve contacting the support with a solution or slurry of the catalytic material, then drying or calcining to fix the active phase. This falls under chemical or physical processes for catalyst fabrication within the broader category of separating operations.
[B01J 37/0203] the impregnation liquid containing organic compounds -- B01J 37/0203 covers catalyst-preparation processes that involve soaking a support material with a liquid that contains organic compounds, such as solvents, binders or organometallic precursors, to deposit the active phase before drying or activation. The entry is part of the broader class for chemical or physical processes used in separating and preparing catalysts.
[B01J 37/0205] in several steps -- B01J 37/0205 covers multi-step processes for preparing catalysts, situated within the broader class of separating operations and chemical or physical processes such as catalysis and colloid chemistry. It addresses sequential or staged methods used to produce catalytic materials.
[B01J 37/0207] Pretreatment of the support -- B01J 37/0207 covers methods for pretreating the support material used in heterogeneous catalysts, such as cleaning, activation, or modification of the carrier before the active phase is applied. These steps aim to improve dispersion, adherence, or surface properties of the support to enhance catalytic performance. The code falls under chemical or physical processes for separating operations.
[B01J 37/0209] involving a reaction between the support and a fluid -- This subclass covers processes for preparing catalysts in which a solid support chemically reacts with a fluid-such as a gas, liquid or vapor-during the preparation step, forming active sites or functional groups on the support surface. It is part of the broader category of separating operations involving chemical or physical processes.
[B01J 37/0211] using a colloidal suspension -- **Definition:** This place covers:  reverse micelle
[B01J 37/0213] Preparation of the impregnating solution -- This subclass covers the preparation of the liquid solution used to impregnate catalyst supports in catalyst manufacturing. It includes selection of solvents, dissolution of active ingredients, concentration adjustment and any steps required to obtain a stable impregnating medium.

[B01J 37/0217] Pretreatment of the substrate before coating -- B01J 37/0217 pertains to the preparatory steps taken to treat a substrate prior to applying a coating, specifically within the context of catalyst preparation processes. This treatment is aimed at enhancing the effectiveness or adhesion of the subsequent coating.
[B01J 37/0219] the coating containing organic compounds -- B01J 37/0219 covers processes for preparing catalysts that involve applying a coating containing organic compounds, such as polymers, surfactants or other organic agents, to catalyst particles or supports. The coating is used to modify surface properties, improve dispersion, protect active sites, or enhance catalytic performance in chemical or physical separation operations.
[B01J 37/0221] of particles -- B01J 37/0221 pertains to processes for preparing catalysts specifically involving the preparation of particles. This classification is part of a detailed hierarchy focusing on chemical and physical processes, particularly those related to catalysis.
[B01J 37/0223] by rotation -- B01J 37/0223 covers processes for preparing catalysts in which the reaction mixture or support is subjected to rotation, such as in rotary drums, wheel reactors or centrifugally forced coating, to achieve uniform distribution of active phases.
[B01J 37/0225] of metal substrates -- B01J 37/0225 relates to processes for preparing catalysts that involve metal substrates, covering methods of treating, coating or modifying metal surfaces to serve as catalyst supports. It sits within the hierarchy of performing operations, separating, and chemical or physical processes such as catalysis or colloid chemistry. The entry focuses on the preparation steps that enable metal substrates to function effectively in catalytic applications.
[B01J 37/0226] Oxidation of the substrate, e.g. anodisation -- B01J 37/0226 covers processes in which the catalyst substrate is oxidised, for example by anodisation, to form or modify the active surface before use. This step is part of broader chemical or physical operations for preparing catalysts, nested under separating and performing operations.

The entry addresses methods where the catalyst components are dissolved, mixed, or treated in a molten state to achieve the desired catalytic material.
[B01J 37/0232] by pulverisation -- The subclass B01J 37/0232 covers processes for producing catalysts by pulverisation, meaning the grinding or crushing of solid precursor materials into fine-particle forms that serve as active catalytic phases. It lies within the broader category of chemical or physical processes for separating, under performing operations.
[B01J 37/0234] Impregnation and coating simultaneously -- B01J 37/0234 covers catalytic preparation methods where the active material is introduced into a support while at the same time a protective or functional coating is applied. The technique combines impregnation of active components with simultaneous coating of the carrier, streamlining catalyst fabrication and improving uniformity.
[B01J 37/0236] Drying, e.g. preparing a suspension, adding a soluble salt and drying -- This CPC subclass covers methods of drying materials, particularly for catalyst preparation, including steps such as forming a suspension, adding a soluble salt and subsequent drying. It falls under chemical or physical processes for separating and is part of the broader category of processes for preparing catalysts.

[B01J 37/024] Multiple impregnation or coating -- B01J 37/024 covers processes for preparing catalysts that involve applying more than one impregnation or coating step to a support material, allowing successive deposition of active components or binders. The methods are used to build up catalyst structures with layered or composite characteristics, often to improve activity, selectivity or stability.
[B01J 37/0242] Coating followed by impregnation -- B01J 37/0242 covers processes in which a substrate is first coated with a layer and subsequently impregnated with a catalytic material to produce a catalyst. It belongs to the hierarchy of performing operations, separating, chemical or physical processes, specifically addressing general methods for preparing catalysts.
[B01J 37/0244] Coatings comprising several layers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  catalysts having a multi-layer structure, such as some exhaust gas catalysts
[B01J 37/0246] Coatings comprising a zeolite -- B01J 37/0246 covers the preparation of coatings that incorporate a zeolite as an active component. The entry lies in CPC class B01J, dealing with chemical or physical processes for preparing catalysts.
[B01J 37/0248] Coatings comprising impregnated particles -- B01J 37/0248 covers processes for preparing catalyst-containing coatings in which solid particles are impregnated with active chemical or physical agents, then applied as a coating layer to a substrate. The classification addresses the formulation and deposition steps used to create such impregnated particle coatings for catalytic or separative applications.
[B01J 37/03] Precipitation -- B01J 37/03 refers to processes that prepare catalysts by precipitation, a separating operation in which solid catalyst particles are formed from a liquid phase and isolated. The subclass falls under performing operations → separating → chemical or physical processes such as catalysis or colloid chemistry. This entry covers the precipitation step used to obtain catalyst powders or granules for further use.
[B01J 37/031] Precipitation -- The CPC entry B01J 37/031 relates to processes for preparing catalysts by precipitation, where solid catalyst particles are formed from a solution through controlled chemical or physical means. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and chemical or physical processes such as catalysis or colloid chemistry.
[B01J 37/033] Using Hydrolysis -- The B01J 37/033 subclass covers catalyst-preparation processes that employ hydrolysis, wherein water-mediated cleavage of precursor compounds generates active catalytic sites or modifies existing ones. Typical applications include converting metal salts, oxides or organometallic precursors into hydrated or hydroxide forms that are subsequently activated, calcined or otherwise treated to produce homogeneous or heterogeneous catalysts. This falls under the broader category of chemical or physical separating operations for catalyst production.
[B01J 37/035] Precipitation on carriers -- B01J 37/035 - Precipitation on carriers covers processes for preparing catalysts in which the active component is precipitated onto a solid support. The methods use chemical or physical precipitation techniques to deposit catalytic material onto carriers, commonly employed in the production of heterogeneous catalysts.
[B01J 37/036] to form a gel or a cogel -- B01J 37/036 covers processes for preparing catalysts that involve forming a gel or a cogel, typically by mixing a catalytic precursor with a gel-forming agent and inducing gelation through chemical or physical means. The resulting gel or cogel serves as a support matrix that immobilises the active catalytic species and can be further treated to develop the final catalyst material.
[B01J 37/038] to form slurries or suspensions, e.g. a washcoat -- This subclass covers processes that create liquid-solid mixtures, notably slurries or suspensions, such as applying a washcoat onto a support during catalyst fabrication. It includes steps for dispersing catalytic material in a fluid medium before shaping or drying.
[B01J 37/04] Mixing -- This subgroup covers methods of mixing components when producing catalytic materials, including blending active phases, supports, binders or additives to form homogeneous catalytic compositions. It is classified under chemical or physical processes for separating and preparing catalysts within the performing operations hierarchy.

[B01J 37/08] Heat treatment -- The B01J 37/08 subclass defines heat-treatment operations used when preparing catalysts, such as calcination, sintering or annealing to activate or modify the catalyst's surface and structure. It includes methods of applying controlled temperature, time and atmosphere to catalyst materials in furnaces, ovens or reactors as part of the overall chemical or physical process of catalyst production.
[B01J 37/082] Decomposition and pyrolysis -- The subclass B01J 37/082 covers catalyst-preparation methods that employ decomposition or pyrolysis, i.e., thermal breakdown of precursor compounds in an inert or reactive atmosphere to generate active catalytic phases. It includes processes where heat-induced conversion produces solid, metallic, or carbon-based catalysts.
[B01J 37/084] Decomposition of carbon-containing compounds into carbon -- B01J 37/084 covers processes that decompose carbon-containing compounds to produce elemental carbon, such as carbon black or graphite, typically through thermal, catalytic or plasma methods. [B01J 37/086] Decomposition of an organometallic compound, a metal complex or a metal salt of a carboxylic acid -- This CPC subclass covers methods for breaking down organometallic compounds, metal complexes or metal carboxylate salts, often used in the preparation of catalysts, and includes thermal, chemical or reactive decomposition techniques.
[B01J 37/088] Decomposition of a metal salt -- B01J 37/088 covers processes that decompose metal salts, typically by thermal, chemical or electrolytic means, to produce metal oxides, metals or other catalytic phases. The methods are used in the preparation of catalysts and fall under separating operations within chemical or physical processes.
[B01J 37/10] in the presence of water, e.g. steam -- A sub-group of processes for preparing catalysts that specifically involve water, such as steam, as a reaction medium, additive or solvent. It covers methods where the presence of water influences catalyst formation, activation, or modification during the preparation steps.
[B01J 37/105] Hydropyrolysis -- Hydropyrolysis (CPC B01J 37/105) covers catalytic processes that combine hydrogenation with pyrolysis to convert solid or liquid feedstocks into valuable products while simultaneously preparing or modifying catalysts. The method involves high-pressure hydrogen, elevated temperatures and rapid thermal cracking, and is classified under performing operations, separating, and chemical or physical processes such as catalysis.
[B01J 37/12] Oxidising -- B01J 37/12 covers methods for oxidising catalysts during their preparation, including chemical and physical treatments that modify catalyst surfaces or composition. The subclass falls under performing operations, separating, and chemical or physical processes such as catalysis or colloid chemistry.
[B01J 37/14] with gases containing free oxygen -- B01J 37/14 covers processes for preparing catalysts in which the reaction medium includes gases that contain free oxygen. It falls under the separating section of chemical or physical processes, specifically within the broader category of performing operations. The entry addresses methods that use oxygen-bearing gases to form or modify catalytic materials.
[B01J 37/16] Reducing -- This subclass covers methods for reducing catalyst substances, such as converting metal oxides to their metallic state, usually by hydrogen, carbon monoxide or other reducing agents, as part of catalyst preparation. It falls under separating processes within chemical or physical operations for making catalysts.
[B01J 37/18] with gases containing free hydrogen -- B01J 37/18 covers processes for preparing catalysts that involve treating or reacting with gases that contain free hydrogen. The methods fall under performing operations, separating, and chemical or physical processes such as catalysis. It is used by the EPO and USPTO to classify catalyst preparation using hydrogen-bearing gases.
[B01J 37/20] Sulfiding -- B01J 37/20 - Sulfiding covers processes for preparing catalysts by converting a metal component to its sulfide form, usually through treatment with sulfur-containing gases or liquids. These methods are employed to enhance catalytic activity in operations such as hydrodesulfurization, hydrogenation and related chemical or physical processes.
[B01J 37/22] Halogenating -- B01J 37/22 covers the halogenation of catalysts or catalytic materials, i.e. methods that introduce fluorine, chlorine, bromine or iodine into a substance to alter its surface or active sites. The processes include gas-phase, liquid-phase or solid-state reactions that attach halogen atoms, often to improve activity, selectivity or stability of the catalyst.
[B01J 37/24] Chlorinating -- B01J 37/24 (Chlorinating) covers processes that involve adding chlorine or chlorine-containing reagents to a substance in order to prepare or modify a catalyst. It falls within the separating chemical or physical processes branch of the CPC, specifically under performing operations for catalyst preparation.
[B01J 37/26] Fluorinating -- Fluorinating processes for preparing catalysts involve introducing fluorine atoms onto catalyst surfaces or into their structures to modify activity, selectivity or stability. Typical methods employ gaseous fluorine, fluorine-containing gases, or liquid fluorinating agents under controlled temperature and pressure, often followed by treatment to remove excess fluorine. These steps are used in the production of acid-stable or high-oxidation-state catalysts for petrochemical and fine-chemical applications.
[B01J 37/28] Phosphorising -- B01J 37/28 - Phosphorising covers processes for incorporating phosphorus into catalyst substances, such as impregnation, precipitation, or other chemical treatments that produce phosphorous-containing active phases, used to enhance catalytic performance in chemical reactions. The class lies within the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and chemical or physical processes for preparing catalysts.
[B01J 37/34] Irradiation by, or application of, electric, magnetic or wave energy, e.g. ultrasonic waves -- The B01J 37/34 subclass covers processes for preparing catalysts that involve exposing the material to electric, magnetic or other wave energy, such as ultrasonic irradiation. It is situated under performing operations, separating, and catalytic or colloid chemistry, and details thermal methods for restoring catalyst activity.
[B01J 38/04] Gas or vapour treating -- The B01J 38/04 entry covers processes that treat gases or vapors, typically to remove, recover, or modify components, often employing catalytic or colloidal methods. It includes operations for separating substances from the gas phase and may involve regeneration or reactivation of the catalysts used in the treatment.
[B01J 38/06] using steam -- B01J 38/06 pertains to the use of steam in the regeneration or reactivation of catalysts, covering processes where steam acts as a reagent, heating medium or carrier to restore catalyst activity in chemical or physical operations such as separation, catalysis, or colloid chemistry.

[B01J 38/12] Treating with free oxygen-containing gas -- The CPC entry B01J 38/12 covers processes for regenerating or reactivating solid catalysts by exposing them to a free oxygen-containing gas such as air, oxygen, or ozone. It is classified under performing operations, separating chemical or physical processes, specifically dealing with catalyst regeneration through oxidative treatment.
[B01J 38/14] with control of oxygen content in oxidation gas -- B01J 38/14 covers methods for regenerating or reactivating spent catalysts in which the oxygen concentration of the oxidation gas is deliberately controlled, allowing selective oxidation or reduction steps that restore catalyst activity while preventing over-oxidation or sintering.
[B01J 38/16] Oxidation gas comprising essentially steam and oxygen -- The CPC entry B01J 38/16 falls under the group for regeneration or reactivation of catalysts within chemical or physical processes. It specifically covers oxidation gases that consist mainly of steam mixed with oxygen, which are used to restore catalyst activity by removing coke or other poisons. This classification is relevant to processes that employ a steam-oxygen atmosphere for catalyst cleaning or re-activation.
[B01J 38/18] with subsequent reactive gas treating -- The B01J 38/18 subclass covers processes that regenerate or reactivate a catalyst and then treat it with a reactive gas, such as oxidation, reduction, or halogenation, to restore activity or modify surface properties. It falls under performing operations, separating, and chemical or physical processes like catalysis and colloid chemistry. This subcategory addresses the combined step of catalyst reconditioning followed by a gas-phase reaction.
[B01J 38/20] Plural distinct oxidation stages -- The CPC subclass B01J 38/20 relates to processes that employ several distinct oxidation steps, often in a sequence, to regenerate or reactivate a catalyst. It covers methods where a material is oxidised in multiple stages to restore its activity, fitting within chemical or physical separating operations.
[B01J 38/22] Moving bed, e.g. vertically or horizontally moving bulk -- The subclass B01J 38/22 covers processes that employ a moving-bed arrangement, whether the bulk is shifted vertically or horizontally, for the regeneration or reactivation of catalysts within chemical or physical operations. This includes any system where the catalyst particles themselves are transported as a flowing mass during the treatment.
[B01J 38/24] having mainly transverse, i.e. lateral, flow of oxygen-containing gas and material -- B01J 38/24 pertains to the regeneration or reactivation of catalysts where processes involve a primarily transverse, or lateral, flow of an oxygen-containing gas through the material. This method is used to enhance the efficiency of catalyst revitalization by optimizing gas-material interaction.
[B01J 38/26] having mainly counter-current flow of oxygen-containing gas and material -- B01J 38/26 pertains to methods for regenerating or reactivating catalysts in which an oxygen-containing gas is introduced primarily in a counter-current direction relative to the solid or liquid material being treated, facilitating oxidation-based cleaning or re-activation within catalytic reactors.
[B01J 38/28] having mainly concurrent flow of oxygen-containing gas and material -- The code B01J 38/28 covers processes where an oxygen-containing gas and a solid or liquid material flow mainly together, enabling simultaneous contact for regeneration or reactivation of catalysts. Such concurrent flow regimes are employed in chemical or physical operations that refresh a catalyst by oxidative treatment while the material passes through the same conduit.
[B01J 38/30] in gaseous suspension, e.g. fluidised bed -- This subclass deals with the regeneration or reactivation of catalysts carried out in a gaseous suspension, such as a fluidised-bed reactor, where catalyst particles are suspended in a flowing gas to facilitate heat and mass transfer. The process restores catalyst activity by removing poisons or deposits while the particles remain fluidised.
[B01J 38/32] Indirectly heating or cooling material within regeneration zone or prior to entry into regeneration zone -- B01J 38/32 covers methods for indirectly heating or cooling a material either within a catalyst regeneration zone or before it enters that zone. The indirect temperature control may involve heat exchangers, surrounding fluid streams, or external energy sources that do not contact the material directly. This allows regeneration or reactivation of catalysts without direct thermal contact, improving safety and energy efficiency.
[B01J 38/34] with plural distinct serial combustion stages -- The code B01J 38/34 covers methods for restoring spent catalysts by subjecting them to a series of distinct combustion steps performed one after another. Each stage burns off deposited material, allowing the catalyst to be reused in processes such as chemical synthesis or pollution control.
[B01J 38/36] and with substantially complete oxidation of carbon monoxide to carbon dioxide within regeneration zone -- This subclass covers methods for regenerating or reactivating catalysts where carbon monoxide is essentially completely oxidized to carbon dioxide within the regeneration zone, typically as part of a chemical or physical separation process.
[B01J 38/38] and adding heat by solid heat carrier -- This sub-category covers regeneration or reactivation of catalysts in which heat is supplied by a solid heat carrier. It includes processes that use hot solid particles such as sand, metal granules or ceramic beads to transfer thermal energy to the spent catalyst and restore its activity.
[B01J 38/40] and forming useful by-products -- B01J 38/40 covers methods for restoring the activity of spent catalysts while simultaneously generating valuable by-products, such as regenerated metal surfaces, reclaimed supports, or secondary chemicals that can be harvested for commercial use. The subclass includes chemical or physical regeneration techniques that improve catalyst life and add economic benefit.
[B01J 38/42] using halogen-containing material -- B01J 38/42 covers processes that use halogen-containing materials to regenerate or reactivate catalysts, especially in chemical or physical operations such as catalysis or colloid chemistry. [B01J 38/44] and adding simultaneously or subsequently free oxygen -- B01J 38/44 relates to catalyst regeneration or reactivation procedures in which free oxygen is introduced either simultaneously with, or subsequently after, the regeneration step, providing oxidative conditions that restore catalyst activity. [B01J 38/62] organic -- B01J 38/62 pertains to the regeneration or reactivation of catalysts using organic compounds. [B01J 38/64] using alkaline material -- B01J 38/64 covers methods for regenerating or reactivating spent catalysts by treating them with an alkaline material. [B01J 38/66] using ammonia or derivatives thereof -- This CPC subgroup B01J 38/66 covers processes for regenerating or reactivating catalysts where ammonia or its derivatives are employed as the regenerating agent, typically in chemical or physical catalytic cycles such as those involving metal-ammonia complexes. [B01J 38/68] including substantial dissolution or chemical precipitation of a catalyst component in the ultimate reconstitution of the catalyst -- The subclass covers procedures for restoring a spent catalyst by substantially dissolving or chemically precipitating a major portion of its active component, followed by reassembly of the catalyst in its active form. These methods go beyond simple cleaning, employing dissolution or precipitation steps to reconstitute the catalyst for further use.
[B01J 38/70] Wet oxidation of material submerged in liquid -- B01J 38/70 covers wet oxidation of substances that are immersed in a liquid medium, a method in which oxygenated water at elevated temperature and pressure oxidizes the material. The process is used for catalyst regeneration, waste treatment, or chemical conversion and is classified under separating operations involving chemical or physical processes such as catalysis.
[B01J 38/72] including segregation of diverse particles -- This subclass deals with methods for restoring catalysts that involve separating mixed particles, for example isolating active from deactivated or inert phases by filtration, sedimentation, magnetic or other physical means. It is a specific aspect of chemical or physical processes used to regenerate or reactivate catalysts.
[B01J 38/74] utilising ion-exchange -- The B01J 38/74 entry concerns the regeneration or reactivation of catalysts by means of ion-exchange processes. It falls under the broader class of separating operations within chemical or physical processes such as catalysis. This subclass specifically addresses the use of ion-exchange media to restore catalyst activity.
[B01J 39/00] Ion-exchange -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preparative chromatography processes involving cation exchange -> B01D15/36
[B01J 39/02] Processes using inorganic exchangers -- The B01J 39/02 subclass covers processes that employ inorganic ion-exchangers to separate, purify, or transform substances by exchanging ions between a solid inorganic matrix and a fluid phase. Typical applications include water demineralisation, recovery of metal ions, and regeneration of catalysts, where the exchange material is a mineral or synthetic inorganic compound.
[B01J 39/04] Processes using organic exchangers -- The entry B01J 39/04 covers processes that employ organic ion-exchange materials-such as polymeric resins or functionalised organic sorbents-to separate, purify, or modify chemical species. It includes methods where the organic exchanger mediates ion exchange, adsorption, or related interactions within catalytic, colloidal, or other chemical-physical operations.
[B01J 39/05] in the strongly acidic form -- B01J 39/05 relates to ion-exchange operations carried out with the exchanger in its strongly acidic form, meaning the active sites are fully protonated. It covers processes where the acidic resin or material is used to remove basic or neutral species, regenerate other exchangers, or facilitate acid-catalyzed reactions within the broader class of separating and chemical/physical processes.
[B01J 39/07] in the weakly acidic form -- This subclass covers ion-exchange operations in which the exchange material is employed in its weakly acidic form, meaning it contains a limited amount of hydrogen ions. It is used for selective removal, recovery or conversion of ions in aqueous media, commonly in water treatment, separations and catalysis. The classification groups processes that rely on the resin's weak acid functionality rather than a strong acid form.
[B01J 39/08] Use of material as cation exchangers -- The subclass B01J 39/08 covers the use of materials that function as cation exchangers in chemical or physical processes, particularly for ion-exchange separations. It includes applications where the material's capacity to exchange positively charged ions is exploited for purification, recovery, or catalytic purposes within the broader category of separating operations.
[B01J 39/09] Inorganic material -- The B01J 39/09 class covers inorganic substances employed in ion-exchange operations, such as zeolites, metal oxides, metal-sulphides and related solid phases, used to separate or modify chemical components in catalytic, colloidal or other physical-chemical processes.
[B01J 39/10] Oxides or hydroxides -- This CPC subclass covers ion-exchange processes that employ oxide or hydroxide materials as the solid phase for separating ions from liquids or gases, including their preparation, activation, regeneration and use in water treatment, metal recovery and catalysis. It includes compounds such as alumina, zirconia, magnesium hydroxide and related mixed oxides.
[B01J 39/12] Compounds containing phosphorus -- B01J 39/12 in the Cooperative Patent Classification (EPO/USPTO) pertains to ion-exchange processes involving compounds that contain phosphorus. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with chemical or physical processes, specifically focusing on catalysis and colloid chemistry.
[B01J 39/14] Base exchange silicates, e.g. zeolites -- B01J 39/14 covers base exchange silicates such as zeolites, which serve as ion-exchange materials in chemical and physical separation processes. Their porous frameworks allow cation exchange, making them valuable for catalysis, water softening, and various purification applications.
[B01J 39/16] Organic material -- B01J 39/16 in the Cooperative Patent Classification (EPO/USPTO) pertains to the use of organic materials in ion-exchange processes, which are part of broader chemical or physical processes such as catalysis or colloid chemistry. This classification specifically addresses the composition and application of organic substances used for ion exchange.
[B01J 39/17] containing also inorganic materials, e.g. inert material coated with an ion-exchange resin -- B01J 39/17 relates to ion-exchange compositions that incorporate inorganic, generally inert, materials-such as particles or substrates-coated with or impregnated by an ion-exchange resin. The inclusion of the solid support improves mechanical stability, facilitates handling, and can enhance the efficiency of separation or catalytic processes.
[B01J 39/18] Macromolecular compounds -- B01J 39/18 covers ion-exchange methods that employ macromolecular compounds such as polymeric resins, grafted fibers or functionalised membranes. The classification includes the design, preparation and use of these large-molecule exchangers for separating ions, catalysts or colloids in chemical or physical processes, often involving targeted selectivity or enhanced stability. It is a sub-group of separating operations within the broader category of chemical or physical processes.
[B01J 39/19] obtained otherwise than by reactions only involving unsaturated carbon-to-carbon bonds -- The B01J 39/19 subclass covers ion-exchange substances that are produced by methods other than reactions confined to unsaturated carbon-carbon bonds, such as polymerisation, grafting, or other chemical or physical routes that do not rely solely on C-C double-bond chemistry. It falls under separating operations within chemical or physical processes, specifically under ion-exchange.
[B01J 39/20] obtained by reactions only involving unsaturated carbon-to-carbon bonds -- B01J 39/20 refers to ion-exchange processes in which the desired product is produced solely by reactions involving unsaturated carbon-to-carbon bonds, such as addition or polymerisation, without the use of other functional groups. It is situated under performing operations, separating, chemical or physical processes in the CPC hierarchy.
[B01J 39/22] Cellulose or wood -- B01J 39/22 relates to ion-exchange methods used to separate or treat cellulose or wood substances. It includes processes that purify, fractionate or modify lignocellulosic material by exchanging ions in aqueous or non-aqueous media. The classification falls under performing operations, separating, chemical or physical processes.
[B01J 39/24] Carbon, coal or tar -- This CPC subclass B01J 39/24 covers ion-exchange processes involving carbon, coal or tar, including preparation, regeneration and use of ion-exchange media to purify, separate or modify these carbonaceous materials. It falls under Performing Operations, Separating, Chemical or Physical Processes, Ion-exchange.
[B01J 39/26] Cation exchangers for chromatographic processes -- The B01J 39/26 subclass defines cation-exchange materials employed in chromatographic separations, such as resins, beads or membranes that contain functional groups able to bind positively charged ions. It covers their design, preparation and use for selective retention, elution and purification of cationic species in analytical or preparative chromatography.
[B01J 4/00] Feed devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus moving the material into or out the chemically or physically processor  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Feed or outlet devices for pressure vessels -> B01J3/02 - Feeding of particles into and evacuation of particles out of the reactor -> B01J8/0015
[B01J 4/001] Feed or outlet devices as such, e.g. feeding tubes -- This subclass falls under performing operations, separating, chemical or physical processes and includes devices that introduce or discharge material in a process, such as feeding tubes or outlet fittings, without specifying particular mechanisms.
[B01J 4/002] Nozzle-type elements  nozzle-type reactors  B01J19/26 -- The CPC subclass B01J 4/002 pertains to feed devices that employ nozzle-type elements or nozzle-type reactors. It includes apparatus in which reactants are introduced, atomized or mixed by a nozzle to facilitate chemical or physical transformations such as catalysis or colloid formation. The classification is nested under performing operations, separating, and chemical or physical processes.
[B01J 4/004] Sparger-type elements -- B01J 4/004 covers sparger-type elements used as feed devices in processes that separate chemical or physical phases, such as catalytic reactors or colloid systems. These elements disperse a gas or liquid stream into a fluid medium to promote mixing, mass-transfer and reaction efficiency.
[B01J 4/005] provided with baffles -- The subclass B01J 4/005 covers feed devices used in separating operations that incorporate baffles, which are internal barriers designed to disrupt flow, prevent vortex formation and improve mixing or mass-transfer efficiency during chemical or physical processing.
[B01J 4/007] provided with moving parts -- B01J 4/007 covers feed devices that incorporate moving parts for delivering materials in separating operations, particularly in chemical or physical processes such as catalysis or colloid chemistry. The moving components may be stirrers, pistons, or other mechanisms that assist in feeding reactants or suspensions into the reaction zone.
[B01J 4/008] Feed or outlet control devices -- Feed or outlet control devices are mechanisms that regulate the introduction or removal of fluids, gases, powders or catalysts in reactors, separators or other process equipment, ensuring precise flow rates, pressure maintenance and batch timing. They include valves, regulators, throttles and dosing units used in chemical and physical processing operations.
[B01J 4/02] for feeding measured quantities of reagents -- The CPC subclass B01J 4/02 covers devices that deliver precisely measured quantities of reagents into a reaction or separation system. It belongs to the hierarchy of performing operations → separating → chemical or physical processes such as catalysis or colloid chemistry and relates specifically to feed devices for controlled reagent dosing.
[B01J 4/04] using osmotic pressure -- B01J 4/04 covers feed devices that utilize osmotic pressure to regulate the introduction of material into a reaction or separation system. The method exploits the natural pressure difference across a semi-permeable membrane to drive or control flow without mechanical pumps, and is applied in catalytic, colloidal or other chemical processes.
[B01J 41/00] Anion exchange -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preparative chromatography processes involving anion exchange -> B01D15/36
[B01J 41/02] Processes using inorganic exchangers -- B01J 41/02 covers processes that use inorganic solid exchangers to remove or replace anions from a fluid medium. Such ion-exchange methods are applied in water purification, metal recovery and chemical synthesis, relying on the exchange of anions with the inorganic material rather than organic resins.
[B01J 41/04] Processes using organic exchangers -- The subclass B01J 41/04 includes processes that employ organic ion-exchange resins or other organic exchangers to separate, recover or purify chemical species, such as removing anions or cations from liquids. It covers applications in water treatment, chromatography, metal recovery and other chemical or physical separation operations.
[B01J 41/05] in the strongly basic form -- B01J 41/05 covers anion-exchange processes in which the exchange medium is used in its strongly basic form, such as alkaline resins or surfaces that provide OH- groups to capture anionic species from a fluid. [B01J 41/07] in the weakly basic form -- B01J 41/07 covers anion-exchange processes that employ the exchange material in its weakly basic form, where the functional groups are only partially deprotonated, allowing selective removal of anionic species from solutions.
[B01J 41/08] Use of material as anion exchangers -- This classification covers processes where a material is employed to exchange or remove anionic species from a liquid or gas phase, such as ion-exchange resins, functionalised membranes and sorbents used in water treatment, industrial separations or analytical methods. It falls under CPC B01J 41/08 within the hierarchy of performing operations → separating → chemical or physical processes (catalysis or colloid chemistry).

These materials are employed in catalysis, ion-exchange, and chromatography to remove or recover target anions.

[B01J 41/18] Carbon, coal or tar -- B01J 41/18 covers anion-exchange processes that employ carbon, coal or tar as the exchange media. It relates to methods and apparatus for separating ions using these porous carbonaceous materials, typically in chemical or physical treatment operations.
[B01J 41/20] Anion exchangers for chromatographic processes -- Anion exchangers for chromatographic processes are materials-often synthetic resins, inorganic frameworks or functionalized membranes-designed to selectively bind and release negatively charged ions during liquid or gas chromatography. By exploiting ion-exchange mechanisms, they act as stationary phases that separate mixtures of anionic species based on charge, size or affinity, enabling purification, analysis or fractionation of complex chemical samples.
[B01J 43/00] Amphoteric ion-exchange, i.e. using ion-exchangers having cationic and anionic groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preparative chromatography processes involving ion exchange -> B01D15/361

[B01J 47/00] Ion-exchange processes in general -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes in general involving ion-exchange and apparatus therefor, comprising cationic, anionic or amphoteric ion exchange resin.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Ion-exchange chromatography processes or apparatus -> B01D15/08
[B01J 47/011] using batch processes -- B01J 47/011 relates to ion-exchange operations that are carried out in batch mode. The method involves loading a fixed quantity of ion-exchange material with a feed solution, allowing the exchange to occur, then removing and often regenerating the material before the next batch. This classification is used for processes where the exchange step is not continuous but performed as discrete batches.
[B01J 47/012] using portable ion-exchange apparatus -- B01J 47/012 covers portable ion-exchange devices and methods that employ a mobile apparatus to perform ion-exchange separation processes. The classification pertains to movable equipment designed to treat fluids by swapping ions between the fluid and a solid resin or membrane, enabling on-site or field-use separation.
[B01J 47/014] in which the adsorbent properties of the ion-exchanger are involved, e.g. recovery of proteins or other high-molecular compounds -- The subclass deals with ion-exchange operations in which the sorptive character of the exchanger is exploited to bind and recover macromolecular substances such as proteins, enzymes, or other high-molecular-weight compounds from a liquid phase. Typical methods involve loading the feed onto the resin, retaining the target molecules, then releasing them by changing pH, ionic strength, or using a competing ion.
[B01J 47/015] Electron-exchangers -- The B01J 47/015 entry covers electron-exchangers, which are substances or devices that mediate the transfer of electrons during ion-exchange operations. These materials facilitate redox reactions in chemical or physical separation processes such as catalysis, electro-refining, or electro-plating, and are classified under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and chemical or physical processes.
[B01J 47/016] Modification or after-treatment of ion-exchangers -- The entry B01J 47/016 covers methods for altering or post-processing ion-exchange materials, including regeneration, coating, functionalisation, or other treatments that enhance their selectivity, capacity, stability or durability in chemical or physical ion-exchange operations. It is part of the broader category of separating processes under performing operations.
[B01J 47/018] Granulation -- B01J 47/018 covers granulation, a process that forms larger particles or aggregates from powders, slurries or droplets, often using ion-exchange, agglomeration or binding agents. It falls under separating operations within chemical or physical processes and is used to improve handling, flow and performance of solid materials in various industrial applications.
[B01J 47/019] Mixtures in form of tablets -- B01J 47/019 pertains to ion-exchange processes involving mixtures formed into tablets, a specific method within chemical and physical processes for catalysis or colloid chemistry.
[B01J 47/02] Column or bed processes -- B01J 47/02 covers column or bed processes used in ion-exchange operations, where a packed column or bed facilitates the separation of ions from a fluid stream. It is a subclass of separating operations within chemical or physical processes such as catalysis or colloid chemistry.
[B01J 47/022] characterised by the construction of the column or container -- This CPC subclass covers ion-exchange separation methods where the defining feature is the design of the column or vessel that holds the ion-exchange material, such as shape, dimensions, packing arrangement, or internal flow-guiding structures. It falls under B01J 47 for ion-exchange processes within chemical/physical separations.
[B01J 47/024] where the ion-exchangers are in a removable cartridge -- The entry defines ion-exchange processes in which the ion-exchange material is housed in a removable cartridge, enabling simple removal, replacement or regeneration of the exchange media. It falls under separating operations and the broader class of chemical or physical processes, specifically ion-exchange processes in general.
[B01J 47/026] using columns or beds of different ion exchange materials in series -- B01J 47/026 covers ion-exchange separations that employ two or more columns or packed beds containing different ion-exchange resins arranged in series. By passing the feed sequentially through media with distinct selectivities, the process enhances removal of target ions or improves product purity. This technique is used in chemical and physical processing operations where precise separation of ionic species is required.
[B01J 47/028] with alternately arranged cationic and anionic exchangers -- The B01J 47/028 subclass covers ion-exchange processes that employ alternating layers or zones of cationic and anionic exchangers, creating a periodic arrangement of opposite charge media. This configuration is used to enhance selective separation or purification of mixed ionic species in chemical or physical processing.
[B01J 47/04] Mixed-bed processes -- B01J 47/04 covers mixed-bed ion-exchange processes where two or more ion-exchange media of opposite charge are combined in a single column to improve separation efficiency. The arrangement provides simultaneous removal of different ionic species and regeneration without separate stages, applicable to water treatment, purification, and chemical recovery.
[B01J 47/06] during which the ion-exchange material is subjected to a physical treatment, e.g. heat, electric current, irradiation or vibration -- B01J 47/06 pertains to ion-exchange processes where the exchange material is subjected to a physical treatment such as heating, an electric current, irradiation or vibration. The treatment is applied to alter the material's properties or performance while the ion-exchange operation is carried out.
[B01J 47/08] subjected to a direct electric current -- B01J 47/08 covers ion-exchange operations in which the exchange medium is driven by a direct electric current, such as electrodialysis, electrodeionization and related electrophoretic separations. The code applies to processes that use an applied DC field to enhance migration of ions across ion-exchange membranes or resins, improving separation efficiency.
[B01J 47/10] with moving ion-exchange material -- The subclass B01J 47/10 concerns ion-exchange processes in which the exchange material itself is moved, such as in fluidized beds, moving beds, rotating cartridges or other configurations where the ion-exchange phase circulates relative to the feed stream, enabling separation or purification of components. It includes the design of apparatus and methods that rely on this motion to enhance mass transfer and process efficiency.
[B01J 47/11] in rotating beds -- This subclass pertains to ion-exchange processes performed in rotating-bed reactors, where the solid phase is continuously turned to enhance contact with the fluid. It is situated under chemical or physical separating operations, specifically within the broader category of performing operations for separation.
[B01J 47/12] characterised by the use of ion-exchange material in the form of ribbons, filaments, fibres or sheets, e.g. membranes -- This subclass covers ion-exchange operations that employ the exchange medium in the form of ribbons, filaments, fibres or sheets, such as membranes, to effect separation. It falls under separating processes within chemical or physical technologies, specifically the broader group of ion-exchange processes.
[B01J 47/127] in the form of filaments or fibres -- B01J 47/127 relates to ion-exchange operations in which the exchange medium is presented as filaments or fibres. Such fibrous or filamentous ion-exchange materials are employed for separating, purifying or treating fluids in chemical and physical processes.
[B01J 47/133] Precoat filters -- Precoat filters are filtration units where a porous support is coated with a filtrate-retentive material such as diatomite or activated carbon, forming a porous cake that captures suspended solids and facilitates ion-exchange or catalytic reactions. They are employed in separating phases in chemical processing, especially where fine particles or colloids must be removed before further treatment. Under CPC B01J 47/133 they cover the design and operation of such filters in ion-exchange processes.
[B01J 47/14] Controlling or regulating -- B01J 47/14 pertains to the control and regulation of ion-exchange processes within chemical or physical separation operations. It includes methods and devices that adjust parameters such as flow, temperature, pH, or concentration, or employ feedback systems to maintain desired ion-exchange performance.
[B01J 47/15] for obtaining a solution having a fixed pH -- B01J 47/15 covers ion-exchange methods used to adjust and maintain a prescribed pH in a liquid, typically by passing the solution through a resin or membrane that adds or removes H⁺ or OH⁻ ions. The process is employed in water treatment, industrial chemistry and laboratory settings to produce a solution with a stable, target acidity or alkalinity.

[B01J 49/05] of fixed beds -- This subclass covers processes for regenerating or reactivating ion-exchange materials that are housed in fixed-bed reactors, such as packed columns. [B01J 49/09] of mixed beds -- This CPC subclass covers methods for restoring the capacity of mixed ion-exchange beds through regeneration or reactivation, commonly applied in water and wastewater treatment. [B01J 49/12] containing cationic exchangers -- B01J 49/12 pertains to processes for regenerating or reactivating ion-exchangers, specifically those that involve cationic exchangers. [B01J 49/14] containing anionic exchangers -- B01J 49/14 covers processes for restoring or reactivating ion-exchange media that contain anionic exchangers. [B01J 49/16] containing cationic and anionic exchangers in separate beds -- The B01J 49/16 subclass deals with processes that regenerate or reactivate ion-exchangers by using separate beds for cationic and anionic exchangers. [B01J 49/18] of mixed beds -- This subclass covers processes for regenerating or reactivating ion-exchange media arranged in mixed-bed systems, commonly used in separation operations such as water softening or chemical purification. It includes methods that restore the exchange capacity of the media after use.
[B01J 49/20] of membranes -- B01J 49/20 covers processes that use membranes to regenerate or reactivate ion-exchange materials. It includes methods in which a membrane facilitates the removal, transfer, or replacement of exchanged ions, restoring the ion-exchanger's capacity for further use. The subclass falls under separating operations involving chemical or physical processes such as catalysis or colloid chemistry.
[B01J 49/30] Electrical regeneration -- B01J 49/30 covers methods for restoring the capacity of ion-exchange media by applying an electrical current or voltage. The process passes electricity through the exhausted ion-exchanger to desorb retained ions, allowing the resin or membrane to be reused. It falls under chemical or physical separation operations focused on regeneration of ion-exchangers.
[B01J 49/40] Thermal regeneration -- B01J 49/40 covers thermal regeneration, i.e., the use of heat to restore the capacity of ion-exchange materials that have become spent. The method involves raising temperature to desorb or decompose adsorbed species, allowing the resin or other ion-exchanger to be reused in separation processes. This falls under chemical or physical processes for separating via ion-exchange regeneration.
[B01J 49/45] of amphoteric ion-exchangers -- This subclass deals with methods for regenerating or reactivating amphoteric ion-exchangers, materials capable of exchanging both positively and negatively charged ions. It is situated in the separation domain under chemical or physical processes such as catalysis and colloid chemistry, focusing on restoring exchange capacity after use.
[B01J 49/50] characterised by the regeneration reagents -- The B01J 49/50 subclass covers processes for restoring the capacity of ion-exchange media by applying specific regeneration reagents. It details the chemical or physical methods used to reactivate exhausted ion-exchangers, typically in water treatment or industrial separations, and includes the selection and use of those reagents.
[B01J 49/53] for cationic exchangers -- The CPC entry B01J 49/53 pertains to processes that regenerate or reactivate ion-exchangers of the cationic type. It falls under the broader class of performing operations, specifically separating, and deals with chemical or physical methods such as catalytic or colloidal techniques used to restore the exchange capacity of cationic exchangers.
[B01J 49/57] for anionic exchangers -- The CPC subclass B01J 49/57 covers processes for regenerating or reactivating ion-exchangers that operate with anions. It includes chemical or physical treatments-such as washing, elution, or conversion of the active sites-to restore the exchange capacity of anionic exchangers after use.
[B01J 49/60] Cleaning or rinsing ion-exchange beds -- This subclass covers methods for cleaning or rinsing ion-exchange beds, including regeneration or reactivation of ion-exchangers to restore their capacity after use.
