[B01L 2300/165] Specific details about hydrophobic, oleophobic surfaces -- B01L 2300/165 covers laboratory separation apparatus whose surfaces are engineered to repel water and oils, such as hydrophobic or oleophobic coatings, textured finishes, or material choices that create non-wetting properties. The subclass includes specifications for surface treatments, coatings, and designs that give the equipment water- and oil-resistance, improving performance in filtration, chromatography, or microfluidic processes.
[B01L 2300/166] Suprahydrophobic -- Suprahydrophobic refers to laboratory apparatus whose surfaces are engineered to strongly repel water, enhancing separation processes by preventing wetting and allowing liquids to bead and roll off. In the CPC hierarchy, this falls under chemical or physical laboratory equipment for general use, specifically within the broader categories of performing operations and separating. Such devices exploit extreme water-repellent properties to improve efficiency and cleanliness in analytical or preparative laboratory tasks.
[B01L 2300/168] Specific optical properties, e.g. reflective coatings -- B01L 2300/168 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system refers to specific optical properties of chemical or physical laboratory apparatus, such as reflective coatings, which are additional constructional details designed to enhance functionality or performance.
[B01L 2300/18] Means for temperature control -- The B01L 2300/18 subclass covers devices integrated into general-purpose chemical or physical laboratory equipment that provide temperature regulation during separating operations. It includes heating, cooling or thermostatic systems designed to maintain a set temperature for reactors, columns, mixers or other apparatus used in analytical or preparative processes. These means may be built-in or add-on components that ensure consistent thermal conditions for reliable separation results.
[B01L 2300/1805] Conductive heating, heat from thermostatted solids is conducted to receptacles, e.g. heating plates, blocks -- The B01L 2300/1805 entry covers apparatus that conducts heat from thermostatted solid sources to receptacles such as heating plates or blocks, providing controlled temperature for laboratory processes, particularly those involving separating operations performed with general-use chemical or physical equipment.
[B01L 2300/1811] using electromagnetic induction heating -- B01L 2300/1811 covers laboratory equipment that utilizes electromagnetic induction heating to carry out separating operations, such as melting, drying or distilling samples in general-use chemical or physical apparatus. The classification applies to devices where a varying magnetic field induces heat directly in the material or a susceptor, enabling efficient, controlled separation processes.
[B01L 2300/1816] using induction heating -- B01L 2300/1816 covers laboratory devices for separating chemical or physical mixtures that are heated by induction. The code applies to general-use apparatus where an alternating magnetic field generates heat directly in the material, enabling efficient, contact-less separation processes.
[B01L 2300/1822] using Peltier elements -- This class covers laboratory apparatus that employs Peltier thermoelectric elements to provide controllable heating or cooling during separation operations such as chromatography, crystallisation or other temperature-sensitive processes. The Peltier device serves as the primary means of temperature regulation integrated into the equipment. The entry applies to general-use chemical or physical laboratory apparatus designed for performing separating operations.
[B01L 2300/1827] using resistive heater -- B01L 2300/1827 covers laboratory separation equipment that incorporates a resistive heater to provide heat during the process. The heating element is used to raise the temperature of the sample, aid dissolution, evaporation or drying while the material is being separated. This subclass applies to general-purpose apparatus where the resistive heater is integral to the separating operation.
[B01L 2300/1833] using electrical currents in the sample itself -- This subclass concerns laboratory devices that separate substances by passing an electric current directly through the sample itself, such as in electrophoretic or electrolytic separation methods. The apparatus is intended for general-use chemical or physical laboratory work and integrates the power-supply elements with the sample container to effect the separation.
[B01L 2300/1838] using fluid heat transfer medium -- This subclass concerns the use of a fluid heat-transfer medium such as water, oil, steam or gas to supply or remove heat in laboratory separating equipment. It applies to general-purpose chemical or physical apparatus where heat is transferred by the moving fluid during the operation.
[B01L 2300/1844] using fans -- The subgroup B01L 2300/1844 covers chemical or physical laboratory apparatus that uses fans to generate airflow for separating or processing materials, such as drying, aerating, or gas-liquid separation. It pertains to general-use equipment where fan-driven movement of gases or vapors is the primary operating principle.
[B01L 2300/185] using a liquid as fluid -- B01L 2300/185 covers laboratory apparatus in which a liquid is employed as the working fluid for separating operations, such as washing, transporting, or assisting the movement of solid or liquid phases within the equipment. It includes devices designed for general-use chemical or physical laboratory settings where the fluid's role is integral to the separation process.
[B01L 2300/1855] using phase changes in a medium -- The subclass B01L 2300/1855 covers laboratory apparatus that perform separations by exploiting phase changes of a medium, such as solid-to-liquid or liquid-to-gas transitions, to aid or drive the separating action. It includes devices where the phase change is an essential functional element.
[B01L 2300/1861] using radiation -- B01L 2300/1861 covers laboratory apparatus that separates substances by applying radiation, such as ultraviolet, infrared, or X-ray energy, to induce or assist the separation process. The classification applies to general-use chemical or physical lab equipment where radiation is an integral part of the separating operation, often combined with detection or analysis steps.
[B01L 2300/1866] Microwaves -- The B01L 2300/1866 class covers microwave-based equipment used in laboratories for general-purpose chemical or physical operations, particularly for separating substances. Such apparatus employ microwave energy to heat, extract, or treat samples, facilitating rapid and efficient processing in research and analytical settings.
[B01L 2300/1872] Infrared light -- The B01L 2300/1872 subclass covers laboratory apparatus that uses infrared light as part of separation operations. It includes devices such as infrared heaters, lamps or emitters employed to dry, evaporate, or otherwise manipulate samples during chemical or physical analysis. This classification situates the technology under performing operations, separating, and general-use laboratory equipment.
[B01L 2300/1877] using chemical reactions -- A subclass of CPC for chemical or physical laboratory apparatus for general use that is designed to perform chemical reactions. It covers equipment such as reactors, mixers, or vessels in which reagents are combined and transformed, enabling experimental synthesis or analysis.
[B01L 2300/1883] using thermal insulation -- The subclass B01L 2300/1883 pertains to laboratory separation equipment that incorporates thermal insulation to protect or maintain the temperature of the apparatus during operation. It includes devices such as insulated columns, trays, or chambers used for chromatography, distillation, or other separating processes where heat loss or gain must be controlled.
[B01L 2300/1888] Pipettes or dispensers with temperature control -- B01L 2300/1888 covers laboratory pipettes or dispensing devices that incorporate a temperature-control feature, allowing fluids to be heated or cooled as they are drawn and delivered. These apparatuses are used in separating operations and are classified under chemical or physical laboratory equipment for general use.
[B01L 2300/1894] Cooling means -- B01L 2300/1894 covers cooling means incorporated in laboratory equipment used for separating chemical or physical mixtures. It includes devices such as condensers, chillers, cold plates and cooling coils that lower the temperature of a process stream or reaction vessel during separation operations. The classification is part of the broader categories of performing operations and separating apparatus.
[B01L 2400/00] Moving or stopping fluids -- The class covers laboratory equipment designed to move or stop fluids, including pumps, syringes, compressors, and valves used in chemical or physical experiments. It includes mechanisms that generate flow, regulate pressure, or block the passage of liquids or gases in general-use lab apparatus.
[B01L 2400/02] Drop detachment mechanisms of single droplets from nozzles or pins -- The code B01L 2400/02 covers apparatus and methods that release individual droplets from nozzles or pins, focusing on the mechanism that detaches a single drop as it leaves the fluid source. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and laboratory devices for moving or stopping fluids.
[B01L 2400/021] non contact spotting by inertia, i.e. abrupt deceleration of the nozzle or pin -- This subclass refers to laboratory equipment for moving or stopping fluids that uses non-contact spotting by inertia, meaning the fluid is deposited when the nozzle or pin is abruptly decelerated, without touching the substrate.
[B01L 2400/022] droplet contacts the surface of the receptacle -- The code covers laboratory devices where a liquid droplet is directed onto, or allowed to touch, the inner surface of a container or vessel, such as in dispensing, coating or analytical processes. It falls under moving or stopping fluids in general-use chemical or physical lab apparatus.
[B01L 2400/024] touch-off at the side wall of the receptacle -- B01L 2400/024 refers to arrangements in which a fluid stream is halted by contacting the side wall of a receptacle, effectively creating a touch-off point. This feature is used in laboratory separating equipment that moves or stops fluids as part of general-use chemical or physical apparatus.
[B01L 2400/025] tapping tip on substrate -- B01L 2400/025 covers a tapping tip mounted on a substrate that is used to direct, control or stop the flow of fluids during laboratory operations. The device is a general-use chemical or physical apparatus employed in separating processes and other performing operations.
[B01L 2400/027] electrostatic forces between substrate and tip -- This CPC subclass covers devices or techniques that employ electrostatic attraction or repulsion between a substrate and a probe tip to control the motion or blockage of liquids or gases in a laboratory setting. It includes mechanisms for moving, dispensing, or halting fluids using such electrostatic forces, typically in micro-scale or analytical equipment.
[B01L 2400/028] Pin is moved through a ring which is filled with a fluid -- This group includes apparatus where a pin is driven through an annular chamber that is filled with liquid or gas, thereby initiating, interrupting, or regulating the flow of the fluid within the ring. It is used in laboratory settings for separating operations such as filtering, dispensing, or mixing.
[B01L 2400/04] Moving fluids with specific forces or mechanical means -- The CPC subclass B01L 2400/04 covers laboratory equipment that transports liquids or gases by applying specific forces or mechanical devices such as pumps, pistons or agitators. It falls under the broader groups for performing operations, separating, and general-use chemical or physical laboratory apparatus that move or stop fluids.
[B01L 2400/0403] specific forces -- B01L 2400/0403 covers laboratory devices that move or stop fluids by applying specific forces such as pressure, vacuum, centrifugal or magnetic fields. The classification is used for general-purpose chemical or physical lab apparatus designed for separating operations. It includes pumps, valves and other mechanisms where a defined force controls fluid flow.
[B01L 2400/0406] capillary forces -- The class includes laboratory devices that use capillary forces to move, hold, or stop fluids, such as capillary-driven pumps, wicks, or microfluidic channels that transport liquids without external power, enabling separation or precise fluid handling.
[B01L 2400/0409] centrifugal forces -- The B01L 2400/0409 subclass refers to laboratory apparatus that employs centrifugal forces to move or stop fluids, typically for separating mixtures. It falls under Performing Operations → Separating → Chemical or Physical Laboratory Apparatus for General Use → Moving or stopping fluids, and covers devices such as centrifuges and related equipment used in general-purpose lab settings.
[B01L 2400/0412] using additionally coriolis forces -- The B01L 2400/0412 subclass covers laboratory apparatus for separating fluids that adds Coriolis-force effects to influence fluid movement. Such devices generate or harness rotational forces to enhance or control the separation, stopping or directing the flow as part of the process.
[B01L 2400/0415] electrical forces, e.g. electrokinetic -- This CPC subclass covers laboratory devices that use electrical forces such as electrokinetic or electrophoretic effects to move, control or stop fluid flow during separation processes. It includes equipment like electrophoresis chambers, electroosmotic pumps and similar systems intended for general-use chemical or physical laboratory applications.
[B01L 2400/0418] electro-osmotic flow [EOF] -- This subclass covers devices or methods that induce fluid movement by electro-osmotic flow, where an electric field across a porous medium or capillary causes bulk liquid transport. It falls under chemical or physical laboratory apparatus for general use, specifically the moving or stopping fluids category.
[B01L 2400/0421] electrophoretic flow -- The B01L 2400/0421 class covers laboratory apparatus that moves or controls fluids by employing electrophoretic flow, where an electric field induces the migration of charged particles or liquid phases. It is used in general-purpose chemical or physical lab equipment for separation processes, enabling precise fluid handling without mechanical pumps.
[B01L 2400/0424] Dielectrophoretic forces -- The B01L 2400/0424 entry covers laboratory apparatus that uses dielectrophoretic forces-non-uniform electric fields-to manipulate, move, or halt fluids and suspended particles. It falls under separating operations for general-use chemical or physical equipment, applying electric field gradients to control fluid flow without mechanical contact. This technology is used in microfluidic and lab-on-a-chip devices for precise fluid handling.
[B01L 2400/0427] Electrowetting -- Electrowetting is a technique that uses an electric field to change the wettability of a surface, allowing precise control of fluid movement on a solid substrate. In the CPC it falls under chemical or physical laboratory apparatus for general use, specifically moving or stopping fluids, and is classified under B01L 2400/0427.
[B01L 2400/043] magnetic forces -- B01L 2400/043 magnetic forces refers to laboratory apparatus that uses magnetic forces to move, control or stop fluids, such as magnetic stirrers, magnetic pumps or similar devices, intended for general chemical or physical laboratory use.
[B01L 2400/0433] vibrational forces -- The B01L 2400/0433 subclass covers laboratory equipment that employs vibrational forces to move, agitate, or stop fluids, typically for separating mixtures. It includes shakers, sonicators and similar devices where oscillatory motion is used to enhance fluid handling or separation processes.
[B01L 2400/0436] acoustic forces, e.g. surface acoustic waves [SAW] -- The subclass deals with using acoustic forces, particularly surface acoustic waves, to manipulate fluid flow in laboratory equipment. It covers devices that employ acoustic radiation pressure or streaming to move, mix or halt liquids, typically within general-use chemical or physical laboratory apparatus.
[B01L 2400/0439] ultrasonic vibrations, vibrating piezo elements -- The entry refers to laboratory apparatus that uses ultrasonic vibrations produced by piezoelectric elements to move, stop, or otherwise manipulate fluids, such as for pumping, mixing, or aiding separation. These devices are classified as general-use chemical or physical laboratory equipment for fluid handling.
[B01L 2400/0442] thermal energy, e.g. vaporisation, bubble jet -- The B01L 2400/0442 subclass covers chemical or physical laboratory equipment that moves or stops fluids by applying thermal energy, such as devices that vaporise liquids or generate bubble-jets to transport or control fluid flow. It falls under separating operations within general-purpose lab apparatus.
[B01L 2400/0445] Natural or forced convection -- The CPC subclass B01L 2400/0445 covers laboratory apparatus that moves or stops fluids by using natural or forced convection. It includes devices such as heated or cooled columns, convection ovens and flow-inducing mixers employed for general chemical or physical testing.
[B01L 2400/0448] Marangoni flow -- Marangoni flow refers to fluid motion driven by surface-tension gradients, usually caused by temperature or concentration differences, causing fluids to move spontaneously along interfaces. In laboratory apparatus this effect is used to control or halt fluid streams without mechanical parts, enabling precise mixing or separation in chemical processes.
[B01L 2400/0451] Thermophoresis -- Thermophoresis refers to the migration of particles or molecules in a fluid when a temperature gradient is applied, causing them to move from hotter to cooler regions. In laboratory equipment, this principle is used to separate or control the flow of fluids by intentionally heating or cooling specific zones, allowing selective movement or confinement of substances without mechanical parts.
[B01L 2400/0454] radiation pressure, optical tweezers -- This subclass covers devices that use light-induced radiation pressure to trap, move, or immobilise particles or fluids, commonly known as optical tweezers. Such apparatus are employed in laboratory settings for precise, contact-free manipulation of microscopic objects.
[B01L 2400/0457] passive flow or gravitation -- The B01L 2400/0457 subclass concerns laboratory equipment that guides fluids without active pumping, relying on gravity or capillary action to move or halt liquids. It includes passive flow devices such as tubes, channels and reservoirs used in chemical or physical experiments.
[B01L 2400/046] Chemical or electrochemical formation of bubbles -- This subclass covers apparatus or methods that generate gas bubbles in liquids by chemical reactions or electrochemical processes to aid separation or mixing. It includes devices such as bubble generators, spargers or electrolytic cells that intentionally produce bubbles within a fluid stream.
[B01L 2400/0463] Hydrodynamic forces, venturi nozzles -- B01L 2400/0463 covers laboratory apparatus that utilizes venturi nozzles to generate and control hydrodynamic forces for moving or stopping fluids. It includes designs that harness pressure differentials created by fluid flow through a narrowed section to achieve suction, mixing, or flow regulation, and is applied in general-use chemical or physical laboratory equipment.
[B01L 2400/0466] Evaporation to induce underpressure -- This subclass covers laboratory apparatus that generates a vacuum by evaporating a liquid, creating underpressure to move or stop fluid flow in separation operations. Such systems induce reduced pressure without mechanical pumps to assist in separating mixtures.
[B01L 2400/0469] Buoyancy -- B01L 2400/0469 covers laboratory apparatus that uses buoyancy to move or stop fluids, such as float-based valves, float chambers, or other buoyancy-driven devices for controlling fluid levels in chemical or physical experiments.
[B01L 2400/0472] Diffusion -- A laboratory apparatus designed to facilitate or control diffusion, allowing substances to spread through a medium or be separated by the natural movement of molecules. It is used in general-purpose chemical or physical lab settings to manage the flow of fluids for analytical, preparative, or experimental purposes.
[B01L 2400/0475] specific mechanical means and fluid pressure -- B01L 2400/0475 covers laboratory devices that use particular mechanical arrangements-such as pistons, valves or diaphragms-combined with fluid pressure to start, stop, direct or regulate the flow of liquids or gases in chemical or physical experiments. It pertains to general-purpose apparatus for moving or stopping fluids, where the motion is generated by pressure-driven mechanical elements.
[B01L 2400/0478] pistons -- Pistons are reciprocating elements that fit within a cylinder to move or restrict the flow of liquids or gases. In laboratory equipment they are used to generate pressure, displace fluids, or create a seal for separation processes, serving as a fundamental component in pumps, compressors and related apparatus.
[B01L 2400/0481] squeezing of channels or chambers -- The classification B01L 2400/0481 covers laboratory devices that compress or narrow channels or chambers to control the movement of fluids, such as pumps, valves or similar apparatus that squeeze passages to start, stop or regulate liquids or gases in chemical or physical experiments.
[B01L 2400/0484] Cantilevers -- Cantilevers are flexible beam components incorporated in laboratory devices to control fluid flow, acting as movable or stoppable elements that deflect or block passage of liquids or gases. They are used in apparatuses where precise positioning or rapid actuation of a fluid stream is required, often in microfluidic or analytical equipment.
[B01L 2400/0487] fluid pressure, pneumatics -- The B01L 2400/0487 subclass includes laboratory devices that employ fluid pressure, particularly pneumatic mechanisms, to move, control or stop fluids. It covers apparatus such as pneumatic valves, pistons, compressors and related components used for fluid handling in general-use chemical or physical lab equipment.
[B01L 2400/049] vacuum -- This subclass includes laboratory devices that employ a vacuum to move, stop, or regulate fluids during separating operations, such as vacuum pumps, chambers and associated handling equipment. It covers general-use chemical or physical apparatus where suction or reduced pressure is the primary means of fluid control.
[B01L 2400/0493] Specific techniques used -- The CPC entry B01L 2400/0493 covers specific techniques employed to move or stop fluids when using chemical or physical laboratory apparatus for general use. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, separating, and apparatus for separating, focusing on fluid-handling methods such as valves, pumps and flow-control devices.
[B01L 2400/0496] Travelling waves, e.g. in combination with electrical or acoustic forces -- The code B01L 2400/0496 covers laboratory apparatus that employs travelling-wave phenomena, such as acoustic or electrical waves, to move, stop or otherwise manipulate fluids during separation operations. It includes devices that use these waves to generate controlled fluid motion or pressure gradients for precise handling of liquids in general-use chemical or physical laboratory equipment.
[B01L 2400/06] Valves, specific forms thereof -- B01L 2400/06 covers valves of particular designs that are used in laboratory equipment for moving or stopping fluids, typically as part of chemical or physical processes that involve separating substances. These specific forms of valves are classified under apparatus for general use in the performing-operations hierarchy.
[B01L 2400/0605] check valves -- This CPC subclass covers check valves, a type of laboratory apparatus that permits fluid to flow only in one direction and automatically stops reverse flow. They are used to control the movement of liquids or gases in chemical and physical experiments, ensuring safe and efficient operation of fluid-handling systems.
[B01L 2400/0611] duck bill valves -- The B01L 2400/0611 entry covers duck-bill valves, a type of moving-or-stopping fluid valve used in chemical or physical laboratory apparatus for general use. These flexible, conical seals open when pressure is applied and close to halt flow, fitting within the separating operations subgroup of performing operations.
[B01L 2400/0616] Ball valves -- Ball valves are rotating-ball devices that open or close a fluid pathway by aligning a hole through the sphere with the inlet and outlet ports. When the ball is turned 90°, flow stops; when aligned, fluid passes with a tight seal, making them ideal for quick on-off control in laboratory and process piping. This classification covers such valves used to move or stop fluids in general-use chemical or physical laboratory apparatus.
[B01L 2400/0622] distribution valves, valves having multiple inlets and/or outlets, e.g. metering valves, multi-way valves -- The CPC subclass B01L 2400/0622 relates to distribution valves, including multi-inlet or multi-outlet designs such as metering and multi-way valves, used for directing or controlling the flow of fluids in chemical or physical laboratory apparatus.
[B01L 2400/0627] Molecular gates forcing or inhibiting diffusion -- B01L 2400/0627 covers laboratory devices that use molecular gates to control the diffusion of fluids, either promoting or restricting flow through a membrane or channel. These apparatus are employed in separation processes where precise regulation of molecular transport is required, such as in selective filtration, controlled release, or analytic sampling.
[B01L 2400/0633] with moving parts -- B01L 2400/0633 covers laboratory apparatus used for separating or controlling fluids that incorporates moving parts such as pumps, stirrers, valves or similar mechanical elements. It applies to general-use chemical or physical lab equipment where fluid motion or stoppage is achieved by these movable components.
[B01L 2400/0638] membrane valves, flap valves -- This subclass covers valve types that use a flexible membrane or a hinged flap to control the flow of liquids or gases. Such devices are employed in chemical or physical laboratory apparatus for starting, stopping, or regulating fluid movement. They are part of the broader group of separating operations within performing operations.
[B01L 2400/0644] rotary valves -- Rotary valves are laboratory devices that control the flow of liquids or gases by rotating a plug or disc to open or close passages. They are used in separating processes to direct, start, or stop fluid streams without interrupting the system, and are classified under B01L 2400/0644 within the Cooperative Patent Classification for chemical or physical laboratory apparatus.
[B01L 2400/065] sliding valves -- Sliding valves are moving or stopping devices that regulate the flow of liquids or gases within chemical or physical laboratory apparatus used for general purposes. They consist of a controllable barrier that slides across a fluid passage to open, restrict, or shut off flow, enabling precise control of fluid movement during experiments.
[B01L 2400/0655] pinch valves -- Pinch valves are laboratory flow-control devices that stop or regulate fluids by compressing a flexible sleeve or tube, effectively pinching the passage to halt flow. They are employed in general-use chemical and physical apparatus where precise starting, stopping, or throttling of liquids or gases is required.
[B01L 2400/0661] shape memory polymer valves -- B01L 2400/0661 covers shape-memory polymer valves, a type of laboratory apparatus used to control fluid flow by opening or closing in response to temperature changes. The devices belong to the "moving or stopping fluids" subgroup of separating equipment within the broader performing-operations category.
[B01L 2400/0666] Solenoid valves -- The CPC subclass B01L 2400/0666 covers solenoid valves, devices that use an electromagnetic coil to open or close a fluid passage for controlling the movement or stopping of liquids or gases in laboratory apparatus.
[B01L 2400/0672] Swellable plugs -- Swellable plugs are devices that expand on contact with a liquid or gas to seal or block a passage in chemical or physical laboratory equipment. They are used to stop or control fluid flow in separations and other general-use apparatus, providing a reversible or permanent barrier depending on the material composition.
[B01L 2400/0677] phase change valves -- Phase-change valves are devices that control the movement of fluids by using a material that changes state, such as melting or vaporising, to open or close a passage. They are employed in laboratory apparatus for separating and regulating fluid streams, allowing precise handling of phase transitions in chemical and physical processes.
[B01L 2400/0683] mechanically breaking a wall or membrane within a channel or chamber -- The entry B01L 2400/0683 covers apparatus or methods that mechanically rupture a wall or membrane located inside a fluid channel or chamber, typically for separating substances in a laboratory setting. It relates to devices that move or stop fluids by breaking barriers to enable further processing.
[B01L 2400/0688] surface tension valves, capillary stop, capillary break -- A surface-tension valve or capillary stop/break is a fluid-control device that relies on liquid surface-tension forces to start, stop, or release flow in a narrow channel. It typically consists of a micro-scale constriction or geometry that holds fluid until a pressure threshold is exceeded, then allows passage, and is used in laboratory, diagnostic or microfluidic applications.
[B01L 2400/0694] vents used to stop and induce flow, backpressure valves -- Vents used to stop and induce flow, also known as backpressure valves, are devices installed in fluid handling systems to control the rate and direction of fluid movement. They can temporarily block flow or create a pressure differential that drives fluid through a process, improving safety and efficiency in laboratory and industrial settings.
[B01L 2400/08] Regulating or influencing the flow resistance -- B01L 2400/08 covers devices that modify the hydraulic resistance of a fluid stream in laboratory equipment, such as valves, flow restrictors and throttling mechanisms. These components are used to regulate or influence fluid flow during separation or other operations, enabling precise control of pressure and volume in chemical or physical laboratory apparatus.
[B01L 2400/082] Active control of flow resistance, e.g. flow controllers -- The entry B01L 2400/082 relates to apparatus that actively adjusts fluid flow resistance, such as electronically or mechanically controlled flow controllers and throttling devices. It covers systems that can increase, decrease, or maintain pressure drops in pipes, tubes, or channels during laboratory or industrial processes. These devices are used to achieve precise regulation of liquid or gas streams in chemical or physical laboratory equipment.
[B01L 2400/084] Passive control of flow resistance -- B01L 2400/084 covers devices that regulate fluid flow by passive means, such as fixed or variable geometry restrictors, or structures that create pressure drop without moving parts or external power. It applies to laboratory apparatus that set or modify flow resistance simply through design.
[B01L 2400/086] using baffles or other fixed flow obstructions -- The subclass covers laboratory equipment that controls fluid movement by employing baffles, plates or other fixed obstacles to direct, slow or block flow, typically used in separating operations such as sedimentation, filtration or phase separation.
[B01L 2400/088] by specific surface properties -- Laboratory apparatus that moves or stops fluids by exploiting the material's surface characteristics, such as hydrophobic or hydrophilic coatings, micro-texture, or capillary-action designs, to direct flow without mechanical valves. The classification covers devices that rely on these specific surface properties to control fluid movement in chemical or physical laboratory settings.
[B01L 3/00] Containers or dishes for laboratory use, e.g. laboratory glassware -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Simple fluid transfer means, such as tubes, valves or fittings.  Various containers, in particular for sample or reagent storage also comprising microfluidic structures.  Droppers comprising various fluid dispensing means such as pipettes, burettes and droplet dispensers.  Spatulas.
[B01L 3/02] Burettes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Burettes, pipettes, pipette tips, droplet counters, droplet dispensers (droppers), and other laboratory devices to withdraw and discharge a liquid.  **Glossary:** - Pipette: Means with one port - Burette: Means with two ports - Dropper: Means to dispense the liquid dropwise
[B01L 3/0203] Burettes, i.e. for withdrawing and redistributing liquids through different conduits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Burettes, i.e. for withdrawing and redistributing liquids through different conduits.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - If the volume dispensed is droplet sized -> B01L3/0241
[B01L 3/0206] of the plunger pump type -- The CPC subclass B01L 3/0206 covers laboratory containers or dishes that incorporate a plunger-pump mechanism for moving fluids, such as glass or plastic vessels equipped with a piston-driven inlet and outlet. These devices are used in chemical or physical laboratory work to dispense, transfer, or extract liquids without the need for separate pumping equipment.
[B01L 3/021] Pipettes, i.e. with only one conduit for withdrawing and redistributing liquids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pipettes, i.e. with only one conduit for withdrawing and redistributing liquids.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - If the volume dispensed is droplet sized -> B01L3/0241
[B01L 3/0213] Accessories for glass pipettes; Gun-type pipettes, e.g. safety devices, pumps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This group covers accesories for (traditional) glass pipettes, such as safety devices to protect the user from inhaling hazardous fluids or pumps. This group also encompasses gun-type pipettors.
[B01L 3/0217] of the plunger pump type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Piston or plunger-type pipettes.
[B01L 3/022] Capillary pipettes, i.e. having very small bore  B01L3/0224  -  B01L3/0237  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pipettes where a piston or plunger is moved within a capillary.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Capillary pipette without piston -> B01L3/021 , B01L3/0241 - Pippetes having mechanical means to set stroke length -> B01L3/0224 - Details of electronic control, e.g. relating to user interface -> B01L3/0237
[B01L 3/0224] having mechanical means to set stroke length, e.g. movable stops  B01L3/0231 ,  B01L3/0234  take precedence -- The subclass B01L 3/0224 covers containers or dishes for laboratory use that incorporate mechanical means for setting the stroke length, such as movable stops, to control the movement of a piston or plunger. It is situated under performing operations → separating → chemical or physical laboratory apparatus for general use, and is superseded by the more specific entries B01L 3/0231 and B01L 3/0234.
[B01L 3/0227] Details of motor drive means  B01L3/0231 ,  B01L3/0234  take precedence -- This subgroup covers the motor-drive features of laboratory containers or dishes, such as mixers, shakers or stirrers, describing the arrangement, speed regulation and power transmission of the motor. More specific aspects are treated in the subordinate groups 0231 and 0234, which have priority over this entry.
[B01L 3/0231] having several coaxial pistons -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Plunger pipettes with coaxial pistons. This group does NOT cover multiple parallel plungers.
[B01L 3/0234] Repeating pipettes, i.e. for dispensing multiple doses from a single charge -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Drop counters; Drop formers -> B01L3/0241
[B01L 3/0237] Details of electronic control, e.g. relating to user interface -- This subclass covers laboratory containers or dishes equipped with electronic control features, focusing on aspects such as user-interface design, display elements, input methods, and software functions that manage the container's operation or monitoring. It pertains to the integration of electronic control systems into general-use chemical or physical laboratory apparatus.
[B01L 3/0241] Drop counters; Drop formers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Droplet counters, the constructional details of the dispensing portion of droplet dispensers or how droplets form and fall.
[B01L 3/0244] using pins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Droplet dispensers using pins.
[B01L 3/0248] Prongs, quill pen type dispenser -- B01L 3/0248 covers a quill-pen style dispenser equipped with prongs for delivering liquids or powders in a laboratory setting. The device is a small, handheld container that releases measured amounts of reagents, fitting within the class of general-use laboratory glassware and other containers.
[B01L 3/0251] Pin and ring type or pin in tube type dispenser -- A pin or ring type dispenser, or a pin-in-tube dispenser, is a laboratory container equipped with a small moving pin or ring that releases measured amounts of liquid or reagent when pressed or rotated, enabling precise dosing in analytical or preparative work. It is classified under chemical or physical laboratory apparatus for general use, specifically containers or dishes for laboratory use.
[B01L 3/0255] characterised by the form or material of the pin tip -- B01L 3/0255 classifies laboratory containers or dishes used in separating operations that are distinguished by the shape or material of the pin tip employed in the apparatus. The subclass highlights how variations in the pin tip's form or composition affect the performance of chemical or physical separation tasks.
[B01L 3/0258] using stamps -- The code B01L 3/0258 covers laboratory containers or dishes that incorporate stamp-type devices to aid separation processes, such as perforating, imprinting or applying pressure to samples within glassware. These stamp mechanisms are used to facilitate solid-liquid or solid-solid separation in chemical or physical laboratory operations.
[B01L 3/0262] using touch-off at substrate or container -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In touch-off dispensers the liquid contacts the target surface while it still hangs at the dispenser thus leading to a bridge between the two bodies. Capillary forces draw the liquid subsequently, at least in part, to the target surface.
[B01L 3/0265] using valves to interrupt or meter fluid flow, e.g. using solenoids or metering valves -- The code B01L 3/0265 covers laboratory containers or dishes that incorporate valves for interrupting or metering fluid flow, such as solenoid-actuated or precision metering valves. These valve-equipped vessels are used in separating operations to control the transfer or dispensing of liquids and gases within chemical or physical laboratory apparatus.
[B01L 3/0268] using pulse dispensing or spraying, eg. inkjet type, piezo actuated ejection of droplets from capillaries -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Ink jet printing of ink -> B41J2/01
[B01L 3/0272] Dropper bottles -- B01L 3/0272 defines dropper bottles, a type of laboratory container or dish used in chemical or physical laboratory apparatus for general use. These bottles are designed to hold liquids and dispense them in controlled drops for experiments, testing, or analytical procedures.
[B01L 3/0275] Interchangeable or disposable dispensing tips -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pipette tips, note that the subgroup also covers ejection means for pipette tips.
[B01L 3/0279] co-operating with positive ejection means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pipette tip ejectors.
[B01L 3/0282] mounted within a receptacle  wash bottles  B01L3/10 -- This subclass covers wash-bottles that are mounted within a receptacle, serving as laboratory containers for dispensing cleaning liquids in chemical or physical laboratory apparatus.
[B01L 3/0286] Ergonomic aspects, e.g. form or arrangement of controls -- B01L 3/0286 covers the ergonomic design of laboratory containers or dishes, such as glassware, focusing on shape, size, handle placement and the arrangement of any controls or accessories to improve user comfort, safety and operational efficiency.
[B01L 3/0289] Apparatus for withdrawing or distributing predetermined quantities of fluid -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus for handling fluids, e.g. gas, beads or pulverised materials.
[B01L 3/0293] for liquids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Liquid handling apparatus not provided for in B01L3/02 .
[B01L 3/0296] from piercable tubing, e.g. in extracorporeal blood sampling -- B01L 3/0296 covers laboratory containers or dishes formed from piercable tubing, such as flexible, sealable tubes that can be punctured for fluid access. They are used in applications like extracorporeal blood sampling, allowing withdrawal or addition of liquids without disconnecting the tubing.
[B01L 3/04] Crucibles -- B01L 3/04 covers crucibles, heat-resistant containers used to hold substances during heating, melting or chemical reactions in laboratory or industrial settings. They belong to chemical or physical laboratory apparatus for general use and fall under separating operations.
[B01L 3/06] Crystallising dishes -- B01L 3/06 covers crystallising dishes, laboratory containers designed for growing crystals from solutions or melts. These vessels are typically made of glass or other inert materials and provide controlled cooling or evaporation conditions to promote crystal formation.
[B01L 3/08] Flasks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Flasks used in the lab and not otherwise provided for. Flasks usually comprise an inlet which is smaller than the main body.
[B01L 3/10] Wash bottles -- Wash bottles are small, often glass or plastic containers with a squeezable top used to deliver a jet of liquid for rinsing or washing laboratory glassware, equipment, or surfaces. They are considered general-use chemical or physical laboratory apparatus for separating operations and fall under the B01L 3/10 subclass.
[B01L 3/12] Gas jars or cylinders -- B01L 3/12 refers to gas jars or cylinders that serve as containers or dishes in laboratory settings, used for storing, dispensing or reacting gases during separating operations. These vessels are typically made of glass or metal and are intended for general laboratory use.
[B01L 3/16] Retorts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Retorts are simple distillation apparatuses. They often comprise round bases and turned down necks.
[B01L 3/18] Spatulas -- B01L 3/18 covers spatulas employed as laboratory tools for separating, mixing or transferring materials. These instruments are simple hand-held implements, often made of metal, plastic or glass, designed to scoop, slide or lift solids, powders or liquids in a lab setting.
[B01L 3/50] Containers for the purpose of retaining a material to be analysed, e.g. test tubes  devices for taking samples of blood  A61B5/15 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Simple containers that only store the sample and also more complex containers with integrated passive or active parts, which inter alia transport samples within them.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Devices for taking samples of blood -> A61B5/15
[B01L 3/502] with fluid transport, e.g. in multi-compartment structures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sample container where fluid is transported during use between different parts of the container.
[B01L 3/5021] Test tubes specially adapted for centrifugation purposes -- Test tubes specially adapted for centrifugation are laboratory glassware designed with features such as reinforced walls, sealing caps, or rotor-compatible shapes to withstand high centrifugal forces. They fall under the CPC class B01L 3/5021, a subgroup of containers for general laboratory use within the separating operations section. These tubes enable efficient separation of solids and liquids in scientific and industrial settings.
[B01L 3/50215] using a float to separate phases -- B01L 3/50215 covers laboratory containers or dishes that employ a floating element to separate liquid phases, such as a buoyant pad or bead, enabling the distinct layers to remain apart during processes like extraction or decanting. This method is used in general-purpose chemical or physical lab apparatus.
[B01L 3/5023] with a sample being transported to, and subsequently stored in an absorbent for analysis -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Constructional details or flow shaping in sample containers where liquid is transported through an absorbent, e.g. lateral flow devices.
[B01L 3/5025] for parallel transport of multiple samples -- B01L 3/5025 covers laboratory containers or dishes designed to hold several specimens at once for parallel transport, such as multi-well plates, sample trays or rack systems used in chemical or physical analysis.
[B01L 3/50255] Multi-well filtration -- The B01L 3/50255 multi-well filtration refers to a laboratory container made of glass or other material that incorporates an array of separate wells, each equipped with a filter medium to separate solids from liquids simultaneously in parallel. It is used for high-throughput analyses, cell culture assays, or sample preparation where multiple samples are processed under identical conditions.
[B01L 3/5027] by integrated microfluidic structures, i.e. dimensions of channels and chambers are such that surface tension forces are important, e.g. lab-on-a-chip -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sample containers with integrated microfluidic components, which are not restricted to capillary transport but are defined by their small size.
[B01L 3/502707] characterised by the manufacture of the container or its components -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sample containers with integrated microfluidic components with specific manufacturing details.
[B01L 3/502715] characterised by interfacing components, e.g. fluidic, electrical, optical or mechanical interfaces -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Integrated microfluidic interfaces, i.e. the section of the interface which forms part of the container.  Fluidic interfaces comprise inlets and outlets.  Optical interfaces may comprise lenses, prisms, mirrors.  Mechanical interfaces may comprise aligning marks, guides.
[B01L 3/502723] characterised by venting arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means to remove air or gases from a microfluidic sample container.  Also comprises documents where bubble formation is avoided.
[B01L 3/50273] characterised by the means or forces applied to move the fluids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means or forces to move fluid in microfluidic sample containers, e.g. integrated pumps.
[B01L 3/502738] characterised by integrated valves  throttle valves in microfluidic sample containers  B01L3/502746 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The constructional details of valves specific to microfluidic sample containers or valving schemes when they depend on integrated structures, e.g. cascades of capillary valves.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Throttle valves in microfluidic sample containers -> B01L3/502746  **Glossary:** - valve: means to stop or divert the whole flow of a fluid
[B01L 3/502746] characterised by the means for controlling flow resistance, e.g. flow controllers, baffles or throttle valves -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling the flow resistance in a microfluidic sample container with neither valving, which stops the flow, nor pumping, which is the source of the force for the flow.  For example:
[B01L 3/502753] characterised by bulk separation arrangements on lab-on-a-chip devices, e.g. for filtration or centrifugation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sample containers with integrated microfluidic components with bulk separation, such as filtration or centrifugation. In general, the flow direction of the bulk fluid is the same as the component that is separated. In a bulk separation a certain type of particle or fluid is usually separated from another type as a group, based on similarity of their properties. For example, centrifugation leads to classification of particle groups based on density.
[B01L 3/502761] specially adapted for handling suspended solids or molecules independently from the bulk fluid flow, e.g. for trapping or sorting beads or physically stretching molecules -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Handling of suspended solids or molecules independent from the bulk fluid flow. In general, the fluid flow direction of the bulk fluid is different from the flow direction of the component to be separated. Handling may comprise trapping, sorting, stretching or orientating particles or molecules.  Usually, particles or molecules are handled individually and thus can be selectively handled irrespective of whether more particles or molecules of the same type or class exist in the sample. For example, a single DNA molecule may be stretched in a nanochannel, although the sample comprises many more DNA molecules.
[B01L 3/502769] characterised by multiphase flow arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sample containers with integrated microfluidic components having multiphase flow arrangements. Multiphase flow is meant to encompass flows where gas/liquid or liquid/liquid interfaces exist and kept during flow of the fluids. The fluids may be immiscible but under certain conditions this may not be necessary.
[B01L 3/502776] specially adapted for focusing or laminating flows -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sample containers with integrated microfluidic components having laminated flow arrangements, e.g. sheath flow.
[B01L 3/502784] specially adapted for droplet or plug flow, e.g. digital microfluidics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Droplets or plug flow in sample containers with integrated microfluidic components, e.g. digital microfluidics.
[B01L 3/502792] for moving individual droplets on a plate, e.g. by locally altering surface tension -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Moving droplets individually on a plate, for example by electrowetting.  The droplet may also be bordered by a cover plate in addition to a bottom plate. No side walls are present to guide the droplet.
[B01L 3/5029] using swabs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sample containers comprising both fluid transport and swabs. The invention is usually directed to the handling of the swab within the container and not the swab itself.
[B01L 3/505] Flexible containers without fluid transport within -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Flexible sample containers such as bags or foldable sample cards.
[B01L 3/5055] Hinged, e.g. opposable surfaces -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Folded sample cards.
[B01L 3/508] Rigid containers without fluid transport within -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sample containers that have rigid walls and do not comprise any internal sample transport.
[B01L 3/5082] Test tubes  per se -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sample containers having the form of tubes.
[B01L 3/50825] Closing or opening means, corks, bungs -- This subclass covers devices used to seal or unseal laboratory containers such as caps, corks, plugs and other closure mechanisms. It includes designs for removable or permanent closures for glassware and other lab vessels.
[B01L 3/5085] for multiple samples, e.g. microtitration plates -- B01L 3/5085 covers laboratory containers designed to hold multiple samples at once, such as microtitration plates used in assays and screening. These devices enable parallel processing of small-volume reactions in chemistry and biology.
[B01L 3/50851] specially adapted for heating or cooling samples -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sample containers for thermal treatment of samples, e.g. PCR containers.
[B01L 3/50853] with covers or lids  closures for test tubes  B01L3/50825 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Constructional details of closures for multisample containers.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Closures for test tubes -> B01L3/50825
[B01L 3/50855] using modular assemblies of strips or of individual wells -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Multisample containers which can be assembled and/or disassembled during use but which form a single unit when they are assembled. These are typically assembled into a standard format using a frame.
[B01L 3/50857] using arrays or bundles of open capillaries for holding samples -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Multiple parallel capillaries which hold a sample by surface tension at a specific location. The sample is not transported within the device except for filling or draining.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Parallel capillaries as sample containers involving transport within said capillaries -> B01L3/5027 , B01L3/5025
[B01L 3/5088] confining liquids at a location by surface tension, e.g. virtual wells on plates, wires  B01L3/50857  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Droplet is also moved, transported on the substrate -> B01L3/502
[B01L 3/52] Containers specially adapted for storing or dispensing a reagent  B01L3/02  takes precedence; storing or dispensing test elements  G01N33/4875 ; automated reagent dispensing  G01N35/1002 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Reagent containers that are modular units that can be inserted into a sample container and are thus not an integral part of the sample container.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Burettes; Pipettes -> B01L3/02 - Storing or dispensing test elements -> G01N33/4875 - Automated reagent dispensers -> G01N35/1002
[B01L 3/523] with means for closing or opening -- The code B01L 3/523 concerns laboratory containers such as beakers, flasks, or other vessels that incorporate features for closing or opening, for example caps, lids, stopcocks or similar sealing mechanisms, enabling the containment and controlled handling of substances during chemical or physical laboratory operations.
[B01L 3/527] for a plurality of reagents -- B01L 3/527 covers laboratory containers designed to hold a plurality of reagents simultaneously, such as multi-compartment vessels, reagent reservoirs, or multiwell plates. These devices enable storage, mixing or dispensing of several chemicals within a single apparatus.
[B01L 3/54] Labware with identification means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Labels, barcodes, RFIDs or other identification means used for labware.
[B01L 3/545] for laboratory containers -- B01L 3/545 designates containers or dishes used in laboratories, such as glassware, plastic vessels and similar receptacles for holding, mixing or processing samples during chemical or physical operations. The subclass is part of the broader group for chemical or physical laboratory apparatus for general use, under the performing-operations and separating hierarchy.
[B01L 3/5453] for test tubes -- B01L 3/5453 covers laboratory glassware used as containers, specifically test tubes, falling under chemical or physical laboratory apparatus for general use within the separating operations of the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[B01L 3/5457] for container closures -- The CPC subclass B01L 3/5457 covers closure devices for laboratory containers or dishes, such as caps, stoppers, and sealing mechanisms used with glassware and other lab vessels. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, separating, and chemical or physical laboratory apparatus for general use. This subclass addresses the design and functionality of closures that ensure containment, safety, and integrity of laboratory contents.
[B01L 3/56] Labware specially adapted for transferring fluids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Simple labware to transfer fluids, in particular traditional glassware and its accessories, such as connectors, valves, tubes, funnels.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Pipettes, Burettes, Droppers -> B01L3/02 - Devices for withdrawing samples -> G01N1/02
[B01L 3/561] Tubes; Conduits -- The CPC sub-group B01L 3/561 covers tubes and conduits employed as containers or flow paths in chemical or physical laboratory apparatus. It includes glass, plastic or metal tubes, pipes, and similar tubular components used for holding, transferring or reacting substances in general-use lab equipment.
[B01L 3/563] Joints or fittings; Separable fluid transfer means to transfer fluids between at least two containers, e.g. connectors -- B01L 3/563 covers joints, fittings and detachable fluid-transfer components that join at least two laboratory containers to allow the movement of liquids or gases, such as connectors, couplings or quick-release couplers used in glassware or other lab vessels.
[B01L 3/5635] connecting two containers face to face, e.g. comprising a filter -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Direct connection between only two containers.
[B01L 3/565] Seals -- The subclass B01L 3/565 covers seals for laboratory containers or dishes, including caps, stoppers, gaskets and other devices that provide airtight or watertight closure for glassware and similar lab vessels.
[B01L 3/567] Valves, taps or stop-cocks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Valves specifically used in connection with laboratory equipment.
[B01L 3/569] Glassware -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Residual group for glassware not falling in any of the above groups, i.e. must by made of glass.
[B01L 5/00] Gas handling apparatus -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Gas jars or cylinders -> B01L3/12 - Cold traps or cold baffles -> B01D8/00
[B01L 5/02] Gas collection apparatus, e.g. by bubbling under water -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - For sampling -> G01N1/22
[B01L 5/04] Gas washing apparatus, e.g. by bubbling -- A gas washing apparatus is a laboratory device that cleans or purifies a gas stream by passing it through a liquid medium, typically by bubbling the gas into the liquid. The contact between gas and liquid removes soluble impurities, moisture, or reactive components, making the gas suitable for further experimental use.
[B01L 7/00] Heating or cooling apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Thermostats for heating or cooling or heat insulation devices.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Autoclaves -> B01J3/04
[B01L 7/02] Water baths -- A water bath is a laboratory heating or cooling device that circulates water at a controlled temperature to uniformly raise or lower the temperature of samples, reagents, or test tubes. It provides a stable thermal environment for incubation, melting, or temperature-sensitive processes in chemical and biological work.
[B01L 7/04] Heat insulating devices, e.g. jackets for flasks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Stand alone heat insulation device.
[B01L 7/50] Cryostats -- Cryostats are temperature-controlled devices that maintain samples at very low temperatures for experimental or preservation purposes. They are classified under heating or cooling apparatus in laboratory equipment, providing precise cryogenic conditions for chemical or physical processes such as separation, analysis, and material testing.
[B01L 7/52] with provision for submitting samples to a predetermined sequence of different temperatures, e.g. for treating nucleic acid samples -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Thermocyclers or other thermostats where a sample is exposed to a defined sequence of different temperatures, e.g. the sequences used for polymerase chain reactions [PCR].
[B01L 7/525] with physical movement of samples between temperature zones -- B01L 7/525 covers laboratory heating or cooling equipment that automatically moves samples from one temperature zone to another, such as conveyors, rotary carriers or robotic arms that transport tubes, plates or reactors between hot and cold sections. The movement is integral to the apparatus, enabling controlled temperature changes for separation, reaction or analysis without manual handling.
[B01L 7/5255] by moving sample containers -- A heating or cooling laboratory device that transports sample containers while they are being conditioned, typically moving them through different temperature zones to achieve uniform temperature changes. The movement can be achieved by conveyors, rotors or robotic arms and is used in processes such as drying, annealing or precipitation where precise thermal control of many samples is required.
[B01L 7/54] using spatial temperature gradients -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Thermostats creating a defined temperature gradient in space.
[B01L 9/00] Supporting devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Holding frame constructions, trays for drying labware or holders for labware not provided for in a subgroup.
[B01L 9/02] Laboratory benches or tables -- B01L 9/02 covers laboratory benches or tables that serve as supporting devices for separating operations in chemical or physical laboratory work, falling under the broader categories of performing operations, separating, and general-use laboratory apparatus. These items provide stable work surfaces for various analytical, preparative, or testing tasks.
[B01L 9/04] Retort stands -- Retort stands are supporting devices used in chemical or physical laboratory apparatus for general use, specifically in separating operations. They hold and secure retorts, providing adjustable height and stable positioning during heating or distillation processes. Such stands are classified under B01L 9/04 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[B01L 9/06] Test-tube stands -- Test-tube stands are devices that hold test tubes upright during experiments, typically consisting of a rack or holder with slots that provide stable positioning for handling, heating or storage. They are classified under CPC B01L 9/06, a subclass of supporting devices for separating operations in chemical or physical laboratory apparatus for general use.
[B01L 9/065] specially adapted for capillary tubes -- Specially adapted devices for holding or supporting capillary tubes within laboratory apparatus used for separation operations, such as chromatography or electrophoresis, enabling precise positioning and consistent flow while the tubes are engaged in chemical or physical analysis.
[B01L 9/50] Clamping means, e.g. tongs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Indicating positions of wells or other receptacles by numbers, characters, grooves or other means.
[B01L 9/52] Supports specially adapted for flat sample carriers, e.g. for plates, slides, chips -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Supports specially adapted for flat sample carriers.
[B01L 9/523] for multisample carriers, e.g. used for microtitration plates -- The subgroup B01L 9/523 includes supporting devices that hold or handle multisample carriers, such as microtitration plates, used in chemical or physical laboratory apparatus for separating operations.
[B01L 9/527] for microfluidic devices, e.g. used for lab-on-a-chip -- The B01L 9/527 class covers microfluidic devices that are incorporated into lab-on-a-chip systems for handling, transporting, mixing or separating minute fluid volumes. It is a sub-category of supporting devices for chemical or physical laboratory apparatus used in separating operations.
[B01L 9/54] Supports specially adapted for pipettes and burettes -- B01L 9/54 covers laboratory supports that are specially adapted to hold pipettes or burettes, providing stable positioning for accurate dispensing or titration. These devices, such as customized racks or rests, facilitate separating operations by keeping the instruments securely in place during chemical or physical analyses.
[B01L 9/543] for disposable pipette tips, e.g. racks or cassettes -- B01L 9/543 covers supporting devices for disposable pipette tips, such as racks or cassettes, used in laboratory apparatus for separating chemical or physical samples. The class falls under performing operations, separating, and chemical or physical laboratory apparatus for general use.
[B01L 9/547] for dispensing pins -- B01L 9/547 covers laboratory devices that hold and release individual pins for use in experiments, such as pin dispensers that feed pins into reaction vessels or testing setups. These are supporting apparatus used in separating operations within general chemical or physical laboratory equipment.
[B01L 9/56] Means for indicating position of a recipient or sample in an array -- B01L 9/56 refers to devices that mark or display the location of a container, well or sample within an array such as a microtiter plate, test strip or other multi-well format, helping users identify and handle each element during separation operations; the indicators may be visual codes, printed symbols, RFID tags or similar positioning cues incorporated into the laboratory apparatus.
[B01L 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- B01L 99/00 covers general-purpose laboratory apparatus used for separating substances that does not fall into any of the more specific groups within the "chemical or physical laboratory apparatus for general use" subclass. It includes devices for filtration, centrifugation, chromatography or other separation techniques when the particular design or function is not elsewhere classified. This catch-all category ensures that all separation equipment can be indexed even if its features are not detailed in other groups.
[B02] CRUSHING, PULVERISING, OR DISINTEGRATING -- B02 covers technologies and methods for reducing the size of solid materials by crushing, grinding, or other disintegration processes, including equipment such as crushers, mills, pulverisers and related accessories. It also classifies the operational principles, designs and applications of these size-reduction devices across various industries.
[B02B] PREPARING GRAIN FOR MILLING -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[B02B 1/00] Preparing grain for milling or like processes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for the pre-treatment of grain prior to milling.
[B02B 1/02] Dry treatment -- Dry treatment covers methods of preparing grain for milling that use no added liquids, employing mechanical, thermal or pneumatic actions such as air-classification, sieving, heating or low-moisture grinding to reduce particle size, alter texture or remove impurities before the milling step.
[B02B 1/04] Wet treatment, e.g. washing, wetting, softening -- B02B 1/04 covers wet treatment of grain during the preparation for milling. Methods such as washing, wetting or softening are used to clean the kernels, adjust moisture content or make them easier to crush before grinding.
[B02B 1/06] Devices with rotary parts -- The B02B 1/06 subclass relates to devices that use rotary elements to crush, pulverize or otherwise break down grain in preparation for milling. It covers machines such as rotary crushers, rollers and similar equipment that perform the initial grain reduction step before final milling.
[B02B 1/08] Conditioning grain with respect to temperature or water content -- B02B 1/08 covers methods for adjusting a grain's temperature or moisture content before it is milled, including drying, cooling, tempering or other conditioning steps that make the grain suitable for efficient grinding.
[B02B 3/00] Hulling -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All kind of apparatus for hulling, husking, polishing etc. of grains having discs, rollers, screws or worms, beaters, brushes or combinations thereof or having fluid means.
[B02B 3/02] by means of discs -- The CPC subclass B02B 3/02 covers hulling processes in which the hulls are removed from grain by means of disc-type equipment. It includes methods that use rotating or reciprocating discs to crush, split or separate the outer layer of cereal grains as part of grain-preparation operations.
[B02B 3/04] by means of rollers -- B02B 3/04 covers the hulling of grain by means of rollers, where kernels are passed between rotating rollers that split or crush them enough to separate the husk from the edible part. This method prepares grain for milling while avoiding excessive pulverisation.
[B02B 3/045] cooperating rollers -- The CPC subclass B02B 3/045 covers cooperating rollers employed in crushing, pulverising or disintegrating operations for preparing grain for milling, particularly for hulling. These rollers work together to grip, feed and compress the material, facilitating efficient grain separation before subsequent milling steps.
[B02B 3/06] by means of screws or worms -- B02B 3/06 concerns hulling operations in which grain is processed by screw or worm conveyors that crush, grind or otherwise separate the husk from the kernel. The classification applies to machinery that uses rotating helical elements to feed and disintegrate the material while effecting hull removal. This is a sub-category of crushing and pulverising techniques for grain preparation.
[B02B 3/08] by means of beaters or blades -- B02B 3/08 covers techniques for hulling grain by using beaters or blades, a subcategory of crushing, pulverising or disintegrating operations applied in grain preparation for milling.
[B02B 3/10] by means of brushes -- The subclass B02B 3/10 covers the hulling of grain-removing the outer husk as part of preparing grain for milling-by using brush-type tools that rub or sweep the kernels to dislodge the hulls. It falls under the broader category of crushing, pulverising or disintegrating operations.
[B02B 3/12] by means of fluid -- This subgroup concerns hulling of grain in which the removal of the hull is accomplished by a fluid, such as water, air or gas jets, to separate or break the husk from the seed. It specifies the use of a fluid medium as the operative means within crushing, pulverising, or disintegrating steps for grain preparation.
[B02B 3/14] Producing flour or meal directly -- The sub-class covers methods that transform whole grain into flour or meal in a single step, typically by grinding or milling the grain after removal of the husk. It includes equipment and processes that produce the final milled product directly, without intermediate separation or handling stages. This falls under the broader category of crushing, pulverising, or disintegrating operations for grain preparation.
[B02B 5/00] Grain treatment not otherwise provided for -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[B02B 5/02] Combined processes -- B02B 5/02 refers to combined processes used to treat grain before milling, where multiple operations such as crushing, pulverising, cleaning, conditioning or other treatments are performed together in a single step and are not covered by any more specific subclass.
[B02B 7/00] Auxiliary devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[B02B 7/02] Feeding or discharging devices -- B02B 7/02 covers auxiliary feeding and discharging equipment used in the crushing, pulverising or disintegrating of grain in preparation for milling. The devices guide material into the processing zone or remove it after treatment, ensuring a continuous flow and efficient operation of the primary crushing or pulverising machinery.
[B02C] CRUSHING, PULVERISING, OR DISINTEGRATING IN GENERAL -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[B02C 1/00] Crushing or disintegrating by reciprocating members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Crushing where the crushing or disintegrating members are only reciprocating.
[B02C 1/005] hydraulically or pneumatically operated -- The B02C 1/005 subclass covers crushing or disintegrating processes that use reciprocating members driven by hydraulic or pneumatic power. It concerns machines where the crushing action is produced by a back-and-forth motion generated by fluid pressure, typically in pulverisers, grinders or similar equipment. This subclass falls under the broader category of general crushing, pulverising or disintegrating operations.
[B02C 1/02] Jaw crushers or pulverisers -- B02C 1/02 covers machines that crush, pulverise or disintegrate material using a pair of reciprocating members that form a jaw-type crushing action. The classification includes jaw crushers and similar pulverisers where one member is fixed and the other moves back and forth to break the feed.
[B02C 1/025] Jaw clearance or overload control -- The B02C 1/025 subclass covers mechanisms that regulate the clearance of the crushing jaws or protect a jaw crusher against overload, typically by adjusting the gap between the reciprocating jaw members or by releasing pressure when a blockage occurs. It includes devices such as clearance-adjustment systems, overload-release levers and hydraulic or pneumatic controls that act during the reciprocating motion.
[B02C 1/04] with single-acting jaws -- B02C 1/04 covers crushing or disintegrating devices that use reciprocating members with single-acting jaws, where one jaw remains stationary while the other moves toward it to apply compressive force. This arrangement is typical of primary jaw crushers that break material by direct squeezing between the two jaws.
[B02C 1/043] with cooperating single acting jaws -- This subgroup of the CPC deals with crushing or pulverising devices that use a pair of single-acting jaws that work together, one moving while the other remains stationary, to apply a reciprocating crushing action. The mechanism is typically employed in laboratory or small-scale crushers for size reduction of solid materials.
[B02C 1/046] of the plural stage type -- B02C 1/046 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to crushing or disintegrating equipment that uses reciprocating members, specifically of the plural stage type, where multiple stages are employed for more efficient material reduction.
[B02C 1/06] with double-acting jaws -- B02C 1/06 pertains to crushing or disintegrating machines that employ two jaws moving reciprocally, with both jaws acting to exert compressive force on the material, as found in double-acting jaw crushers.
[B02C 1/08] with jaws coacting with rotating roller -- B02C 1/08 covers crushing or pulverising devices in which a pair of jaws work together with a rotating roller to perform the operation, the jaws moving reciprocally while the roller turns to grind, crush or disintegrate material. This sub-class falls under the broader category of crushing, pulverising, or disintegrating by reciprocating members, and specifies the combination of jaw and roller mechanisms.
[B02C 1/10] Shape or construction of jaws -- The CPC subclass B02C 1/10 covers the shape or construction of jaws used in crushing or disintegrating machines that employ reciprocating members. It addresses the geometry, mounting and design features of jaw plates that influence crushing performance and material flow.
[B02C 11/00] Other auxiliary devices or accessories specially adapted for grain mills -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All auxiliary devices and accessories specially adapted for grain mills like disagglomerators, special sifting and separating, feeding, cooling, heating, conditioning arrangements.
[B02C 11/02] Breaking up amassed particles, e.g. flakes -- This subclass covers auxiliary devices or accessories for grain mills that are specifically adapted to break up accumulated particles such as flakes, improving material flow and processing efficiency. It falls under general crushing and pulverising operations and relates to mechanisms that separate or disintegrate gathered material rather than primary grinding.
[B02C 11/04] Feeding devices -- The CPC subclass B02C 11/04 covers auxiliary devices or accessories specifically designed for feeding material into grain-mill crushing or pulverising units. It includes mechanisms that regulate, direct or supply grain to the mill's grinding chamber, ensuring continuous operation and consistent feed rates. This subclass falls under the broader categories of performing operations and crushing, pulverising, or disintegrating in general.
[B02C 11/06] Arrangements for preventing fire or explosion -- B02C 11/06 covers safety arrangements built into grain-mill auxiliary devices to prevent fire or explosion during crushing, pulverising, or disintegrating operations. It includes fire-suppression systems, spark-arresting mechanisms, inert-gas or dust-removal installations and other protective measures specifically adapted for milling equipment.
[B02C 11/08] Cooling, heating, ventilating, conditioning with respect to temperature or water content -- B02C 11/08 covers auxiliary devices for grain mills that adjust the temperature or moisture of the material being processed. It includes coolers, heaters, ventilators and conditioning units used to heat, cool, ventilate or otherwise control the water content of grain during crushing, pulverising or disintegrating operations.
[B02C 1/12] Mills with non-rotating spiked members -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Disintegrating devices with spiked members specially adapted for breaking-up bottles: -> B02C19/0081
[B02C 1/14] Stamping mills -- Stamping mills are machines that crush or pulverise material by means of reciprocating members that repeatedly strike the feed. The rapid up-and-down action breaks the material into smaller particles, commonly used in ore-processing and mineral-reduction operations.
[B02C 13/00] Disintegrating by mills having rotary beater elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mills with beaters rigidly connected or hinged for swinging movement to a horizontally or vertically arranged rotor for crushing material by impact. The material may be thrown against an anvil or impact member.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Devices for disintegrating materials by collision of these materials against a breaking surface or breaking body and/or by friction between the material particles (also for gra) -> B02C19/0012
[B02C 13/02] with horizontal rotor shaft -- B02C 13/02 covers crushing, pulverising or disintegrating processes that use mills equipped with rotary beater elements whose rotor shaft is oriented horizontally, enabling the material to be broken down by the rotating beaters during operation.
[B02C 13/04] with beaters hinged to the rotor -- This CPC subclass B02C 13/04 covers crushing, pulverising or disintegrating processes performed with mills that have beaters hinged to the rotating rotor. The hinged beaters permit flexible impact on the material as the rotor turns, enhancing grinding efficiency in general disintegration operations.
[B02C 13/06] with beaters rigidly connected to the rotor -- The subclass B02C 13/06 describes mills that crush, pulverise or disintegrate material by means of rotary beater elements that are rigidly attached to the rotor, so the beaters rotate as an integral part of the rotor assembly.
