[B03C 1/025] High gradient magnetic separators -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic separation devices that use high gradient magnetic fields, wherein matrix elements, e.g. of steel wool, are disposed within the magnetic fields.
[B03C 1/027] with reciprocating canisters -- The B03C 1/027 subclass defines magnetic separation apparatus that employs one or more canisters moving back and forth to isolate magnetic particles from non-magnetic material. The reciprocating canisters generate alternating magnetic fields or convey material through a magnetic zone, enhancing extraction efficiency in solid-solid or solid-fluid streams.
[B03C 1/029] with circulating matrix or matrix elements -- The subclass B03C 1/029 covers magnetic separation processes that employ a circulating matrix or matrix elements, such as rotating drums, conveyors, or fluidized beds, to continuously move the material while a magnetic field extracts ferromagnetic particles from other solids or fluids. This arrangement allows ongoing separation without stopping the flow of the bulk material.
[B03C 1/03] rotating, e.g. of the carousel type -- This subclass concerns magnetic separation apparatus in which the magnetic field or the separation unit is rotated, for example in carousel-type separators. The rotation helps improve particle exposure to the magnetic field and is used for separating solid particles from other solids or fluids.
[B03C 1/031] Component parts -- B03C 1/031 relates to the individual component parts of magnetic separation equipment, such as magnetic drums, rollers, sleeves or magnetic field generators, which are used to separate solid particles from other solids or fluids by magnetic forces.
[B03C 1/032] Matrix cleaning systems -- B03C 1/032 covers matrix-cleaning systems used in magnetic separation equipment, where the magnetic or electrostatic matrix (such as a drum, belt or plate) is periodically cleared of retained particles to maintain separation efficiency. The claim typically includes mechanisms for removing, scraping or washing the matrix without interrupting the processing flow.
[B03C 1/033] characterised by the magnetic circuit -- The subclass B03C 1/033 covers magnetic separation processes that are defined by the use of a magnetic circuit to create the separating field. It includes devices where the arrangement of magnetic poles, yokes or cores directs magnetic flux through the material stream, enabling selective attraction or repulsion of ferromagnetic particles.
[B03C 1/0332] using permanent magnets -- It covers magnetic-separation processes that employ permanent magnets to create the magnetic field and draw magnetic particles out of solid mixtures or solid-fluid suspensions. The method is used in recycling, mining and waste handling to isolate ferrous or paramagnetic components without the need for external power.
[B03C 1/0335] using coils -- The CPC subclass B03C 1/0335 covers magnetic or electrostatic separation methods that employ coils to create the magnetic field used for separating solid particles from other solids or from fluids. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, separation of solid materials using liquids or pneumatic means, and specifically magnetic or electrostatic separation.
[B03C 1/0337] superconductive -- This subclass covers magnetic-separation techniques that use superconducting magnets or superconductive components to produce the magnetic field for separating solid particles from other solids or fluids, including apparatus and processes where a superconductive coil supplies the force that attracts or repels magnetic material.
[B03C 1/034] characterised by the matrix elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Component parts or auxiliary operations of high gradient magnetic separators characterised by the matrix elements, e.g. details about the construction of the magnetic matrix elements.
[B03C 1/035] Open gradient magnetic separators, i.e. separators in which the gap is unobstructed, characterised by the configuration of the gap -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic separators characterised by the configuration of an unobstructed or open gap employed within the magnetic field, such that the gap lacks a matrix element.
[B03C 1/0355] using superconductive coils -- This subclass covers magnetic separation processes in which the separating field is produced by superconductive coils, allowing very high magnetic flux densities to pull or repel solid particles from other solids or from fluids. The use of superconductivity enhances efficiency and enables separation of finely magnetic or weakly magnetic materials.
[B03C 1/04] with the material carriers in the form of trays or with tables -- This subclass deals with magnetic or electrostatic separation in which the material to be processed rests on or moves across carriers such as trays, tables or similar surfaces. The carriers support the solid feed while a magnetic field extracts magnetic particles, enabling continuous or batch separation.
[B03C 1/06] with magnets moving during operation -- Magnetic separation in which the magnets themselves are set in motion during the process, such as rotating, walking, or sliding, to create a changing magnetic field that captures or releases ferromagnetic particles from a solid or fluid mixture. This dynamic approach enhances separation efficiency compared with static magnetic fields.
[B03C 1/08] with non-movable magnets -- B03C 1/08 pertains to magnetic separation processes in which the magnetic field is generated by magnets that are fixed in position rather than moving with the material stream. The classification covers apparatus and methods that separate solid particles from other solids or fluids by passing them over or through stationary magnets, allowing magnetic components to be retained while non-magnetic matter continues onward.
[B03C 1/10] with cylindrical material carriers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic separation in which either (a) the material to be separated or (b) the separated material is moved with cylindrical means, e.g. drums or discs.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Magnetic separation with material carried by travelling fields obtained by a rotating magnetic drum -> B03C1/247
[B03C 11/00] Separation by high-voltage electrical fields, not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This group is used for electrostatically separating liquids from liquids by high-voltage electrical fields, not provided for in other groups of this subclass.  **Glossary:** - high-voltage: voltage of 1000V (RMS) or more for alternating current and 1500V or more for direct current
[B03C 1/12] with magnets moving during operation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic separation with cylindrical material carriers in which either (a) the magnets are moving during operation or (b) the magnets include movable pole pieces during operation.
[B03C 1/14] with non-movable magnets -- This subclass describes magnetic separation techniques that use fixed, non-movable magnets to attract and remove magnetic particles from solid or fluid streams, such as in conveyor belts or static separators. It excludes systems where the magnetic field source moves relative to the material.
[B03C 1/145] with rotating annular or disc-shaped material carriers -- This subclass covers magnetic or electrostatic separation processes that employ rotating annular or disc-shaped carriers to transport the material through the separating field. The rotating carriers continuously present the feed to the magnetic field, allowing efficient extraction of ferrous or paramagnetic particles from solids or fluids.
[B03C 1/16] with material carriers in the form of belts -- This CPC subclass covers magnetic separation processes where the material to be separated is conveyed on belt-type carriers, enabling continuous removal of magnetic particles from a flow of solids or liquids. It falls under separation of solid materials using liquids or pneumatic tables, specifically magnetic or electrostatic separation.
[B03C 1/18] with magnets moving during operation -- B03C 1/18 covers magnetic separation processes in which the magnets are set in motion during operation, such as rotating, oscillating, or reciprocating magnetic fields applied to a material stream. The moving magnets enhance the capture and transport of magnetic particles, allowing continuous or batch separation of solids from solids or fluids.
[B03C 1/20] in the form of belts, e.g. cross-belt type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Magnetic separation with magnets moving during operation and with material carriers in the form of belts, e.g. of cross-belt type, multiple belt carriers characterised by their mutual disposition or combinations of magnetic separating belts with material carrying belts.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1. Cross belt magnetic separator  This example shows a cross belt magnetic separator having a main conveyor belt (6) and cross belts (21).  2. Multiple belt carriers  This example shows multiple belt carriers characterised by their mutual disposition in which magnetic bars (6) are moved by endless conveyor chains (5) and a set of conveyor chains (13) having drive scrapers (14) at an oblique angle with respect to the magnetic bars (6).  3. Combination of magnetic separating belts with material carrying belts  This example shows a combination of magnetic separating belts with material carrying belts in which a material carrying belt (8) is working in conjunction with another belt (1) that is magnetized by a magnetic roll (20).
[B03C 1/22] with non-movable magnets -- B03C 1/22 covers magnetic separation processes that employ permanent, non-movable magnets to attract and separate magnetic particles from a mixture of solids or fluids. The classification applies when the magnetic field is generated by fixed magnets rather than rotating or electromagnetic devices.
[B03C 1/23] with material carried by oscillating fields -- This subclass covers magnetic-separation processes in which the material is moved by an oscillating magnetic field that is alternately switched on, off or changes direction, causing the particles to be conveyed through the field. It includes devices that rely on the field itself to transport the material rather than using mechanical conveyors.
[B03C 1/24] with material carried by travelling fields -- Magnetic-separation processes in which the material to be separated is conveyed by a travelling magnetic field, such as a moving or rotating field that transports particles while magnetically attracting the magnetic fraction, are classified under B03C 1/24.
[B03C 1/247] obtained by a rotating magnetic drum -- This subclass covers magnetic separation methods that use a rotating magnetic drum to create a moving magnetic field, allowing magnetic particles to be drawn onto the drum while non-magnetic material is released. The process is applied to separate solid substances from other solids or fluids.
[B03C 1/253] obtained by a linear motor -- B03C 1/253 covers magnetic-separation equipment in which the separating field is generated by a linear motor, using the motor's motion to produce a controllable magnetic field that separates solid particles from other solids or fluids. This subclass falls under magnetic or electrostatic separation of solid materials and specifies the use of linear-motor technology for field generation.
[B03C 1/26] with free falling material -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Open gradient magnetic separators, i.e. separators in which the gap is unobstructed, characterised by the configuration of the gap -> B03C1/035
[B03C 1/28] Magnetic plugs and dipsticks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices or methods for separating particles contained in a liquid by using magnetic plugs or dipsticks.
[B03C 1/282] with associated accumulation indicator, e.g. Hall sensor -- The subclass B03C 1/282 covers magnetic separators that incorporate an accumulation indicator, such as a Hall-effect sensor, to detect or signal the amount of material collected on the magnetic element during operation. This feature enables monitoring of the separation efficiency or the need for material removal.
[B03C 1/284] with associated cleaning means, e.g. retractable non-magnetic sleeve -- This subclass covers magnetic separation apparatus that incorporate cleaning features, such as a retractable non-magnetic sleeve that can be withdrawn to clear material adhering to the magnetic elements while the separator remains in operation.
[B03C 1/286] disposed at the inner circumference of a recipient, e.g. magnetic drain bolt -- The subclass B03C 1/286 covers magnetic separation devices that are installed on the inner circumference of a vessel or similar recipient, such as magnetic drain bolts used to capture ferromagnetic particles as liquids or slurries are removed. These elements employ a magnetic field to attract and retain solid contaminants while permitting the fluid to flow out.
[B03C 1/288] disposed at the outer circumference of a recipient -- Magnetic separators of this type locate the magnetic elements around the outer wall of a container or jig, creating a peripheral field that draws magnetic particles from a solid-solid or solid-fluid mixture inside the vessel, while non-magnetic material remains in the bulk. This arrangement enables separation without internal components interfering with the process.
[B03C 1/30] Combinations with other devices, not otherwise provided for -- Magnetic separation devices combined with other apparatuses that are not specifically covered elsewhere fall under CPC class B03C 1/30. This subclass includes integrated systems where magnetic separators are coupled with additional equipment, such as conveyors, filters, or handling mechanisms, to perform joint operations.
[B03C 1/32] acting on the medium containing the substance being separated, e.g. magneto-gravimetric-, magnetohydrostatic-, or magnetohydrodynamic separation -- The code B03C 1/32 covers magnetic separation techniques in which the fluid or other medium containing the material to be separated is itself acted upon by magnetic forces, such as magneto-gravimetric, magnetohydrostatic or magnetohydrodynamic processes, to separate solid particles from other solids or from fluids.
[B03C 2201/00] Details of magnetic or electrostatic separation -- B03C 2201/00 covers the detailed aspects of magnetic or electrostatic separation techniques used to separate solid particles from other solids or fluids. It includes the design of magnetic fields, electrode configurations, and operating parameters that affect separation efficiency. This subclass is located under the broader groups dealing with solid material separation using liquids, pneumatic tables, or jigs.
[B03C 2201/02] Electrostatic separation of liquids from liquids -- B03C 2201/02 deals with the use of electrostatic forces to separate one liquid from another. The method applies an electric field to exploit differences in dielectric constant, conductivity or chargeability, causing the liquids to migrate to opposite electrodes or to be collected separately. This is a sub-category of magnetic or electrostatic separation within the performing-operations section.
[B03C 2201/04] Ionising electrode being a wire -- A subclass for electrostatic separators where the ionising element is a wire that creates a corona discharge to charge solid particles, enabling their subsequent separation by electrostatic forces.
[B03C 2201/06] Ionising electrode being a needle -- This subgroup covers electrostatic separators that use a needle-shaped ionising electrode to charge particles for magnetic or electrostatic separation of solid materials from other solids or fluids.
[B03C 2201/08] Ionising electrode being a rod -- This CPC subclass (B03C 2201/08) concerns magnetic or electrostatic separation where the ionising electrode is a rod, used to charge particles so they can be separated from other solids or fluids. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, separation of solid materials with liquids or pneumatic means, and specifically magnetic/electrostatic separation.
[B03C 2201/10] Ionising electrode with two or more serrated ends or sides -- The code B03C 2201/10 designates an ionising electrode that features two or more serrated ends or sides, used in magnetic or electrostatic processes for separating solid particles from other solids or fluids. This falls within the broader class for separation of solid materials by liquids, pneumatic tables or jigs, specifically under magnetic or electrostatic separation methods.
[B03C 2201/12] Cleaning the device by burning the trapped particles -- The entry covers methods for cleaning magnetic or electrostatic separators in which trapped particles are removed by burning or incinerating them on the device, restoring its functional surfaces and preventing buildup that could impair separation efficiency. This technique is applied after solid-material separation using liquid, pneumatic tables, or jigs and is intended to maintain performance without disassembly.
[B03C 2201/14] the gas being moved electro-kinetically -- Methods that move a gas by electro-kinetic forces such as electrophoresis or electro-osmotic flow within magnetic or electrostatic separation apparatus. The gas is driven through porous media or channels by an applied electric field to aid the separation of solid particles from solids or fluids.
[B03C 2201/16] Magnetic separation of gases from gases, e.g. oxygen from air -- This subclass covers methods and apparatuses that use magnetic forces to separate one gas from another, such as extracting oxygen from air by exploiting the paramagnetic properties of oxygen molecules. The techniques involve generating magnetic fields that preferentially attract or repel specific gases to achieve purification or concentration.
[B03C 2201/18] Magnetic separation whereby the particles are suspended in a liquid -- The code B03C 2201/18 covers magnetic separation processes in which solid particles are dispersed in a liquid medium and then separated by applying a magnetic field. It includes techniques that use magnetic forces to attract, repel or align the particles while they remain suspended, enabling the recovery of magnetic or magnetizable material from slurry or other liquid suspensions.
[B03C 2201/20] Magnetic separation of bulk or dry particles in mixtures -- This sub-class covers methods and apparatus for separating dry or bulk solid particles from a mixture by applying magnetic forces, typically using magnets, magnetic drums or conveyors to attract ferromagnetic or paramagnetic components while non-magnetic material remains. It includes processes where the particles are not suspended in a liquid or gas.
[B03C 2201/22] characterised by the magnetic field, e.g. its shape or generation -- This CPC subclass B03C 2201/22 covers magnetic or electrostatic separation where the process is defined by the magnetic field itself, such as its shape, configuration, or method of generation. It applies to separating solid materials using magnetic fields tailored to influence particle trajectories.
[B03C 2201/24] for measuring or calculating of parameters, e.g. efficiency -- The subclass B03C 2201/24 covers methods and devices for measuring or calculating performance parameters of magnetic or electrostatic solid-material separation, such as separation efficiency, throughput, energy use or related indices. It includes sensors, instrumentation, computational models and data-analysis techniques used to assess and optimise the separation process.
[B03C 2201/26] for use in medical or biological applications -- B03C 2201/26 relates to magnetic or electrostatic separation techniques that are adapted for medical or biological purposes, such as isolating cells, tissues, biomolecules, or sanitary waste. The subclass covers apparatuses and processes that employ magnetic fields or electrostatic charges to sort solid particles in a clinical or research setting.
[B03C 2201/28] Parts being designed to be removed for cleaning purposes -- The code B03C 2201/28 covers removable components of magnetic or electrostatic separation equipment that are intended to be taken out for cleaning. It includes detachable screens, rollers, electrodes or similar parts that can be separated from the main device to allow routine maintenance and hygiene.
[B03C 2201/30] for use in or with vehicles -- The B03C 2201/30 subclass concerns magnetic or electrostatic separation technologies that are intended for use in or with vehicles, encompassing apparatuses, methods, or components that remove solid particles from other solids or fluids inside automotive or transport systems. This includes onboard sorting or filtration devices that rely on magnetic or electrostatic forces to separate materials while the vehicle is operating.
[B03C 2201/32] Checking the quality of the result or the well-functioning of the device -- The code B03C 2201/32 relates to procedures for verifying the quality of the separation outcome or the proper operation of magnetic or electrostatic separators used to separate solid materials from other solids or fluids. It covers testing, inspection, or monitoring methods that ensure the device performs as intended and that the separated product meets required specifications.
[B03C 3/00] Separating dispersed particles from gases or vapour, e.g. air, by electrostatic effect -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or devices using an electrostatic effect for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapours, e.g. devices that use electrostatic effects for filtering air.
[B03C 3/01] Pretreatment of the gases prior to electrostatic precipitation -- B03C 3/01 covers methods for conditioning or cleaning gases before they enter an electrostatic precipitator, such as removing moisture, contaminants, or adjusting temperature to improve particle capture efficiency. It falls under separation of dispersed particles from gases by electrostatic effect within the broader category of solid-material separation using liquids or pneumatic means.
[B03C 3/011] Prefiltering -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanical filtering or flow control before the actual ESP filter.
[B03C 3/013] Conditioning by chemical additives, e.g. with SO -- The subclass B03C 3/013 covers the use of chemical additives, such as sulfur oxides, to condition a gas stream before electrostatic separation of dispersed particles. By introducing these additives the particles are more readily charged or agglomerated, which improves the efficiency of electrostatic precipitators and related separation equipment.
[B03C 3/014] Addition of water -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Adding water for the purpose of changing the characteristics of the gas mixture to be treated.
[B03C 3/016] by acoustic or electromagnetic energy, e.g. ultraviolet light -- B03C 3/016 covers methods for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapours by applying acoustic or electromagnetic energy, such as ultraviolet light, to induce forces that cause the particles to migrate or be collected, often in conjunction with electrostatic or magnetic capture techniques.
[B03C 3/017] Combinations of electrostatic separation with other processes, not otherwise provided for -- This subclass covers methods that integrate electrostatic separation of particles from gas or vapor with additional treatment steps such as filtration, drying, or chemical reactions, where the combination is not specifically listed elsewhere in the scheme. It includes hybrid systems that use the electrostatic effect together with mechanical, thermal, or chemical processes to improve separation efficiency or product recovery.
[B03C 3/0175] Amassing particles by electric fields, e.g. agglomeration -- B03C 3/0175 pertains to the process of amassing dispersed particles from gases or vapour through the use of electric fields, such as agglomeration techniques, within the broader context of magnetic or electrostatic separation methods.
[B03C 3/019] Post-treatment of gases -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanical filtering or flow control after the actual ESP filter.
[B03C 3/02] Plant or installations having external electricity supply -- B03C 3/02 covers plants or installations equipped with an external electricity supply to generate electrostatic fields for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapours, such as air. The classification applies to equipment used in magnetic or electrostatic solid-material separation processes where external power is required.
[B03C 3/025] Combinations of electrostatic separators, e.g. in parallel or in series, stacked separators or dry-wet separator combinations -- B03C 3/025 pertains to arrangements that combine two or more electrostatic separators, such as parallel or series units, stacked configurations, or hybrids of dry and wet separators, for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapors by electrostatic action.
[B03C 3/04] dry type -- The B03C 3/04 class covers dry electrostatic separators that remove dispersed solid particles from gases or vapours by charging the particles and collecting them on oppositely charged electrodes, without using liquids. It applies to air-filtration, dust extraction and similar gas-cleaning processes.
[B03C 3/06] characterised by presence of stationary tube electrodes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices wherein stationary tube electrodes are used, such as in a bundle of stationary tube electrodes.
[B03C 3/08] characterised by presence of stationary flat electrodes arranged with their flat surfaces parallel to the gas stream -- The code covers electrostatic separators that remove dispersed particles from a gas or vapour stream by using stationary flat electrodes whose flat faces are aligned parallel to the direction of the gas flow, creating an electric field that attracts or repels the particles as they pass. This arrangement is used for separating solid particles from gases such as air.
[B03C 3/09] characterised by presence of stationary flat electrodes arranged with their flat surfaces at right angles to the gas stream -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Dry type plants or installations having external electricity supply for separating of dispersed particles from gases or vapour by electrostatic effect, characterised by the presence of stationary flat electrodes arranged with their flat surfaces at right angles to the gas stream, e.g. where the gas stream is forced to change direction to flow between flat electrodes or where the gas stream passes through porous electrodes.
[B03C 3/10] characterised by presence of electrodes moving during separating action -- The CPC subclass B03C 3/10 covers devices that separate particulate matter from gases or vapors by electrostatic forces in which the electrodes are not fixed but move during the separating action. The moving electrodes improve particle collection efficiency by varying the electric field geometry as the gas stream passes through. This falls under electrostatic separation of solids from gases within the broader performing-operations hierarchy.
[B03C 3/12] characterised by separation of ionising and collecting stations -- This CPC subclass covers electrostatic separation of dispersed particles from gases or vapours where the ionising region and the particle-collecting region are provided as separate stations. Such arrangements are used in electrostatic precipitators and similar equipment that first charge particles in one zone and then collect them on electrodes in another zone.
[B03C 3/14] characterised by the additional use of mechanical effects, e.g. gravity -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Transportable units, e.g. for cleaning room air -> B03C3/32
[B03C 3/145] Inertia -- B03C 3/145 covers the use of inertial forces to separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours, such as cyclones and other inertial separators that divert particles from the carrier stream. It falls under the broader category of magnetic or electrostatic separation of solid materials from fluids.
[B03C 3/15] Centrifugal forces -- B03C 3/15 concerns the use of centrifugal forces to assist the separation of solid particles from gases or vapours, typically within magnetic or electrostatic separation devices. The classification covers methods and apparatuses that generate a rotating motion to drive particles toward a collection surface, enhancing removal efficiency in air cleaning, dust collection and related processes.
[B03C 3/155] Filtration -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanical filtering combined with the actual ESP filter.
[B03C 3/16] wet type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices where the added liquid (e.g. water) is not completely absorbed by the treated gas.
[B03C 3/28] Plant or installations without electricity supply, e.g. using electrets -- B03C 3/28 covers electrostatic separation plants that operate without an external electricity source, typically using electrets or permanently charged surfaces to impart charge to dispersed particles in gases or vapours for their collection or removal.
[B03C 3/30] in which electrostatic charge is generated by passage of the gases, i.e. tribo-electricity -- B03C 3/30 covers techniques for removing solid particles from a gas or vapor stream in which the electrostatic charge needed for separation is created by the gases themselves, typically through triboelectric friction as they flow. The method relies on the charge generated during passage of the gas to attract and collect dispersed particles without external charging electrodes.
[B03C 3/32] Transportable units, e.g. for cleaning room air -- B03C 3/32 covers portable devices that clean ambient air by removing suspended particles through electrostatic action, typically using a charging electrode and a collection electrode to attract and capture dust, smoke or aerosol particles; examples include mobile air-purifiers and transportable filtration units for indoor environments.
[B03C 3/34] Constructional details or accessories or operation thereof -- B03C 3/34 deals with the design, components and operating features of equipment that separates dispersed particles from gases or vapour by electrostatic forces. It falls under the magnetic or electrostatic separation of solid materials from solids, liquids or fluids and specifically addresses constructional details, accessories and the way such apparatuses are operated.
[B03C 3/36] Controlling flow of gases or vapour -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Flow control in the actual ESP filter.
[B03C 3/361] by static mechanical means, e.g. deflector -- This subgroup covers methods that remove particles from a gas stream using static mechanical devices such as deflectors that rely on electrostatic forces. It is part of magnetic or electrostatic separation of solid materials from solids or fluids, aimed at separating dispersed particles from gases or vapour.
[B03C 3/363] located before the filter -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The flow control is located at the entrance of the ESP
[B03C 3/365] located after the filter -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The flow control is located at the exit of the ESP
[B03C 3/366] located in the filter, e.g. special shape of the electrodes -- This subclass addresses electrostatic separation of dispersed particles from gases or vapour, specifically where the separating elements are positioned inside the filter. It covers the design and shape of electrodes placed within the filter to improve particle capture and collection efficiency.
[B03C 3/368] by other than static mechanical means, e.g. internal ventilator or recycler -- The code B03C 3/368 covers electrostatic separation of particles from a gas or vapour where the airflow is generated by active devices such as internal ventilators or recyclers rather than by static mechanical structures. It pertains to methods that move the gas stream dynamically to enhance particle charging and collection without relying on fixed ducts or tables.
[B03C 3/38] Particle charging or ionising stations, e.g. using electric discharge, radioactive radiation or flames -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Particle charging or ionising stations in which particles are electrostatically charged for the purpose of separating them, e.g. using electric discharge, radioactive radiation or flames.
[B03C 3/383] using radiation -- B03C 3/383 covers methods that employ radiation to impart charge on dispersed particles so they can be separated from gases or vapours by electrostatic forces. The radiation, such as ultraviolet or ionizing beams, is used to ionise or photo-charge the particles before they are drawn to oppositely charged electrodes or collectors.
[B03C 3/386] using flames -- B03C 3/386 concerns the use of flames in electrostatic or magnetic separation processes that remove dispersed solid particles from gases or vapours. The flame creates ionisation or a temperature gradient that enhances the electrostatic force acting on the particles, allowing their collection on electrodes or magnetic collectors. This subclass falls under the CPC hierarchy for separation of solid materials using liquids, pneumatic tables or jigs and magnetic or electrostatic separation.
[B03C 3/40] Electrode constructions -- B03C 3/40 covers the design and arrangement of electrodes used in electrostatic separation processes, particularly for removing dispersed solid particles from gases or vapours such as air. It includes various electrode shapes, materials, spacing and mounting methods that create the electric field needed to charge particles and direct them toward collection electrodes or plates. The subclass focuses on the construction details that affect efficiency, durability and safety of the electrostatic separator.
[B03C 3/41] Ionising-electrodes -- B03C 3/41 Ionising-electrodes refers to electrodes that produce ions to charge dispersed particles in a gas or vapour stream, enabling their removal by electrostatic separation. The class includes designs of ion-generating devices used in air-cleaning, exhaust-gas treatment and similar applications where charged particles are collected on oppositely charged surfaces. It is a subcategory of magnetic or electrostatic separation of solid materials from gases.
[B03C 3/43] radioactive -- B03C 3/43 covers the electrostatic removal of radioactive particles from gas or vapor streams. It pertains to methods that separate dispersed radioactive solids from a gaseous phase by inducing an electrostatic charge and collecting the charged particles on an electrode or similar collector. This subclass falls under magnetic or electrostatic solid-material separation techniques used in air-cleaning and filtration processes.
[B03C 3/45] Collecting-electrodes -- B03C 3/45 concerns collecting electrodes used in electrostatic separation of dispersed particles from gases or vapours. The electrodes capture charged particles after they are deflected by an electric field, allowing their removal from the gas stream.
[B03C 3/455] specially adapted for heat exchange with the gas stream  B03C3/53  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Liquid, or liquid-film, electrodes -> B03C3/53
[B03C 3/47] flat, e.g. plates, discs, gratings -- B03C 3/47 relates to electrostatic separation techniques that treat flat solid items such as plates, discs or gratings, removing dispersed particles from a gas or vapour stream. The process uses an electric field to charge the particles, which are then collected while the flat workpiece passes through the separator.
[B03C 3/49] tubular -- B03C 3/49 relates to tubular electrostatic precipitators and similar cylindrical or tube-shaped apparatus that remove dispersed particles from a gas or vapor stream by generating an electrostatic field to attract and collect the particles on internal surfaces.
[B03C 3/51] Catch- space electrodes, e.g. slotted-box form -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Constructional details of a collecting-electrode where the collecting-electrode is a catch-space electrode used in separating dispersed particles from gases or vapor by electrostatic effect.
[B03C 3/53] Liquid, or liquid-film, electrodes -- B03C 3/53 covers the use of liquid or liquid-film electrodes in electrostatic or magnetoelectric separators that remove dispersed particles from gases or vapours. The liquid layer serves as the charging or collecting electrode, providing a conductive surface while remaining in contact with the gas stream.
[B03C 3/60] Use of special materials other than liquids -- B03C 3/60 covers methods that employ non-liquid special materials such as powders, granules or membranes to enhance electrostatic separation of dispersed particles from gases or vapours. The materials are used to improve charge transfer, particle capture or filtration during the separation process.
[B03C 3/62] ceramics -- B03C 3/62 deals with electrostatic separation techniques applied to ceramic particles dispersed in a gas or vapor, such as air. It falls under performing operations, specifically magnetic or electrostatic separation of solids from gases, and concerns the removal or classification of ceramic dust or fine particles using electrostatic fields.
[B03C 3/64] synthetic resins -- The CPC subclass B03C 3/64 covers the electrostatic separation of dispersed particles from gases or vapours, where the material being treated is synthetic resin. It falls under magnetic or electrostatic separation of solid materials from fluids, specifically the separation of solid particles from air-borne streams using electrostatic forces. This operation is used to remove or collect resin particles from gaseous streams.
[B03C 3/66] Applications of electricity supply techniques -- B03C 3/66 covers the use of electrical power techniques to generate the electrostatic fields required for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapours, such as in dust collectors or air-cleaning devices. It includes wiring, voltage control and supply arrangements that enable magnetic or electrostatic separation in solid-liquid or solid-solid processes.
[B03C 3/68] Control systems therefor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details about the electrical power supply of the ESP, except the emergency control aspects.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electricity supply or control systems for cleaning the electrodes -> B03C3/746 , B03C3/763
[B03C 3/70] insulating in electric separators -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Liquid, or liquid-film, electrodes -> B03C3/53
[B03C 3/72] Emergency control systems -- B03C 3/72 covers emergency control systems for magnetic or electrostatic separators that handle solid materials or fluids, providing rapid shutdown, safety interlocks, and fault monitoring to protect equipment and operators when abnormal conditions such as overloads, fires, or power failures occur.
[B03C 3/74] Cleaning the electrodes -- B03C 3/74 covers methods and apparatus for cleaning the electrodes used in electrostatic or magnetic separation of solid particles from gases or vapour. Maintaining electrode surfaces free of deposits ensures consistent electrostatic charging and efficient particle capture in air-based separation systems.
[B03C 3/743] by using friction, e.g. by brushes or sliding elements -- B03C 3/743 relates to methods that separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours by generating an electrostatic charge through friction, for example using brushes or sliding contacts. The technique falls under magnetic or electrostatic separation of solid materials from solids or fluids, within the broader class of separation of solid materials using liquids or pneumatic tables.
[B03C 3/746] Electricity supply or control systems therefor -- B03C 3/746 covers the electrical power and control circuitry needed for electrostatic separators that remove dispersed particles from gases or vapours. It includes the design of voltage sources, electrode arrangements and control mechanisms that create and maintain the electrostatic field used in such separation processes.
[B03C 3/76] by using a mechanical vibrator, e.g. rapping gear -- The B03C 3/76 subgroup covers methods for separating dispersed particles from gases or vapours using electrostatic forces while a mechanical vibrator, such as a rapping gear or shaking device, is applied to the separating element to enhance particle detachment and improve collection efficiency.
[B03C 3/761] Drive-transmitting devices therefor, e.g. insulated shafts -- The CPC subclass B03C 3/761 covers the mechanical components that convey power to electrostatic particle separators, for example insulated shafts, couplings, or other drive-transmitting elements that connect a motor to the separator electrodes. These devices ensure safe and efficient transmission of rotational or linear motion while maintaining electrical insulation. They are used in systems that remove dispersed particles from gases or vapours by electrostatic means.
[B03C 3/763] Electricity supply or control systems therefor -- B03C 3/763 relates to the electrical power and control arrangements employed in magnetic or electrostatic separators that handle solid particles. The entry covers the supply of electricity and the circuitry that manages separator operation, such as voltage regulation, switching, and safety devices.
[B03C 3/765] with electromagnetic rappers -- B03C 3/765 covers devices that use electromagnetic rappers to aid the electrostatic separation of dispersed particles from gases or vapours. The rappers generate a varying magnetic or electric field that excites or lifts the particles, improving their capture on electrodes. This sub-class falls under magnetic or electrostatic separation of solid materials from fluids.
[B03C 3/766] with pneumatic rappers -- B03C 3/766 covers electrostatic or magnetic separation of solid particles from gases or vapors in which the material is fed and transported by pneumatic rappers. The method uses a rapid pulsed air stream to dislodge particles and enhance their collection on charged electrodes. It is a sub-group of magnetic/electrostatic separation of solid materials from gases.
[B03C 3/768] with free falling masses, e.g. dropped metal balls -- This sub-group covers electrostatic or magnetostatic separation of particles suspended in a gas or vapor where the particles are introduced as free-falling masses, such as dropped metal balls, allowing charge or field effects to separate them from the carrier stream.
[B03C 3/78] by washing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices or methods using a liquid where the purpose of the liquid is to clean the electrodes.
[B03C 3/80] by gas or solid particle blasting -- B03C 3/80 covers methods that separate dispersed particles from a gaseous or vapour stream by blasting the stream with a high-velocity gas jet or solid particles, creating electrostatic forces that cause the particles to be removed.
[B03C 3/82] Housings -- The B03C 3/82 class covers housings that contain or enclose equipment used for magnetic or electrostatic separation of solid particles from gases or vapours. These structures provide the necessary space, support and sealing to direct the gas stream, protect the separator components and ensure safe, efficient operation.
[B03C 3/84] Protective coatings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Coatings or special layers of the housing, not of the electrodes.
[B03C 3/86] Electrode-carrying means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details about the (mechanical) fixation of the electrodes (including the electrical isolators).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electrode constructions -> B03C3/40
[B03C 3/88] Cleaning-out collected particles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cleaning out collected particles that have already been removed from the electrodes or walls.
[B03C 3/885] by travelling or oscillating electric fields, e.g. electric field curtains -- The B03C 3/885 subclass covers methods that separate dispersed particles from gases or vapours by applying travelling or oscillating electric fields, such as electric-field curtains, to create an electrostatic force that deflects or captures the particles. It is a specific form of electrostatic separation within the broader category of solid-material separation using liquids or pneumatic devices.
[B03C 5/00] Separating dispersed particles from liquids by electrostatic effect -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Centrifuges combined with other apparatus, e.g. electrostatic separators -> B04B5/10  **Glossary:** - electrostatic field or electrostatic effect: caused by electric charges that are either stationary or move very slowly (no induced magnetic forces) - electrodynamic field or electrodynamic effect: caused by electric charges that are moving with high frequency, e.g. for creation of electromagnetic radiation from aerial or antenna - separating: includes separation of particles from liquids as is conventionally understood, as well as the immobilisation, caging, translation or rotational motion of particles
[B03C 5/005] Dielectrophoresis, i.e. dielectric particles migrating towards the region of highest field strength -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Separating dispersed particles from liquids by electrostatic effect by using di-electrophoresis, or the motion of polarizable particles under the influence of an applied non-uniform electric field, with the force arising from the interaction of the field and the dipole moment induced in the particle.
[B03C 5/02] Separators -- B03C 5/02 defines separators that remove dispersed particles from liquids by exploiting electrostatic forces. These devices belong to the group of magnetic or electrostatic separators used for solid-material separation from fluids, typically in processes where particles are charged and attracted to oppositely charged electrodes.
[B03C 5/022] Non-uniform field separators -- The B03C 5/022 subclass includes separators that use a non-uniform magnetic or electrostatic field to move dispersed particles toward regions of higher or lower field intensity, allowing solid particles to be collected from liquids or other solids based on magnetic susceptibility or charge. It covers devices with pole pieces, electrodes or similar structures that create the required field gradient.
[B03C 5/024] using high-gradient differential dielectric separation, i.e. using a dielectric matrix polarised by an external field -- This subclass covers separation techniques that employ a dielectric matrix polarized by an external electric field to create a high-gradient differential dielectric force, causing particles with different dielectric constants to migrate and be collected. The method is used for extracting dispersed solid particles from liquids by exploiting differences in their polarization response.
[B03C 5/026] using open-gradient differential dielectric separation, i.e. using electrodes of special shapes for non-uniform field creation, e.g. Fluid Integrated Circuit [FIC] -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for separating particles from fluids with electrodes arranged, such that an open-gradient electrostatic field is created.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1. Electrode arrangement having an open-gradient electrostatic field  This example shows an electrode arrangement comprising interdigitated electrodes (11) and (12) that create an open-gradient electrostatic field.  2. Fluid filter having a dielectrophoretically active electrode element  This example shows a fluid filter that includes a dielectrophoretically active electrode element comprising a coiled substrate (200) upon which a pair of electrode arrays (201) are disposed.
[B03C 5/028] using travelling electric fields, i.e. travelling wave dielectrophoresis [TWD] -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1. Electrode arrangement having a time-varying electric field for movement of a biological cell  This example shows an electrode arrangement to which a time-varying electric field is applied for movement of a biological cell when polarised by the electric field.  2. Response of particles exposed to a travelling wave  This example shows the response of particles exposed to a travelling wave field with a phase shift in between neighbouring electrodes.
[B03C 7/00] Separating solids from solids by electrostatic effect -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Separating solids from solids by electrostatic effect, e.g. separating particles.
[B03C 7/003] Pretreatment of the solids prior to electrostatic separation -- The B03C 7/003 subclass concerns processes that condition or prepare solid particles before they are subjected to electrostatic separation, including cleaning, drying, size-reduction, de-agglomeration or surface modification steps that improve the efficiency or selectivity of the subsequent electrostatic sorting operation.
[B03C 7/006] Charging without electricity supply, e.g. by tribo-electricity or pyroelectricity -- The subclass B03C 7/006 covers methods of imparting an electrostatic charge to solid particles without an external power source, such as by triboelectric or pyroelectric effects. These techniques generate charge through friction, temperature changes, or material properties, allowing solids to be separated electrostatically without supplied electricity.
[B03C 7/02] Separators -- The subclass covers devices that separate solid particles from other solid particles by means of electrostatic forces, fitting within the broader group of magnetic or electrostatic separation methods for solid materials. It is part of the hierarchy for separation of solids using liquids, pneumatic tables or jigs.
[B03C 7/023] Non-uniform field separators -- B03C 7/023 covers non-uniform field separators that use electrostatic forces to separate solid particles from other solids, typically by creating a varying electric field that causes particles with different charge or conductivity to move differently. This subclass falls under magnetic and electrostatic separation methods within the broader category of separating solids from solids using liquids, pneumatic tables, or jigs. It is part of the CPC system shared by the EPO and USPTO.
[B03C 7/026] using travelling or oscillating electric fields -- B03C 7/026 covers the use of travelling or oscillating electric fields to effect electrostatic separation of solid particles, typically by inducing differing charges or dipoles that cause solids to separate from each other or from a fluid. This subclass falls under magnetic or electrostatic separation of solids, itself a subcategory of separation of solid materials using liquids, pneumatic tables or jigs, within the broader performing-operations group.
[B03C 7/04] with material carriers in the form of trays, troughs, or tables -- The B03C 7/04 subclass covers magnetic or electrostatic separation processes in which solid particles are moved over or through material carriers such as trays, troughs or tables, allowing the solids to be separated from other solids by electrostatic forces. It specifies the use of these shaped carriers to facilitate the separation operation.
[B03C 7/06] with cylindrical material carriers -- B03C 7/06 covers the use of cylindrical carriers such as rods, tubes or drums in electrostatic separation processes that separate solid particles from other solid particles. The method relies on charging the material on the cylinder and creating an electric field that attracts or repels specific fractions, enabling their collection or removal.
[B03C 7/08] with material carriers in the form of belts -- The code B03C 7/08 covers magnetic or electrostatic separation of solid materials in which the material to be separated is conveyed on a belt-type carrier. The belt moves the solids past a field that imparts opposite charges or magnetic attractions, allowing particles of different properties to be collected separately as they travel along the belt.
[B03C 7/10] with material falling in cascades -- B03C 7/10 covers magnetic or electrostatic separation processes in which solid particles are made to fall in cascading streams, allowing the electrostatic effect to separate one solid from another or from a fluid medium. The cascading motion enhances charge distribution and improves separation efficiency.
[B03C 7/12] with material falling free -- This subclass covers electrostatic separation processes in which solid particles are separated from other solids by allowing the material to fall freely through an electric field, rather than being transported on a conveyor or table. The falling-free configuration exploits charge differences to achieve segregation and is used in mineral processing and recycling operations.
[B03C 9/00] Electrostatic separation not provided for in any single one of the other main groups of this subclass -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electrostatic separation not provided for in any single one of the other main groups of this subclass, e.g. other types of electrostatic separation, except for electrostatically separating liquids from liquids by high-voltage electrical fields.
[B03D] FLOTATION -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus or methods using liquids for the separation of solids from solids, such apparatus or methods consisting of the following:  Flotation type separation apparatus or methods that may include one or more of the following:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Combinations of dry separating apparatus with wet separating apparatus -> B03B - Sink-float separation -> B03B5/28  **Glossary:** - Coagulation: The process by which a liquid changes to a thickened, curdlike, insoluble state by some kind of chemical or physical process, excluding by evaporation. - Differential sedimentation: A separation process using gravity or centrifugal force to initiate differential settling of suspended solids, for example at the bottom of a tank. Different solid materials settle at different rates. - Flocculation: This refers to a process where a solute comes out of solution in the form of floc or "flakes". The term is also used to refer to the process by which fine particulates are caused to clump together into floc. The floc may then float to the top of the liquid, settle to the bottom of the liquid, or can be readily filtered from the liquid. Sodium silicate is a common flocculating agent. - Flotation: (Also known as froth flotation) A separation process whereby water and chemicals are added to a mixture of solids to form a pulp while developing differences in surface tension between the various species in the solids mixture. The pulp is then copiously aerated and the preferred (aerophilic) species clings to bubbles and floats as a mineralized froth, which is then skimmed off.
[B03D 1/00] Flotation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Flotation type separation apparatus or methods that include one or more of the following:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Foam dispersion -> B01D19/02 - Separation of liquids and solids by sedimentation -> B01D21/00 - Mixing gases and vapours with liquids -> B01F23/00 - Mixing according to the phases to be mixed -> B01F23/20 - Devices for feeding measured quantities of reagents -> B01J4/02 - Conditioners -> B03B1/00 - Sink-float separation -> B03B5/28 - General arrangement of separating plant -> B03B9/00 - Treatment of water, waste water, sewage, or sludge by flotation when no structural details of general interest are disclosed. -> C02F1/24 - Electroflotation in waste water treatment -> C02F1/465  **Glossary:** - Coagulation: Formation of aggregates of particles, the coagula, induced by substances for reducing interparticle repulsion forces, such as an electrolyte. - Flocculation: Formation of aggregates of particles, the flocs or "flakes", induced by a bridging action of large molecules, such as modified starch or polymers. - Agglomeration: Formation of aggregates of particles, the agglomerates, induced by a bridging liquid such as oil. - Flotation: Froth flotation (or simply flotation) is a physico-chemical separation process that utilises the effect that different materials normally show also differences in surface properties (e.g. surface tension), for separating a mixture of materials into its fractions. This difference in surface properties yields in a different tendency of the particles to attach to air bubbles. The surface properties are often modified so as to achieve a desired behaviour of a material fraction. Froth flotation involves thus three phases (solids, water, gas) with complex interactions and subprocesses. In a typical flotation process water and chemicals are added to a mixture of solids to form a pulp. The pulp is then copiously aerated and the preferred (aerophilic) species clings to bubbles and floats as a mineralized froth, which is then skimmed off. - Collector: Substance for modifying the surface properties of a specified material (e.g the ore) so as to enhance floatability. It involves hydrophobising the particle surface by adsorption of molecules or ions. - Frother: Substance for stabilising the bubbles when reaching the surface of the pulp by forming a layer of reasonably stable froth, thus preventing remixing of the floated fraction. - Depressant: Substance for modifying the surface properties of a specified material (e.g the gangue) so as to reduce floatability. - Dispersant: Substance for preventing unwanted aggregation of the solid fractions, e.g. for enhancing the selectivity of the flotation process. - Coagulants and Flocculants: Substance for increasing the particle size such as to induce flocculation, coagulation or agglomeration, e.g. for flotation processes for water purification or for fine particle processing. - Modifying reagents: Substances (other than depressants, dispersants, coagulants and flocculants) for controlling the physico-chemical state of the flotation process e.g. the pH, conductivity etc. or altering the effect of the other chemicals i.e. the collector or the frother.
[B03D 1/001] Flotation agents -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Conditioners -> B03B1/00
[B03D 1/002] Inorganic compounds -- The subclass B03D 1/002 addresses the use of inorganic compounds as reagents in froth flotation, a method for separating solid particles by attaching them to bubbles in a liquid medium. It includes inorganic collectors, activators, depressants and other chemicals that modify mineral surfaces to aid their recovery.
[B03D 1/004] Organic compounds -- The B03D 1/004 subclass covers flotation methods that employ organic compounds as reagents, such as collectors, frothers or surfactants, to aid the separation of solid particles in liquids or on pneumatic tables or jigs. It focuses on the chemical action of these organic substances in stabilising bubbles and modifying particle surfaces to improve recovery.
[B03D 1/0043] modified so as to contain a polyether group -- This subclass (B03D 1/0043) covers flotation processes in which the reagents or collectors are chemically modified to contain a polyether group. The polyether moiety is used to enhance the dispersion, stability or selectivity of the flotation agent when separating solid particles from a liquid medium. It falls under performing operations - separation of solid materials using liquids or pneumatic tables or jigs - flotation.
[B03D 1/0046] containing silicon -- The subclass B03D 1/0046 covers flotation techniques in which the solid particles being separated contain silicon, such as silica, silicate minerals or silicon-bearing ores. It includes methods and apparatus for using aqueous or other liquid media to selectively float these silicon-containing solids from other materials. This falls under the broader category of separation of solids by liquid flotation processes.
[B03D 1/006] Hydrocarbons -- The CPC subclass B03D 1/006 covers flotation methods that employ hydrocarbons as reagents or modifiers to separate solid particles from liquids. It includes the use of petroleum-derived oils, synthetic oils or other hydrocarbon fluids to alter particle surface properties and enhance froth formation in mineral processing and related separations.
[B03D 1/008] containing oxygen -- B03D 1/008 covers flotation methods in which oxygen is present in the process medium, reagents, or added gases, such as aerated water, oxygen-enriched chemicals, or oxygen-containing foams used to improve separation of solid particles. The oxygen is intended to modify surface properties or bubble formation during the flotation step.
[B03D 1/01] containing nitrogen -- B03D 1/01 in the Cooperative Patent Classification (EPO/USPTO) pertains to flotation processes specifically for materials containing nitrogen. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with the separation of solid materials using liquids or pneumatic tables or jigs.
[B03D 1/011] Quaternary ammonium compounds -- This subclass covers the use of quaternary ammonium compounds as reagents in flotation processes that separate solid particles from liquids, such as in mineral processing. The compounds act as collectors or frothers, modifying surface properties to promote attachment to air bubbles and improve separation efficiency.
[B03D 1/012] containing sulfur -- B03D 1/012 covers flotation methods in which the froth or slurry contains sulfur, typically used to separate sulfur-bearing particles from other solids during mineral processing. The classification applies to processes where sulfur is present in the flotation medium or is a key component of the floated material.
[B03D 1/014] containing phosphorus -- B03D 1/014 classifies flotation processes that employ reagents containing phosphorus. It covers the separation of solid particles by suspending them in a liquid and introducing air bubbles, where phosphorus-based chemicals act as collectors, depressants or modifiers to improve selectivity. This subclass falls within the broader categories of performing operations, separation of solid materials using liquids and flotation.
[B03D 1/016] Macromolecular compounds -- B03D 1/016 addresses the use of macromolecular compounds, such as polymers or natural biopolymers, as reagents in flotation processes. These agents serve as collectors, frothers, depressants or modifiers that influence bubble stability and particle surface chemistry to improve separation of solid materials.
[B03D 1/018] Mixtures of inorganic and organic compounds -- B03D 1/018 concerns flotation processes in which the feed is a mixture of inorganic and organic compounds. The subclass covers techniques that use reagents or conditions to selectively make one component hydrophobic so it attaches to bubbles and can be separated from the other material.
[B03D 1/02] Froth-flotation processes -- B03D 1/02 covers froth-flotation processes, a method of separating solid particles by creating a frothy layer of air bubbles in a liquid where the desired particles attach to the bubbles and rise to the surface for removal. It is applied mainly in mineral processing and ore beneficiation to extract valuable minerals from gangue.
[B03D 1/021] for treatment of phosphate ores -- The CPC subclass B03D 1/021 covers flotation methods applied to phosphate ores, where the ore is mixed with water and reagents and air bubbles are introduced to preferentially attach phosphate particles for removal. It is used in mineral processing to separate valuable phosphate minerals from surrounding waste material.
[B03D 1/023] Carrier flotation; Flotation of a carrier material to which the target material attaches -- B03D 1/023 covers flotation processes in which a carrier material is first floated and then serves as a substrate onto which the desired particles attach, allowing their subsequent separation from the bulk. The method is used to improve recovery of target solids by exploiting the carrier's buoyancy and surface properties.
[B03D 1/025] adapted for the flotation of fines -- The B03D 1/025 subclass pertains to flotation processes that have been specially modified to handle very fine solid particles, using adjustments such as surface treatment, bubble-size control or altered agitation to improve the collection and separation efficiency of fines.
[B03D 1/026] Using an immiscible liquid in place of a gas for flotation -- The subclass covers flotation techniques in which an immiscible liquid, rather than a gas, is introduced to create buoyant media that attach to solid particles and lift them from the slurry for separation. It includes methods of generating, dispensing and maintaining the liquid phase to achieve mineral or material recovery without using air bubbles.
[B03D 1/028] Control and monitoring of flotation processes; computer models therefor -- The B03D 1/028 subclass covers technologies for controlling and monitoring flotation operations, including sensors, feedback systems, and automation schemes that regulate bubble, reagent and slurry conditions. It also encompasses computer models and simulation tools used to predict and optimise flotation performance.
[B03D 1/04] by varying ambient atmospheric pressure -- B03D 1/04 covers flotation processes that separate solid particles from a liquid by deliberately varying the ambient atmospheric pressure, such as applying reduced or increased pressure to influence bubble formation and particle attachment, thereby enhancing the efficiency of the separation.
[B03D 1/06] differential -- Differential flotation covers processes that separate solid particles by exploiting differences in their surface properties, usually by making selected particles hydrophobic so they attach to rising bubbles while others remain in the slurry. The method involves adding reagents and controlling conditions so that multiple mineral phases can be floated sequentially or simultaneously to achieve selective recovery.
[B03D 1/08] Subsequent treatment of concentrated product -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Froth dispersion -> B01D19/02
[B03D 1/082] of the froth product, e.g. washing -- This subclass covers actions performed on the froth produced in flotation, such as washing or cleaning the froth to remove contaminants or recover valuable material. It is part of the broader group dealing with separation of solids by liquids, specifically flotation.
[B03D 1/085] of the feed, e.g. conditioning, de-sliming -- The B03D 1/085 subclass covers preparatory treatments applied to the feed before flotation, such as conditioning and de-sliming, which modify particle size, surface chemistry or moisture to improve the efficiency of solid-liquid separation by flotation.
[B03D 1/087] of the sediment, e.g. regrinding -- The subclass B03D 1/087 deals with processing of the sediment produced in a flotation operation, such as regrinding the recovered solids to improve subsequent separation or product quality. It falls under the broader category of separating solid materials using liquids and specifically addresses operations performed on the sediment after flotation.
[B03D 1/10] Removing adhering liquid from separated materials -- B03D 1/10 covers techniques for eliminating liquid that remains attached to solids after they have been separated by flotation or similar processes. The methods include draining, squeezing, centrifuging, applying air or heat, and other actions that release the adhering fluid and improve the dryness of the material.
[B03D 1/12] Agent recovery -- B03D 1/12 covers techniques for recovering the chemical agents used in flotation processes, such as reagents, surfactants or collectors, after they have performed their function. It includes methods to separate, concentrate or recycle these agents from the slurry or process water for reuse.
[B03D 1/14] Flotation machines -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Devices for feeding measured quantities of reagents -> B01J4/02 - Flotation apparatus for enzymology or microbiology -> C12M1/09
[B03D 1/1406] with special arrangement of a plurality of flotation cells, e.g. positioning a flotation cell inside another -- This subclass covers flotation devices that use a special arrangement of multiple cells, such as nesting one flotation cell inside another, to improve the separation of solid particles from a liquid. It is indexed as CPC B03D 1/1406 within the broader group for separation of solids by flotation.
[B03D 1/1412] with baffles, e.g. at the wall for redirecting settling solids -- This subclass concerns flotation operations where the separation vessel is fitted with baffles, often wall-mounted, to channel liquid flow and redirect settling solid particles, thereby enhancing the efficiency of solid-liquid separation.
[B03D 1/1418] using centrifugal forces -- A method of flotation in which centrifugal force is applied to a liquid-solid mixture to accelerate the separation of solid particles. The rotation creates higher apparent gravity, improving the rise of froth-bound particles and the settlement of rejected material.
[B03D 1/1425] air-sparged hydrocyclones -- Air-sparged hydrocyclones are separation devices that inject air into a hydrocyclone to generate fine bubbles, which attach to solid particles in a slurry and lift them for removal. The added aeration improves flotation efficiency and allows finer particles to be recovered compared with a conventional hydrocyclone. This technology falls under the CPC subclass for flotation methods within solid-liquid separation.
[B03D 1/1431] Dissolved air flotation machines -- B03D 1/1431 covers machines that perform dissolved-air flotation, a solid-liquid separation technique in which air-saturated water is injected into a tank to generate fine bubbles that attach to suspended particles, causing them to float to the surface for removal. These devices are used in water treatment, mining and industrial effluent processing.
[B03D 1/1437] using electroflotation  waste water treatment using electroflotation  C02F1/465 -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Waste water treatment using electroflotation -> C02F1/465
[B03D 1/1443] Feed or discharge mechanisms for flotation tanks -- The B03D 1/1443 subclass concerns devices that introduce material into or remove material from flotation tanks used in the separation of solid particles by bubbles in liquids. It covers feed hoppers, screw conveyors, rotary valves and discharge elevators designed to handle slurry or ore feed and to transport floated material out of the tank.
