[B05D 2701/30] withstanding bending -- The B05D 2701/30 subclass relates to spraying or atomising processes that apply fluent materials capable of maintaining their integrity when the coated substrate is bent, deformed or subjected to welding, preventing cracking, flaking or delamination. It covers both the coating compositions and the application methods designed for such shape-changing or welded surfaces.
[B05D 2701/40] withstanding welding -- The subclass B05D 2701/40 addresses processes for applying fluent coatings that remain intact when the substrate is deformed or welded, ensuring the coating can withstand shape changes or the heat of welding. It falls under spraying or atomising operations for general coating applications.
[B05D 3/00] Pretreatment of surfaces to which liquids or other fluent materials are to be applied -- B05D 3/00 covers methods and apparatus for preparing surfaces before they receive liquids, powders or other fluent substances. This includes cleaning, roughening, priming or otherwise treating a substrate to improve adhesion, uniformity or performance of the subsequent coating or spray application.
[B05D 3/002] Pretreatement -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pretreatments when not covered by the other subgroups of B05D3/00
[B05D 3/005] Pretreatment for allowing a non-conductive substrate to be electrostatically coated -- B05D 3/005 covers methods of preparing non-conductive substrates so they can receive an electrostatic coating. It includes surface pretreatments such as applying conductive primers, corona or plasma treatment, or other techniques that render the material electrically conductive or improve charge acceptance before spraying.
[B05D 3/007] After-treatment -- This subclass covers post-application operations performed on a surface after a fluent material has been applied, such as drying, curing, cooling, cleaning or other treatments that modify the coating or substrate. It falls under B05D 3/007, dealing with after-treatment steps following pretreatment and spraying or atomising.
[B05D 3/02] by baking -- B05D 3/02 covers the pretreatment of a surface by baking it before any spraying, atomising or other application of liquids or fluent materials. The method uses heat to cure, dry or otherwise modify the substrate, improving adhesion or quality of the subsequently applied coating. It is a sub-category of processes for applying fluent materials to surfaces.
[B05D 3/0209] Multistage baking -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes wherein several baking/curing steps are used even when they are radiation post treatments ( B05D3/06 ).
[B05D 3/0218] Pretreatment, e.g. heating the substrate -- The B05D 3/0218 entry covers pretreatment steps applied to a surface before liquids or other fluent materials are sprayed or atomised onto it, such as heating, cleaning or conditioning the substrate. These preparatory actions aim to improve adhesion, wetting or coating quality and are part of the broader category of processes for applying fluent materials to surfaces.
[B05D 3/0227] with IR heaters -- The B05D 3/0227 subclass covers pretreatment steps for surfaces before liquids or other fluent materials are applied, where the pretreatment is carried out using infrared (IR) heating. It falls under spraying or atomising processes for applying fluent materials to surfaces.
[B05D 3/0236] with ovens  B05D3/0227  takes precedence -- B05D 3/0236 pertains to the pretreatment of surfaces for applying fluent materials, specifically involving processes that use ovens. This classification is part of a broader hierarchy dealing with spraying and atomising techniques and takes precedence over B05D 3/0227 when applicable.
[B05D 3/0245] with induction heating -- The code B05D 3/0245 covers pretreatment of a surface before applying liquids or other fluent materials, where the pretreatment is carried out using induction heating. This sub-class falls under processes for applying fluent materials to surfaces, specifically under spraying or atomising operations. It relates to preparing the substrate by heating it inductively to improve adhesion or other surface properties.
[B05D 3/0254] After-treatment -- The subclass B05D 3/0254 covers operations performed after a surface has been pre-treated, such as drying, curing, cleaning, or other modifications that prepare the surface for the subsequent application of liquids or other fluent materials. It falls under spraying or atomising processes for applying fluent substances.
[B05D 3/0263] with IR heaters -- B05D 3/0263 refers to pretreating a surface before applying liquids or other fluent materials, where the pretreatment is performed with infrared heaters. It falls under the broader category of spraying or atomising processes for applying fluent substances, specifically covering heating methods that prepare the substrate for better adhesion or spreading.
[B05D 3/0272] with ovens -- B05D 3/0272 covers pretreatment of surfaces before liquids or other fluent materials are applied, using ovens to heat, dry or otherwise condition the substrate. It falls under spraying or atomising processes for applying fluent materials to surfaces.
[B05D 3/0281] with induction heating -- The subclass B05D 3/0281 covers pretreatment of substrates in which induction heating is used to raise the temperature of the surface before applying liquids, powders or other fluent materials. The electromagnetic heating prepares the material for better adhesion, cleaning or curing prior to spraying or atomising.
[B05D 3/029] with microwaves -- This subclass covers methods where microwave energy is used to pre-heat, dry, or otherwise condition a surface before a liquid or other fluent material is sprayed or applied. It falls under processes for applying fluent materials, specifically pretreatment of surfaces, within the broader category of spraying or atomising.
[B05D 3/04] by exposure to gases -- The subgroup B05D 3/04 covers pretreatment of a surface before applying liquids or other fluids by exposing the surface to gases, such as plasma, ozone, or reactive vapours, to clean, activate, or otherwise modify its properties for improved adhesion or coating performance. It falls under processes for applying fluent materials to surfaces, specifically the pretreatment stage within spraying or atomising operations.
[B05D 3/0406] the gas being air -- B05D 3/0406 covers pretreatment processes in which air is used as the carrier gas for spraying or atomising fluent materials onto a surface before further treatment. It applies to methods that employ air to deliver cleaning, coating, or other preparatory liquids or powders to improve adhesion or surface properties.
[B05D 3/0413] Heating with air -- B05D 3/0413 relates to pretreatment processes in which air is heated and directed onto a surface to dry, cure or otherwise prepare it before the application of liquids or other fluent materials. The classification covers techniques such as heated air jets, circulating hot air and similar methods of using warm airflow for surface conditioning.
[B05D 3/042] Directing or stopping the fluid to be coated with air -- B05D 3/042 covers techniques that use a stream of air to control the movement of a coating fluid, either directing it onto a surface or stopping its flow during pretreatment operations. The methods are applied when preparing surfaces for subsequent liquid or other fluent material application.
[B05D 3/0426] Cooling with air -- B05D 3/0426 relates to pretreatment steps where a surface is cooled with air before a spray or other fluent material is applied. The cooling reduces temperature, helps control viscosity or drying, and improves coating quality. It is part of the CPC class for spraying and atomising processes.
[B05D 3/0433] the gas being a reactive gas -- B05D 3/0433 pertains to surface-pretreatment processes that use a reactive gas as the carrier or assisting gas during spraying or atomising of fluent materials. The reactive gas participates chemically with the surface or the deposited material to modify properties before further coating.
[B05D 3/044] Pretreatment -- B05D 3/044 covers processes for preparing a surface before liquids or other fluent materials are applied, such as cleaning, roughening, chemical or mechanical conditioning, or applying a primer to improve adhesion of subsequent sprays or coatings.
[B05D 3/0446] of a polymeric substrate -- B05D 3/0446 covers methods for pretreating polymeric substrates before applying liquids or other fluent materials, such as cleaning, surface activation, etching or chemical modification to improve adhesion or wettability. It is a subclass within spraying or atomising processes for surface preparation.
[B05D 3/0453] After-treatment -- B05D 3/0453 covers post-treatment steps applied to a surface after it has received a fluent coating or spray, such as drying, curing, sealing, or other modifications that enhance the final properties. It lies within the hierarchy of spraying/atomising operations, specifically under pretreatment of surfaces intended for liquid or fluent material application.
[B05D 3/046] Curing or evaporating the solvent -- B05D 3/046 concerns methods for curing or evaporating the solvent after a fluent material has been applied to a surface, typically by heating, radiation, or chemical action that removes or hardens the solvent to solidify the coating and prepare the surface for further treatment.
[B05D 3/0466] the gas being a non-reacting gas  B05D3/0406  takes precedence -- B05D 3/0466 pertains to processes where surfaces are pretreated with a non-reacting gas before applying fluent materials, ensuring that this specific treatment is prioritized over other methods. This classification is part of the broader category dealing with the application of liquids or fluent materials to surfaces.
[B05D 3/0473] for heating, e.g. vapour heating -- This subclass covers surface-pretreatment methods in which the substrate is heated, for example by vapor, before applying liquids or other fluent materials during spraying or atomising operations.
[B05D 3/048] for cooling -- The subclass B05D 3/048 covers methods where the surface to be coated is cooled prior to the application of liquid or other fluent material, typically as a pretreatment step in spraying or atomising processes.
[B05D 3/0486] Operating the coating or treatment in a controlled atmosphere -- This subclass covers methods of carrying out coating or surface-treatment operations inside a controlled atmosphere, such as a vacuum, inert gas, or filtered enclosure, to prevent oxidation, contamination, or moisture interference. It applies to pretreatments and applications of fluent materials where the surrounding gas composition or pressure is deliberately regulated.
[B05D 3/0493] using vacuum -- B05D 3/0493 covers the use of a vacuum to pretreat a substrate before applying liquids or other fluent materials, such as in spray or atomising processes. The vacuum removes contaminants, gases or moisture, improving adhesion and coating quality.
[B05D 3/06] by exposure to radiation -- This code covers pretreatment methods that modify a surface by exposing it to radiation such as ultraviolet, electron-beam, laser or plasma before a liquid or other fluent material is applied, improving adhesion or cleaning the surface.
[B05D 3/061] using U.V. -- B05D 3/061 covers methods for pretreating surfaces before applying liquids or other fluent materials, where the pretreatment is carried out using ultraviolet radiation to alter surface properties such as adhesion, cleanliness or reactivity.
[B05D 3/062] Pretreatment -- The B05D 3/062 subclass concerns methods for pretreating surfaces before liquids, powders or other fluent materials are applied, such as cleaning, etching, priming or roughening to improve adhesion, wettability or coating performance. It falls under spraying or atomising in general, specifically processes for applying fluent materials to surfaces.
[B05D 3/063] of polymeric substrates  B05D3/064  takes precedence -- B05D 3/063 pertains to the pretreatment of polymeric substrates before applying liquids or other fluent materials, with specific processes outlined under this code. However, for more specialized treatments, B05D 3/064 should be consulted as it takes precedence.
[B05D 3/064] involving also the use of a gas -- This subgroup covers methods for preparing a surface before applying liquids or other fluent media, where a gas is employed as part of the pretreatment such as gas cleaning, drying or activation of the surface. It is nested under B05D 3/06 for pretreatment of surfaces within the broader class of spraying or atomising in general.
[B05D 3/065] After-treatment -- B05D 3/065 covers after-treatment operations applied to surfaces that have been pre-treated before the application of liquids or other fluent materials. Such processes may include drying, heating, curing, cleaning, or surface modification performed after the initial pretreatment and before or during spraying or atomising. The code falls under the CPC hierarchy for performing operations, spraying or atomising in general, and processes for applying fluent materials to surfaces.
[B05D 3/066] involving also the use of a gas -- B05D 3/066 covers pretreatment steps for surfaces before applying liquids or other fluent materials when a gas is also used, such as gas-assisted cleaning, drying, activation or other conditioning of the substrate prior to coating.
[B05D 3/067] Curing or cross-linking the coating -- **Definition:** This place covers:  UV after-treatment of applied coatings being the curing or cross-linking of the coatings
[B05D 3/068] using ionising radiations (gamma, X, electrons) -- The subclass B05D 3/068 addresses pretreatment of surfaces using ionising radiation such as gamma rays, X-rays or electron beams before the application of liquids or other fluent materials in spraying or atomising processes.
[B05D 3/08] by flames -- B05D 3/08 concerns the pretreatment of surfaces by flames before liquids or other fluent materials are applied, falling within the broader class of spraying or atomising processes for surface treatment.
[B05D 3/10] by other chemical means -- A pretreatment method that chemically prepares a surface before liquids, pastes or powders are applied, using chemical agents or reactions not otherwise specified in the classification. It covers processes such as applying corrosion inhibitors, surfactant-based cleaners, or other specialized chemicals to modify surface properties prior to spraying, coating or other atomising operations.
[B05D 3/101] Pretreatment of polymeric substrate -- B05D 3/101 relates to methods for pretreating polymeric substrates before liquids or other fluent materials are applied, such as cleaning, roughening, or chemically modifying the surface to improve adhesion or coating performance. It is part of the broader category of processes for applying fluent materials, especially spraying or atomising.
[B05D 3/102] Pretreatment of metallic substrates  C23C  takes precedence -- B05D 3/102 deals with processes that condition metal surfaces before they are coated with liquids, powders or other fluent materials; it includes cleaning, etching, roughening or applying primer layers to improve adhesion. When the treatment is chemical in nature, the C23C group takes precedence.
[B05D 3/104] Pretreatment of other substrates -- B05D 3/104 relates to methods for preparing surfaces before applying liquids or other fluent materials by spraying or atomising, covering pretreatment steps such as cleaning, roughening, priming or otherwise conditioning substrates that are not otherwise specified. This subclass is part of the broader group for processes applying fluent materials to surfaces.
[B05D 3/105] Intermediate treatments -- The B05D 3/105 class covers intermediate surface treatments performed after a pretreatment step and before the final application of liquids or fluent materials. These operations include processes such as drying, heating, roughening, primer or adhesive application that modify the substrate to improve adhesion or performance of the subsequent coating.
[B05D 3/107] Post-treatment of applied coatings -- B05D 3/107 covers operations carried out on a coating after it has been applied to a substrate, including curing, drying, heating, annealing, or other chemical or physical treatments that modify the coating's properties or performance. This subclass falls under processes for applying fluent materials to surfaces, specifically the pretreatment of surfaces before liquid or other fluent applications.
[B05D 3/108] Curing -- The B05D 3/108 subclass covers curing operations performed on a surface that has been pre-treated before or during the application of liquids, gases or powders. It includes methods that cause polymerisation, drying, hardening or other solidification of the applied material, such as heat, radiation or chemical agents, and is situated within the broader category of spraying or atomising processes for applying fluent substances to surfaces.
[B05D 3/12] by mechanical means -- This subclass covers mechanical pretreatment of surfaces before liquids or other fluent materials are applied, including actions such as abrasion, grinding, impact or other physical means used to clean, roughen or otherwise condition a surface to improve subsequent coating or spraying.
[B05D 3/14] by electrical means -- The B05D 3/14 subclass covers pretreatment of surfaces using electrical techniques before liquids or other fluent materials are applied, such as the use of electric fields, plasma, corona discharge or other electrically generated processes to modify the substrate. This falls within the broader category of spraying or atomising operations for applying fluent materials to surfaces.
[B05D 3/141] Plasma treatment -- Plasma treatment refers to the use of ionised gases to clean, activate or modify surfaces before applying liquids, powders or other fluent materials, improving adhesion, wetting or coating performance. It is a pretreatment step within processes for applying fluent materials to surfaces.
[B05D 3/142] Pretreatment -- B05D 3/142 covers methods for conditioning or preparing surfaces before liquids, powders, or other fluent materials are applied, including cleaning, roughening, priming, or other treatments that improve adhesion, uniformity, or performance of the subsequent coating or spray process.
[B05D 3/144] of polymeric substrates -- B05D 3/144 pertains to the pretreatment of polymeric substrates before applying liquids or other fluent materials. This process is crucial for enhancing adhesion and ensuring a uniform coating.
[B05D 3/145] After-treatment -- The code B05D 3/145 covers operations performed on a surface after a fluent material has been applied, such as drying, curing, heating, cooling, or other post-application treatments that modify or finalize the coating. It is a sub-category of processes for applying fluent materials to surfaces, following pretreatment steps.
[B05D 3/147] Curing -- B05D 3/147 - Curing - covers methods for hardening or setting a surface pretreatment layer before fluids are applied, typically as part of spraying or atomising operations. It includes processes that use heat, radiation, chemical agents or other means to cure the treated surface, preparing it for subsequent coating or bonding.
[B05D 3/148] affecting the surface properties of the coating -- B05D 3/148 pertains to methods that alter the surface properties of a coating as part of the pretreatment process before applying fluent materials. This includes techniques aimed at enhancing adhesion, modifying texture, or improving the functional characteristics of the coated surface.
[B05D 3/20] by magnetic fields -- This subgroup covers pretreatment of surfaces prior to spraying, atomising or other fluent-material application where magnetic fields are employed to modify the substrate, influencing adhesion, wetting or coating behavior. It falls under processes for applying fluent materials to surfaces in general.
[B05D 3/203] pre-treatment by magnetic fields -- B05D 3/203 covers methods that treat a surface with magnetic fields before liquids or other fluent substances are applied, typically as a preparatory step in spraying or atomising operations. The magnetic pretreatment aims to modify surface properties such as adhesion, wettability or particle alignment to improve coating quality.
[B05D 3/207] post-treatment by magnetic fields -- B05D 3/207 concerns the use of magnetic fields as a post-treatment step on surfaces that have been pretreated before the application of liquids or other fluent coatings. The magnetic exposure may be employed to alter surface characteristics, improve adhesion or affect the behavior of the subsequently applied material.
[B05D 5/00] Processes for applying liquids or other fluent materials to surfaces to obtain special surface effects, finishes or structures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for applying liquid or other fluent materials to surfaces in order to have surface effects, finishes or structures, e.g. decorative effects.  The subgroups characterise a specific surface effect or structure, e.g. B05D5/06 concerns multicolour or optical effects
[B05D 5/005] Repairing damaged coatings -- The subclass B05D 5/005 covers methods for restoring or repairing damaged surface coatings, including removal of defects and re-application of coating material to recover the original appearance, protection or functional properties. Techniques may involve cleaning, patching, repolishing or recoating by spraying, brushing, rolling or other means to rebuild the coating layer.
[B05D 5/02] to obtain a matt or rough surface -- The code B05D 5/02 covers processes in which liquids or other fluent substances are applied to a substrate in order to produce a matte or rough finish. It includes spraying, atomising, coating or similar techniques aimed at modifying surface texture rather than chemical composition. This classification is used for methods that create non-glossy, structured surfaces through material deposition.
[B05D 5/04] to obtain a surface receptive to ink or other liquid -- The B05D 5/04 subclass covers processes that treat a surface so it can receive ink or other liquids, such as priming, sizing, or other conditioning steps that increase wettability or adhesion before printing, coating or similar applications.
[B05D 5/06] to obtain multicolour or other optical effects -- This subclass of B05D covers processes that apply liquids or other fluent materials to a surface in order to create multicolour or other optical effects such as iridescence, diffraction or luminescence, forming special surface finishes or structures. It falls under the broader category of spraying or atomising methods for surface treatment.
[B05D 5/061] Special surface effect -- B05D 5/061 covers processes that apply liquids or other fluent materials to a substrate in order to create special surface effects such as patterns, textures, decorative finishes or functional structures. These methods may involve spraying, atomising or other techniques to modify the surface appearance or properties beyond a simple coating.
[B05D 5/062] Wrinkled, cracked or ancient-looking effect -- The CPC subclass B05D 5/062 covers techniques for applying liquids or other fluent materials to a surface to produce a wrinkled, cracked or antiqued appearance, often by controlled drying, curing or mechanical deformation. It is used for processes that create texture or surface effects that mimic age or damage.
[B05D 5/063] Reflective effect  B05D5/067  takes precedence -- The CPC code B05D 5/063 classifies processes that apply liquids or other fluent materials to a surface in order to produce a reflective effect, such as mirror-like or specular finishes. It is a sub-group of spraying/atomising operations, but the more specific subclass B05D 5/067 takes precedence over it.
[B05D 5/065] having colour interferences or colour shifts or opalescent looking, flip-flop, two tones -- B05D 5/065 covers spray- or atomising-based processes that apply liquids or other fluent materials to a surface in order to create visual effects such as colour interferences, colour shifts, opalescence, flip-flop or two-tone appearances, where the resulting finish exhibits varying hues depending on viewing angle or lighting.
[B05D 5/066] achieved by multilayers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for applying liquids or other fluents materials to surfaces to obtain colour interferences or colour shifts or opalescent looking by multilayers
[B05D 5/067] Metallic effect -- The B05D 5/067 class covers processes that apply liquid or other fluent substances to a substrate in order to create a metallic effect on the surface. It falls under spraying or atomising operations for producing special finishes, such as a shiny or reflective metal-like appearance.
[B05D 5/068] achieved by multilayers  B05D5/066  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for applying liquids or other fluents materials to surfaces to obtain metallic effect by multilayers
[B05D 5/08] to obtain an anti-friction or anti-adhesive surface -- B05D 5/08 covers methods of applying liquids or other fluent substances to a substrate in order to create a surface that reduces friction or prevents adhesion. The techniques include spraying, atomising or other coating processes that produce anti-friction or anti-adhesive layers for mechanical or functional purposes.
[B05D 5/083] involving the use of fluoropolymers -- This subclass covers processes that apply liquids or other fluent materials to a substrate to create special surface characteristics, where the coating or treatment incorporates fluoropolymers. It includes spraying, atomising, or other application methods that use fluoropolymer-containing fluids to achieve desired finishes or structures.
[B05D 5/086] having an anchoring layer -- B05D 5/86 covers coating methods that first apply an anchoring layer to a substrate so that later liquid or fluent coatings adhere more securely, often creating a graded or bonding interface that enhances durability, corrosion resistance or functional performance of the final surface treatment.
[B05D 5/10] to obtain an adhesive surface -- B05D 5/10 falls under the CPC group for spraying or atomising in general, specifically processes for applying liquids or other fluent materials to surfaces to obtain an adhesive surface that can be bonded or attached to other objects. This subclass deals with techniques that create stick-on coatings for subsequent joining operations.
[B05D 5/12] to obtain a coating with specific electrical properties -- B05D 5/12 classifies processes that apply liquids or other fluent materials to a surface in order to produce a coating with specific electrical characteristics, such as conductivity, insulation or dielectric behavior. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, spraying or atomising in general, and processes for applying fluent materials to surfaces.
[B05D 7/00] Processes, other than flocking, specially adapted for applying liquids or other fluent materials to particular surfaces or for applying particular liquids or other fluent materials -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Coating of foodstuff -> A23P20/17 , A23P20/15 , A23P20/18
[B05D 7/02] to macromolecular substances, e.g. rubber -- B05D 7/02 covers processes for applying macromolecular substances such as rubber to surfaces. It falls within the spraying or atomising category and relates to methods specially adapted for depositing liquid or other fluent materials onto particular substrates.
[B05D 7/04] to surfaces of films or sheets -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Production of layered product by applying coatings of pasty or pulverulent plastics if the layers are not coated on a definitive substrate (e.g. if the substrate is used as moulding surface and separated afterwards or if there is no substrate) -> B29C41/00
[B05D 7/06] to wood -- B05D 7/06 covers processes for applying liquids, pastes, powders or other fluent materials specifically to wood surfaces, such as spraying, brushing, dipping or rolling of paints, stains, preservatives, adhesives or fire-retardant treatments. The subclass excludes flocking and deals with any method adapted to treat wood with such materials.
[B05D 7/08] using synthetic lacquers or varnishes -- B05D 7/08 covers processes that apply synthetic lacquers or varnishes by spraying or atomising onto a surface. It includes the preparation, delivery and deposition steps specifically adapted for liquid synthetic coatings rather than flocking or other material types.
[B05D 7/10] based on cellulose derivatives -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes other than flocking, specially adapted for applying synthetic lacquers or varnishes based on cellulose derivatives to wood
[B05D 7/12] to leather -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Dyeing of leather -> D06P
[B05D 7/14] to metal, e.g. car bodies -- The B05D 7/14 subclass covers processes for applying liquids, powders or other fluent substances by spraying or atomising onto metal surfaces, such as automobile bodies. It includes methods specially adapted to treat metal, for coating, painting, cleaning or surface modification, falling under the broader category of processes for applying fluent materials to surfaces.
[B05D 7/142] Auto-deposited coatings, i.e. autophoretic coatings -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Autophoretic compositions. -> C09D5/08
[B05D 7/144] After-treatment of auto-deposited coatings -- B05D 7/144 covers methods applied to coatings that have been automatically deposited by spraying or atomising, focusing on post-application treatments such as curing, drying, heat-treating, cleaning, polishing or surface modification to improve performance or appearance.
[B05D 7/146] to metallic pipes or tubes  processes for coating the interior of pipes  B05D7/222 -- This subclass pertains to methods of applying liquids, powders or other fluent substances to the interior surfaces of metallic pipes or tubes. It includes techniques for coating the inside of such vessels for purposes such as protection, lining or functional modification. The processes are distinguished from flocking and relate to interior pipe-coating operations.
[B05D 7/148] using epoxy-polyolefin systems in mono- or multilayers -- The B05D 7/148 subclass covers processes that apply epoxy-polyolefin systems as a single coat or in multiple layers to surfaces, using spraying or atomising techniques. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, spraying or atomising in general, and processes for applying fluent materials to surfaces, excluding flocking. This subclass focuses on the use of epoxy-polyolefin mixtures for coating applications.
[B05D 7/16] using synthetic lacquers or varnishes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes other than flocking, specially adapted for applying synthethic lacquers or varnishes to metal
[B05D 7/18] based on cellulose derivatives -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes other than flocking, specially adapted for applying synthetic lacquers or varnishes based on cellulose derivatives to metal
[B05D 7/20] to wires -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Apparatus or processes specially adapted for insulating conductors or cables -> H01B13/06
[B05D 7/22] to internal surfaces, e.g. of tubes -- B05D 7/22 covers processes for applying fluent materials to internal surfaces, such as the inside of tubes or other hollow members. It deals with methods that spray, atomise, coat or otherwise deposit liquids, pastes, powders or similar substances onto the interior walls of these structures, distinct from flocking or external-surface applications.
[B05D 7/222] of pipes -- B05D 7/222 pertains to processes for applying liquids or other fluent materials specifically to the surfaces of pipes, excluding flocking techniques. This classification is part of a detailed hierarchy focusing on spraying and atomising methods adapted for specific applications.
[B05D 7/225] Coating inside the pipe -- This subclass covers methods of applying liquids or other fluent materials to the interior surfaces of pipes, typically by spraying, atomising or other pipe-coating techniques. It includes processes that deposit protective, functional or decorative layers inside tubular conduits.
[B05D 7/227] of containers, cans or the like -- This subclass covers spraying or atomising techniques specially adapted for applying liquids or other fluent materials to the surfaces of containers such as cans, bottles or similar vessels, whether during manufacture or as a subsequent coating operation.
[B05D 7/24] for applying particular liquids or other fluent materials -- B05D 7/24 covers processes, other than flocking, that are specially adapted for applying particular liquids or other fluent materials to surfaces. It includes methods where the material is sprayed, atomised or otherwise deposited in a controlled manner onto designated substrates.
[B05D 7/26] synthetic lacquers or varnishes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for applying synthethic lacquers or varnishes  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Processes other than flocking, specially adapted for applying synthethic lacquers or varnishes to wood -> B05D7/08 - Processes other than flocking, specially adapted for applying synthethic lacquers or varnishes to metal -> B05D7/16
[B05D 7/50] Multilayers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Multilayers are made from several coatings made from fluid materials or powders on a substrate.
[B05D 7/51] One specific pretreatment, e.g. phosphatation, chromatation, in combination with one specific coating  pretreatment of metallic substrates  C23C ; pretreatment before coating in general  B05D3/00 -- B05D 7/51 covers processes for applying fluent materials to surfaces that involve a single pretreatment-such as phosphating or chromating-combined with a single coating pretreatment of metallic substrates. It belongs to the spraying and atomising group and specifies methods other than flocking for preparing metal surfaces before coating.
[B05D 7/52] Two layers -- The B05D 7/52 class covers processes that deposit a first fluent coating on a surface and then apply a second distinct layer of another fluent material, typically in a sequential or simultaneous manner, to create a multi-layered coating with specific functional or protective properties. It is used for non-flocking applications where two separate layers are applied.
[B05D 7/53] Base coat plus clear coat type -- The B05D 7/53 subclass concerns methods that first deposit a base coating on a surface and then apply a clear protective or decorative layer, typically by spraying or other atomising techniques, to create a combined finish with specific performance or aesthetic properties.
[B05D 7/532] the two layers being cured or baked together, i.e. wet on wet -- B05D 7/532 covers processes in which two freshly applied layers of liquid or other fluent material are cured or baked together without drying the first layer first, a "wet-on-wet" technique for bonding or curing successive coatings during spraying or atomising operations.
[B05D 7/5323] the two layers being applied simultaneously -- B05D 7/5323 covers processes in which two different liquid or fluent layers are deposited on a substrate at the same time, typically by coordinated spraying or atomising devices that deliver both materials concurrently. The method enables simultaneous formation of a composite coating without separate application steps.
[B05D 7/534] the first layer being let to dry at least partially before applying the second layer  B05D7/538  takes precedence -- B05D 7/534 covers processes for applying fluent materials in which the first applied layer is allowed to dry at least partially before a second layer is applied, typically using spraying or atomising techniques. It falls under the hierarchy of Performing Operations → Spraying or Atomising in General → Processes for Applying Fluent Materials to Surfaces, in General. This entry is superseded by the more specific B05D 7/538.
[B05D 7/536] each layer being cured, at least partially, separately -- This subclass covers methods in which a coating is built up by applying successive layers of liquid or other fluent material, each layer being at least partially cured before the next is applied, typically in spraying or atomising operations. It applies to processes other than flocking that are specially adapted for particular surfaces.
[B05D 7/538] No curing step for the last layer -- B05D 7/538 covers processes for applying liquids or other fluent materials to a surface where the final layer is deposited without a subsequent curing step. It falls under spraying or atomising in general and is used for applications that do not require polymerisation, drying or other post-application treatments of the last coat.
[B05D 7/5383] No curing step for any layer -- B05D 7/5383 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to processes where no curing step is required for any applied layer, specifically within the context of applying liquids or other fluent materials to surfaces. This classification excludes flocking and focuses on particular surface treatments or material applications that do not necessitate a curing process.
[B05D 7/5385] the two layers being applied simultaneously -- The B05D 7/5385 subclass covers methods in which two distinct layers of a liquid or other fluent material are deposited on a surface at the same time, typically by simultaneous spraying, atomising or similar application techniques. It pertains to processes that create multilayer coatings in a single pass rather than applying each layer sequentially.
[B05D 7/54] No clear coat specified -- B05D 7/54 covers processes for applying liquids or other fluent materials to surfaces by spraying or atomising where a clear protective coat is not required or specified. The subclass includes methods for depositing paints, inks, adhesives or other functional layers directly onto the target without a subsequent clear-coat step, and is situated under the broader group for spraying or atomising in general.
[B05D 7/542] the two layers being cured or baked together -- B05D 7/542 covers methods that apply two coating layers in a single operation and then cure or bake them together, such as a one-step application of a primer and topcoat that are hardened as a combined film. It belongs to the group of spraying or atomising processes for applying fluent materials, specifically where the layers are jointly cured.
[B05D 7/5423] the two layers being applied simultaneously -- This subclass covers methods where two distinct layers of fluid or other fluent material are deposited at the same time onto a surface, typically by coordinated spray or atomising devices, allowing simultaneous formation of a composite coating without a sequential step. It is used for applications where a base layer and a functional top layer are needed together.
[B05D 7/544] the first layer is let to dry at least partially before applying the second layer -- It covers methods of applying multiple coats of liquid or other fluent materials by spraying or atomising where the first layer is allowed to dry at least partially before the second layer is deposited, typically to improve adhesion or finish in processes other than flocking.
[B05D 7/546] each layer being cured, at least partially, separately -- The CPC subclass B05D 7/546 concerns methods of applying fluent materials in successive layers where each layer is at least partially cured before the next layer is deposited, as in spray-curing, UV-curing or other partial-cure processes for multi-layer coatings or finishes.
[B05D 7/548] No curing step for the last layer -- B05D 7/548 covers processes for applying liquids or other fluent materials to a surface where the final layer is deposited without any subsequent curing step. It falls under spraying or atomising operations and relates to methods that differ from flocking, focusing on the direct placement of the last coating layer.
[B05D 7/5483] No curing step for any layer -- This subclass includes spraying or atomising techniques in which a liquid or other fluent material is deposited on a surface but no curing step - thermal, UV, chemical or otherwise - is applied to any layer after deposition. The process may be used for coating, cleaning, lubrication or similar operations where the material remains uncured.
[B05D 7/5485] the two layers being applied simultaneously -- B05D 7/5485 covers methods in which two distinct liquid or fluent layers are deposited on a surface in a single operation, such as simultaneous spraying or atomising of both layers to form a composite coating. The technique creates multilayer structures without separate application steps.
[B05D 7/56] Three layers or more -- The B05D 7/56 subclass covers processes that apply fluent materials in three or more successive layers to a surface, such as multilayer coating or printing operations where each layer is deposited before the next. It includes methods specially adapted for building up thicker structures by successive applications of liquids, powders, or other fluid media. This subclass falls under spraying or atomising processes for applying materials to surfaces.
[B05D 7/57] the last layer being a clear coat -- B05D 7/57 covers processes in which the final applied layer is a clear coat, a transparent protective film deposited over previously applied pigmented layers. The classification includes spraying or other atomising methods used to form a clear, often glossy, finish that enhances durability, corrosion resistance, or aesthetic appearance without adding color.
[B05D 7/572] all layers being cured or baked together -- This subclass covers methods in which multiple applied layers, such as paints, coatings or adhesives, are cured or baked together in a single step rather than sequentially, allowing the entire stack to be treated as one unit during the heat or radiation curing process.
[B05D 7/5723] all layers being applied simultaneously -- This subclass covers processes where two or more coating layers are deposited in a single operation rather than sequentially. It includes techniques that simultaneously apply different material layers to a surface, such as co-spraying, dual-nozzle atomisation, or combined deposition of binders and pigments in one pass.
[B05D 7/574] at least some layers being let to dry at least partially before applying the next layer  B05D7/577  takes precedence -- B05D 7/574 in the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to processes where at least some layers are allowed to dry partially before the application of subsequent layers, specifically excluding flocking and giving precedence to B05D 7/577 for more specialized cases.
[B05D 7/576] each layer being cured, at least partially, separately -- This subclass deals with multilayer coating processes where each applied layer is cured at least partially before the next layer is deposited, typically used for paints, inks, adhesives and similar fluent materials. It falls under spraying or atomising operations for applying fluent materials and specifies sequential curing of each layer.
[B05D 7/577] some layers being coated "wet-on-wet", the others not -- This subclass covers methods where a fresh wet coating is deposited on top of a still-wet preceding layer while other areas are left dry or receive no further coating, enabling selective wet-on-wet application of liquids or other fluent materials to a substrate. It pertains to spraying or atomising techniques that apply multiple layers in a single operation under these conditions.
[B05D 7/578] No curing step for the last layer -- The code B05D 7/578 covers processes for applying liquids or other fluent materials to a surface in which the final coating layer is deposited without any subsequent curing step, such as drying, polymerisation or heat treatment. This sub-class falls under spraying or atomising operations that are specially adapted for particular liquids and surfaces, but expressly excludes methods that involve curing of the last applied layer.
[B05D 7/5783] No curing step for any layer -- The subclass B05D 7/5783 covers processes for applying liquids or other fluent materials to a surface in which none of the applied layers undergoes a curing step, such as polymerisation, drying or heat treatment, after deposition. It includes methods where the material remains uncured while still achieving the intended functional coating or treatment.
[B05D 7/5785] all layers being applied simultaneously -- B05D 7/5785 relates to methods that deposit several coating layers in a single step, for example by co-extruding or simultaneously spraying different fluids onto a substrate. The process applies multiple fluent materials at once rather than sequentially, aiming to create multilayer structures in a single operation.
[B05D 7/58] No clear coat specified -- B05D 7/58 covers processes, other than flocking, that are specially adapted for applying liquids or other fluent materials to particular surfaces without specifying a clear-coat layer. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, spraying or atomising in general, and processes for applying fluent materials to surfaces.
[B05D 7/582] all layers being cured or baked together -- A process in which several applied layers of liquid or fluent material are cured, baked or otherwise hardened simultaneously, rather than drying each layer before the next is deposited. It covers methods where the whole coating stack is treated in a single heat or curing step, often used in spray-coating or similar applications to speed production and achieve uniform properties throughout the multilayer structure.
[B05D 7/5823] all layers being applied simultaneously -- A process for applying fluent materials in which multiple coating layers are deposited at the same time rather than sequentially, such as co-extrusion, simultaneous spray or dip coating, and other techniques that create a multilayer film in a single operation. This approach is used to achieve integrated properties, reduce processing steps, and ensure uniform bonding between layers.
[B05D 7/584] at least some layers being let to dry, at least partially, before applying the next layer  B05D7/587  takes precedence -- This subclass deals with spray or atomising processes in which each coat is allowed to dry at least partially before the next layer is applied, covering multi-layer coating operations where intermediate drying is required. The more specific subclass B05D 7/587 supersedes it.
[B05D 7/586] each layer being cured, at least partially, separately -- This subclass concerns methods in which successive layers of a liquid or other fluent coating are applied and each layer is at least partially cured before the next layer is deposited. It is used for controlling curing in multi-layer spray or coating processes, such as paints, inks or polymer films.
[B05D 7/587] some layers being coated "wet-on-wet", the others not -- B05D 7/587 pertains to processes where some layers are applied wet-on-wet, while other layers are not, in the context of applying liquids or fluent materials to surfaces. This method is used for specific surface treatments or material applications.
[B05D 7/588] No curing step for the last layer -- B05D 7/588 covers processes for applying liquids or other fluent materials to a surface where the final deposited layer is left uncured, i.e. no subsequent curing step is performed. It falls under spray or atomising operations for applying fluent materials, specifically processes other than flocking that are adapted for particular liquids or surfaces.
[B05D 7/5883] No curing step for any layer -- B05D 7/5883 relates to spraying or atomising techniques for applying liquids or other fluent materials to a surface in which the applied layer is not subjected to any curing, drying or hardening step before subsequent processing or final use.
[B05D 7/5885] all layers being applied simultaneously -- B05D 7/5885 covers spraying or atomising techniques that deposit several coating layers in a single pass, rather than applying each layer sequentially. The entry relates to processes for applying liquids or other fluent materials to surfaces where all layers are applied simultaneously, excluding flocking methods.
[B06] GENERATING OR TRANSMITTING MECHANICAL VIBRATIONS IN GENERAL -- The B06 class covers devices and methods for creating or conveying mechanical vibrations, including vibrators, shakers, ultrasonic generators, and related apparatus that induce oscillatory motion in solids or fluids for purposes such as testing, processing, or signal transmission. It encompasses both the production of the vibration and the means of delivering it to a target medium.
[B06B] METHODS OR APPARATUS FOR GENERATING OR TRANSMITTING MECHANICAL VIBRATIONS OF INFRASONIC, SONIC, OR ULTRASONIC FREQUENCY, FOR PERFORMING MECHANICAL WORK IN GENERAL -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes or apparatus for generating mechanical vibrations of infrasonic, sonic, or ultrasonic frequency  Processes or apparatus specially adapted for transmitting mechanical vibrations of infrasonic, sonic, or ultrasonic frequency
[B06B 1/00] Methods or apparatus for generating mechanical vibrations of infrasonic, sonic, or ultrasonic frequency -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Generating mechanical vibrations making use of electrical energy.  Generating mechanical vibrations making use of mechanical energy.  Generating mechanical vibrations wherein the vibrator is actuated by pressure fluid.  Generating mechanical vibrations making use of a vibrating fluid.
[B06B 1/02] making use of electrical energy -- The B06B 1/02 class covers methods and devices that generate infrasonic, sonic, or ultrasonic mechanical vibrations by converting electrical energy into motion. It includes apparatuses that use electric power to drive vibrating elements for tasks such as cleaning, machining, mixing, or other general mechanical work, falling under the broader category of generating or transmitting mechanical vibrations.
[B06B 1/0207] Driving circuits  specially adapted for particular applications,  see  the relevant subclass, e.g.  G01 ; circuits for steering transducer arrays  G10K11/34 ; basic circuits  H03 -- Category B06B 1/0207 covers driving circuits that are customized for specific uses in devices that generate infrasonic, sonic, or ultrasonic mechanical vibrations. It includes circuits referenced in related subclasses such as G01, transducer-array steering circuits (G10K11/34), and basic circuit designs (H03).
[B06B 1/0215] for generating pulses, e.g. bursts of oscillations, envelopes -- The B06B 1/0215 subclass covers devices or techniques that produce mechanical vibrations in the form of pulses, such as bursts of oscillations or shaped envelopes, rather than continuous waves. These systems are used to control the timing and waveform of infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic energy for tasks like material processing, cleaning or medical treatment.
[B06B 1/0223] for generating signals continuous in time -- The CPC subclass B06B 1/0223 covers methods or devices that produce mechanical vibrations-infra-, sonic or ultrasonic-by generating signals that are continuous in time rather than pulsed or intermittent, enabling steady-state vibration for operations such as machining, cleaning or material processing.
[B06B 1/023] and stepped in amplitude, e.g. square wave, 2-level signal -- The subclass covers devices or methods that generate mechanical vibrations in the infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic range using waveforms whose amplitude changes in discrete steps, such as square-wave or two-level signals, to perform mechanical work.
[B06B 1/0238] of a single frequency, e.g. a sine-wave -- This subclass covers devices or processes that produce mechanical vibrations at one constant frequency, typically a sinusoidal waveform, across infrasonic, audible, or ultrasonic ranges. The focus is on generating a single-frequency vibration for general mechanical work.
[B06B 1/0246] with a feedback signal -- The B06B 1/0246 class concerns methods or apparatus that generate infrasonic, sonic, or ultrasonic mechanical vibrations while employing a feedback signal to monitor and adjust the vibration parameters, enabling closed-loop control of amplitude, frequency, or phase during operation.
[B06B 1/0253] taken directly from the generator circuit -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry B06B 1/0253 pertains to methods or apparatus for generating mechanical vibrations of infrasonic, sonic, or ultrasonic frequency, specifically focusing on components or aspects that are taken directly from the generator circuit.
[B06B 1/0261] taken from a transducer or electrode connected to the driving transducer -- This classification entry pertains to methods or apparatus for generating mechanical vibrations of infrasonic, sonic, or ultrasonic frequency, specifically focusing on configurations where feedback is taken from a transducer or electrode connected to the driving transducer.
[B06B 1/0269] for generating multiple frequencies -- This subclass covers methods or apparatus that produce mechanical vibrations at two or more distinct frequencies, whether infrasonic, audible or ultrasonic, for general mechanical work. It includes devices designed to switch, combine or simultaneously output multiple vibration frequencies to achieve varied or enhanced performance.
[B06B 1/0276] with simultaneous generation, e.g. with modulation, harmonics -- B06B 1/0276 covers devices or processes that produce mechanical vibrations in the infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic range while simultaneously generating additional vibration components such as harmonics or modulated signals. The classification is used for systems that combine a primary vibration source with secondary waveforms to achieve complex motion or enhanced performance in mechanical work.
[B06B 1/0284] with consecutive, i.e. sequential generation, e.g. with frequency sweep -- The subclass pertains to devices or methods that produce mechanical vibrations in the infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic range by generating them consecutively, for example through a frequency sweep or sequential bursts, to perform mechanical work.
[B06B 1/0292] Electrostatic transducers, e.g. electret-type -- Electrostatic transducers use static electric fields to turn electrical energy into mechanical vibrations, and electret-type devices incorporate a permanently polarized dielectric that emits acoustic waves when a voltage is applied, covering infrasonic to ultrasonic ranges for uses such as sound generation, sensing, or actuation.
[B06B 1/04] operating with electromagnetism -- **Definition:** This place covers:  EMATs
[B06B 1/045] using vibrating magnet, armature or coil system -- This subclass covers methods and devices that produce mechanical vibrations by employing a magnet, armature or coil arrangement, where electromagnetic forces cause the component to oscillate. The resulting infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic motion is used to perform mechanical work such as shaking, stirring or powering tools.
[B06B 1/06] operating with piezoelectric effect or with electrostriction -- The subclass covers methods and apparatus that generate mechanical vibrations by exploiting the piezoelectric effect or electrostriction, converting electrical energy into acoustic waves across infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic ranges for general mechanical work.
[B06B 1/0603] using a piezoelectric bender, e.g. bimorph -- The CPC entry B06B 1/0603 relates to devices or methods that generate mechanical vibrations of infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic frequency by employing a piezoelectric bender, such as a bimorph element. These vibrators are used to perform mechanical work through the controlled oscillatory motion of the piezoelectric actuator.
[B06B 1/0607] using multiple elements  B06B1/064  and  B06B1/0688  take precedence -- This subclass covers methods or devices that generate mechanical vibrations by employing multiple vibrating elements that operate together to produce infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic motion, taking precedence over the earlier subclasses B06B1/064 and B06B1/0688.
[B06B 1/0611] in a pile -- B06B 1/0611 covers methods and apparatus that generate infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic mechanical vibrations directly within a pile for purposes such as driving, compaction or soil improvement. The vibrations are produced by devices integral to the pile or inserted into it, enabling efficient transmission of energy into the ground.
[B06B 1/0614] for generating several frequencies -- B06B 1/0614 covers methods or devices that produce mechanical vibrations at two or more distinct frequencies, whether infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic, to perform work such as driving, cleaning or material processing. The classification is used for systems that can switch or combine frequencies to achieve desired effects.
[B06B 1/0618] of piezo- and non-piezoelectric elements, e.g. 'Tonpilz' -- This subclass deals with devices that generate or transmit mechanical vibrations using piezoelectric and non-piezoelectric elements, such as a tonpilz-type transducer. It covers methods and apparatus where these components produce infrasonic, sonic, or ultrasonic waves for performing mechanical work.
[B06B 1/0622] on one surface -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Matrices of rods; line arrays
[B06B 1/0625] Annular array -- The B06B 1/0625 subclass addresses annular-array arrangements of transducers that generate infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic mechanical vibrations for performing work. It includes designs in which the vibrating elements are positioned in one or more concentric rings to control and direct the vibration field, and is situated within the broader group on methods and apparatus for generating or transmitting mechanical vibrations.
[B06B 1/0629] Square array -- The B06B 1/0629 subclass pertains to square-array arrangements of transducers or other devices that generate mechanical vibrations in the infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic range, used to produce a uniform or controllable vibration field for general mechanical work. It covers the geometry, spacing and synchronization of the elements within the square grid to achieve desired vibratory performance.
[B06B 1/0633] Cylindrical array -- The code B06B 1/0633 designates cylindrical arrays employed in devices that generate or transmit infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic mechanical vibrations for performing mechanical work. Such arrays consist of multiple transducers arranged in a cylindrical geometry to produce a directed vibration field.
[B06B 1/0637] Spherical array -- B06B 1/0637 covers spherical arrays of transducers that generate or transmit mechanical vibrations in the infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic range for performing mechanical work. The classification includes arrangements of elements positioned on a sphere to produce directionally controlled vibration fields, useful in applications such as medical imaging, non-destructive testing and acoustic levitation.
[B06B 1/064] with multiple active layers -- The subclass covers devices that produce mechanical vibrations by employing two or more active layers arranged together, each layer capable of converting energy into vibratory motion. Examples include multilayer piezoelectric or magnetostrictive stacks used for infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic work.
[B06B 1/0644] using a single piezoelectric element  B06B1/0688  takes precedence -- This subclass covers methods or devices that generate mechanical vibrations-infra-, sonic or ultrasonic-by employing a single piezoelectric element as the source of excitation. The element is driven electrically to produce the desired vibration for tasks such as cleaning, mixing, or material processing. It excludes configurations that use multiple piezo elements or other actuation mechanisms.
[B06B 1/0648] of rectangular shape -- The subclass B06B 1/0648 covers devices or components having a rectangular shape that are used to generate mechanical vibrations in the infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic range for performing mechanical work. It pertains to methods or apparatuses where the rectangular geometry is a defining feature of the vibration-producing element.
[B06B 1/0651] of circular shape -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Spherical shape
[B06B 1/0655] of cylindrical shape -- The CPC subclass B06B 1/0655 covers methods or devices that generate mechanical vibrations-whether infrasonic, sonic, or ultrasonic-using elements that have a substantially cylindrical geometry. It is a refinement of the broader B06B 1/06 series dealing with vibration generation for general mechanical work.
[B06B 1/0659] of U-shape -- The entry covers devices that use a U-shaped configuration to produce or convey infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic vibrations for mechanical work, such as resonators, transducers or waveguides formed in a U shape. It includes methods and apparatus where the U geometry is integral to the vibration generation or transmission process.
[B06B 1/0662] with an electrode on the sensitive surface -- Covers methods or apparatus that generate infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic vibrations in which an electrode is applied directly to the vibration-sensitive surface, typically for piezoelectric or electro-mechanical transducers that convert electrical signals into mechanical motion.
[B06B 1/0666] used as a diaphragm -- The CPC subclass B06B 1/0666 covers devices in which a diaphragm serves as the vibrating element to generate infrasonic, sonic, or ultrasonic mechanical vibrations for performing mechanical work. It includes methods and apparatus where the diaphragm converts input energy into oscillatory motion, acting as the primary source of the vibration.
[B06B 1/067] which is used as, or combined with, an impedance matching layer -- B06B 1/067 relates to vibration generators that incorporate an impedance-matching layer between the source and the workpiece. The layer is a material or structure designed to minimise acoustic reflection and improve energy transmission for infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic vibrations, and may be used alone or together with other device components.
[B06B 1/0674] and a low impedance backing, e.g. air -- The subclass covers methods or apparatus that generate infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic mechanical vibrations by coupling the vibrating element to a low-impedance backing, such as air, to improve transmission of the acoustic energy. Using a low-impedance medium enhances the efficient radiation of vibrations into the surrounding environment.
[B06B 1/0677] and a high impedance backing -- B06B 1/0677 pertains to methods or apparatus that generate infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic mechanical vibrations employing a high-impedance backing, which reflects and enhances the vibratory energy for use in general mechanical work.
[B06B 1/0681] and a damping structure -- This subclass covers devices or processes that generate infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic vibrations and incorporate a damping structure, such as absorbers or isolators, to control or reduce unwanted vibration transmission, improve stability, or protect surrounding components. The damping element may be integrated with the vibration source or positioned in the transmission path.
[B06B 1/0685] on the back only of piezoelectric elements -- The subclass B06B 1/0685 covers devices that generate mechanical vibrations by exciting only the rear side of a piezoelectric element, using the back surface for coupling, mounting or transmission rather than employing both faces. It applies to actuators and sensors where the back-only configuration is essential for the intended vibration mode.
[B06B 1/0688] with foil-type piezoelectric elements, e.g. PVDF -- This subclass covers devices that generate mechanical vibrations using foil-type piezoelectric elements such as polyvinylidene fluoride. It pertains to methods or apparatus for producing infrasonic, sonic, or ultrasonic waves for general mechanical work. The code B06B 1/0688 falls under the broader categories of performing operations and generating or transmitting mechanical vibrations.
[B06B 1/0692] with a continuous electrode on one side and a plurality of electrodes on the other side -- These inventions relate to electro-actuated transducers that produce infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic vibrations, featuring a continuous electrode on one surface and several discrete electrodes on the opposite surface to drive the vibration in a controlled manner.
[B06B 1/0696] with a plurality of electrodes on both sides -- This CPC subclass covers devices that generate infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic vibrations for mechanical work and are equipped with a plurality of electrodes on both sides of the active element, allowing precise excitation and enhanced vibration control of the material.
[B06B 1/08] operating with magnetostriction -- B06B 1/08 pertains to methods and apparatus for generating mechanical vibrations using magnetostriction, a process where magnetic materials change their shape in response to an applied magnetic field. This classification is specific to devices that convert magnetic energy into mechanical vibrations for various applications.
[B06B 1/085] using multiple elements, e.g. arrays -- This subclass covers methods and devices that produce infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic mechanical vibrations by employing several vibrating elements arranged as an array, such as multiple transducers or resonators, to achieve the desired work or transmission.
[B06B 1/10] making use of mechanical energy -- The B06B 1/10 subclass covers technologies that convert stored mechanical energy into controlled vibrations, typically infrasonic, sonic, or ultrasonic, to perform work such as machining, cleaning, or material handling. It includes methods and devices that harness mechanical energy for vibration generation and transmission rather than using electrical or fluid power.
[B06B 1/12] operating with systems involving reciprocating masses -- B06B 1/12 covers methods and devices that generate mechanical vibrations by using reciprocating masses, such as pistons, diaphragms or oscillating plates, to produce infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic waves for performing mechanical work. The classification includes systems where the motion of the masses is driven directly or indirectly to transmit vibration energy to a work medium.
[B06B 1/14] the masses being elastically coupled -- The entry covers devices or methods that generate mechanical vibrations-infra-, sonic, or ultrasonic-by using two or more masses linked through elastic elements such as springs, compliant connectors or flexible couplings, where the relative motion of the masses creates the desired vibration. It includes designs where the elastic coupling defines the frequency and amplitude of the output vibration.
[B06B 1/16] operating with systems involving rotary unbalanced masses -- The subclass B06B 1/16 covers methods and devices that create mechanical vibrations by rotating unbalanced masses such as eccentrically weighted wheels, shafts or flywheels, producing infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic frequencies for tasks like cleaning, mixing or material handling. It is nested under broader categories for generating or transmitting mechanical vibrations.
[B06B 1/161] Adjustable systems, i.e. where amplitude or direction of frequency of vibration can be varied -- B06B 1/161 pertains to adjustable vibration-generating systems that operate in the infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic range, in which the amplitude, frequency or direction of the vibration can be varied during use, allowing the device to be tuned for different mechanical work requirements.
[B06B 1/162] Making use of masses with adjustable amount of eccentricity -- The subclass covers devices that employ masses whose eccentricity can be varied to generate or adjust mechanical vibrations, allowing control of vibration amplitude or frequency. Adjustable eccentric masses are used in generators, tools, and other machines that rely on infrasonic, sonic, or ultrasonic oscillations.
[B06B 1/163] the amount of eccentricity being only adjustable when the system is stationary  B06B1/165  takes precedence -- This subclass covers vibration generators where the eccentric mass that creates the oscillation can be set only while the rotor is at rest, requiring the machine to be stopped before changing the imbalance. It relates to mechanisms for infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic work and is superseded by the more specific B06B1/165.
[B06B 1/164] the amount of eccentricity being automatically variable as a function of the running condition, e.g. speed, direction  B06B1/165  takes precedence -- B06B 1/164 pertains to methods or apparatus for generating mechanical vibrations where the amount of eccentricity is automatically adjusted based on operational conditions such as speed or direction, with B06B 1/165 taking precedence in classification.
[B06B 1/165] with fluid masses or the like -- B06B 1/165 covers methods or apparatus that generate or transmit mechanical vibrations-whether infrasonic, sonic, or ultrasonic-by using fluid masses, such as liquids or gases, as the vibrating element. The fluid may be displaced, oscillated, or forced through passages or cavities to produce the desired vibration for general mechanical work.
[B06B 1/166] Where the phase-angle of masses mounted on counter-rotating shafts can be varied, e.g. variation of the vibration phase -- The B06B 1/166 subclass covers methods or devices in which the phase-angle of masses mounted on counter-rotating shafts is adjustable, allowing the timing of the generated mechanical vibrations to be varied. By changing the relative angular position of these masses, the vibration phase can be controlled to suit different operating conditions or desired effects. This capability is used in systems that produce infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic vibrations for general mechanical work.
[B06B 1/167] Orbital vibrators having masses being driven by planetary gearings, rotating cranks or the like -- This subclass covers orbital vibrators in which the vibrating masses are set into motion by planetary gear systems, rotating cranks or similar mechanisms. The arrangement causes the masses to follow an orbital path, producing infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic vibrations for general mechanical work.
[B06B 1/168] Rotary pendulum vibrators -- B06B 1/168 classifies rotary pendulum vibrators, devices that mount a pendulum on a rotating shaft to create mechanical vibrations ranging from infrasonic to ultrasonic frequencies for general mechanical work such as material handling, mixing or processing, and falls under methods and apparatus for generating or transmitting mechanical vibrations.
[B06B 1/18] wherein the vibrator is actuated by pressure fluid -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Water-hammers
[B06B 1/183] operating with reciprocating masses -- B06B 1/183 covers devices or processes that generate mechanical vibrations by moving a mass back and forth, typically using pistons, shakers or similar reciprocating elements to produce infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic waves for tasks such as mixing, cleaning or material handling.
[B06B 1/186] operating with rotary unbalanced masses -- The entry covers methods and apparatus that employ rotating unbalanced masses to create mechanical vibrations across infrasonic to ultrasonic frequencies for general work such as shaking, feeding or compacting. By spinning a mass offset from the rotation axis, centrifugal forces generate the desired vibratory motion, which can be transmitted to a workpiece or system. This approach is used in vibratory conveyors, compactors and testing equipment.
[B06B 1/20] making use of a vibrating fluid -- B06B 1/20 covers methods and apparatus that employ a fluid set into vibration-typically by ultrasonic, sonic or infrasonic means-to carry out a mechanical function such as mixing, cleaning, pumping, levitation or material processing. The classification includes systems where the vibrating fluid itself is the energy-transmitting medium rather than a solid transducer.
[B06B 2201/00] Indexing scheme associated with -- B06B 2201/00 covers indexing schemes used to catalogue or reference devices and methods that generate or transmit mechanical vibrations at infrasonic, sonic, or ultrasonic frequencies for general mechanical work. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations and generating or transmitting mechanical vibrations.
[B06B 2201/20] Application to multi-element transducer -- The subclass covers devices and methods that use a transducer made up of multiple elements to generate or transmit mechanical vibrations, from infrasonic through ultrasonic frequencies, for performing mechanical work in general.
[B06B 2201/30] with electronic damping -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry B06B 2201/30 pertains to methods or apparatus for generating or transmitting mechanical vibrations, specifically addressing systems that incorporate electronic damping to control or modify the vibrational output. This classification is used by the EPO and USPTO to categorize inventions involving mechanical work performed through infrasonic, sonic, or ultrasonic frequencies.
[B06B 2201/40] with testing, calibrating, safety devices, built-in protection, construction details -- The subclass B06B 2201/40 covers devices that generate or transmit mechanical vibrations of infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic frequency where the invention includes built-in testing, calibration, safety or protection mechanisms, or details of construction. It pertains to apparatuses designed for performing mechanical work while incorporating integrated safeguards and verification features. This entry falls under the broader category of performing operations involving the generation or transmission of mechanical vibrations.
[B06B 2201/50] Application to a particular transducer type -- B06B 2201/50 covers methods or apparatus that generate or transmit mechanical vibrations-whether infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic-for performing mechanical work, specifically when the invention is directed to a particular type of transducer used to produce those vibrations. It falls under the broader class of performing operations that generate or transmit mechanical vibrations in general.
[B06B 2201/51] Electrostatic transducer -- The code B06B 2201/51 covers electrostatic transducers that convert electrical energy into mechanical vibrations, typically in infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic ranges, for use in devices that perform mechanical work such as actuators or sound-generating equipment. It falls under the broader classes dealing with generating or transmitting mechanical vibrations.
[B06B 2201/52] Electrodynamic transducer -- Electrodynamic transducers are devices that convert electrical energy into mechanical vibrations across infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic frequencies by using interacting magnetic fields and a moving coil or armature. They are employed to perform mechanical work such as actuation, sound production, sonar operation or ultrasonic cleaning.
[B06B 2201/53] with vibrating magnet or coil -- The CPC subclass B06B 2201/53 covers devices that produce or transmit mechanical vibrations by means of a vibrating magnet or an electromagnetic coil. It applies to systems operating at infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic frequencies and is used for performing general mechanical work such as stirring, grinding, or ultrasonic cleaning.
[B06B 2201/54] Electromagnetic acoustic transducers [EMAT] -- B06B 2201/54 covers electromagnetic acoustic transducers, devices that use a pulsed magnetic field and a bias current to induce and receive acoustic waves in a material without physical contact. EMATs are employed to generate or detect infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic vibrations for tasks such as nondestructive testing, material characterization or other mechanical work.
[B06B 2201/55] Piezoelectric transducer -- A piezoelectric transducer converts electrical energy into mechanical vibrations, or the reverse, by exploiting the piezoelectric effect, and is employed to generate or transmit infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic waves for performing mechanical work such as actuation, sensing or ultrasonic processing.
[B06B 2201/56] Foil type, e.g. PVDF -- This subclass concerns foil-type transducers, such as those made from polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), that generate or transmit mechanical vibrations-whether infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic-to perform general mechanical work. It includes methods and apparatus where the foil acts as the active vibrating element.
[B06B 2201/57] Electrostrictive transducer -- An electrostrictive transducer is a device that transforms an electric field into mechanical vibrations via electrostrictive materials, enabling the generation or transmission of infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic waves for general mechanical work such as precision actuation, sensing or ultrasonic cleaning.
[B06B 2201/58] Magnetostrictive transducer -- A magnetostrictive transducer uses the magnetostriction effect, where certain materials change shape in response to a magnetic field, to convert electrical energy into mechanical vibrations or the reverse. This type of device is employed for generating or transmitting infrasonic, sonic, or ultrasonic waves in applications such as ultrasonic cleaning, nondestructive testing, and actuation. The CPC code B06B 2201/58 covers such transducers within the broader class of methods and apparatus for generating mechanical vibrations.
[B06B 2201/70] Specific application -- B06B 2201/70 is a CPC subclass that deals with specific applications of devices or processes that generate or transmit mechanical vibrations in the infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic range to perform mechanical work. It falls under the broader group of methods and apparatus for producing mechanical vibrations, within the larger field of performing operations. The entry covers particular uses such as cleaning, cutting, mixing or medical therapies that rely on these vibration technologies.
[B06B 2201/71] Cleaning in a tank -- The classification B06B 2201/71 covers apparatuses and processes that employ mechanical vibrations, typically ultrasonic, to clean the interior surfaces of tanks or vessels. It includes sonication probes, transducers, and associated fluid handling that generate vibration energy to dislodge contaminants. The entry is used for technologies such as ultrasonic tank cleaners in industrial or laboratory settings.
[B06B 2201/72] Welding, joining, soldering -- The B06B 2201/72 subclass covers methods and apparatus that employ mechanical vibrations-typically ultrasonic, sonic or infrasonic-to weld, join or solder materials, using vibratory energy to create a solid bond between workpieces. It includes ultrasonic welding, friction-based joining and related vibration-assisted soldering techniques used for general mechanical work.
[B06B 2201/73] Drilling -- This subclass relates to devices and methods that use infrasonic, sonic or ultrasonic mechanical vibrations to accomplish drilling, i.e., the penetration or removal of material by vibration-assisted tools such as ultrasonic or percussive drill bits. It covers any apparatus where vibratory energy is the primary means of generating the drilling action.
