[B07C] POSTAL SORTING -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Machines for sorting heel lifts -> A43D33/06 - Sorting arrangements -> B23Q7/12
[B07C 1/00] Postal sorting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Involving measures immediately/directly enabling postal sorting by spacing, orienting mail pieces for automated sorting/processing, separating mail pieces by type/size.
[B07C 1/02] Forming articles into a stream -- In the CPC, B07C 1/02 covers methods for arranging mail items into a continuous stream to enable automated postal sorting, situated under the broader class of separating solids from solids in performing operations. It includes mechanisms that collect, align and feed articles so they can be processed in a moving line.
[B07C 1/025] Devices for the temporary stacking of objects provided with a stacking and destacking device (interstack device) -- These interstack devices are mechanisms in postal sorting systems that temporarily collect mail items after they have been stacked and before they are destacked for further processing. The device holds the objects in a compact arrangement, enabling controlled release to downstream sorting stages, improving throughput and preventing back-up.
[B07C 1/04] Forming a stream from a bulk -- The code B07C 1/04 covers methods and apparatuses that create a continuous flow or stream of items from a bulk collection to facilitate postal sorting. It includes devices that feed scattered mail into a single, orderly line for subsequent processing.
[B07C 1/06] Orientating -- The B07C 1/06 entry relates to orientating solid items, particularly in postal sorting systems, where mail pieces are detected and turned to a predetermined position to enable downstream handling such as labeling or dispensing. It covers mechanisms and control methods that achieve this alignment.
[B07C 1/10] Sorting according to size or flexibility -- The code B07C 1/10 refers to postal-sorting devices or methods that separate solid items by their size or flexibility, such as mechanisms that route letters, parcels or other mail pieces according to dimensions or material pliability. It covers conveyors, sieves, flexible-item detectors and similar apparatus designed to sort mail based on these physical characteristics.
[B07C 1/12] Separating letters from parcels -- B07C 1/12 covers devices or methods that separate letters from parcels during postal sorting. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for performing operations that separate solids from solids, specifically within the postal sorting sub-area.
[B07C 1/14] Sorting according to length or width -- The B07C 1/14 subclass covers apparatuses or methods that separate solid items, such as mail pieces, by measuring and sorting them according to their length or width. This technique is used in postal sorting systems to organize items based on size before further processing. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating solids from solids, and postal sorting.
[B07C 1/16] Sorting according to thickness or stiffness -- B07C 1/16 covers postal sorting devices that separate mail items by their thickness or stiffness, a method of separating solids from solids within the broader performing-operations field. It applies to systems that sort letters or parcels according to these physical characteristics.
[B07C 1/18] Orientating articles other than in a stream -- B07C 1/18 pertains to techniques and devices that orient individual mail items, such as letters or parcels, when they are not conveyed in a continuous stream. It addresses positioning, turning or aligning each article so it can be correctly processed in subsequent postal-sorting operations.
[B07C 1/20] Sorting according to orientation, e.g. according to position of stamp -- The subclass B07C 1/20 covers devices or methods for sorting mail items by their orientation, such as the position of a stamp, within postal-sorting operations that separate solid items from other solids. It is part of the broader performing-operations hierarchy for separating solids from solids in postal sorting.
[B07C 2201/00] Measures preceding sorting according to destination -- B07C 2201/00 covers measures taken before mail is sorted by destination, including any pre-sorting steps, groupings, routing or identification actions that arrange or separate items according to their intended delivery area prior to the primary sorting process.
[B07C 2201/003] Cancellation, e.g. stamps by stamping, printing -- The entry B07C 2201/003 relates to postal sorting operations that include cancelling postage stamps or other indicia, typically by stamping, printing or otherwise marking them to prevent reuse after the item has been processed for delivery. It covers devices and methods for applying the cancellation during the sorting stage.
[B07C 2201/006] Hazardous Substances, e.g. checking for contamination, explosives, anthrax -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus that examine mail before it is sorted, detecting hazardous substances such as explosives, anthrax or other contaminants in order to ensure safety prior to postal processing.
[B07C 2301/00] Sorting according to destination -- The B07C 2301/00 class covers devices and methods for sorting mail or similar items by their intended destination, typically using automated or semi-automated mechanisms that separate individual pieces from a bulk stream. It falls within the broader categories of performing operations, separating solids from solids, and specifically postal sorting, and includes systems that direct items to different output bins or conveyors based on address, zip code or other destination criteria.
[B07C 2301/0008] Electronic Devices, e.g. keyboard, displays -- B07C 2301/0008 covers electronic components such as keyboards, displays and related interfaces that are incorporated into postal sorting equipment to facilitate destination-based mail sorting. The code falls under performing operations, specifically separating solids from solids, in the postal sorting segment.
[B07C 2301/0016] RFID-tags -- B07C 2301/0016 concerns the use of RFID tags in postal sorting systems, whereby mail pieces are marked with radio-frequency identifiers that enable automated detection and routing to the appropriate destination.
[B07C 2301/0025] Recipient identification providing a unique ID for a mail recipient -- The CPC subclass B07C 2301/0025 covers methods for identifying a mail recipient by assigning a unique identifier, such as a barcode or RFID tag, to enable precise sorting by destination. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating solids from solids, and postal sorting.
[B07C 2301/0033] Returning of unsold articles, e.g. magazines -- The CPC subclass B07C 2301/0033 relates to postal sorting operations that handle the return of unsold printed materials such as magazines. It covers systems or methods for identifying, collecting and routing these items back to the sender or distributor.
[B07C 2301/0041] Sequencing items to a carrier walk sequence or delivery order -- The CPC subclass B07C 2301/0041 covers methods for arranging items on a transport carrier in a specific walk sequence or delivery order during postal sorting. It falls under the broader operation of separating solids from solids and focuses on sorting according to destination.
[B07C 2301/005] Redirection or forwarding of mail pieces -- B07C 2301/005 covers the redirection or forwarding of mail pieces, describing methods and devices that re-route letters and parcels to their intended destinations after initial sorting, often as part of postal handling or delivery adjustments. It lies within the broader categories of performing operations, separating solids from solids, and postal sorting by destination.
[B07C 2301/0058] Verification for the correct postage amount -- B07C 2301/0058 covers methods and devices that verify a mailed item carries the required postage before it is sorted for delivery, including systems that detect, read or measure stamps, printed indicia or electronic tags to confirm the correct amount has been paid, ensuring accurate routing in postal sorting operations.
[B07C 2301/0066] Check for destination address in a database or list -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry B07C 2301/0066 pertains to the process of verifying destination addresses against a database or list during postal sorting operations, ensuring accurate and efficient mail distribution. This classification is part of a broader category that includes separating solids from solids and performing operations related to postal sorting.
[B07C 2301/0075] Packaging or wrapping of sorted mail pieces -- B07C 2301/0075 defines methods and devices for packaging or wrapping mail pieces after they have been sorted by destination. It belongs to the CPC hierarchy under performing operations, separating solids from solids, postal sorting and sorting according to destination, focusing on post-sorting handling of mail.
[B07C 2301/0083] Merging of mail pieces of different categories, e.g. letters with flats -- The entry covers methods and apparatus that combine mail items of different categories, such as letters and flats, into a single stream during postal sorting. It addresses the merging step that follows destination-based sorting, enabling mixed-category handling without separate processing lines.
[B07C 2301/0091] Creating a signature of fingerprint based on graphic appearance of the mail piece, e.g. to avoid barcode printing -- The entry covers a postal-sorting technique that generates a visual "fingerprint" of a mail piece from its graphic appearance, such as patterns or images, to identify and direct the item to its destination, eliminating the need for printed barcodes. This fingerprint is used by automated sorting equipment to perform destination-based separation of mail items.
[B07C 2501/00] Sorting according to a characteristic or feature of the articles or material to be sorted -- B07C 2501/00 covers methods and apparatus that sort solid items by one or more of their characteristics, such as size, shape, color or address information, as used in postal handling systems. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating solids from solids, and specifically postal sorting.
[B07C 2501/0009] Sorting of fasteners, e.g. screws, nuts, bolts -- The code applies to devices or methods that separate and arrange fasteners-screws, nuts, bolts, and similar items-by characteristics such as size, shape, thread type or material. It includes mechanisms that feed, detect and direct each fastener to designated bins or conveyance paths for subsequent handling.
[B07C 2501/0018] Sorting the articles during free fall -- The code B07C 2501/0018 covers systems that sort articles while they are in free fall, typically using air currents or motion-induced trajectories to separate items based on shape, size, weight or other characteristics. It is applied in postal sorting equipment where objects are released and directed into different channels without intermediate handling. This method falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating solids from solids, and postal sorting.
[B07C 2501/0027] Sorting the articles according to a sound -- The CPC subclass B07C 2501/0027 covers techniques for separating solid items by using acoustic signals to identify a distinguishing characteristic. It includes systems that detect or generate sound to classify and direct articles, typically applied in postal sorting operations. The focus is on sorting based on sound rather than visual or mechanical criteria.
[B07C 2501/0036] Sorting out metallic particles -- B07C 2501/0036 covers techniques and devices that separate metallic particles from other solid materials by sorting them according to a physical property such as magnetic susceptibility, conductivity or density. The methods are classified under performing operations, separating solids from solids, and sorting according to a characteristic of the articles.
[B07C 2501/0045] Return vending of articles, e.g. bottles -- The entry covers machines that automatically accept and dispense returned items such as bottles, using a sorting mechanism that identifies each article by a characteristic like shape or material and then controls its release or storage. It relates to automated return-vending systems within the broader field of solid-to-solid separating operations.
[B07C 2501/0054] Sorting of waste or refuse -- The CPC code B07C 2501/0054 relates to methods for sorting waste or refuse, classified under performing operations, separating solids from solids and postal sorting. It addresses the sorting of articles or material based on a characteristic or feature of the items being processed.
[B07C 2501/0063] Using robots -- B07C 2501/0063 covers the use of robotic systems to sort mail items by characteristic, automating the separation of solid objects from other solids in postal handling operations.
[B07C 2501/0072] Sorting of glass -- The code B07C2501/0072 refers to methods and devices for sorting glass items, typically in postal handling, by separating them from other solid materials based on physical characteristics such as size, shape or composition. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations and separating solids from solids.
[B07C 2501/0081] Sorting of food items -- B07C 2501/0081 covers technologies for sorting food items by one or more intrinsic or extrinsic characteristics such as size, shape, color, density or ripeness. The classification applies to apparatuses, systems and methods that separate edible solids from each other using mechanical, optical, acoustic or sensor-based techniques. It falls under performing operations, separating solids from solids, postal sorting and sorting according to a characteristic of the material.
[B07C 2501/009] Sorting of fruit -- B07C 2501/009 relates to equipment and methods for sorting fruit, a specific type of postal sorting that separates solid items from other solids. The entry covers systems that classify fruit according to characteristics such as size, shape, color or ripeness using mechanical or optical means.
[B07C 3/00] Sorting according to destination -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sorting of physical items (letters, parcels, photographic order bags, cheques) which will be routed according to a destination indicated on the item (address, barcode) or with the item (e.g. RFID tag).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - collating the pages of a book -> B42C1/00
[B07C 3/001] Code converters for keyboards in coding stations  code converters in reading devices  B07C3/10 -- The subclass B07C 3/001 deals with code-converter devices used in postal sorting stations, specifically those that translate coded information-such as barcodes or magnetic symbols-into keyboard input for coding stations or reading equipment. It covers hardware that adapts encoded data so it can be entered or processed by conventional keyboards in mail-handling systems.
[B07C 3/003] Destination control; Electro-mechanical or electro- magnetic delay memories -- The code B07C 3/003 covers postal sorting systems that use destination-control mechanisms, specifically electro-mechanical or electromagnetic delay-memory devices that temporarily store sorting information before releasing mail to the appropriate destination. This enables automated handling of items based on their final delivery address.
[B07C 3/005] the transport holders of objects being provided with means for storing the destination signals  sorting devices for transport holders  B07C3/082 -- B07C 3/005 concerns transport holders, such as mail bins or trays, that incorporate means for storing destination signals. The code covers sorting devices in which the holder itself carries address or barcode information, allowing the contained objects to be routed automatically or manually to their proper destinations during postal sorting.
[B07C 3/006] Electric or electronic control circuits, e.g. delay lines  delay devices  per se   H03H7/30 ,  H03H11/26 -- B07C 3/006 relates to electric or electronic control circuits, including delay lines and similar devices, used in postal sorting machines that separate solid items such as mail according to their destination.
[B07C 3/008] Means for collecting objects, e.g. containers for sorted mail items  pile receivers in general  B65H31/00 -- The CPC entry B07C 3/008 covers devices for gathering sorted objects, notably containers that receive piles of mail items after postal sorting. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, separating solids from solids, and specifically postal sorting.
[B07C 3/02] Apparatus characterised by the means used for distribution -- B07C 3/02 refers to apparatus for postal sorting that is defined by the means used to distribute items to their destinations. It covers machines that separate mail or parcels and direct each piece to a specific bin, tray or conveyor based on destination information.
[B07C 3/04] Drum-type sorting machines -- Drum-type sorting machines are rotary devices that transport mail items through a cylindrical drum where they are directed into separate channels based on destination codes detected by sensors or mechanical selectors. The system speeds up postal handling by continuously feeding, reading, and diverting items without stopping.
[B07C 3/06] Linear sorting machines in which articles are removed from a stream at selected points -- B07C 3/06 covers linear sorting machines that remove articles from a moving stream at predetermined points, enabling the segregation of items such as mail according to their destination as they pass along a conveyor.
[B07C 3/065] construction of switches therefor -- The CPC subclass B07C 3/065 pertains to the construction of switching mechanisms used in postal sorting equipment that separates solid items according to their destination, covering the design of the switches that direct mail along different sorting tracks within performing-operations systems.
[B07C 3/08] using arrangements of conveyors -- This subclass covers postal sorting methods that separate mail items by destination using conveyor systems arranged to transport and direct items to appropriate bins or destinations. It falls under performing operations, separating solids from solids and postal sorting.
[B07C 3/082] In which the objects are carried by transport holders and the transport holders form part of the conveyor belts  conveyors with carriers  B65G47/00 -- The CPC entry B07C 3/082 covers mail-sorting apparatus that separates items by destination using transport holders integrated into the conveyor belt itself; the objects are placed in carriers that move with the belt, enabling continuous sorting as the items progress along the line.
[B07C 3/085] making use of transport holders in the form of clamps or grips  cable post  B65G -- The code addresses methods and devices that use transport holders such as clamps or grips, especially cable posts, to secure items during postal sorting that separates solids from solids. It relates to employing these holders to position mail or parcels for automated routing to their destination.
[B07C 3/087] the objects being taken up in transport files or holders which are not part of the conveyor belts  cable post  B65G -- The entry B07C 3/087 covers components that receive or hold items in a postal sorting system while the items are being transported, but that are not integral parts of the moving conveyor belts, such as detachable trays, pockets, or guiding posts used to convey mail toward the destination-sorting mechanism.
[B07C 3/10] Apparatus characterised by the means used for detection ofthe destination -- B07C 3/10 covers postal-sorting machines that incorporate detection devices to identify each piece's destination, enabling automated separation of mail items based on address or barcode information. The apparatus uses sensors or scanning technology to direct items to the appropriate output stream.
[B07C 3/12] using electric or electronic detecting means -- B07C 3/12 relates to postal sorting techniques that employ electric or electronic detecting means to identify mail items and direct them to the appropriate destination, fitting within the "sorting according to destination" subgroup of separating solids from solids.
[B07C 3/14] using light-responsive detecting means -- B07C 3/14 covers postal sorting systems that use light-responsive sensors to detect marks, codes or bar-codes on mail items, allowing automatic determination of their destination. The technology relies on photodetectors or optical scanners that read light-modulated information on envelopes or parcels for routing.
[B07C 3/16] using magnetic detecting means -- B07C 3/16 covers postal sorting equipment that separates mail according to destination by employing magnetic detecting means. The method uses magnetic fields or sensors to identify magnetic tags or markers on mail items, enabling automated separation of solids from solids in the sorting process.
[B07C 3/18] Devices or arrangements for indicating destination, e.g. by code marks -- This subclass covers devices or arrangements that indicate a mail item's destination, such as printed or electronic code marks, labels or signals applied to letters or parcels to aid automated postal sorting. It includes any system that assigns or displays destination information before or during the sorting operation.
[B07C 3/20] Arrangements for facilitating the visual reading of addresses, e.g. display arrangements -- B07C 3/20 covers devices and arrangements that make address information easier to read visually during mail handling, such as illuminated or angled displays that present the recipient's address to operators of sorting equipment.
[B07C 5/00] Sorting according to a characteristic or feature of the articles or material being sorted, e.g. by control effected by devices which detect or measure such characteristic or feature -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sorting according to a detected property/feature/characteristic of the item into fractions (weight, shape, colour, optical properties, applied code, magnetic property)  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - sorting by hand only -> B07C7/00 - separating solids from solids by sieving, screening, or sifting or by using gas currents or other separating by dry methods applicable to bulk material -> B07B
[B07C 5/02] Measures preceding sorting, e.g. arranging articles in a stream orientating -- B07C 5/02 covers methods applied before a sorting step, such as arranging or orienting items within a material stream to facilitate later separation. It includes pre-sorting actions like aligning, spacing, or positioning articles so that subsequent sorting devices can detect the targeted characteristic more effectively.
[B07C 5/04] Sorting according to size -- B07C 5/04 covers devices and methods that sort solid articles solely on the basis of their size, using mechanisms such as sieves, rollers, air-jets, optical sensors or other size-detecting controls. It includes apparatus that separate particles, packages or other solid objects into size-graded streams for further handling or processing. This sub-class falls under Performing operations - Separating solids from solids - Postal sorting in the CPC hierarchy.
[B07C 5/06] measured mechanically -- The B07C 5/06 entry covers sorting processes in which the characteristic or feature of each solid item is determined by a mechanical measurement, such as size, weight or shape, and the resulting data is used to control the sorting device. This applies to operations that separate solids from solids, particularly in postal sorting, where the measured attribute drives the routing of individual pieces.
[B07C 5/065] with multiple measuring appliances adjusted according to different standards, for example length or thickness, which detect the shape of an object so that if it conforms to the standard set by the measuring appliance, it is removed from the conveyor, e.g. by means of a number of differently calibrated openings -- B07C 5/065 covers postal sorting equipment that employs several measuring appliances, each calibrated to a different standard such as length or thickness, to sense the shape of items on a conveyor. When an object meets the dimension set by a particular appliance, it is directed out of the line through a correspondingly sized opening, allowing simultaneous sorting by multiple size criteria.
[B07C 5/07] by calipering using relatively moving article-engaging means, e.g. clamps -- B07C 5/07 covers postal sorting methods that separate solid items by calipering, using moving clamps or similar article-engaging devices to grip and sort items based on measured dimensions or other characteristics. The classification falls under performing operations, separating solids from solids, and postal sorting.
[B07C 5/08] measured electrically or electronically -- The B07C 5/08 subclass pertains to postal sorting operations in which the attribute used to separate mail pieces is determined by electrical or electronic measurement, for example using sensors that detect weight, dimensions, bar-code, RFID or other electronic signals.
[B07C 5/083] Mechanically moved sensing devices, the movement of which influences the electric contact members -- The class covers sorting apparatus where a mechanically actuated sensor is displaced by the article being processed, and that movement changes electric contact members to generate a detection signal used for directing the item.
[B07C 5/086] Magnetic sensing devices -- Magnetic sensing devices are systems that detect magnetic properties or fields associated with mail items and use that information to control sorting mechanisms. They typically employ Hall-effect, magnetoresistive, fluxgate or other magnetic field sensors to identify tags, ferromagnetic elements or encoded markings on letters and parcels, enabling automated routing based on the sensed characteristic.
[B07C 5/10] measured by light-responsive means -- The B07C 5/10 subclass relates to postal sorting in which articles are separated by devices that detect or measure a characteristic using light-responsive means such as optical scanners, photodetectors or laser sensors. It falls under performing operations, separating solids from solids, and specifically covers sorting based on optical detection of features like reflectance, colour, or coded marks.
[B07C 5/12] characterised by the application to particular articles, not otherwise provided for -- B07C 5/12 designates sorting arrangements that are specially adapted to particular articles such as letters or parcels, where the sorting principle is not otherwise covered in the classification. It includes devices or methods that use characteristic-based detection or measurement to control the sorting of those specific items.
[B07C 5/122] for bottles, ampoules, jars and other glassware  sorting and transport of caps  B67B3/06 -- The B07C 5/122 subclass covers apparatus and methods for sorting and transporting caps used on glass containers such as bottles, ampoules, and jars. It includes devices that detect, separate, and route caps in automated handling systems, typically employing sensors or mechanical means to discriminate caps from other elements and convey them to designated locations.
[B07C 5/124] by means of mechanical measuring devices which may also control electrical contacts -- B07C 5/124 pertains to postal sorting where solid items are separated using mechanical measuring devices that can also activate electrical contacts. The devices detect a characteristic such as size, weight or shape and employ that measurement to control the sorting mechanism that directs each article.
[B07C 5/126] by means of photo-electric sensors, e.g. according to colour  sorting according to optical properties  B07C5/342 ; material testing with the aid of optical means  G01N21/00 -- The code covers postal sorting devices that separate solid items using photo-electric sensors to detect optical properties such as colour, enabling sorting based on those characteristics. It belongs to the hierarchy of performing operations, separating solids from solids, and postal sorting, and references related optical-property sorting (B07C5/342) and material testing by optical means (G01N21/00).
[B07C 5/128] by means of electric, for example electronic measurement -- B07C 5/128 covers postal or other solid-to-solid sorting processes that use electric or electronic measurements to detect a characteristic of each item and control the sorting device accordingly. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating solids from solids, and postal sorting.
[B07C 5/14] Sorting timber or logs -- B07C 5/14 relates to apparatuses or methods for sorting timber or logs, separating individual pieces according to detectable characteristics such as size, shape, species, moisture content or other measured features, using mechanical, optical, acoustic or similar control devices.
[B07C 5/16] Sorting according to weight -- B07C 5/16 covers devices and methods that separate solid items by weighing them and then directing each item according to its measured mass. The classification applies to systems that automatically sort objects-such as letters or packages-based on weight, using sensors, scales, or other measurement mechanisms to control the sorting process.
[B07C 5/165] of letters -- B07C 5/165 pertains to the postal sorting of letters based on a specific characteristic or feature, utilizing detection or measurement devices for control. This classification is part of the broader categories of performing operations and separating solids from solids, focusing on automated systems that sort mail items like letters by attributes such as size, weight, or address.
[B07C 5/18] using a single stationary weighing mechanism -- B07C 5/18 covers postal sorting systems that separate solid mail items by weighing them with a single stationary weighing mechanism, using the measured weight as the sorting characteristic. The method relies on devices that detect or measure weight to direct each item to the appropriate destination.
[B07C 5/20] for separating articles of less than a predetermined weight from those of more than that weight -- The B07C 5/20 subclass covers methods and apparatus for separating items in a postal-sorting stream based on weight, specifically isolating articles that weigh less than a set limit from those that weigh more, using sensors or measuring devices to trigger the separation.
[B07C 5/22] using a plurality of stationary weighing mechanisms -- The B07C 5/22 subclass covers postal sorting systems that separate mail items by employing several fixed weighing devices, each remaining stationary while items pass over or under them, allowing the machine to detect and act on the weight of each piece for sorting according to that characteristic.
[B07C 5/24] using moving weighing mechanisms, e.g. moving along a circular path -- B07C 5/24 covers postal sorting systems that employ moving weighing mechanisms, such as scales that travel along a circular path, to separate letters or parcels based on weight or other measurable characteristics. This method falls within the broader class of operations that separate solids from solids by detecting a specific feature of each item.
[B07C 5/26] wherein the counterbalancing effect of the weighing mechanisms is varied during such movement -- The subclass B07C 5/26 concerns postal sorting machines that sort items by a detected characteristic while dynamically varying the counterbalancing effect of their weighing mechanisms during the movement of the articles, allowing the balance to be adjusted on-the-fly to improve sorting accuracy.
[B07C 5/28] using electrical control means -- B07C 5/28 pertains to postal sorting methods that separate mail items by a detected characteristic or feature using electrical control means, such as sensors and electronic actuators that direct each item to the appropriate destination. This subclass falls under separating solids from solids within performing operations.
[B07C 5/30] with associated counting means -- The B07C 5/30 subgroup covers postal-sorting apparatus that separates solid items from other solids by detecting a characteristic such as size, shape or barcode and includes integrated counting devices that tally the items as they are sorted. It combines automatic detection control with a means to record the quantity of sorted articles.
[B07C 5/32] with associated check-weighing means -- B07C 5/32 covers sorting apparatus that incorporates check-weighing means, where items such as mail are weighed and the weight information is used to verify or control the sorting operation. The device detects a characteristic of each article, measures its weight, and ensures correct classification before discharge.
[B07C 5/34] Sorting according to other particular properties -- B07C 5/34 covers methods and apparatus for sorting solid items such as mail based on characteristics other than size or shape, for example magnetic, optical or chemical properties, using sensors or detectors to control the sorting process.
[B07C 5/3404] according to properties of containers or receptacles, e.g. rigidity, leaks, fill-level  control devices for cigarette or cigar packaging  B65B19/28 ; investigation of fluid-tightness of structures  G01M3/00 -- B07C 5/3404 relates to sorting solid items by attributes of their containers or receptacles, such as rigidity, leak-proofness or fill level, and includes control devices for packaging cigarettes or cigars as well as methods for testing fluid-tightness of structures. This subclass falls under postal sorting operations that separate solids based on detectable characteristics.
[B07C 5/3408] for bottles, jars or other glassware  according to size  B07C5/122 -- This CPC subclass covers apparatus or processes that sort glass containers such as bottles, jars or other glassware by their size. The sorting is performed by detecting the dimensional characteristic and directing each item to the appropriate stream, typically in recycling, packaging or similar handling lines.
[B07C 5/3412] according to a code applied to the object which indicates a property of the object, e.g. quality class, contents or incorrect indication  sorting according to size measured by light-responsive means  B07C5/10 ; sorting according to optical properties  B07C5/342 ; for packages  B65D79/02 ; for information carriers  G06K7/00 -- The CPC subclass B07C 5/3412 covers postal sorting methods that separate solid items by reading a code printed or attached to each object, the code indicating a property such as quality class, contents, or an error flag. It includes sorting by size measured with light-responsive sensors, by optical characteristics, and applies also to packages and information carriers.
[B07C 5/3416] according to radiation transmissivity, e.g. for light, x-rays, particle radiation  detecting hidden objects  G01V -- B07C 5/3416 covers methods for separating solid items by evaluating how they transmit radiation such as light, X-rays or particle beams, enabling the detection of concealed objects during the sorting process. The technique is used in postal handling systems where items are classified based on measured transmissivity characteristics.
[B07C 5/342] according to optical properties, e.g. colour -- This CPC subclass B07C 5/342 relates to postal sorting equipment that separates solid items by their optical properties, such as colour, using detectors that sense or measure light characteristics to direct each article to the appropriate destination.
[B07C 5/3422] using video scanning devices, e.g. TV-cameras -- B07C 5/3422 covers postal sorting systems that employ video scanning devices such as television cameras to detect and measure characteristics of items for automated separation. The method uses visual image analysis to control sorting mechanisms, enabling sorting of solid articles based on observed features.
[B07C 5/3425] of granular material, e.g. ore particles, grain  investigation moving fluids or granular solids using optical means  G01N21/85 -- B07C 5/3425 concerns the use of optical methods to examine or monitor the flow of granular substances such as ore particles or grain, either in moving fluids or as solid streams, enabling sorting or separation based on characteristics detected optically.
[B07C 5/3427] by changing or intensifying the optical properties prior to scanning, e.g. by inducing fluorescence under UV or x-radiation, subjecting the material to a chemical reaction -- It covers methods of preparing items for optical scanning in postal sorting by altering their optical characteristics, for example by applying fluorescence agents, exposing them to UV or X-radiation, or subjecting them to a chemical reaction that intensifies their optical response, thereby enabling the sorting device to detect and sort the items based on the modified optical properties.
[B07C 5/344] according to electric or electromagnetic properties -- B07C 5/344 relates to methods and apparatus for sorting solid objects such as mail items by detecting their electric or electromagnetic characteristics, for example conductivity or an electromagnetic signature, and using that information to direct the items to appropriate destinations.
[B07C 5/346] according to radioactive properties -- B07C 5/346 pertains to sorting methods that utilize the detection of radioactive properties of materials or articles, falling under the broader category of postal sorting and separating solids from solids based on specific characteristics.
[B07C 5/36] Sorting apparatus characterised by the means used for distribution -- B07C 5/36 pertains to sorting apparatus whose distinguishing feature is the means used for distribution, such as conveyors, diverters, ejectors or similar devices that separate solid items-e.g., mail-according to detected characteristics or features.
[B07C 5/361] Processing or control devices therefor, e.g. escort memory -- The CPC subclass B07C 5/361 covers processing or control devices used in postal sorting, such as systems that detect, measure, or otherwise evaluate characteristics of mail items and direct them accordingly. It includes auxiliary components like escort memory that track items through the sorting operation to ensure correct handling.
[B07C 5/362] Separating or distributor mechanisms  switches for postal sorting  B07C3/065 ; particular discharging devices for  B65G47/74 -- B07C 5/362 pertains to specific mechanisms used in the postal sorting process, focusing on switches that direct items based on detected characteristics. It also covers particular discharging devices designed for efficient handling and distribution of sorted mail.
[B07C 5/363] by means of air -- B07C 5/363 relates to postal sorting methods that separate solid items from other solids by using a flow of air to direct each article according to a detected characteristic or feature, such as size, weight or barcode. It is a subcategory of performing operations, specifically separating solids from solids, within the broader postal-sorting field.
[B07C 5/365] using a single separation means -- B07C 5/365 covers postal sorting methods that use a single separation device to sort mail items based on a detected characteristic such as size, weight or barcode.
[B07C 5/366] during free fall of the articles -- B07C 5/366 covers postal sorting methods in which items are separated while they are in free fall, using devices that detect or measure a characteristic of each article-such as size, shape, or encoded data-and then direct the falling articles to the appropriate destination. This sub-class falls under the broader group of operations for separating solids from solids in postal handling.
[B07C 5/367] using a plurality of separation means -- The code refers to postal-sorting equipment that separates mail items by employing several distinct separation mechanisms, each acting on a different characteristic such as size, weight, magnetic or optical properties, to direct articles to the proper destination. It falls under the broader category of separating solids from solids within performing operations.
[B07C 5/368] actuated independently -- The CPC subclass B07C 5/368 pertains to postal sorting systems in which the individual sorting mechanisms are actuated independently, each responding to detected characteristics of the mail such as size, shape, or address. Independent actuation allows separate control of devices that separate solid items from other solids without a common drive linkage.
[B07C 5/38] Collecting or arranging articles in groups -- The CPC subclass B07C 5/38 covers methods and apparatus for gathering sorted items into groups, arranging them after they have been separated by a characteristic such as size, weight or destination. It is used in postal-sorting systems where items are first distinguished from one another and then collected for further handling. This subclass falls under the broader categories of performing operations, separating solids from solids and postal sorting.
[B07C 7/00] Sorting by hand only -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sorting by hand / by an operator, e.g. parcels, final manual mail sorting into pigeon holes or specific furniture.  Arrangements facilitating positioning of the articles, reading the address, marking the drop-off point or the fraction (e.g. lights indicating target area), reading or identifying the address (e.g. headsets)
[B07C 7/005] Computer assisted manual sorting, e.g. for mail -- B07C 7/005 relates to systems that employ computer assistance to support manual sorting of solid items such as mail, where a human operator physically handles each piece while electronic data guides the process. It belongs to the postal-sorting branch of separating solids from solids within the performing-operations class.
[B07C 7/02] Compartmented furniture, e.g. pigeon-holes -- The subclass B07C 7/02 covers compartmented furniture used in manual postal sorting, such as pigeon-hole racks or similar devices that provide separate slots for items to be sorted by hand. It includes structures designed to hold and organize letters, parcels or other solids during the sorting process.
[B07C 7/04] Apparatus or accessories for hand picking -- This subclass covers devices and accessories that assist manual selection of individual solid items during postal sorting, such as trays, scoops, tweezers, guides or other tools used for hand picking of letters or parcels. It is part of the hierarchy for separating solids from solids within performing operations.
[B07C 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sorting, with or without orientating, not otherwise provided for, e.g. sorting of table equipment making use of their magnetic properties or shape.
[B08] CLEANING -- CPC class B08, under the Performing Operations group, pertains to cleaning processes and equipment. It includes methods, apparatuses and systems for removing contaminants from objects, surfaces or substances, employing mechanical, chemical, thermal or other techniques, and covers both continuous and batch cleaning operations.
[B08B] CLEANING IN GENERAL -- B08B covers general cleaning techniques and equipment used to remove contaminants from objects, surfaces, or spaces. It includes methods such as washing, rinsing, scrubbing, and the use of cleaning agents, as well as apparatus designed for these operations across industrial, domestic, and commercial contexts.
[B08B 1/00] Cleaning by methods involving the use of tools -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The mechanical cleaning of articles by means of mechanical members such as tools, brushes, or analogous members, with or without the application of fluid.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cleaning hollow articles by methods or apparatus specially adapted thereto -> B08B9/00  **Glossary:** - wipe(n): a piece of cloth or paper, optionally treated with a cleaning agent or solution, for cleaning articles by wiping - wiper: a cleaning element comprising a flexible blade
[B08B 1/10] characterised by the type of cleaning tool -- Group B08B1/10 is impacted by reclassification into groups B08B1/14 , B08B1/143 , B08B1/145 , B08B1/16 , B08B1/165 and B08B1/40 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[B08B 11/00] Cleaning flexible or delicate articles by methods or apparatus specially adapted thereto -- The code B08B 11/00 covers methods and devices specifically designed for cleaning flexible or delicate items such as fabrics, textiles, or soft goods. It includes specialized techniques and apparatuses that avoid damage while removing soils, stains, or contaminants from these sensitive articles.
[B08B 11/02] Devices for holding articles during cleaning -- This subclass covers apparatus that secure or support items while they are being cleaned, particularly flexible or delicate articles; it includes trays, racks, hooks and other holding mechanisms specially adapted for cleaning operations.
[B08B 11/04] specially adapted for plate glass, e.g. prior to manufacture of windshields -- B08B 11/04 covers cleaning processes and equipment designed specifically for plate glass, such as procedures used before windshield production. The methods focus on gentle cleaning of delicate glass surfaces to remove contaminants without damage.
[B08B 1/12] Brushes -- B08B 1/12 pertains to brushes employed as cleaning tools. It includes any brush designed for sweeping, scrubbing or brushing surfaces in manual or powered cleaning operations, covering various shapes, bristle materials and configurations.
[B08B 1/14] Wipes -- Groups B08B1/14 , B08B1/143 and B08B1/145 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups B08B1/00 and B08B1/10 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[B08B 1/143] Wipes -- The B08B 1/143 subclass covers wipes employed as tools for cleaning surfaces, including cloth, paper, non-woven or other materials that may be used dry or moistened with a cleaning agent. It pertains to the method of applying a wipe to remove dirt, liquids or contaminants during a cleaning operation.
[B08B 1/145] Swabs -- Swabs are flexible, usually stick-shaped tools whose tip is formed from an absorbent material such as cotton, foam or synthetic fibres, mounted on a handle for manual use. They are employed to apply, spread, or remove liquids, powders or particulates on surfaces in medical, laboratory or industrial settings, serving cleaning, disinfecting, sampling or de-contamination functions. This entry falls under Performing Operations → Cleaning → Cleaning in General → Cleaning by methods involving the use of tools.
[B08B 1/16] Rigid blades, e.g. scrapers -- Groups B08B1/16 and B08B1/165 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups B08B1/00 and B08B1/10 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[B08B 1/165] Scrapers -- B08B 1/165 concerns scrapers, tools that remove residues by forcing a blade or edge across a surface. These devices are used to dislodge dirt, scale, or coatings from various substrates in industrial, commercial, or domestic cleaning operations.
[B08B 1/20] Cleaning of moving articles, e.g. of moving webs or of objects on a conveyor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The cleaning of articles while they are travelling past at least one cleaning member.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[B08B 1/30] by movement of cleaning members over a surface -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The cleaning of articles by means of cleaning members comprising guiding means for performing a translatory movement along the articles to be cleaned.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[B08B 13/00] Accessories or details of general applicability for machines or apparatus for cleaning -- B08B 13/00 covers accessories or components that have general applicability for cleaning machines or apparatus. It includes any parts, attachments or details that can be used with a wide range of cleaning equipment, rather than items specific to a particular device. This subclass sits within the hierarchy of performing operations, cleaning, and cleaning in general.
[B08B 1/32] using rotary cleaning members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The cleaning of articles by means of cleaning members which are arranged to perform a rotary movement over a surface.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[B08B 1/34] rotating about an axis parallel to the surface -- B08B 1/34 refers to cleaning tools that rotate around an axis that runs parallel to the surface being cleaned. This includes devices such as rotary brushes or scrapers where the motion is aligned with the workpiece, allowing continuous contact while the tool spins. The class is part of the broader CPC group for cleaning by methods involving the use of tools.
[B08B 1/36] rotating about an axis orthogonal to the surface -- The CPC subclass B08B 1/36 covers cleaning operations in which the cleaning tool rotates about an axis that is perpendicular to the surface being cleaned, such as rotary brushes, scrub pads or rotating pads that sweep across a floor, wall or other substrate. This method uses the orthogonal rotation to generate mechanical action that loosens and removes contaminants.
[B08B 1/40] Cleaning tools with integrated means for dispensing fluids, e.g. water, steam or detergents -- Group B08B1/40 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups B08B1/00 and B08B1/10 . Groups B08B1/00 , B08B1/10 and B08B1/40 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[B08B 1/50] involving cleaning of the cleaning members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The cleaning of articles by methods involving the use of tools, brushes or analogous members, further comprising means to clean or regenerate the cleaning members.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[B08B 15/00] Preventing escape of dirt or fumes from the area where they are produced -- B08B 15/00 covers devices or procedures that contain or seal off dirt, dust, fumes or other pollutants at the point where they are generated, preventing their release into the surrounding environment. Typical solutions include extraction hoods, enclosures, filtration systems and localized suction apparatus.
[B08B 15/002] using a central suction system, e.g. for collecting exhaust gases in workshops  removing dust from textile machines  D01H11/00 ; ventilation of mines, galleries or tunnels, e.g. traffic tunnels,  E21F1/00 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Collecting or removing dirt or fumes from the area where these are produced using a central suction system to which a plurality of collecting or removing devices are connected.
[B08B 15/005] comprising a stationary main duct with one or more branch units, the branch units being freely movable along a sealed longitudinal slit in the main duct -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Branch units configured to move along a sealed longitudinal slit in a main duct, the branch units travelling with a source of dirt or fumes.  Example: EP-A1-0174481
[B08B 15/007] Fume suction nozzles arranged on a closed or semi-closed surface, e.g. on a circular, ring-shaped or rectangular surface adjacent the area where fumes are produced  B08B15/04  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Example: DE-A1-102004031881
[B08B 15/02] using chambers or hoods covering the area -- The B08B 15/02 subclass covers cleaning operations in which the workspace is enclosed by a chamber, hood, or similar enclosure to contain and prevent the release of dust, dirt, fumes, or other contaminants generated during the process. It includes apparatus and methods that isolate the cleaning area from the surrounding environment, ensuring that pollutants do not escape. This is used in industrial, laboratory, or domestic cleaning systems where containment is required.
[B08B 15/023] Fume cabinets or cupboards, e.g. for laboratories -- B08B 15/023 covers fume cabinets or cupboards, such as laboratory hoods, that contain and vent hazardous fumes or dust generated during cleaning or other processes, preventing their escape from the work area.
[B08B 15/026] Boxes for removal of dirt, e.g. for cleaning brakes, glove- boxes -- B08B 15/026 covers sealed or partially sealed boxes used to isolate and contain debris and contaminants generated during cleaning tasks such as brake cleaning or glove-box maintenance, preventing their escape into the surrounding environment.
[B08B 15/04] from a small area, e.g. a tool -- B08B 15/04 covers cleaning methods that prevent dirt, dust or fumes generated in a confined space from escaping, specifically when the source is a small area such as a tool. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, cleaning, and cleaning in general.
[B08B 1/52] using fluids -- Group B08B1/52 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group B08B1/50 . Groups B08B1/50 and B08B1/52 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[B08B 1/54] using mechanical tools -- Group B08B1/54 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group B08B1/50 . Groups B08B1/50 and B08B1/54 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[B08B 17/00] Methods preventing fouling -- B08B 17/00 includes methods that prevent fouling during cleaning operations, such as techniques that inhibit the formation of deposits, scale, biofilm or other unwanted material on treated surfaces.
[B08B 17/02] Preventing deposition of fouling or of dust -- The B08B 17/02 class covers methods and apparatuses designed to stop the accumulation of fouling or dust on surfaces during cleaning processes. It includes preventive measures such as coatings, barriers, or operational techniques that inhibit particle deposition.
[B08B 17/025] Prevention of fouling with liquids by means of devices for containing or collecting said liquids -- B08B 17/025 covers devices that contain or collect liquids used to prevent fouling on surfaces, such as traps, channels or reservoirs that direct cleaning fluids away from areas prone to buildup. The classification applies to systems that employ liquid handling to keep surfaces free of deposits in cleaning operations.
[B08B 17/04] by using removable coverings -- This subclass covers techniques that prevent fouling of surfaces or equipment by applying and later removing protective coverings. It falls under cleaning in general and relates to methods that use removable covers to keep contaminants away during operation.
[B08B 17/06] by giving articles subject to fouling a special shape or arrangement -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preventing fouling or deposition of fouling or of dust by means of giving articles subject to fouling a shape or arrangement such that fouling or deposition of fouling or of dust will be minimized or eliminated, e.g. by application of the lotus effect.  Example: DE-A1-102005013560
[B08B 17/065] the surface having a microscopic surface pattern to achieve the same effect as a lotus flower -- A surface engineered with microscopic patterns that replicate the self-cleaning, super-hydrophobic effect of a lotus leaf, causing water and contaminants to roll off and thereby preventing fouling. This approach reduces the need for manual cleaning by encouraging debris to detach naturally.
[B08B 2203/00] Details of cleaning machines or methods involving the use or presence of liquid or steam -- The code addresses the technical details of cleaning apparatus and procedures that rely on liquid or steam as the cleaning medium, covering aspects such as fluid delivery, steam generation, machine design and operational methods for removing contaminants.
[B08B 2203/002] the liquid being a degassed liquid -- This CPC subclass covers cleaning operations where the liquid applied has been degassed, meaning dissolved gases have been removed before use. It includes machines and methods that employ such degassed liquids to improve cleaning efficiency, reduce bubble formation, or enhance contact with the surface.
[B08B 2203/005] the liquid being ozonated -- B08B 2203/005 covers cleaning operations in which the liquid used is ozonated. It pertains to devices or methods that employ ozone-treated water or steam for disinfection or deodorisation. The classification falls under cleaning in general, focusing on liquid-based processes.
[B08B 2203/007] Heating the liquid -- This subclass concerns methods and apparatus that raise the temperature of the liquid used in cleaning operations, including water, detergent solutions, or steam, before or during the cleaning process. It covers heating mechanisms integrated in washing machines, steam cleaners and similar equipment to improve cleaning efficiency.
[B08B 2203/02] Details of machines or methods for cleaning by the force of jets or sprays -- B08B 2203/02 covers inventions that detail machines or cleaning methods that employ the force of liquid or steam jets or sprays to remove dirt, fitting within the broader category of cleaning in general under performing operations.
[B08B 2203/0205] Bypass pressure relief valves -- The B08B 2203/0205 subclass pertains to bypass pressure relief valves incorporated in cleaning equipment that uses liquid or steam. These valves provide a safety function by diverting excess pressure away from the main system, helping prevent damage during high-temperature or high-pressure cleaning cycles. They are classified under cleaning in general within the performing operations section.
[B08B 2203/0211] Case coverings -- B08B 2203/0211 covers protective or functional coverings that enclose a cleaning machine's case, such as liners, sleeves or seals, designed to contain liquid or steam during cleaning. These case coverings may be removable, replaceable or integral and serve to facilitate cleaning, prevent contamination or protect internal components.
[B08B 2203/0217] Use of a detergent in high pressure cleaners -- B08B 2203/0217 covers the use of detergents or other cleaning agents in high pressure cleaners, detailing how the chemicals are introduced, mixed, or applied during the pressurised liquid or steam cleaning process.
[B08B 2203/0223] Electric motor pumps -- B08B 2203/0223 covers electric motor-driven pumps used in cleaning equipment that relies on liquid or steam. The classification addresses the construction, drive arrangement and control of such pumps as part of the cleaning process.
[B08B 2203/0229] Suction chambers for aspirating the sprayed liquid -- The code refers to suction chambers incorporated in cleaning machines that capture liquid after it has been sprayed onto a surface. These chambers create a vacuum to aspirate the sprayed fluid, improving removal efficiency and reducing waste.
[B08B 2203/0235] Cooling the motor pump -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry B08B 2203/0235 pertains to cooling mechanisms specifically designed for the motor pump in cleaning machines or methods that use liquid or steam. This classification is part of a detailed hierarchy focusing on performing operations, particularly cleaning processes and equipment.
[B08B 2203/0241] Combustion motor pumps -- The CPC entry B08B 2203/0241 covers pumps powered by combustion-engine motors that are incorporated in cleaning equipment using liquid or steam. It relates to the design, operation or integration of such pumps for conveying cleaning fluids or generating steam in machines classified under cleaning in general.
[B08B 2203/0247] Hose joints, e.g. quick joints, rotating joints -- The code B08B 2203/0247 covers hose joints used in cleaning equipment that employ liquid or steam, such as quick-connect and rotating (swivel) fittings. These components provide a sealed, flexible connection for transferring cleaning media while allowing easy assembly, disassembly or angular movement.
[B08B 2203/0252] Ecological type pressure cleaners, e.g. by pressure intensifiers -- Ecological pressure cleaners that use pressure intensifiers to increase cleaning power while minimizing water and energy use, typically employing recycled or low-volume liquids or steam for environmentally friendly operation.
[B08B 2203/0258] Multiple lance high pressure cleaning station -- B08B 2203/0258 covers cleaning machines or methods that employ a high-pressure cleaning station equipped with several spray lances, typically using water or steam to deliver jets for industrial or large-scale cleaning.
[B08B 2203/0264] Splash guards -- Splash guards are protective devices fitted to cleaning machines that deflect or contain liquid or steam sprayed during operation, reducing splatter and safeguarding operators and surrounding areas.
[B08B 2203/027] Pump details -- The entry B08B 2203/027 addresses the technical details of pumps employed in cleaning machines that use liquid or steam, covering aspects such as pump type, drive mechanism, flow control, sealing arrangements and integration with the cleaning system.
[B08B 2203/0276] Hose reels specific for high pressure cleaners -- This classification covers hose reels that are specially designed for use with high-pressure cleaning equipment. The reels accommodate the high-pressure water or steam supply lines and facilitate storage and deployment of the hose during cleaning operations.
[B08B 2203/0282] Safety devices -- B08B 2203/0282 covers safety devices for cleaning machines that operate with liquids or steam, such as guards, interlocks, temperature or pressure limit switches, and emergency stop mechanisms, aimed at protecting users and preventing damage during cleaning operations.
[B08B 2203/0288] Ultra or megasonic jets -- The entry covers cleaning processes that employ ultra- or megasonic jets, where high-frequency sound waves are transmitted through a liquid or steam to produce microscopic cavitation bubbles. These bubbles implode on surfaces, providing precise, gentle removal of contaminants in industrial or laboratory cleaning equipment.
[B08B 2203/0294] Wobbling swash plates for high pressure cleaners -- This subclass covers high-pressure cleaning devices that employ a wobbling swash-plate mechanism to create a pulsating liquid jet, enhancing the cleaning action of liquid or steam. It is used for patents on cleaning machines that feature this specific swash-plate design.
[B08B 2205/00] Details of machines or methods for cleaning by the use of gas or air flow -- The CPC entry B08B 2205/00 covers technical details of machines or methods that clean objects using a directed flow of gas or air, such as pneumatic or jet cleaning systems. It includes designs for equipment that creates high-velocity streams to remove contaminants without liquid or mechanical contact. This subclass falls under the broader categories of performing operations, cleaning, and cleaning in general.
[B08B 2205/005] Using the coanda effect of the gas flow/stream -- This subclass concerns cleaning machines or methods that employ the Coanda effect, letting a gas or air stream cling to a surface and generate a directed flow that lifts or sweeps away contaminants. It includes devices with curved surfaces that guide the airflow for washing, drying or particle removal in industrial or domestic contexts.
[B08B 2209/00] Details of machines or methods for cleaning hollow articles -- The B08B 2209/00 subclass covers the technical features of equipment or procedures used to clean hollow objects such as bottles, tubes, or cans. It details design aspects, mechanisms, and operational steps that ensure the interior surfaces of these articles are effectively cleared of residues, contaminants, or deposits.
[B08B 2209/005] Use of ultrasonics or cavitation, e.g. as primary or secondary action -- This subgroup covers machines or methods that employ ultrasonic waves or cavitation effects to clean the interior of tubular or hollow objects, either as the primary cleaning action or as a secondary assistance.
[B08B 2209/02] Details of apparatuses or methods for cleaning pipes or tubes -- This CPC subclass covers technical features of equipment and processes designed specifically to clean the interior surfaces of pipes, tubes and similar hollow components. It includes descriptions of cleaning mechanisms, fluid delivery, agitation and related system components used for removing deposits, scale or contaminants from the interior of such articles.
[B08B 2209/022] making use of the reversal flow of the cleaning liquid -- The subclass concerns machines or methods for cleaning hollow articles that use a reversal flow of the cleaning liquid, directing the fluid back through the article to enhance removal of residues and improve cleaning efficiency.
[B08B 2209/024] by creating a shock wave in the cleaning liquid -- B08B 2209/024 covers machines or methods that clean hollow objects by generating a shock wave in the cleaning liquid, which rapidly agitates the fluid to dislodge contaminants from interior surfaces. This technique falls under the broader class of cleaning in general.
[B08B 2209/027] for cleaning the internal surfaces -- B08B 2209/027 covers machines or methods specifically designed to clean the interior surfaces of hollow objects, such as pipes, tubes or cavities. The classification includes techniques that remove deposits, contaminants or residues from these internal areas, often using fluid jets, brushes, ultrasonic waves or other mechanical, chemical or thermal actions. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, cleaning, and cleaning in general.
[B08B 2209/032] by the mechanical action of a moving fluid -- This subclass covers machines or methods for cleaning hollow articles that rely on the mechanical action of a moving fluid, such as jets of water, air or steam, to remove contaminants from the interior surfaces. It falls under cleaning operations in general and specifies fluid-driven mechanical cleaning of hollow items.
[B08B 2209/04] using cleaning devices introduced into and moved along the pipes -- This subclass relates to machines or methods that insert cleaning devices into hollow articles such as pipes and move them along the interior to remove contaminants. It covers techniques for propelling or guiding the devices through the pipe, using mechanical, fluid-driven or abrasive actions to achieve internal cleaning.
[B08B 2209/045] Making cleaning devices buoyant within a pipeline to be cleaned -- The code B08B 2209/045 relates to devices used in cleaning hollow articles, specifically methods for providing buoyancy to cleaning tools inside pipelines being cleaned. It falls under the hierarchy Performing Operations - Cleaning - Cleaning in General and addresses ways to make the cleaning device float or stay suspended while moving through the pipe.
[B08B 2209/053] being moved along the pipes by a fluid, e.g. by fluid pressure or by suction -- B08B 2209/053 covers machines or methods for cleaning hollow articles in which the cleaning element is conveyed through the pipe by fluid pressure or suction, using the flow of liquid or gas to move the device along the interior surface. This subclass falls under cleaning in general, specifically for cleaning hollow items by fluid-driven motion.
[B08B 2209/055] the cleaning devices conforming to, or being conformable to, substantially the same cross-section of the pipes, e.g. pigs or moles -- B08B 2209/055 covers cleaning devices that are sized to match the internal cross-section of a pipe, such as pipeline pigs or moles, enabling them to travel through and clean hollow articles without obstruction. The conformity to the pipe geometry ensures effective cleaning of the interior.
[B08B 2209/0553] Pigs provided with detection means -- The CPC subclass B08B 2209/0553 covers cleaning pigs that incorporate detection means, such as sensors or measurement devices, to monitor the condition of the hollow article being cleaned, locate defects, or assess cleaning performance as the pig moves through the conduit. This subclass falls under cleaning in general, specifically detailing machines or methods for cleaning hollow articles.
[B08B 2209/0556] the detection means being magnets -- B08B 2209/0556 relates to cleaning machines or methods for hollow articles that include magnetic detection means, such as magnets used to locate, sense, or verify the presence of the item during the cleaning process.
[B08B 2209/08] Details of machines or methods for cleaning containers, e.g. tanks -- The CPC subclass B08B 2209/08 covers the technical specifics of machines or methods used to clean hollow articles such as containers, tanks, or vessels. It addresses design features, components, and operational details that enable effective removal of residues from these enclosed spaces, falling under the broader cleaning hierarchy.
[B08B 2209/085] Cleaning flexible bags -- B08B 2209/085 covers machines and methods specifically designed to clean flexible bags, such as plastic or fabric pouches, by removing residues, contaminants or odours. It falls under cleaning hollow articles and includes techniques like flushing, air-assisted cleaning, ultrasonic treatment or chemical rinsing to ensure thorough internal cleaning.
[B08B 2215/00] Preventing escape of dirt or fumes from the area where they are produced -- B08B 2215/00 covers methods and devices that stop dust, particles or fumes produced during cleaning operations from leaving the work zone. It includes containment enclosures, local extraction, airflow control and filtration systems designed to keep contaminants confined at the source.
[B08B 2215/003] with the assistance of blowing nozzles -- B08B 2215/003 pertains to cleaning processes that employ blowing nozzles to direct airflow, helping to contain dust, debris or fumes and prevent them from escaping the area where they are generated.
[B08B 2215/006] Suction tables -- Suction tables are work-surface platforms equipped with integrated suction systems that capture dust, fumes, or debris generated during cleaning or machining operations, preventing their escape from the work area. They are used to hold workpieces while a vacuum draws contaminants away, maintaining a cleaner environment.
[B08B 2220/00] Type of materials or objects being removed -- This CPC subclass covers cleaning operations that are classified according to the type of material or object being removed, such as soils, residues, contaminants, or specific items. It groups methods by the nature of the target to be eliminated, helping to select appropriate cleaning techniques.
[B08B 2220/01] Adhesive materials -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry B08B 2220/01 pertains to the removal of adhesive materials during cleaning processes, falling under the broader categories of performing operations and cleaning in general. This classification is used by the European Patent Office (EPO) and the United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO).
[B08B 2220/02] Chewing gum -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry B08B 2220/02 pertains to the removal of chewing gum as part of cleaning processes, falling under the broader categories of performing operations and cleaning in general. This classification is used by the European Patent Office (EPO) and the United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO).
[B08B 2220/04] Polymers -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry B08B 2220/04 pertains to the cleaning process involving the removal of polymers, situated within the broader categories of performing operations and cleaning in general. This classification is used by the European Patent Office (EPO) and the United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO).
[B08B 2230/00] Other cleaning aspects applicable to all -- A subcategory for general cleaning methods and principles that are not limited to a particular material, device or industry. It covers broad-scope cleaning aspects such as universal agents, procedures, and equipment that can be applied across diverse cleaning operations.
[B08B 2230/01] Cleaning with steam -- B08B 2230/01 covers the use of steam for cleaning objects, surfaces or materials, including apparatus that generate and direct saturated vapor to remove contaminants. The classification applies to any steam-based cleaning process regardless of the item being cleaned.
[B08B 2240/00] Type of materials or objects being cleaned -- This CPC subclass groups cleaning inventions based on the kind of material or object that is being cleaned, covering solids, liquids, gases, porous substances and composite items, and identifying the nature of the substrate undergoing the cleaning operation.
[B08B 2240/02] Optical fibers or optical fiber connectors -- B08B 2240/02 covers methods and apparatus for cleaning optical fibers and their connectors, including mechanical, chemical, or ultrasonic techniques to remove contaminants and restore optical performance. The classification falls under performing operations, cleaning, cleaning in general, and type-of-materials or objects being cleaned.

[B08B 3/048] Overflow-type cleaning, e.g. tanks in which the liquid flows over the tank in which the articles are placed -- Overflow-type cleaning refers to a method in which a liquid or steam continuously flows over the tank or vessel that holds the items to be cleaned, ensuring the entire interior surface is washed as the fluid overflows the tank's rim. This approach is used for thorough cleaning of containers, reactors or similar equipment without requiring agitation or manual scraping.

[B08B 3/106] by boiling the liquid -- The subclass B08B 3/106 pertains to cleaning operations that employ a liquid which is heated to its boiling point to effect cleaning. Boiling the liquid creates vigorous agitation and steam, enhancing the removal of soils, residues or contaminants from the object being cleaned.
[B08B 3/108] by lowering and raising the level of the cleaning liquid -- This subgroup covers cleaning operations in which the level of the liquid or steam medium is raised and lowered to immerse and then remove the work-piece, typically by filling, draining, pumping or tilting a vessel. The technique relies on varying the liquid height rather than continuous flow, and is applied to general liquid-based cleaning processes.

This classification is used when the cleaning technique involves unique or combined approaches not covered elsewhere in the system.

[B08B 7/0035] by radiant energy, e.g. UV, laser, light beam or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The cleaning of objects by means of subjecting the objects to the action of radiant energy.  Example: WO-A1-02/052347
