[B08B 7/005] by infrared radiation -- This subclass covers cleaning operations where infrared radiation is applied to heat a material or surface, causing moisture, oils or other soils to evaporate or break down without direct contact. It is used for drying, sterilising or removing contaminants from a wide range of items.
[B08B 7/0057] by ultraviolet radiation -- B08B 7/0057 covers cleaning processes that use ultraviolet radiation to disinfect, degrade contaminants or remove residues, providing a method not otherwise specified in more detailed subclasses.
[B08B 7/0064] by temperature changes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The cleaning of objects by means of subjecting the objects to temperature changes.
[B08B 7/0071] by heating  B08B7/0035  takes precedence -- This classification code, B08B 7/0071, pertains to cleaning methods involving heating, specifically when such techniques are not covered in other subclasses or groups. It is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the EPO and USPTO, under the broader category of cleaning operations.
[B08B 7/0078] in a fluidized bed -- This CPC subclass covers cleaning operations carried out in a fluidized-bed, where the object is exposed to a moving stream of fluidized particles or gas that removes contaminants. It is used for methods not classified in any more specific cleaning subclass.
[B08B 7/0085] by pyrolysis -- B08B 7/0085 covers cleaning operations that employ pyrolysis, a process where material is thermally decomposed in an oxygen-deficient environment to volatilize or burn off contaminants. This subclass falls under cleaning in general and is used for methods not otherwise enumerated in the CPC. It is applicable to industrial or waste-treatment contexts where heat-driven chemical breakdown cleans surfaces or bulk materials.
[B08B 7/0092] by cooling -- B08B 7/0092 covers cleaning processes that employ cooling, such as using liquid gases, ice or other cryogenic media to remove contaminants from surfaces or objects. The method relies on lowering temperature to cause debris to contract, become brittle or detach, and may be used in industrial, automotive or electronic cleaning applications.
[B08B 7/02] by distortion, beating, or vibration of the surface to be cleaned -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Example: GB-A-2336428
[B08B 7/022] Needle scalers -- Needle scalers are hand-operated tools with a flexible rod carrying a series of fine, needle-like tips that vibrate to remove rust, scale or paint from metal surfaces, usually powered pneumatically or electrically. They provide precise, localized cleaning and are placed in the CPC subclass for cleaning methods not covered elsewhere (B08B 7/022).
[B08B 7/024] Rotary scalers -- Rotary scalers are powered cleaning tools that use a rotating head equipped with blades or abrasive elements to mechanically remove scale, rust or other deposits from surfaces such as metal, concrete or pipe interiors. They are typically driven by electric or pneumatic motors and are employed in maintenance, restoration and industrial cleaning where high-velocity removal is required.
[B08B 7/026] Using sound waves -- The subclass B08B 7/026 covers cleaning processes that employ sound waves, typically ultrasonic vibrations, to agitate a liquid medium and detach contaminants from objects. It is used for cleaning items that cannot be effectively treated by mechanical or chemical means, and falls under the broader category of cleaning methods not otherwise specified.
[B08B 7/028] Using ultrasounds -- The subclass B08B 7/028 covers cleaning techniques that employ ultrasonic waves to generate cavitation and agitation in a cleaning medium, enhancing the removal of contaminants from objects or surfaces. It applies when no more specific ultrasonic cleaning subclass exists, encompassing any ultrasonic-based approach irrespective of the material being cleaned.
[B08B 7/04] by a combination of operations -- B08B 7/04 covers cleaning processes that use a combination of operational steps-mechanical, chemical, thermal, ultrasonic or other actions performed together to achieve the desired cleaning effect. It applies when no single subclass adequately describes the method because multiple complementary operations are required.
[B08B 9/00] Cleaning hollow articles by methods or apparatus specially adapted thereto -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The cleaning of hollow objects by methods or apparatus specially adapted thereto, the objects being other than containers or pipes.
[B08B 9/02] Cleaning pipes or tubes or systems of pipes or tubes -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Apparatus for cleaning metal pipes by chemical methods -> C23G3/04
[B08B 9/021] Cleaning pipe ends or pipe fittings, e.g. before soldering -- This class covers methods and apparatus for cleaning pipe ends or pipe fittings, typically before joining operations such as soldering or brazing. It includes mechanical, chemical, ultrasonic or other techniques to remove contaminants and prepare the surfaces for reliable connections.
[B08B 9/023] Cleaning the external surfaces -- The code B08B 9/023 covers methods and apparatus designed to clean the outer surfaces of objects, particularly hollow articles, using techniques or equipment specially adapted for external surface cleaning. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, cleaning, cleaning in general, and cleaning hollow articles.
[B08B 9/027] Cleaning the internal surfaces -- B08B 9/027 covers methods and apparatuses for cleaning the interior surfaces of hollow items such as tubes, pipes, bottles or similar containers. The techniques may involve fluid flow, mechanical agitation, suction, or specialized tools designed to reach and treat the inner walls. This subclass falls under the broader categories of performing operations and cleaning in general.
[B08B 9/032] by the mechanical action of a moving fluid, e.g. by flushing -- The CPC subclass B08B 9/032 covers cleaning of hollow objects by using a moving fluid to generate mechanical action, such as flushing the interior with a stream of liquid or gas. This method relies on the fluid's flow to dislodge contaminants, making it suitable for tubes, pipes, containers, or similar cavities. It is a specific technique within the broader category of cleaning hollow articles.
[B08B 9/0321] using pressurised, pulsating or purging fluid  E04F17/126  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Example: WO-A-97/15406
[B08B 9/0322] in combination with a plug, e.g. inflatable mole, to isolate a part of the tube  plugging pigs for stopping flow in pipes  F16L55/1283 -- This subclass covers methods for cleaning hollow articles, such as tubes or pipes, that use a plug-often an inflatable device like a mole-to isolate a section of the tube while a cleaning operation proceeds. The plug may act as a pig to stop flow and protect upstream areas during cleaning.
[B08B 9/0323] Arrangements specially designed for simultaneous and parallel cleaning of a plurality of conduits -- This subclass covers devices configured to clean multiple hollow tubes or conduits simultaneously, using parallel cleaning stations or shared cleaning mechanisms. It pertains to arrangements that allow simultaneous washing, flushing or debris removal from several conduits to increase efficiency in industrial or laboratory settings.
[B08B 9/0325] Control mechanisms therefor -- The subclass B08B 9/0325 covers the control mechanisms used in apparatus or methods that clean hollow articles, such as tubes or pipes, where the cleaning system is specially adapted to the article's shape. It includes devices that regulate fluid flow, pressure, temperature, or motion to optimise the cleaning process.
[B08B 9/0326] Using pulsations -- This sub-group covers cleaning of hollow items such as tubes, bottles or pipes by applying pulsating actions, for example pressure or acoustic pulses, to dislodge residues. The pulsations may be generated by mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic or ultrasonic means and are employed either alone or with other cleaning agents.
[B08B 9/0327] the fluid being in the form of a mist -- The subclass B08B 9/0327 covers cleaning of hollow objects where the cleaning medium is introduced as a mist, such as aerosolized water, solvent or disinfectant. It pertains to methods and apparatus that generate a fine spray to reach internal surfaces of tubes, bottles or similar cavities.
[B08B 9/0328] by purging the pipe with a gas or a mixture of gas and liquid -- The CPC subclass B08B 9/0328 covers cleaning of hollow articles, such as pipes, by introducing a gas or a gas-liquid mixture that flushes out contaminants. It falls under the hierarchy of Performing Operations → Cleaning → Cleaning in General → Cleaning hollow articles by methods or apparatus specially adapted thereto. This method uses purging action to remove deposits without mechanical scraping.
[B08B 9/035] by suction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Example: WO85/02565
[B08B 9/04] using cleaning devices introduced into and moved along the pipes -- B08B 9/04 relates to cleaning hollow articles, especially pipes, by inserting a cleaning device into the conduit and moving it along its length. The device can be propelled mechanically, hydraulically or pneumatically and is designed to remove deposits from the interior surfaces.
[B08B 9/043] moved by externally powered mechanical linkage, e.g. pushed or drawn through the pipes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Example: WO-A1-03/095792
[B08B 9/0433] provided exclusively with fluid jets as cleaning tools -- B08B 9/0433 covers cleaning of hollow objects using dedicated fluid-jet devices, where the jets are the primary cleaning tools. It includes methods and apparatus designed to direct streams of liquid or gas into the interior of tubes, containers or similar hollow pieces to remove contaminants.
[B08B 9/0436] provided with mechanical cleaning tools, e.g. scrapers, with or without additional fluid jets  B08B9/045 ,  B08B9/047  take precedence -- This subgroup covers cleaning of hollow articles using mechanical tools such as scrapers, either alone or combined with fluid jets, employing apparatus specially adapted for the task.
[B08B 9/045] the cleaning devices being rotated while moved -- B08B 9/045 relates to cleaning devices that rotate while being moved, such as rotating brushes, jets or other mechanisms used to clean the interior of hollow articles like tubes, pipes or bottles. The classification falls under cleaning hollow articles by methods or apparatus specially adapted thereto, within the broader categories of performing operations and cleaning. It covers apparatuses that combine rotational and translational motion to achieve internal cleaning.
[B08B 9/047] the cleaning devices having motors for powering cleaning tools -- B08B 9/047 is a subgroup under the CPC hierarchy for cleaning hollow articles. It pertains to cleaning devices that incorporate a motor to drive the cleaning tool, such as motor-driven brushes or agitators, used in general cleaning operations.
[B08B 9/049] having self-contained propelling means for moving the cleaning devices along the pipes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Example: US-A1-2008/001005
[B08B 9/0492] Heavy-type cleaning devices, e.g. crawlers with plural cleaning members  B08B9/051  takes precedence -- A heavy-type cleaning device designed for hollow articles, such as a crawler equipped with multiple cleaning members that act simultaneously on the interior surfaces. It belongs to the CPC group for cleaning hollow objects by specially adapted apparatus, under the broader categories of performing operations and cleaning in general. This subclass includes machines that move through the article and use plural cleaning elements to achieve thorough internal cleaning.
[B08B 9/0495] Nozzles propelled by fluid jets -- B08B 9/0495 covers nozzles that are driven or propelled by fluid jets and used for cleaning hollow articles. It falls under cleaning hollow articles by specially adapted methods or apparatus, within the broader categories of performing operations and cleaning.
[B08B 9/0497] provided with additional mechanical cleaning tools -- B08B 9/0497 relates to cleaning hollow articles with apparatus that incorporates additional mechanical cleaning tools such as brushes, scrapers or rotating rods to improve residue removal from interior surfaces. The tools are integrated into the cleaning system and are specially adapted for the article's geometry. This subclass falls under cleaning hollow items by methods or apparatus specially adapted thereto.
[B08B 9/051] the cleaning devices having motors for powering cleaning tools -- B08B 9/051 relates to cleaning devices that include a motor to power the attached cleaning tool, such as motor-driven scrubbers, brush heads, or squeegees, used for cleaning hollow articles.
[B08B 9/053] moved along the pipes by a fluid, e.g. by fluid pressure or by suction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Example: US-A1-2010/012151
[B08B 9/0535] the cleaning device being restricted in its movement by a cable or the like -- The code B08B 9/0535 concerns cleaning devices for hollow articles whose motion is limited or guided by a cable, rope, chain or similar tether. It applies to apparatuses that are restrained in their movement to ensure controlled cleaning within confined spaces.
[B08B 9/055] the cleaning devices conforming to, or being conformable to, substantially the same cross-section of the pipes, -- B08B 9/055 covers cleaning devices that are shaped to match, or are substantially conformable to, the cross-section of the pipe being cleaned, enabling efficient removal of deposits from hollow articles such as tubes, ducts or conduits. This subclass includes apparatus specially adapted to fit the internal profile of the pipe for thorough cleaning.
[B08B 9/0551] Control mechanisms therefor -- The B08B 9/0551 subclass covers the devices, sensors, and control systems used to regulate cleaning processes for hollow objects such as tubes, pipes or containers. It includes mechanisms that adjust pressure, temperature, flow rate, timing or movement of cleaning media and ensure consistent, efficient removal of contaminants.
[B08B 9/0552] Spherically shaped pigs -- Spherically shaped pigs are round cleaning inserts designed to travel through hollow articles such as pipelines, removing deposits, scale or other residues as they are pushed by fluid flow or pressure differentials. Their shape enables uniform contact with the interior walls for effective cleaning.
[B08B 9/0553] Cylindrically shaped pigs -- Cylindrically shaped pigs are cleaning devices intended to move through hollow articles such as pipelines, using their tubular form to push, scrape or dislodge contaminants from the interior surface. They are usually made of flexible materials and can be driven by fluid flow or an external propulsion system.
[B08B 9/0554] Diablo shaped pigs -- Diablo shaped pigs are specially contoured cleaning tools inserted into hollow articles such as pipes or tubes, where their star-like or disc-like profile scrapes and removes deposits as they are propelled through the conduit. The geometry improves contact with irregular surfaces, enhancing cleaning efficiency in pipeline pigging operations.
[B08B 9/0555] Gelled or degradable pigs -- The code B08B 9/0555 relates to cleaning hollow articles using pigs that are made from gelled or biodegradable material, which can break down or be absorbed after passing through the article. These consumable pigs simplify removal and disposal in pipe or tube cleaning operations.
[B08B 9/0556] at least partially formed of a frozen liquid or gas -- B08B 9/0556 covers cleaning of hollow articles using a method or apparatus that incorporates a frozen liquid or gas, such as dry-ice pellets or liquid-nitrogen jets, to remove contaminants. The cryogenic material is applied partially or wholly to the interior surface, providing a non-abrasive, low-temperature cleaning action. This subclass falls under cleaning hollow articles with specially adapted equipment.
[B08B 9/0557] Pigs with rings shaped cleaning members, e.g. cup shaped pigs -- B08B 9/0557 relates to cleaning hollow articles with pig devices that have ring-shaped cleaning members, such as cup-shaped pigs. It classifies methods or apparatus where these ring elements rotate or move to scrub interior surfaces of tubes, pipes or similar conduits.
[B08B 9/0558] with additional jet means -- The B08B 9/0558 subclass concerns cleaning hollow items, such as tubes or containers, using apparatus that incorporates additional jet means to direct cleaning fluid into the interior. These jet devices provide targeted agitation or rinsing to enhance removal of residues from hard-to-reach surfaces within the hollow article. This method is applied where conventional washing alone is insufficient for thorough internal cleaning.
[B08B 9/057] the cleaning devices being entrained discrete elements, e.g. balls, grinding elements, brushes -- The entry B08B 9/057 covers cleaning devices in which discrete elements such as balls, grinding media, beads or brushes are entrained with the workpiece to provide the cleaning action. These media move through or across the hollow article, mechanically removing contaminants by abrasion, impact or agitation.
[B08B 9/08] Cleaning containers, e.g. tanks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The cleaning of inner and/or outer surfaces of containers by methods or apparatus specially adapted thereto.

[B08B 9/0813] by the force of jets or sprays -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The cleaning of containers by means of a fluid jet, whereby the shape of the container is essential to the definition of the fluid jet applied.
[B08B 9/0817] by agitating or tumbling containers filled with liquid or liquid and abrasive, e.g. chain -- This subclass relates to cleaning hollow articles by placing them in a liquid or liquid-abrasive mixture and agitating or tumbling them, for example using a chain to move the containers through the cleaning medium.
[B08B 9/0821] Handling or manipulating containers, e.g. moving or rotating containers in cleaning devices, conveying to or from cleaning devices  B08B9/20  takes precedence -- This subclass covers devices and methods for moving, rotating, or otherwise handling hollow containers while they are being cleaned, including conveyors, turntables, and robotic manipulators that position the items in or out of cleaning chambers. It is intended for cleaning apparatus that require precise container handling to ensure thorough cleaning and efficient workflow.
[B08B 9/0826] the containers being brought to the cleaning device -- The entry covers the arrangement or method for delivering containers to a cleaning apparatus, including mechanisms that feed, transport or position hollow articles as they enter the cleaning device. It relates to the handling steps that precede the actual cleaning operation.
[B08B 9/083] Removing scrap from containers, e.g. removing labels -- B08B 9/083 covers techniques for extracting scrap material, such as labels or other residues, from hollow containers during cleaning operations. It includes apparatuses and processes specifically adapted to detach and remove these unwanted items from the interior or surfaces of the containers. This subclass is part of the broader category of cleaning hollow articles.
[B08B 9/0835] Removing remnants of closures from bottle necks -- The code B08B 9/0835 covers a cleaning operation for hollow articles in which any remaining pieces of caps, corks, seals or other closures are stripped from the necks of bottles, typically using specialized mechanical or pneumatic tools. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, cleaning, cleaning in general, and cleaning hollow articles by methods or apparatus specially adapted thereto.
[B08B 9/0839] Cleaning milk churns  B08B9/0804 ,  B08B9/20  take precedence -- The CPC subclass B08B 9/0839 covers cleaning of hollow containers such as milk churns using methods or apparatus specifically designed for that purpose. It falls under the broader groups for cleaning in general and is applied when the cleaning technique is adapted to the shape and material of the churn. This subclass takes precedence over related codes B08B 9/0804 and B08B 9/20.
[B08B 9/0843] for large numbers of milk churns -- The code B08B 9/0843 covers cleaning systems and methods designed specifically for processing large batches of hollow containers such as milk churns, using apparatus adapted to handle many items simultaneously and ensure thorough sanitation.
[B08B 9/0848] Devices for inverting or emptying milk churns, applying or unseating covers -- B08B 9/0848 covers equipment designed to invert or empty milk churns and mechanisms for fitting or removing their lids. The devices are adapted for handling hollow dairy containers, facilitating efficient discharge of milk and secure closure or opening during cleaning or processing operations.
[B08B 9/0852] Drainage racks  see  also  A47J47/20 -- Drainage racks are specialized apparatuses used in the cleaning of hollow articles to support and hold items while allowing water, cleaning solutions, or residues to flow freely away from them. They are typically integrated into washing systems or workstations so that liquids can drain efficiently, preventing pooling and facilitating faster, more uniform drying.
[B08B 9/0856] Cleaning of water-treatment installations -- The subclass covers processes and apparatus for removing deposits, scale, fouling or other contaminants from components of water-treatment plants such as filters, tanks, pipes, reactors or similar hollow installations. It includes mechanical, chemical or combined cleaning techniques that are specially adapted to the geometry and operating conditions of the treatment equipment.
[B08B 9/0861] Cleaning crates, boxes or the like  B08B9/083  takes precedence -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code B08B 9/0861 pertains to cleaning methods and apparatus specifically adapted for hollow articles such as crates, boxes, or similar containers. This classification is part of a broader hierarchy that deals with general cleaning operations, with specific focus on the cleaning of hollow objects. However, it notes that the code B08B 9/083 takes precedence when applicable.
[B08B 9/0865] by burning-out  B08B9/40  takes precedence -- This classification code, B08B 9/0865, pertains to the cleaning of hollow articles through a burning-out process, with specific emphasis on methods and apparatus adapted for this purpose. It is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the EPO and USPTO, under the broader category of cleaning in general.
[B08B 9/087] by methods involving the use of tools, e.g. brushes, scrapers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The cleaning of containers by means of mechanical members, whereby the shape of the container is not essential to the definition of the mechanical member used.  Example: DE-C1-3311117

[B08B 9/0936] using rotating jets -- The subclass B08B 9/0936 covers cleaning of hollow objects by means of rotating jets of fluid, where the jets are directed so that they spin around the interior surface of the article. This method is used to remove contaminants from tubes, pipes, or similar hollow parts by creating a high-speed, swirling flow that enhances mechanical agitation and solvent action. It is a specific technique within the broader category of cleaning hollow articles with specially adapted apparatus.

[B08B 9/205] Conveying containers to or from the cleaning machines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The cleaning method or apparatus being characterized by the method or apparatus for conveying the bottles to or from the cleaning machine.
[B08B 9/22] the apparatus cleaning by soaking alone -- B08B 9/22 covers cleaning apparatuses that use only soaking to cleanse hollow articles such as tubes, pipes or containers. The devices are specially adapted for immersion cleaning and do not employ additional mechanical or chemical actions, fitting within the broader CPC group for cleaning hollow items.
[B08B 9/24] and having conveyors -- This subclass covers cleaning of hollow articles such as pipes, tubes or cavities with specially adapted equipment that incorporates a conveyor to move the items through the cleaning zone, enabling continuous or batch processing. The conveyor may be linear, rotary or other types designed for the specific article.
[B08B 9/26] Rotating conveyors -- Rotating conveyors are apparatuses that move hollow articles, such as bottles or tubes, through cleaning stations by means of a continuously rotating belt, chain or drum, allowing the items to be swept, sprayed or immersed while maintaining a steady flow. They are designed specifically for cleaning hollow objects and are classified under the CPC code B08B 9/26.
[B08B 9/28] the apparatus cleaning by splash, spray, or jet application, with or without soaking -- B08B 9/28 covers apparatus designed to clean hollow objects by applying a liquid or cleaning medium as a splash, spray or jet, optionally combined with a soaking step. The devices may use pumps, nozzles or pressurised streams to direct the cleaning fluid through the interior of the article. This subclass falls under cleaning hollow articles by specialized methods.
[B08B 9/283] by gas jets -- B08B 9/283 designates methods or apparatus that clean the interiors of hollow articles by directing jets of gas to dislodge and remove contaminants, covering tubes, pipes, containers and similar items. It falls under the broader group for cleaning hollow articles with specially adapted means.
[B08B 9/286] the gas being ionized -- This subclass covers cleaning of hollow articles in which the cleaning medium is a gas that is ionized, typically forming a plasma that removes contaminants from interior surfaces. The ionized gas may be generated by electrical discharge or other means to enhance chemical or physical cleaning action. It is applied to tubes, pipes, or other hollow components where conventional liquid cleaning is impractical.
[B08B 9/30] and having conveyors -- B08B 9/30 covers cleaning hollow articles with methods or apparatus specially adapted for that task and incorporating conveyors to move the items through the cleaning process. It is a subclass of cleaning in general within the performing-operations section.
[B08B 9/32] Rotating conveyors -- Rotating conveyors are moving devices that transport hollow articles while they are subjected to cleaning, using a rotating drum, belt, or chain to convey items through cleaning zones. They may be integrated with sprays, brushes or other apparatus to ensure uniform exposure. The classification covers designs where rotation provides the primary means of conveyance.
[B08B 9/34] Arrangements of conduits or nozzles -- This subclass covers the design and layout of fluid-conveying conduits, manifolds and spray nozzles that direct cleaning fluids through hollow objects such as tubes or pipes. It includes arrangements that optimize flow distribution, pressure and coverage for internal surface cleaning.
[B08B 9/36] the apparatus cleaning by using brushes -- B08B 9/36 covers devices designed to clean hollow objects such as bottles or pipes by means of brush systems. The classification includes machinery where rotating or reciprocating brushes contact the interior surfaces to remove residues, often integrated with fluid delivery or suction. It is used for describing brush-based cleaning equipment in the CPC hierarchy.
[B08B 9/38] the apparatus cleaning by using scrapers, chains, grains of shot, sand or other abrasive means -- B08B 9/38 relates to apparatus designed to clean hollow articles by means of mechanical or abrasive actions such as scrapers, chains, shot, sand or other similar media. The classification falls under cleaning hollow items with specialized equipment that employs these abrasive or scraping techniques.
[B08B 9/40] the apparatus cleaning by burning out -- The code B08B 9/40 covers equipment that cleans hollow objects by burning out residues, typically using high-temperature flames or incineration chambers. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, cleaning, cleaning in general, and cleaning hollow articles by specially adapted methods or apparatus.
[B08B 9/42] the apparatus being characterised by means for conveying or carrying containers therethrough -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code B08B 9/42 pertains to cleaning hollow articles using specialized methods or apparatus, with a focus on systems that feature mechanisms for conveying or carrying containers through the cleaning process. This classification is part of a broader hierarchy dealing with performing operations and general cleaning techniques.
[B08B 9/423] Holders for bottles, cell construction -- This CPC subclass B08B 9/423 covers holders that support bottles or similar hollow containers while they are being cleaned, often forming the cell structure of a cleaning apparatus. The devices are specially adapted to secure the articles during washing or rinsing operations.
[B08B 9/426] Grippers for bottles -- B08B 9/426 covers gripper mechanisms designed to hold and manipulate bottles during cleaning operations. [B09] DISPOSAL OF SOLID WASTE -- B09 relates to the removal, handling and final disposal of solid waste, encompassing collection, transport, storage, treatment such as incineration or land-filling and recycling methods. The class covers apparatuses and processes used for waste generated by industrial, municipal or domestic sources.
[B09B] DISPOSAL OF SOLID WASTE NOT OTHERWISE PROVIDED FOR -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The disposal of solid waste involving single or combined operations, comprising features related to the disposal of solid waste. Such features must encompass the combination with:  (a) additional steps specific for waste disposal; or  (b) other aspects of the operation specific for waste disposal that are not fully classifiable in a single other subclass.  **Glossary:** - Disposal: Discarding, e.g. dumping, or destroying of waste or its transformation into something useful or harmless. - Solid waste: Includes waste, which although it has liquid content, is for practical purposes handled as solid.
[B09B 1/00] Dumping solid waste -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Disposing of solid waste by means of dumping, either as bulk material or as  discrete elements of stabilized and/or packaged waste. Dumping may be  within underground cavities.  Example: US-A1-2003/053865
[B09B 1/002] Sea dumping -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Dumping solid waste on or into the seabed.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[B09B 1/004] Covering of dumping sites -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Covering dumped waste, the cover means being applied to the top surface of the dumped waste.  Example: US-A1-2002/067953
[B09B 1/006] Shafts or wells in waste dumps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Shafts or wells and providing these in waste dumps, the shafts or wells being manufactured before, during or after dumping the solid waste.  Example: DE-A-4312267
[B09B 1/008] Subterranean disposal, e.g. in boreholes or subsurface fractures -- B09B 1/008 covers methods of placing solid waste underground, such as injecting it into boreholes or filling subsurface fractures, to isolate the material from the surface environment. This subclass falls under disposal of solid waste not otherwise provided for within the broader performing operations category.
[B09B 2101/00] Indexing scheme associated with groups -- B09B 2101/00 covers the use of indexing schemes to organize groups of solid-waste disposal processes that are not covered by other specific categories. It applies to any method or system that classifies, tags, or otherwise structures such waste-handling operations for management, reporting or control purposes.
[B09B 2101/007] Fuel tanks, especially draining thereof -- Category B09B 2101/007 covers devices, methods and arrangements for draining fuel tanks, typically to remove residual liquids or solid residues before disposal or recycling. It includes mechanisms such as valves, pumps or drainage fittings designed to facilitate safe and efficient emptying of tanks used in vehicles, storage facilities or industrial equipment.
[B09B 2101/014] Shock absorbers, especially draining thereof -- B09B 2101/014 covers shock absorbers, particularly designs or methods for draining them, as a specific aspect of solid-waste disposal not otherwise provided for. The class falls under the CPC section on performing operations, sub-section disposal of solid waste, and addresses equipment that mitigates impact and facilitates removal of collected material.
[B09B 2101/018] Animal meals -- The CPC subclass B09B 2101/018 covers animal meals used as a form of solid waste disposal not otherwise specified, encompassing processes for converting animal by-products into feed or fertilizer within waste management operations.
[B09B 2101/02] Gases or liquids enclosed in discarded articles, e.g. aerosol cans or cooling systems of refrigerators -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Disposal of waste, whereby the type of waste concerns discarded articles comprising gaseous or liquid materials. The disposal typically concerns actively removing the gaseous or liquid materials.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[B09B 2101/05] Vehicles -- In the CPC, B09B 2101/05 falls under Performing Operations - Disposal of Solid Waste - Disposal of Solid Waste Not Otherwise Provided For and relates to vehicles used for collection, transport or disposal of solid waste where no more specific vehicle subclass applies.
[B09B 2101/10] Cans -- Cans refers to the handling and disposal of solid waste in the form of metal or other containers, such as beverage or food cans, that are not covered by more specific waste-disposal categories. It includes methods for collecting, transporting and discarding empty cans as a distinct waste stream.
[B09B 2101/15] Electronic waste -- The CPC subgroup B09B 2101/15 relates to the treatment, recycling or disposal of electronic waste, such as discarded computers, mobile phones, consumer electronics and similar devices. It covers processes for separating hazardous components, recovering valuable materials and safely managing the remaining residues, fitting within the broader category of solid-waste disposal not otherwise specified.
[B09B 2101/16] Batteries -- B09B 2101/16 covers the disposal of batteries as solid-waste when no specific provisions exist. It includes methods, systems, and devices for safely handling, recycling or discarding used batteries to prevent environmental harm.
[B09B 2101/17] Printed circuit boards [PCB] -- The CPC subclass B09B 2101/17 covers technologies for the disposal of printed circuit boards (PCBs) within the broader category of performing operations, specifically under disposal of solid waste not otherwise provided for. It includes methods, apparatus and processes for treating, recycling or otherwise managing PCB waste.
[B09B 2101/18] Mobile phones -- This subclass covers mobile phone devices or communication systems employed in solid-waste disposal operations that are not otherwise specified, such as remote monitoring, data transmission, or control of collection and treatment equipment.
[B09B 2101/20] Filters, e.g. oil filters -- B09B 2101/20 relates to filters employed in the disposal of solid waste, particularly oil filters that separate oil from waste streams. The classification covers designs and structures of such filters used to retain solid particles while allowing liquids or oils to pass, supporting efficient waste handling and recovery processes.
[B09B 2101/25] Non-industrial waste, e.g. household waste -- B09B 2101/25 covers the disposal of non-industrial solid waste such as household refuse. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, disposal of solid waste, and disposal of solid waste not otherwise provided for, and is used to classify methods, apparatuses or systems specifically dealing with everyday domestic waste treatment.
[B09B 2101/30] Incineration ashes -- B09B 2101/30 refers to the handling and disposal of ashes produced by incinerating solid waste. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, disposal of solid waste, and waste disposal not otherwise provided for, and addresses methods for managing, treating, or finalizing incineration residues.
[B09B 2101/35] Asbestos -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Disposal of waste whereby the type of waste concerns asbestos or asbestos-containing waste.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[B09B 2101/40] Asphalt -- The CPC subclass B09B 2101/40 deals with methods and apparatus for disposing of solid waste that involve handling, treating or recycling asphalt, such as crushing, reusing or repurposing it in road construction or other applications. It is nested under performing operations, disposal of solid waste and disposal of solid waste not otherwise provided for.
[B09B 2101/45] Concrete -- B09B 2101/45 covers concrete as a material used in the disposal of solid waste that is not otherwise specified in the CPC hierarchy. It falls under the broader sections for performing operations and waste disposal, specifically addressing concrete's role in stabilising, containing or encapsulating waste materials. This code is used by the EPO and USPTO to classify patents involving concrete-based waste management solutions.
[B09B 2101/50] Glass -- B09B 2101/50 relates to the handling, treatment or disposal of glass as solid waste, covering methods, apparatus or processes for recovering, recycling or safely discarding glass fragments, containers or cullet within waste-management systems. It is a subcategory of disposal of solid waste not otherwise provided for.
[B09B 2101/55] Slag -- B09B 2101/55 classifies slag, the solid by-product generated in metal-working and smelting operations, and addresses its handling, storage or disposal when specific provisions are absent. It falls under disposal of solid waste not otherwise provided for within the performing-operations field.
[B09B 2101/60] Ceramics, e.g. pottery -- B09B 2101/60 refers to the use of ceramic materials, such as pottery, in systems for disposing of solid waste that are not otherwise specified. It includes inventions where ceramic components serve as containers, liners, filters or structural parts in waste-treatment or landfill apparatus.
[B09B 2101/65] Medical waste -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Disposal of waste whereby the type of waste concerns medical waste.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[B09B 2101/67] Diapers or nappies -- The CPC subclass B09B 2101/67 covers methods for disposing of used diapers or nappies, treating these hygiene products as solid waste that is not addressed by other specific disposal categories. It falls under the broader hierarchy of performing operations, disposal of solid waste, and disposal of solid waste not otherwise provided for, describing techniques for collection, handling, and treatment of these items.
[B09B 2101/68] Transdermal patches -- B09B 2101/68 designates the disposal of used transdermal patches, a form of solid waste that does not fall under more specific waste-treatment categories. It addresses methods and apparatus for handling, storing, or discarding these medical patches after use, fitting within the broader performing-operations group for solid-waste disposal.
[B09B 2101/70] Kitchen refuse -- B09B 2101/70 - Kitchen refuse relates to the handling of household food waste, including the collection, storage, treatment and disposal of items such as food scraps, peelings and other organic kitchen residues that are not covered by more specific waste-disposal categories.
[B09B 2101/75] Plastic waste -- This CPC subclass B09B 2101/75 relates to processes for handling and disposing of plastic waste, covering methods and apparatuses used to treat, recycle, or otherwise manage plastic residues that are not otherwise specified in other waste-disposal categories.
[B09B 2101/77] containing chlorine -- The CPC subclass B09B 2101/77 addresses methods for disposing of solid waste that contains chlorine, such as chlorinated polymers or hazardous residues. It is situated within the hierarchy Performing Operations > Disposal of Solid Waste > Disposal of Solid Waste Not Otherwise Provided For.
[B09B 2101/78] containing foamed plastics, e.g. polystyrol -- Group B09B2101/78 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group B09B2101/02 . Groups B09B2101/02 and B09B2101/78 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[B09B 2101/80] Rubber waste, e.g. scrap tyres -- B09B 2101/80 covers the handling of rubber waste, including scrap tyres, within the broader category of disposal of solid waste not otherwise provided for. It relates to methods and systems for collecting, pre-treating, recycling, or otherwise managing waste rubber materials.
[B09B 2101/85] Paper -- The CPC subclass B09B 2101/85 falls under Performing Operations → Disposal of Solid Waste → Disposal of Solid Waste Not Otherwise Provided For and covers technologies for handling, treating, recycling, or otherwise disposing of paper waste, including processes such as shredding, pulping, incineration, composting and recovery of fibers.
[B09B 2101/90] Soil, e.g. excavated soil from construction sites -- B09B 2101/90 covers the handling of soil, such as excavated material from construction sites, within the CPC hierarchy for disposal of solid waste not otherwise provided for. It includes methods, devices or systems for transporting, storing, treating or otherwise managing this type of waste soil.
[B09B 2101/95] Waste catalysts -- Waste catalysts are spent or used catalytic materials that, after serving their intended chemical function, become solid waste requiring disposal. The entry covers the handling, treatment, or disposal methods for these exhausted catalysts when they are not covered by other specific waste categories, fitting within the broader class of disposal of solid waste not otherwise provided for.
[B09B 3/00] Destroying solid waste or transforming solid waste into something useful or harmless -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Destroying solid waste or transforming solid waste or contaminated solids into  something useful or harmless, as long as this is not fully classifiable in a  single other subclass.

Example: US-A-5656248
[B09B 3/10] involving an adsorption step -- The B09B 3/10 subclass covers processes for disposing of solid waste by first employing an adsorption step to bind contaminants or hazardous constituents before further treatment or conversion. It falls under the broader category of destroying solid waste or turning it into harmless or useful products, specifically within disposal methods not otherwise provided for. This approach is used to enhance removal efficiency and reduce the environmental impact of the waste.

[B09B 3/45] Steam treatment, e.g. supercritical water gasification or oxidation -- B09B 3/45 covers the use of high-temperature steam processes, such as supercritical water gasification or oxidation, to destroy solid waste or convert it into harmless or useful products. The methods involve treating waste in a steam-rich environment to achieve chemical breakdown, gasification, or oxidation, facilitating safe disposal or resource recovery.

[B09B 3/60] Biochemical treatment, e.g. by using enzymes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Features related to processes that dispose solid waste via biochemical treatment, e.g. by using enzymes.
[B09B 3/65] Anaerobic treatment -- The B09B 3/65 entry covers anaerobic treatment methods applied to solid waste that is not otherwise specified, using oxygen-free biological processes to break down material. It includes technologies such as anaerobic digestion and fermentation that convert waste into biogas, compost, or other harmless, useful products.

[B09B 3/80] involving an extraction step -- **Glossary:** - Extraction: Treatment of solid waste with solvents to separate the desired substance by using the difference in solubility.

All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[B09C] RECLAMATION OF CONTAMINATED SOIL -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Different devices and methods of reclaiming contaminated soil.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - gatherers for removing stone or the like from the soil -> A01B43/00 - sterilising soil by steam -> A01G11/00 - removing undesirable matter, e.g. rubbish, from the land -> E01H15/00  **Glossary:** - Reclamation: the partial or total elimination or the fixing of contaminants in soil.
[B09C 1/00] Reclamation of contaminated soil -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Removing contaminants from contaminated soil. Reclamation of contaminated  soil comprising systems and methods generally applicable to any type of  reclamation of contaminated soil.  Example: FR-A1-2872719
[B09C 1/002] involving in-situ ground water treatment -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Any type of in-situ ground water treatment.  Example: CH-A5-696639
[B09C 1/005] Extraction of vapours or gases using vacuum or venting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Removing contaminants from contaminated soil by means of soil venting.  Example: US-A1-2009/080979
[B09C 1/007] by removing contaminants floating on the water table -- B09C 1/007 pertains to methods for reclaiming contaminated soil specifically by addressing and removing contaminants that are present on the surface of the water table. This involves techniques aimed at cleaning groundwater while it remains in situ, focusing on pollutants that have accumulated or are floating atop the water.
[B09C 1/02] Extraction using liquids, e.g. washing, leaching -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Removing contaminants from contaminated soil by means of contacting the  contaminated soil with a liquid.  Example: DE-A1-4228966
[B09C 1/025] using an oil as solvent or extracting agent -- This subclass covers methods that employ an oil, either mineral or vegetable, as a solvent or extracting medium to remove contaminants from polluted soil. It is used in soil reclamation processes within the broader categories of performing operations and disposal of solid waste.
[B09C 1/06] thermally -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Removing contaminants from contaminated soil by means of a thermal  treatment, such as desorbing, volatilizing.  Example: WO-A1-2007046688
[B09C 1/062] by using electrode or resistance heating elements -- B09C 1/062 covers techniques for reclaiming contaminated soil that employ electrical or resistance heating elements to raise temperature and facilitate removal or decomposition of pollutants. The approach typically involves inserting electrodes into the ground and passing current to thermally treat the soil, enabling efficient remediation of hazardous substances.
[B09C 1/065] by pyrolysis -- This subclass addresses reclamation of contaminated soil by pyrolysis, a thermal process that decomposes organic pollutants into gases, liquids and solid residues, reducing toxicity and allowing soil reuse. It falls under performing operations for disposal of solid waste, specifically the reclamation of polluted ground.
[B09C 1/067] by vitrification -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Rendering contaminated soil harmless by means of vitrification.  Example: WO-A1-94/04761
[B09C 1/08] chemically -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Rendering contaminated soil harmless by means of chemical treatment.  Removing contaminants from contaminated soil by means of chemical  treatment. Binding of soil contaminants.
[B09C 1/085] electrochemically, e.g. by electrokinetics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electrochemical treatment of contaminated soil.  Example: US-A-5458747
[B09C 1/10] microbiologically, or by using enzymes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Rendering contaminated soil harmless by means of biological treatment.  Removing contaminants from contaminated soil by means of biological  treatment.  Example: US-B1-6796741
[B09C 1/105] using fungi or plants -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Removing contaminants from contaminated soil by means of fungi or plants.  Example: US-A1-2004/101945
[B09C 2101/00] In situ -- The B09C 2101/00 subclass covers technologies for reclaiming contaminated soil directly at the site, employing in-situ methods that treat or stabilize the waste without excavation. It includes processes such as on-site bioremediation, chemical stabilization, and physical containment aimed at restoring soil quality while minimizing transport and handling of hazardous material.
